It may not however be amik to give the pa- tient half an ounce of Glauber's fait, and the fame quantity ef manna, diffolved.in four or five ounces of barley-water. This may be taken at a draught, and repeated, if it does not operate, in a few hours. Ten or twelve grains of nitre may be taken in a glafs of cold water and vinegar every hour, or oftener, if the ftomach will bear!::. If a ftronger medicine be necefLry, a tea-cupful of the tindure of rofes, with twenty or thirty drops of the weak fpirit of vitriol, may be taken every hour. When thefe things cannot be had, the patient may drink water, with a litde common fait in it,. or equal parts of water and vinegar*. » If the genitals be immerfed for fome time in cold water, it will generally ftcp a bleeding at the nofe. I have not known this fail. Sometimes,when the bleeding is ftopped outwardly, it continues inwardly. This is very troubkfome,and requires particular atten- tion, as the patient is apt to be fuffocated with the blood, efpecially if he falls afleep, which he is very ready to do after loofing a great quantity of blood. When the patient is in danger of fuffocation from the blood get- ing into his throat,the paffages may be flopped by drawing threads up the no(lrils,an4 bringing them out at the mouth, then faflening pieces of fponge, or fmall rolls of linen cloth to their extremities ; afterwards drawing them back, and tying them on the outfide with a fufficient degree of tightnefs. After the bleeding is ftopped, the patient ought to be kept as eafy and as quiet as poflibk. He fliould not pick his nofe, nor take away the tents or clotted blood, till they fall off of their own accord, and fhould not lie with his head low. Thofe who are affeded with frequent bleeding at the nofe ought to bathe their feet often in warm water,and to keep them waim and dry. They ought to wear nothing tight about their necks, to keep their body as much in an ered pofture as poflibk, and never to view any objed obliquely. If they have too much blood a vegetable diet, with now & then a cooling purge, is the fafeft way to leffen it. But when the difeafe proceeds from a thin diffolved ftate of the blood, die diet fhould be rich and nourifhing; as ftrong broths and jellies, fago-gruel with wine and fugar, Sec, Infufions of the Pe- ruvian bark in wine ought Iikewife to be taken and pe: filled in for a confiderable time. OF THE BLEEDING AND BLIND PILES. A DISCHARGE of blood from the hemorrhoidal veffels is tailed the bleeding piles. When the veffels only fwell, and dif- charge * From ten to twenty drops o>f the oil of turpentine in a little water given ii&. quently, feldom fail to flop a Heeding at the nofe, or from 2ny other part 212 BLEEDING akb BLIND PILES. charge no blood,but are exceeding pamful, the difeafe ia called the blind piles. Perfons of a loofe fpongy fibre, of a bulky fize, who live high, and lead a fedentary, inadivc life, are moft fubjed to this difeafe. It is often owing to an hereditary difprfitfon. Where this is the cafe,it attacks perfons more early in life than when it is accidental. Men are more liable to it than women, efpecially thofe of a fan- guine, plethoric, or feorbutic habit, or of a melancholy difpofition. The piles may be occafioned by, an excefs of blood, by firing aloetic purges, high feafoned food, drinking great quantities of fweet wmes.thenegled of bkeding.or other cuftomary evacuations, much Tiding, great coftivenefs, or any thing that occafions hard or difficult ftools.' Anger, grief, or ofher violent paflions will Iikewife occafion the piks. I have often known them brought on by fitting on the damp ground. A pair of thin breeches will excite the diforder in a perfon who is fubjed to it, and fometimes even in thofe wno never had it before. Pregnant women are often afflided With the piles. ,.rA flux of blood from the anus, is not always to be treated as a difeafe. It is even more falatary than bleeding at the nofe, and often prevents or carries off difeafes. It is peculiarly beneficial in the gout, rheumatifm,afthma,and hypochondriacal complaints,and often proves critical in colics, and v flammatory fevers. In the management of the patient, regaid muft behad to his ha- bit of body, his age, ftrength, and manner of living. A difcharge which might be exceffive and prove hurtful to one.may be very mo- derate, and even falutary to another. That only is to be efteemcd dangerous which continues too long, and is in fuch quantity as to wafte the patient's ftrength, hurt the digeftion, nutrition, and other fundions neceffary to life. When this is the cafe, die difchargemuft be checked by a proper regimen, and aftringent medicines. The DIET muft be cool but nourifhing, confifting chiefly of bread,milk, cooling vegetables.and broths. The drink may be chalybeate water, orange-whey, decoc- tions or infufions of the aftringent and mucilaginous plants, as the tormentil root, birtort, the marinmallow-roots, &c. Old conferve of rofes is a very good medicine in this cafe. It maty be mixed in new milk, and may be taken in the quantity of an ounce three or four times a-day. This medicine is in no great re- pute, owing to its being feldom taken in fuch quantity as to pro- duce any effeds ; but when taken as here direded,and duly perfift- ed in, 1 have know it perform very extraordinary cures in violent haemorrhages, efpecially when affifted by the tindure of roles ; a tea-cupful of which may be taken about an hour after every dofe of the conferve. The Peruvian bark is Iikewife proper in this cafe, both as a ftrengthener and aftringent. Half a drachm of it may be taken in a glals of red wine, fharpened with a few drops of the elixir of vi- triol, three or four times a-day. The bleeding piles are fomtimes periodical, and return regular- ly once a month, or once ia three weeks. In this cafe they are always SPITTING or BLOOD. *»S always to be confidered as a falutary difcharge, and by no means to be ftopped. Some have entirely ruined their health by 'lopping a periodical difcharge "of blood from the hxmorrhoidal veins. In the bind pile* bleeding is generally of ufe. The diet mull be light and thin, and the drink cool and diluting. It is likewne re- ceffary that the body be kept generally open. This may be nine by fmall dofes of the flower's of brirnftone and cream of tartar. Thefe may be mixed in equal quantities, and a tea-fpoonful taken two cr three times a-day, or oftener if neceflai y. Or an ounce of the flowers of briniitcne and half an ounce of purified nitre may be mixed with three or four ounces iof the lenitive efeduary, and a tea-fpoonful of jt taken three m four times a-ciay. Emollient-clyfters are here Iikewife beneficial ; but diere is fome times inch an aftridion of the anus, that they cannot be dirown up. In this cafe I have known a vomit have a very good effed. When the piles are exceeding painful and fwelled, but difcharge nothing, the patient muft fit over the fleams of warm water. He may Iikewife apply a linen cloth dipped in warm fpirits <$f wine to die part, or poultices made of bread and milk, or of keks fried with butter. If thefe-do not produce a difcharge, and the piles ap- pear large, feeches muft be applied as near them ab poflibk, o., if they will fix upon the piles thtmfelvep, fo much the better. When leeches will not fix, the piles may be opened with a lancent. The operation is very eafy and is attended with no danger. Variou? ointments and other external applications, are recommended in the piles but I do not remember to have feen any effeds from tiiefe worth-mentioning. Their principal ufe is to keep the part mo; ft, which may be done as well by a foft poultice,, or an emollient 'cataplafm. When the pain however is very great, a liniment made of two ounces of emollient ointment, and half an ounce of liquid laudanum, beat up with the yolk of an egg, may be applied. SPITTING OF BLOOD. We mean hare to treat of that difcharge of blood from the lungs nnly which is called an hamoptoe, cr fpitt ng of blood. Perions ot a flender make, and a lax fibre, who have long necks and ftrait breafts, are rn<?ft liable to this difeafe. It is moft common in die. fpring and generally attacks peopk before they are at the prime or- middle period cf life. It is a common obfervation, that thofe who ha.ve been fubjed to bleeding at the nofe when young, arc after- wards mo.ft liable to an haemoptoe. CAUSES.—An hsemoptoe may proceed from exce:s cf blood,. from a peculiar weaknefs of the lungs, or a bad coi.formation cfr the breaft. It is often occafioned by exceffive drinking, running, wreftling, finging,or fpeaking aloud. Such as have weak luugs ought to avoid all violent exertions of that organ, as they value life. They fhould Iikewife guard againft violent paflions, excef- five drinking, and every thing that occafions a rapid circulation ot the blood. This *'4 SPITTING of BLOOD. This difeafe may Iikewife proceed from wounds of the lungs. Thefe may either be received from without, or they may be occa- fioned by hard bodies getting into the wind-pipe, and fo falling down upon die lungs.and hurting that tender organ. The obftruc- ,tion of any cuftomary evacuation may occafion a fpitting of blood ; as negled of bleeding or purging at the ufual feafons, the ftoppage of the bleeding piles in men, or the menfes in women, &c. It may Iikewife proceed from a polypus, fcirrhous concretions; or any thing that obftruds the circulation of the blood in the lungs. It is often the effed of a long and violent cough; in which cafe it is generally the forerunner of a confumption. A violent degree of cold fuddenly applied to the external parts of the body will occa- fion an h«moptoe. It may Iikewife be occafioned by breathing air which is too much ratified to be able properly to expand the lungs. This is often the cafe with thofe who work in hot places, as fur- naces, glafs-houfes, or the like. It is Iikewife faid to happen to fuch as afeend to the top of very high mountains, as the Peak of Teneriff, &c. Spitting of blood is not always to be confidered as a primary dis- eafe. It is often only a fymptom,and in fome difeafes not, an unfa- vourable one. This is the cafe in pleurifies, peripueumonies, and fundry other fevers. In a dropfy, fciarvy, or confumption, it is a bad fymtom, and fhews that the lungs are ulcerated. SYMPTOMS.----Spitting of blood is generally preceded by a fenfe of weight and oppreffion of the breaft, a dry tickling cough, hoarfenefs, and a difficulty ofbreathing. Sometimes it is ulheredin with fhivering, coldnefs of the extremities, coftivenefs, great las- fitude, flatulence, pain of the back and loins, &c. As thefe fhew a general ftridure upon the veffels, and a tendency of the blood to inflammation, they are commonly the forerunners of a vary copi- ous difcharge. The above fymptoms do not attend a difcharge of blood from the gums or fauces, by which means thefe may always be diftinguifhed from an hcemoptoe. Sometimes the blood that is fpit up is thin, and of a florid red colour; and at other times it is thick, and of a dark or blakifh colour; nothing however can be inferred from this circumftance, but that the blood has lain a longer cr fhorter time in the breaft before it was difeharged. f^ * Spitting of blood, in a ftrong healthy perfon, of a found confti- tution, is not very dangerous; but when it attacks the tender and . delicate, or perfons of a weak lax fibre, it is with difficulty remov- ed. When it proceeds from a fcirrhus or polypus of the lungs) it is bad. The danger is greater when the difcharge proceeds from the rupture of a large veffel than a fmall one. When the extravafated blood is not fpit up, but lodges in the breaft, it corrupts, and great- ly increafes the danger. When the blood proceeds from an ulcer in ■die lungs, it is generally fatal. REGIMEN.----The patient ought to be keptcool and eafy. Eve- ry thing that heats the body or quickens the circulation, increafes the danger. The mind ought Iikewife to be foothed, and every oc- cafion of exciting the paflions avoided. The diet fhould be foft, cooling, and flender; as rice boiled jwith milk, fmall broths, bar- ley-gruels, VOMITING of BLOOD 21-i ley-gruels, panado, &c. Tbe diet, in this cafe, can fcarce be too low. Even water-gruel is fufficient to fupport the patient for fome days. All ftrong liquors muft be avoided. The patient may drink milk and water, barley water, whey, butter-milk, and fuch like. Every thing however fhould be drank cold, and in fmall quantities at a time. He fhould obferve the ftrideft filcnce or at leaft fpeak with a very low voice. MEDICINE.---This like the other involuntary difcharges of blood, ought not to be fuddenly ftopped by aftringent medicines. More mifchief is often done by thefe than if it were' fuffered to go on. It may however proceed fo far as to weaken the patient, and even endanger his life; in which cafe proper means muft be ufed for reftraining it. The body fhould be kept gently open by laxative diet; as roafted apples, hewed prunes, and fuch like. If thefe fliould not have the defired effed, a tea-fpoonful of the lenetive eleduary may be taken twice or thrice a-day,as is found neceffary. If the bleeding proves violent,ligatures may be applied to the extremities, as direded for a bleeding at the nofe. if the patient be hot or feverifh, bleeding and fmall dofes of nitre will be of ufe ; a fcruple or half a drachm of nitre, may be taken in a cup of his ordinary drink twice or thrice a-day. His drink may Iikewife be fharpened with acids, as juice of lemon, or a few drops of the fpirit of vitriol; or he may take frequently a cup of the tindure of rofes. Bathing the feet and legs in lukewarm water has Iikewife a very good effed in this difeafe. Opiates too are fometimes beneficial; but thefe muft be adminiftered with caution. Ten or twelve drops of laudanum nay be given in a cup of barley-water twice a-day, and continued for fome time, provided they be found bene- ficial. The conferve of rofes is Iikewife a very good medicine in this cafe, provided it be taken in fufficient quantity, and long enough perfifted in. It may be taken, to the quantity of three or four oun- ces a-day; and, if the patient be troubled with a cough, it fhould be made into an eleduary with balfamic fyrup, and a little of the fyrup of poppies. If ftronger aftringents be neceffary, fifteen or twenty drops of the elixir of vitriol may be given in a glafs of water three or four times-a-day. Thofe who are fubjed to frequent returns of this difeafe fhould avoid ali excefe. Their diet fhould be light and cool, confiding chiefly of milk and vegetables. Above all, let them beware of vigourous efforts of the body, and violent agitations of the mind. VOMITING OF BLOOD. THIS isnot fo common as the other difcharges of blood which have already been mentioned; but it is very dangerous, and re- quires particular attention. Vamiting cf blood is generally preceded by pain cf the ftomach, fickne&, n6 Of BLOODY URINE. ficknefs, and n.iufca; and is accompanied with great anxiety, and frcouent famting-fus. This difeafe is fometimes periodical; in which cafe it is lefs dan- gerous. It ofttn proceed-, from an obftrudion of the menfes in wo- men; and fometimes from the ftoppage of the hemorrhoidal flux in men. It may be occafioned by any thing that greatly ftimulates or wounds the ftomach, as ftrong vomits or p'ugrs, acrid poifon, fliarp or hard fubftances taken into the ftomach, gc. It is often the effed of obftrudion ki the liver, the fpleen, or fome of the other vifcera. It may Iikewife proceed from external violence, and blows,brui- fes,'or from any of the caufes which produce inflammation. Ia hyfteiic women, vomiting of blood is a very common, but by no means a dangerous fymptom. A great part of the danger in this difeafe arifes from the extrava- fa ted blood lodging in die bowels, and becoming putrid, bv which means a dyfentery cr putrid fever may be occafioned. The bell way of preventing this, is to keep the body gently open, by fre- quently exhibiting emollient clyfters. Purges muft not be given till the difcharge is ftopt, otherwife they will irritate the ftomach, and increafe the diforder. All the food and drink muft be of a mild cooling nature, and taken in fmall quantities. Even drinking cold water has fometimes proved a remedy, but it will fucceed bettet when fharpened with the weak fpirits of vitriol. When there are fign; of an inflammation, bleeding may be neceffary; but the patient's weaknefs will feldom permit it. Opiates may be of ufe; but they muft be given in very fmall dofes, as four or five drops of liquid laudanum twice or thrice a-day. After the difcharge is over, as the patient is generally troubled with gripes occafioned by the acrimony of the blood lodged ia the inteftines, gentle purges will be necefiary. OF BLOODY URINE. TFIIS k a difcharge of blood from the veffels of the kidneys c? bladder occafioned by their being either enlarged, broken, or ero- ded. It is more or lefs dangerous according to the different cir- cumftances which attend it. When pure blood is voided fuddenly without interruption, and without pain, it proceeds from the kidneys; but if the blood be in fmall quantity, of a dark colour, and emitted with heat and pain about the bottom of the belly, it proceeds from the bladder. When bloody urine is occafioned by a rough ftone decending from the kidneys to the bladder, which wounds the ureters, it is attended with a fliarp pain in the back, and difficulty of making water. If the coats of the bladder are hurt by a ftone, and the bloody urine follows, it is artended with the moft acute pain, and a previous ftoppage of urine. Bloody urine may Iikewife be occafioned by foils, blows, the liftiag or carrying of heavy burdens,, hard riding pr any violent motion. Or tmb DYSENTERY, &tc. 317 (notion. It may alfo proceed from ulcers of the bladder, from a ftone lodged in the kidneys, or from violent purges, or fliarp diu- retic medicines, efoecially cantharides. Bloody urine is always attended with fome degree of danger : but it is peculiarly fo whenmixed with purulent ma*;er,asthi dlicv/s an ulcer fomewhere in the urinary paTages. Sometimes this dif- charge proceed;- from excefs of bleed, in which cafe it is rather to be confidered as a falutary evacuation than a difeafe. If the dif- charge however be very gret.:, it may waf'e the patient's'ftrength, and occafion an ill habit of body, a dropfy or a cuafomption. The treatment of this diford<$r' mull be varied according to the different caufes from which it proceeds. When it is owing to a flone in the bladder, the cure depends Mpon an operation, a defer iptiun of which would be foreign to our purpofe. If it be attended with a plethora, and fymptoms of inflammation, bleeding will be neceffary. The body muft Iikewife be kept open by emollient clyfters,or cooling purgative medicines ; as cream of tartar, rhubarb, manna, or fmall doles cf lenitive eleduary. When bloody urine proceeds from a diffolved ftate of the blood, it is commonly the fymptom of fome malignant difeafe ; as the fmall-pox, a putrid fever or the like. In this cafe the patkr.r?- life depends on the liberal ufe af the Peruvian bark and ac"Ji,us has al- ready been fhewn. When there is reafon to fufped an ulcer in the kidneys or blad- der, the patient's diet muft be ccol,and his drink of a foft, healing, ,balfamic quality, as decodions of mr.rfii-mullow roots whh li<p;<>;- ice, folutions of gum-arabic, £:c. Three ousccs of marih-maUow roots, and half an ounce of liquorice, may be boiled, in two Englifh quarts of water to one ; two ounces of gum-arabic, and half .\.n ounce of purified nitre may be diffolved in the fliained liquor, and a tea-cupful of it taken four or five times a-day. The early ufe of aftringents in this difeafe has often bad confe- guenccs. When the flux is ftopped too foon,the grumons blood,by eing confined in the veffels, may produce inflammations, abfcefs, and ulcers. If however the cafe be urgent, or the patient feems to fpff&r from the lofs of blood, gentle aftringents may be necefiary. In this cafe the patientmay take three or four ounces of lime-water, with half an ounce of the tindure of,Peruvian bark, three times a-day. OF THE DYSENTERY OR BLOODY FLUX. THIS difeafe prevails in the fpring and autumn. It is moft common in marfhy countries, where, affor hot and dry fommers, it k apt to becme epidemic. Perfons are moft liable to it who ar? much expofed to the night air, or who live in phtece, where the air is confined and unwholefome. Hence it often proves fatal incarnps, •n fhipboard, 'in jails, hofpitals, and fuch i'ke places. CAUSE J-~—The dyfentery may b# ce^aA-ncd by any thing D d HUH 218 OF THE DYSENTERY, that obftruds the pei fpiration, or renders the humours putrid; a* damp beds, wet clothes, unwholefome diet, bad air, &c. But it i* moft frequently communicated by infedion. This ought to male people extiemely cautious in going near fuch perfons as labour un- der the difeafe. Even the fmell of the patient's excrements has been known to communicate the infedion. SYMPTOMS.----It is known by a flux of die belly, attended by violent pain of the bowels, a conftant inclination to go to ftool, and generally more or lefs blood in the ftools. It begins like other fevers, with chillinefs, lofs of ftrength, a quick pulfe, great thirft, and an inclination to vomit. The ftools are at fir ft grcafy and frothy, afterwards they are ftreaked with blood, and at laft have frcquent- lv the appearance of pure blood,mixed with fmall filaments refem- bling bits of fkin. Worms are fometimes pafkd both upwards and downwards through the whole courfe of the difeafe. When the patient goes to ftool, he feels a bearing down, as if the whole bowels were falling out, and fometimes a part of the inteftine is adnally protruded, which proves exceeding troublefome, efpecially in children. Flatulency is Iikewife a troublefome fymptom, efpe- cially towards the end of the difeafe. This difeafe may be diftinguifhed from a diarrhoea or loofenefs by the acute pain of the bowels, and the blood which generally "appears in the ftools. It may be diftinguifhed from the cholera morbus by its not being attended with fuch violent and frequent fits of vomiting, Sec. When the dyfentery attacks the old, the delicate, or fuch as have been wafted by the gout, the fcurvy, or ofner lingering difeafe, it generally proves fatal. Vomiting and hjccuping are bad figns, as they fhew an inflammation of the flomach. When the ftools are green, black, or have an exceeding difagreeable cadaverous fmell, ,the danger is very great, as it fhews the difeafe to be of the putrid kin J. It is an unfavourable fymptom when clyfters are immedi- ately returned ; but ftill morefo when the paffage is fo obflinately fhut, that they cannot be injeded. A feeble pulfe, coldnefs of the extremities, with difficulty of fwallowing, and convulfions, are figns of approaching death. REGIMEN.—Nothing is of more importance in this difeafe, than cleanlinefs. It contributes greatly to the recovery of the pa- tient, and no kfs to the fafety of fuch as attend him. In all conta- gious difeafes the danger is increafed, and the infedion fpread, by the negled of cleanlinefs ; but in no one more than this. Every thing about the patient fhould be frequently changed. The excre- ment fhould n.ver be fuffered to continue in his chamber, but re- moved immediately and buried under ground. A conftant ftream cf frefh air fhould be admitted into the chamber ; and it ought fre- quently to befprinkkd with vinegar,juice of lemon, or fome other iirong acid. The patient muft not be difcouraged, but his fpirits kept up in hopes of a cure. Nothing tends more to render any putrid difeafe mortal, than the fears and apprehenfions of the fick. Al! difeafes Of this jjiitmc have a tendency to fink and deptefs the fpirits mv! wfon ©r BLOODY FLUX. 219 ■i when that is increafed by fears and alarms from thofe whom the patient believes to be perfons of flcill, it cannot fail to have the worft effeds. A flannel waiftcoat worn next the fkin has often a very good ef- fed in the dyfentery. This promotes the perfpiration without over heating the body. Great caution however is neceffary in leaving it off. I have known a dyfentery brought on by ' imprudently throwing off a flannel waiftcoat before the feafon was fufficiently warm. For whatever purpofe this piece of drefs is worn, it fhould never be left off but in a warm feafon. In this difeafe the greateft attention muft be paid to the patient's diet. Flefh, fifh, and every thing that has a tendency to turn pu- trid or rancid on the ftomach, muft be abftained from. Apples boiled in milk, water pap, and plain light pudding, with broth made of the gelatinous parts of animals, may conftitute the princi- pal part of the patient's food. Gelatinous brorh not only anfwers the purpofe of food, but Iikewife of medicine. I have often known dyfenteries, which were not of a putrid nature, cured by it, after pompous medicines had proved ineffedual*. Another kind of food very proper in the dyfentery,which may be ufed by fuch as cannot take the broth mentioned above, is made by boiling a few handfuls of fine flower, tied in a cloth, for fix orfeve* hours,till it becomes as hard as ftarch. Two or three table-fpoon- fuls of this may be grated down,and boiled in fuch a quantity of new milk and water, as to be of the thicknefs of pap. This may be fweetened to the patient's tafle, and taken for his ordinary foodf. In a putrid dyfentery the patient may be allowed to eat freely of moft kinds of good ripe fruit ; as apples, grapes, goofberries, turrant-berries, flraw-berries, &c. Thefe may either be eaten raw or * The manner of making this broth ii, to take a fbwp's h-e-d and feet wiib t'ir fkin upon them,and to burn the wool off with a hot iron ; afterwards to boil then till the broth is quite a jelly, A little cinnamon or mace may be :A'\i !, to give Vat broth an agreeable flavour, and the patient may take a little of it warm with toafted bread three or four times a-day. Aclyfter of it may Iikewife be given twice a-day. Such as cannot ufe the broth made in this way, may have the head and feet fkinned ; but we have reafon to believe that this injures the medicine. It is not uur bufinefs here to reafon upon the nature and qualities of medicine, otherwife this might be fhewn to poifefs virtues every way fuited to the cure of a dyfentery v.-Inch does not proceed from a putrid ftate of the humours. One thing we know, which is pref- erable to all rcatoning, that whole f.imilies have often been cure) by it, af'.ev they had ufed many other medicines in vain. It will however, be proper, that the •, ••- tient take a vomit,and a dofe or two of rhubarb, before be begins to ufe the broth. It will Iikewife be neceffary to continue the ufe of it for a confiderable time, and to make it the principal food. t The learned and humane Dr. Rutheford, late profeffor cf medicine in the uvu- vcrfity of Edinburgh, ufed to mention this food in bis public lectures with ; --v encomiums. He direfted it to be made by tying a pound or two of thi !i:ic'l Hour, as tight as pofiible, in a linnen rag, afterwards to dip it frequently i;i water, and to dridge the outfide with flour, till a cake or cruet was formed around it, which prevents the water from foaking into it while boiling. It is thin to be b<>\\- •d till it becomes a hard dry mafs, as directed above. Tins, when mixed with milk and water, will not oaly anfwer the purpoie of food, but may Iikewife be gtfcon in clyfWf. • 220 Of thi DYSENTERY, or boiled ; with or without milk, as the patient choofes. The pt*. jndice againft fruit in this difeafe is fo great, that many believe it to be the common caufe of dyfentcries. This however is an egre- gious miftake. Both reafon and experience fhew, that good fruit is one of the bell medicines, both for the prevention and cure of the dyfentery. Good fruit is in every refped calculated to counteract that tendency to putrcfadion, from whence the moft dangerous kind of dyfencry proceeds. The patient in fuch a cafe ought there- fore to be allowed to eat as much fruit as he pleafes, provided it be ripe*. The moft proper drink in this diforder is whey. The dyfentery has often been cured by the ufe of clear whey alone. It may be taken both for drink and in form of a clyftcr. When wh.y cannot be had, barky-water fharpened with cream of tartar may be drank, or a decoetion of barley and tamarinds ; two ounces of the former and one of the latter may be boiled in two Englifh quarts of wa- ter to one. Warm water, water gruel, or water wherein hot iron has been frequently quencbed, are all very proper, and may be drank in turns. Camomile tea, if the ftomach will bear it, is an exceeding proper drink. It both ilrengthens the ftomach, and by' its anticeptic quality, tends to prevent a mortification of the bow- els. MEDICINE____At the beginning of this difeafe it is always nece'riry to cleanfe the firft puffages. For this purpofe a vomit of ipecacuanha muft be given, and wrought off with weak camomile- tea. Strong vomits are feldom neceffary htrr. A fcruple, or at moft half a drachm of ipecacuanha, is generally fufficient for ani- dult, and fometimes a very few graiss will fufhee. The day after the vomit, half a drachm, or two fcruples of rhubarb, mutl be tak. en ; or what will aniwer the purpofe rather better, an ounce or an ounce and an half of Epiom fait. This dofe may be repeated every other day for two or three times. Afterwards fmall dofes of ipecacuanha may be taken for fometime. Two or three grains of the powder may be mixed in a table fpoonful ef the fyrup of pop- pies, and taken three times a-day. Thefe evacuations, and the regimen prefcribed above, will often be fufficient to effed a cure. Should it however happen otheiwife, the following aftringent medicines may be ufed : A clyftcr of flarch orfat mutton-broth, with thirty or forty drops of liquid laudanum in it may be adminiftered twice a-day. At die fame * I lately faw a young man who had Ween feizei with a dyfatery in North America. Many things had been tried there f«r his relief, but to no purpofe. At length, tired out with difappointincnti from inedgint, and reduced to ftm wd "bone, he came over to Britain, rather with a view to dia among his r«lation», than with any hopes of a cure. After taking fundrvmediciwes fieri with no better fuc- ccb than abroad, I advifed him to leave off the mfe of drugs, and to truft ei.tr.ly to a ditt of milk and fruits, -with gentle txercile. Strawberries wa. the only fruit he could procure at that feafon. Thele be ate w ah milk twice and fometimes thrice •-day. The confequence was, that in a fhort time his ftoola were reduaed from upwards of twenty in a day, to three or fouf, and fometimes not fo »any. He ufed the other fruits as they came in, and was in a few weeks fo well as to leave that part of the country where I waa, with a view W retWB *• AjM1^- or BLOODY FLUX, 324 fame time an ounce -of gum-arabic, and half an ounce of gum-tra- gacanth, may be diffolved in an Englifh pint of barley water, over a flow fire, and a table-fpoonful of it taken every hour. If thefe have not the defired effed, die patient may take, four times a-day, about the bulk of a nutmeg of the japonic Confcdi<^ drinking after it a tea-cupful of the decodion of logwood*. Perfons who have been cured of this difeafe are very liable ta fuffer a relapfe ; to prevent which, great circumfpedion with ref- ped to diet is necefiary. The patient muft abftain from all fer- mented liquors, exsept now and then a glafs of good wine ; but he muft drink no kind of malt liquor. He fhould Iikewife abitamfrom animal food, as filh and flefh, and live principally on milk aud veg* etables. Gentle exercife and wholefome air are Iikewife of importance— The patient fhould go to the country as foon as his ftrength will permit,and fhould take exercife daily on horfeback,or in a carriage. He may Iikewife ufe bitters infufed in wine or brandy, and may drink twice a-day a gill of lime-water mixed with an equal quanti- ty of new milk. When dyfenteries prevail, we would recommend a ftrid atten- tion t© cleanlinefs, a fpare ufe of animal food, and the iiee ufe of found ripe fruits,and other vegetables. The night air is to be care- fully avoided, and all communication with the fick. Bad fimells are iikewife to be fhunned, efpecially thofe which arife from putrid animal fubftances. The neceffaries where die fick go are carefully to be avoided. When the firft fymptoms ef the dyfentery appear, the patient ought immediately to take a vomit, to go to bed, and drink plenti- fully of weak warm liquor, to promote a fweat. Thi-, with adefc or two of rhubarb at the beginning, would often carry off the dif- eafe. In countries where dyfenteries prevaugwe would advife fuch as are liable to them,to take either a vomit or a purge e.cry fpring or autumn, as a preventive. There are fundry other fluxes of the belly, as the LIENTERY and CCELIAC PASSION, which though kfs dangerous than the dyjentery, yet merit confederation. Thefe difeafes generally pro- ceed from a relaxed ftate of the ftomach and inteftines, which is fometimes fo great, that the food paffes through them with hardly any fenfible alteration ; and the patient dies merely from die want ■ef nourifhment. When the lientery or cccliac paffion fucceed to a dyfentery, the «afe is bad. They are always dangerous in old age, efpecially when the conftitution has been broken by excefs or acute difeafes. If the ftools be very frequent, and quite crude, the thirft great with little urine, the mouth ulcerated, and the face marked widi fpots of different colours the danger is very great. The treatment of the patient is in general the fame as in die dyfentery. In all obftinate fluxes of the belly, the cure muft be at- tempted, by firft cleanfing the ftomach and bowels with gentle vo- mits * See Appendix, Decoction of L©*we»#. *22 Or the HEAD-ACH. mits and purges; afterwards fuch a diet as has a tendenccy to heal and ftrengthen the bowels, with opiates and aftringent medicines, will generally complete the cure. The fame obfervation holds with refped to a TENESMUS, or frequent defire of going to ftool. This difeafe refembles the' dyfen- tery fo much, both in Its fymptoms and method of cure that we lhink it needkfs to infill upon it. CHAP. XXXIV. OF THE HEAD-ACH. ACHES and pains proceedfrom very different caufes and may affed any part of the body, but we fhall point out thofe only which occur moft frequendy, and are attended with the greateft ianger. * When the head-ach is flight, and affeds a particular part of tin head only, it is called cephalalgia ; when the whole head is affecled, sephalim ; and when on one fide only, hemicrania. A fixed pain in the forehead, which may be covered with the end of the thumb, is called the clavis hyftericus. There are alfo other diftindions. Sometimes the pain is internal, fometimes external; fometimes it is au original difeafe, and at other times fymptomatic. When the head-ach proceeds from a hot bi- lious habit the pain is very acute and throbbing, with a confider- able heat of dig part affeded. When from a cold phlegmatic hab- it, the patient complains of a dull heavy pain, and has a fenfe of coldnefs in the part. This kind of head-ach is fometimes attended with a degree of flupidity or folly. Whatever obftruds the free circulation of the blood through the veffels of the head, may occafion a head-ach. In perfons of a full habit, who abound with blood, or other liumours, the head-ach of- ten proceeds from the fuppreffion of cuftomary evacuations ; z% bleeding at the nofe, fweating of the feet. &c. It may Iikewife proceed from any caufe that determines a great flux of blood to- wards the head ; as coldnefs of the extremities, or hanging down the head for a long time. Whatever prevents the return of the blood from the head will Iikewife occafion a head-ach ; as looking long obliquely at any objed, wearing any thing tight about th» peck, a new hat or the like. When a head-ach proceeds from a ftoppage of a running at the nofe, there is a heavy, obtufe, prefling pain in the fore part of the head, in which there feems to be fuch a weight, that the patient can fcarce hold it up. When it is occafioned by the cauftic matter of the venerial difeafe, it generally affeds the fkull,and often pro duces a caries of the bones. Sometimes the head-ach proceeds from therepulfiort or retrocef- fccmof the gout, the eryfipelas, the iimall-pox, meafles, itch, or •thet Of the HEAD-ACH. - 223 edier eruptive difeafes. What is called a htmicrania generally pro- ceeds from crudities or indigeftion. Inanition, or emptinefs, wfll often alfo occafion head-achs. I have often feen inftances of this in nurfes who gave fuck too long, or who did not take a fufficient quantity of folid food. There is Iikewife a moft violent, fixed, conftant, and almoft in- tolerable head-ach, which occafions great debility both of body and mind, prevents fleep, deftroys the appetite, caufes a vertigo dimnefs of fight, a noife in the ears, convulfions, epileptic fits, and fometimes vomiting, coftivenefs, coldnefs of the extremities, &c. The head ache is often fymptomatic in continual and intermit* ting fevers, efpecially quartans. It is Iikewife a very commoiS fymptom in hyfteric and hypocondriac complaints. When a head-ache attends an acute fever, with pale urine, it ii an unfavourable fymptom. In exeefllve head-achs, coldnefs of xht extremities is a bad fign. When die difeafe continues long, and is very violent, it often ter- minates in blindnefs, an apoplexy, deafnefs, a vertigo, the palfy, or the epilepfy. In this difeafe the cool regimen in general is to be obferved. The diet ought to confift of fuch emollient fubftances as will cor- red the acrimony of the humours, and keep the body open ; as apples boiled in milk, fpinage, turnips, and fuch like. The drink ought to be diluting ; as barley-water, infufions of mild mucilagin- ous vegetables, decodions of the fudorific woods, Sec. The feet and legs ought to be kept warm, and frequently bathed in luke- warm water ; the head fhould be fhaved, and bathed with water and vinegar. The patient ought as much as poflibk to keep in an ered pofture, and not lie with his head too low. When the head-ache is owing to excefs of blood, or an hot bil- ious conftitution, bleeding is neceffary. The patient may be bled in the jugular vein, and the operation repeated if there be occafion. Cupping alfo, or the application of leeches to the temples, and be- hind the ears, will be of fervice. Afterwards a bliftering-plafter may be applied to the neck behind the ears, or to any part of the head that is moft affeded. In fome cafes it will be proper to blif- tcr the whole head. In perfons of a grofs habit, iffues or perpetu- al biifters will be of fervice. The body ought Iikewife to be kept open by gentle laxatives. But wdien the head-ach proceeds from a copious vitiated ferum ftagnating in the membranes, either within or without the fkulh with a dull, heavy, continual pain, which will neither yield to bleed- ing nor gentle laxatives, then more powerful purgatives are nec- effary, as pills made of alces, refin of jalap, or the like. It will alfo be neceffary in this cafe to blifter the whole head, and to keep die back part of the neck open for a confiderable time by a perpet- ual blifter. When the head-ache is occafioned by the ftoppage of a running at the nofe, the patient fhould frequently fmell to a bottle of vol- atile falts ; he may Iikewife t:tkefnuff,or anything that will irritate the nofe, fo as to promote a difcharge from it ; as the h*rb maf- tkli, ground ivy, &c. \A 2S4 Gr thi T00TK-ACH. A htmierania, efpecially a periodical on«, is generally owing to a fWrnei; of th; ftomach, for which cg.nde vomits mull be adminis- tered, a? aifo purgei of rhubarb. Affsr die bowels have been fuffi- ciently cleared, chalybeate waters, and fuch bitters as ftrengthen the ftomach, wall be necefiary. A periodical head-ach has been cured by wearing a piece of flannel over the forehead dui ing the night. When the head-ach arifes from a vitiated ftate of the humours, as in the fcurvy and venerial difeafe, the patient, after proper evacu- ations, muft drink freely of the decoction of woods, or the decoc- tion of farfaparilla, with raifins and liquorice*. Thefe, if duly perfifted in, will produce very happy effeds. When a colledion of matter is felt under the fkin, it muft be difeharged by an incifion, Otherwife it will render the bone carious. When the head-ach is fo intolerable as to endanger the patient's life or is attended with continual watching and delirium recourfe muft be had to opiates. Thefe, after proper evacuations by clyfkri or m7d purgatives, may be applied both externally and internal. ly. The affeded part may be rubbed with Bate's anodyne balfam, or a cloth dipped in it may be applied to the part. The patient may, at the fame time, take twenty drops of laudanum, in a cup 6f valerian or penny-royal tea, twice or thrice a day. This is only to be done in cafe of extreme pain. Proper evacuations oimht ill- ways to accompany and follow the ufe of opiatesf. When the patient cannot bear the lofs of blood, his feet ought frequently to be bathed in lukewarm water, and well rubbed with a coarfe cloth. Cataplafms with muftard or horfe radifh, ought Iikewife to be applied to them. This courfe is peculiarly neceffary when die pain proceeds from a gouty humour affeding die head. When the head-ack is occafioned by great heat, hard labour or ▼iolent exercife of any kind, it may be allayed by cooling medicines; fc, the faline draught, with nkr«, and the like. A little aether,dropt into the palm of the hand,and applied to the forehead, will fometimes remove a rioleat head-ach. OF THE TOOTH-ACM. THIS difeafe i« fo well known, that it needs no defcription. it fcus great aflSnity with the rheumatifm,and often fucceeds pains of the flioulders and other parts of the body. It may proceed from obftruded perfpiration, or any of die other caufes of inflammation. I have often known the toodi-ach occafi- oned by negleding fome part of the ufual coverings of the head, by fitting *Sce Appendix,Dbcocti«"n osSaijapaiilla. + When pain is very violet, teid does not yield to fmall dofes qr laudanum, the quantity mjy be inrrea ei. I Ikve knawn a patient in extreme pain, taka tjiree b-.i.J.reil Hiop.. in iw.rity-1'jar feturs; but Ucti dob* wughtoaly to h* adj»»jftcr*i '•f - p«rio» ef fkiH. Ob th» TOOTH-ACH. 225 fitting with the head bare near ar\ open window, or expoflng it to a draught ot cold air. Food or drink ta^en el.her too hot or too cold is very hurtful to the teeth. Great quantises of fugar, or other fweet meats, are Iikewife hmrtful. Nothing is more detlrudiveto the teeth than cracking nuts, or chewing any kind of hard fub- ftances, picking the teeth with pins, needks, or any thing that may huit the enamel with which they are coveied, does great mifchief, as the tooth is fure to be fpoikd whenever the air gets into it. Breeding women are very fubjed to the tooth-ache, efpecially dur- ing the firft three or four months of pregnancy. The tooth-ache often proceeds from fcorbutic liumours affecting the gums. In this cafe the teeth are fometimes waited, and fall out without any confiderable degree of pain. The more immediate caufe of the tooth-ach is a rotten or carious tooth. In order to relieve the tooth-ach, we muft firft endeavour to lef- fen the flux of humours to the part affeded. This may be done by mild purgatives, Icarifying the gums, or applying leeches to them, and bathing the feet frequently with warm water. The perfpiration ought Iikewife to be promoted, by drinking freely of weak wine-whey, cr other diluting liqu »rs, with fmall do'es of ni- tre. Vomits to* bare often an exceeding good effed in the to.oth- acli. It is feldom fafe to adminifter opiates or any kind of heat- ing medicines, or even to draw a tooth, till proper evacuations have been premifed ; and thefe alone will often effed die cure. If this fails, and the pain and inflammation ftill increafe, a fup- puration may be expeded, to promote w] fch a toafted fig fhould be held between the gum and the cheek ; bags filled with boiled chamomile-flowers, flowers of elder, or the like, may be applied near the part affeded, with as great a degi ee of warmth as the pa- tient can bear, and renewed as they grow cool; the patient may Iikewife receive the fleams of warm water into lbs mouth, through an inverted funnel, or by holding his head over the mouth of a porringer filled with warm water. Such things as promote the difcharg? of faliva, or caufe die pa- tient to fpit, are generally of fervice. For this purpofe, bitter, hot, or pungent vegetables may be chev ed ; as gentian, calamus aromaticus, or pellitoiy of Spain. Allen lecommends the root of yellow water jlowe>-de-luce in this cafe. Tnis root may either be rubbed upon the tooth, or a little of it chewed. Brookes fays he hardly ever knew it fail to eafe the tooth-ach. It.ought however to be ufed with caution. Many other heibs, roots, and feeds, are recommended for curing the tooth-ach ; as the leaves or roots of nn.kfoil or yairow chewed, tobacco fmoked or chewed, ftaves-acre, or the feeds of muilard chewed, &c. Thefe bitter, hot, and pungent things, by occafion- ing a greater flow of faliva, frequently give eafe in the tooth-ach. Opiates often relieve the tooth-ach. For this purpofe a little cot- ton wet with laudanum may be held between the teeth ; or a piece of it ck.ng plafter, about the b'gnefs of a foilling, with a bit of opium hi the middle of it,of a fi/e not to prevent 'he flicking of the other, may be laid on the temporal u.tery, where the puliation is moft Ee 22$ Or rut EAR-ACH. moft fenfible. De la Motte affii ms, that there are few cafes wherew in thfs will not give relief. If diere be a hollow tooth, a fmall pill made of equal parts of r.imphire and opium, put into the hollow is often beneficial. When this camict be had, the follow tooth maybe filled with gum maftich, wa.\, lead, or any fobflance that will flick in it, and keep out the external air. Few applications give more relief in the tooth-ach than biiftering- plafters. Thefe may be applied between the tfomlders; but they have the beft effed when put behind the ears, and made fo large as to cover a great part of the lower jaw. After all, when a tooth is carious, it is often impoffible to remove the pain without extrading it; and, as a fpoilt tooth never becomes found again, it is prudent to draw it foon, left it fhould affeel the raft. Tooth drawing, like bleeding, is very much praJtifed by me- chanics, as well as perfons of the medical profeffion. The ope-a- tion however is not without danger, and ought always to be per- formed with care. A perfon unacquainted with the ftrudure of the parts will be in danger of hurting the jaw-bone, or of drawing a found tooth inftead of a rotten one*. When the tooth-ach returns periodically, and the pain chiefly af- feds the gums, it may be cured by the bark. Some pretend to have found great benefit in the tooth-ach, from the application of an artificial magnet to the affeded tooth. We fhall not attempt to account for its mode of operation; but if it be found to anfwer, though only m particular cafes, it certainly de- ferves a trial, as it is attended with no expence, and cannot do any harm. Eledricity has Iikewife been recommended, and particu- lar inftruments have been invented for fending a fhock through the affected tooth. Perfons who have returns of the tooth-ach at certain feafons, as fpring and autumn, might often prevent it by taking a purge at thefe times. Keeping the teeth clean has no doubt a tendency to prevent the tooth-ach. The beft method of doing this is to wafh them daily with fait and water, a decodion of the bark, or with cold water alone. All brufhing and feiaping of the teeth is dangerous, and, unlefs it be performed with great care, does mifchief. OF THE EAR-ACH. THIS diforder chiefly affects the membrain which lines \hr 'rm r cavity of the ear, called the meatus auditorius. It is often fo vie lent as to occafion great reflkffncfs, anxiety, and even delirium.— Sometimes epileptic fits, and other convulfive diforders, haw been brought on by extreme pain in the ear. The ear-ach may proceed from any of the caufes wjiich produce inf. >i m.foon This may alwavs be prevented by the operator ftrikin* upen tl. t-e.tl. w ith ai y P'o.t of metal, as this never hn. to e:;cia- the pain in the tiarioi^ . jo'i, PAIN or the STOMACH, ice. t2J inflammation. It often proceeds from a fubden fuppreffion of per- fpiration, or from the head being expofed to cold wnen covered with fweat. It may alfo be occafioned by worms, or other infeds get- ting into the car, or being bred there ; or from any hard body flick- ing in the ear. Sometimes it proceeds from the translation of mor- bific matter to the ear. This often happens inthe decline of malig- nant fevers, and occafions deafnefs, which is generally reckoued a favourable fymptom. When the ear-ach proceeds from infeds, or any hard body flick- ing in the ear every method muft be taken to remove them as foon as pcffiblc. The membranes may be relaxed, by dropping into the ear, oil of fweet almonds, or olive oil. Afterwards the patient fhould be made to fneeze, by taking fnuff, or fome ftrong fternuta- tory. If this fhould not force cut the body, it muft be extraded by art, I have feen infefk; which had got into the car, come out of their own accord upon pouring in oil. When the pain of the ear proceeds from inflammation, it muft be treated like other topical inflammations, by a cooling regimen, and -opening medicines. Bleeding at die beginning, either in the arm or juguiar vein, of cupping in the r.eck, will be proper. The car may Iikewife be fomented with fleams of warm water; or flan- nel* bags filled with boiled mallows and camomile-flowers may be applied to it warm; or bladders filled with warm milk and water. An exceeding good method of fomenting the ear, is to apply it clofe to the mouth of a jug filled with warm water, or a ftrong decodion of camomile-flowers. The patient's feet fhould be frequently bathed in lukewarm water, and he ought to take fmall dofes of nitre and rhubarb, vh. a fcru- ple of the former, and ten grains of the latter, three times a-day— His drink may.be whey, or decodion of barley and liquorice, with figs or rafins. The part* behind the ear ought frequentiy to be rub- bed with camphorated oil, or a litde of the volatile liniment. When the inflammation cannot be difcuffed, a poultice of bread and milk, or roaftcd onions, may be applied to the ear, and fre- quently renewed, till the abfcefs break, or can be opened. After- wards tiie humours may be diverted from the part by gende laxa- tives, blifters, or iffues; but the difcharge muft not be fuddenly dried up by any external application. PAIN OF THE STOMACH&: THIS may proceed from various caufes, as indigeftion; wind; the acrimony of the bile ; fliarp, acrid, or poifonous fubftances taken into the ftomach, Sec. It may Iikewife be occafioned by worms: the ftoppage of cuftomary evacuations; a tranflation of gouty matter to the ftomach, the bowels, Sec. Women in the decline of life are very liable to pains of the fto- mach and bowels, efpecially foch as areafllided with.liyfteric com- plaints. It is Iikewife rery common to hypochondriac men of a fed- entary 228 PAIN of thi JTOMACH, &c. tary and luxurious life. In foch perfons it often prove* fo extreme- ly obftinate as to bafik all the powers of medicine. » When the pain of the ftomach is moft violent after eating, there is reafon to fufped that it proceeds from fome fault, either in the digeftion or the food. In this cafe the patient ought to change his diet, till he finds v.h:v kind of food agrees beft with his ftomach, •^ and fhould continue chiefly t-s ufe it. If a change of diet does not remove the complaint, the patient may take a gentle vomit, and af- terwards a dofe or two of rhubarb. He ought Iikewife to take an infufion of camomile flowers, or fome other flomachic bitter, cither in wine or water. I have often known eiercife remove this com- plaint, efpecially failing, or a long journey on horfeback, cr in a carriage. When a pain of the flumach proceeds from fiaiukncy, the pa- tient is conftantly belching up wind, and feels an uneafy diftention of the , icomach after meak. This is a moft deplorable difeafe, and is feldom thoroughly cured. In general, the patient ought to avoid vtll windy diet, and every thing that four a on the ftomacfoas greeni, roots, &c. This rule however admits of fome exceptions. There are many inftances of perfons very much troubled with wind, who have received great benefit dom eating parched peas, though that grain is generally fuppofed to be of a windy nature*. _ This complaint may 3 ike wife be greatly relieved by labour, efpe- cially digging, reaping, mowing, or any kind of aflive employment by which the bowels are alternately compreffed and dilated. The moft obftinate cafe of this kind I ever met with, was in a perfon of a fedentary occupation, whom I advifed, after he had tried every kind of medicine in vain, to turn gardener ; which he did, and has ever fince enjoyed good health. When a pain of the ftomach is occafioned by the fwallowing of acrid or poifonous fubftances, they muft be difeharged by vomit; this may be excited by butter, oils, or other foft things, which fhearii and defend the ftomach from the acrimony of its contents. When pain of the ftomach proceeds from a tranflation of gouty matter, warm cordials are neceffaTy, as generous wines, French brandy, &c. Some have drank a whole bottle of brandy or rum, in this cafe, in a few hours, without being in the leaft intoxicated, or even feeling the ftomach warmed by it. It is impoflible to afcertain the quantity neceffary upon thefe occafions. This muft be left to the feelings and difcretion of the patient. The fafer way however, is, not to go too far. When there is an inclination to vomit, it may be promoted by drinking an infufion of camomile-flowers, or carduus bencdiclus. If a pain of the ftomach proceed from the ftoppage of cuftomary evacuations, bleeding will be neceflary, efpecially in fanguine and very full habits. It will Iikewife be of ufe to keep the body gently open by mild purgatives; as rhubarb or fenna. When this difeafe affeds * Thefe arc prepared by ftetping or foaking peafe in water, and afterwards dry. :ng them in a pot or kiln, till they be quitelurd. They may bt ofed at pleafure. Of WORMS. 229 affeds women in the decline of life, after the ftoppage of the menfes making an iffue in the leg or arm will be of peculiar fervice. When the difeafe is occafioned by worms, they muft be deftroy- ed, or expelled by fuch means as are recommended in the following fedion. When the ftomach is greatly relaxed and the digeftion, bad, which often occafion flatulencies, the elixir of vitriol will be of An- gular fervice. Fifteen or twenty drops of it may be taken in a glafs of wine or water twice or thrice a-day. Perfons afflided w idi flatulency are generally unhappy unlefs they be taking fome purgative medicines ; thefe, though they may give immediate eafe,t«nd to weaken and relax the ftomach and bow- els, and confequently increafe the diforder. Their beft method is to mix purgatives and flomachics together. Equal paris of Peru- vian bark and rhubarb may be infufed in brandy or wine, and tak- en in fuch quantity as to keep the body gently open. CHAP. XXXV. OF WORMS. THESE are chiefly of three kinds, viz. the ttwa, or tape-worm; the teres, or round and long worm; and the a/csirides,oi round and fhort worm. There are many other kinds of worms found inthe human body ; but as they proceed, in a great meafure, from fimi- lar caufes, have nearly the fame fymptoms, and require almoft the fame method of treatment as thefe already mentioned, we fhall not fpend time in enumerating them. The tape-worm is white, very long, and full of joints. It is ge- nerally bred either in the flomach*- or fmall inteftines. The round and long worm is Iikewife bred in the fmall guts, and fometimes in the ftomach. The round and fhort worms, commonly lodge in the rrBum, or what is called the end gut, and occafion a difagreea- ble itching about the feat. The long round worms occafion fqueamiflinefs, vomiting, a difagreeable breath, gripes, loofenefs, fweiling of the belly, fwoon- ings, loathing of food, and at other times a voracions appetite, a dry cough, convulfions, epileptic fits, and fometimes a privation of fpeech. Thefe worms have been known to perforate the inteftines, and get into the cavity of the belly. The effeds of the'tape-worm are nearly the fame with thofe of the long and round, but rather more violent. Andryfays, the following fymptoms particularly attend the fo. Hum, which is a fpecies of the tape worm, viz. fwoonings, priva- tion of fpeech, and a voracious appetite. The round woims called afcarides, befides an itching of the anus, caufe fwoonings, and ten- efmus, or an inclination to go to ftool. CAUSES 2^0 Or WORMS. CoLJSES----Worms may proceed from various caufes ; but they are feldom found except in weak and related ftomachs,where the digeftion is bad. Sedentary perfons are more liable to them than the adive and laborious. Thofe who eat great quantities of un- ripe fruit, or who live much on raw herbs and roots, are generally fubjed to worms. There feems to be an hereditary difpolition in fome perfons to this d:feafe. I have often feen all the children of a family fubjed to worms of a particular kind. They feem like- wife frequently to be owing to the nurfe. Children of the fame family, uurfed by one woman, have often worms, when thofe nurf- ed by another have none. SYMPTOMS----The common fymptoms of wcvms are, pale- liefs of the countenance, and at other times, an univerfal flufhing of the face ; itching of the nofe ; this hrwever is doubtful, as chil- dren pick their nofes in all difeafes ; ftarting, and grinding of th« teetii in fleep ; fwelling of the upper lip ; the appetite fometimes bad, at other times quite voracious ; loofenefs ; a four or ftinking breath ; a hard fwelied bely ; great thi:ft ; the urine frothy, and fometimes of a whitifh colour ; griping or colic pains; an invol- unravy difcharge of faliva, especially when afleep ; frequent pains of tUe fide, with a dry cough, and unequal pulfe; palpitations of the heart; fwoonings ; celd fweats ; palfy ; epileptic fits, with ma- ny other unaccountable nervous fymptoms, which were formerlyat- tributed to witchcraft, or the influence of evil fpirits. Small bodies, in the excrements refembling melon or cucumber feed are iymp. toms of the tape-worm. I lately law fome very furprifing effeds of worms, in a girl about five years of age, who ufed to lie tor whole hours as if dead. She at laft expired, and, upon opening her body, a number of the teres, or long round worms, were found in hetguts, which were confider- ably inflamed;and what anatomifts call an intusfufeeptio,or involv- ing of one part of the gut within another,had taken place in noiefs than four different parts of the inteftinal canal*. MEDICINE.—Though numberlefs medicines are extolled for expelling and killing worms*, yet no difeafe more frequently baf- fles the phyfician's fkill. In general,the moft proper medicines for their expulfion are ftrosg purgatives ; and to prevent their breeding, aftomachic bitters, with now and then a glafs of good wine. The beft purge for an adult is jalap and calomel. Five and twen- ty or thirty grainsof the former with fix or feven of the latter .mix- ed in fyrup, may be taken early in the morning, for a dofe. It will be * That worms exift in the-human body there can lw no doubt; ind that they muft fometimes be confidered as a difeafe, is eq-aally certain ; but this is not the cafe fo often as people imagine. The idea that worms occafion many difeafes, givoi an opportunity to the profeffed worm-do£tors,of impofingon the credulity of man- kind, and doing much mifchief. They find worms in every cale,and liberally throw m their antidotes, which generally confift of ftrong, draftic purges. I have known thefe given in delicate conftitutions to the deftruttiun of the patient, v.nere ti.eie was not the leaft fymptom of worms. . t A medical writer of the prefent age has enumerated upwards at nity BriU* pfafi«»: ill c*lebrat«d for kllinj ami expallinj warms. e» WORMS. a$i be proper that the patient keep the houfe all day, and drink nothing coid. The dofe may be reoeated once or twice a week for a foi c- night or three weeks. On the intermediate days the patient may take a drachm of the pjv.der of tin, twice or thrice a-day, mixed with fytup, honey or treacle. Thofe who do n,t chufe to take calomel may make ufe of the bit- ter purgatives ; as aloes, hiera picra, tindure of fenna,and rhu- barb, &c. Oily medicines are fometimes found beneficial for expelling worms. An ounce of fallad oil and a tabk-fpoonful of common fait may be taken in a glafs of red poit wine thrice a-day,cr oftenet, if the ftomach will bear it. But the more common form of ufing oil is in clyfters. Oily clyfters fweetened with fugar or honey, are ve- ry efficacious in bringing away the fhort round worms called afea- rrdes, and Iikewife the teres. The Harrowgatc water is an excellent medicine for expelling Worms, efpecially the afatrides. As this water is impregnated with fulphur, we may hence infer, that fulphur atone muft be a good medicine in this cafe ; which is found to be a fad. Many prac- titioners give flour of fulphur in very large dofes, and with great fuccefs. It fhould be made into an eleduary with honey or trea- cle, and taken in fuch quantity as to purge the patient. Where Harrowgate water cannot be obtained, fea-water may be ufed, which is far from being a contemptible medicine in this cafe. If fea-water cannot be had, common'fait diffolved in water maybe drank. I have often feen this ufed by country nurfes with very good effed. Some flour of fulphur may be taken over night, and the falt-water in the morning- But worms, though expelled, will foon breed again, if the ftom- ach remain weak and relaxed ; to prevent which, we would re- commend the Peruvian bark. Half a drachm of bark in powder may be taken in a glafs of red port wine three or four times a-day, after the above medicines have been ufed. Lime-water is Iikewife good for diis purpofe, or a table fpoonful of the chalybeate wine taken twice or thrice a-day. Infufions or decodions of bitter herb* may Iikewife be drank ; as the infufions of tanfy, water trefoil, ca- momile flowers, tops of wormwood, the leffer centaury, Sec. For a child of four or five years old, fix grains of rhubarb, fire •f jalap, and two of calomel, may be mixed in a fpoonful of fyrup cr honey, and given in the morning. The child fliould keep the houfe all day, and take nothing cold. This dofe may be- repeated twice a week for three or four weeks. On the intermediate days the child may take a fcruple of powdered tin and ten grains of -x- thiops mineral in a fpoonful of treacle twice a-day. This dofe muft be increafed or dim nifhed according to the age of the patient. Built fays, the great ballard black hellebore, or bear's foot, is a rtioU powerful vermifuge for the long lound worm;. He orders th; decod'on of about a drachm of the green leaves, or about fifteen grains of the dried leaves in powder for a defe to a child between four and feven years of age. This dofe is to be repeated two or ■bree time*. He adds, that the green leaves made into a fyr»u> with 232 0» thi JAUNDICE. with courfe fugar, is almoft the only medicine he has ufed for round worms for three years paft. Befoie preffing out the juice he moif- tens the bruited kav:s with vinegar, which correds the medicine. The dofe is a tea-fpoonful at bed-time, and one or two next 'morning. I have frequently known thofe big bellies, Avhich in children are commonly reckoned afign of worms,quite removed by giving them white foap in the;r pottage, or other food. Tanfy, garlic, and rue, are all good againft worms, and may be ufed various ways. We might here mention many other plants, bothfor external and inter- nal ufe, as the cabbage bark, Sec. but think the powder of tin with ajthiops mineral, and the purges of rhubarb and calomel, are more to be depended on. Ball's purging vermifuge powder is a very powerful medicine. It is mad^ ofeq lal parts of rhubarb, feamnmry, and calomel, with as much double refined fugar as is equal to the weight of all the other ingredients. Tiiefe muft be well mixed together,and reduc- ed to a fine poivder. Tiie dofe for a child is from ten grams to twenty, once or twice a week. An adult may take a drachm for a dofe*. Parents who would preferve their children from worms ought to allow them plenty of exercife in the open air ; to take care that their food be wholefome and fufficiently folid ; and, as far as poflibk, to prevent their eating raw herbs, roots, or green trafhy fruits. It will* not be amifs to allow a child who is fubjed to worms, a glafs of red wine after m^als ; as every thing that braces and ftrengthcns the ftomach is good both for preventing and expelling thefe ver- min-f. i * CHAP. XXXVI. OF THE JAUNDICE. THIS difeafe is firft obfervable in the white of the eye, which appears yellow. Afcerwards the whole fkin puts on a yellow appearance. The urine too is of afaffron hue, and dies a white cloth * A powder for the tapeworm rcfemblingthis, was long kept a fecret on the Con- tinent; it was lately purcbaled by the Fiench king, and will be found under the ar- ticle Powder, in the Appendix. ' + We think it.necena/y here to warn people of their danger who buy en tes, pow- ders, and oth<rr warm medicines, at random, from quacks and give them to their- childreu without proper care. The principle ingredient in moft of thele m::hcmes is mercury, whirh is never U be trid.vi with. I lately law a fhac'ang in:Unce ot the danger of this oaduft. A girl who had takc.i a dofe of worm powder b m^it of a travelli-ig quac.i, went out, aad piehaps was fo imprudent as to drink cold w<i- tar dun ip, its operation. She immediately fwelled and died on the. following day with all tae iy.r.proiTU of having been poifoned. J Of the JAUNDICE 233 sloth of the fame colour. There is Iikewife a fpecies of this difeafe called the Black Jaundice. CAUSES.—The immediate caufe of the jaundice is an cbftruc- lion of the bile. The remote or occafional caufes are die bites of poifonoui animals, as the viper, a mad dog, &c. The bilious or hyf- teric colic; violent paflions, as grief, anger, Sec. Strong purges or vomits will Iikewife occafion the jaundice. Sometimes it proceeds from obftinate agues, or from that difeafe being prematurely ftop- ped by aftringent medicines. In infants it is often occafioned by the meconium not being fufficiently purged off. Pregnant women are very fubjed to it. It is Iikewife a fymptom in feveral kinds of fe- ver. Catching cold, or the ftoppage of cuftomary evacuations, as the maifrs, the bleeding piles, infues, &c. will occafion die jaundice. SYMPTOMS.----The patient at firft complains of exceffive wearinefs, and has great averfion to every kind of motion. His fkin is dry, and he generally feels a kind of itching or pricking pain over the whole body. The ftools are of a whitifh or clay colour, *and the urine, as was obferved above, is yellow. The breathing is difficult, and the patient complains of an unufual load or opprcf- fion on his breaft. There is a heat in his noftils, a bifer tafte in the mouth, loathing cf food, ficknefs of the ftomach, vomiting, flatulency, and other fymptoms of indigeftion. If the patient be young, and the difeafe complicated with no oth- er malady, it is ieldom dangerous*; but in old people, where it con- tinues long, returns frequent-y, or is complicated with the dropfy or hypocondriac fymptoms, it generally proves fatal. The black jaundice is more dangerous than the yellow. REGIMEN.----'fhe cfo:t fhould be cool, light, and diluting, confifting chiefly of ripe fruits and mild vegetables; as apples boil- ed or roafled, lie wed prunes, preferved plumbs, boikd foinnage, &c. Veal or chicken broth, with light bread, are likev. ife very proper. Many have been cured by living almoft wholly for fome days on raw eggs. The drink Humid be butter-milk, whey fweet- ened with honey, or decodions of cool opening vegetables ; or marfh-mallow roots, with liquorice, &c. The patient fliould take as much exercife as he can bear, either r>n horfeback, or in a carriage; walking, running, and even jump- ing, are Iikewife proper, provided he can bear them without pain, and there be no fymptoms cf inflammation. Patients have been of- ten cured of tifis difea.e by a long journey, after medicines had pro- ved ineffeduak Amufenicnts are Iikewife of great ufe in the jaundice. The dif- eafe is often occafioned by a fedentary life, joined to a dull melan- choly difpofitien. Whatever therefore tends to promote the circu- lation, arid to cheer the fpirits, mull have a good effed ; as dancing,, laughing, finging, Sec. MEDICINE.----If the patient be young, of a full fanguine ha- bit, and complains of a pain in the right fide about the region of the liver, bleeding will be neceffary. Afeer this a vomit muft be adminiftered, and if the difeafe prove obftinate, it may be repeat- ed once or twiee. No medicines are more b.enefiqkd ia ihe jatndice E f than 234 Or thi DROPSY. than vomits, efpecially where it is not attended widi inflammation. Haifa drachm of ipecacuanha in powder, will be a fufficient dofe for an adult. It may be wrought oft* with weak camomile-tea, or lukewarm-water. The body muft Iikewife be kept open by taking a fuflicient quantity of Caftile foap, 01 the pills for the jaundice re* commended in the Appendix. Fomenting the parts about the region of the ftomach and liver, and rubbing them with a warm hand er flefh-brufh, are Iikewife be- neficial; but it is ftill more fo for the paticnUo fit in a bath of warm water up to the breaft. He ought to do this frequently, and fhould f ontinue in it as long as his ftrength will permit. Many dirty things are recommended for the cure of the jaundice as lice, millepedes, &c. But thefe do more harm than good, as people truft to them, and negled more valuable medicines; befides they are feldom taken in fufficient quantity to .m educe any effecls. People always exped that fuch things fhould ad as charms, and confequently feldom perfifbin the ufe of them. Vomits, purges, fomentations, and exercife, wail feldom fail to cure the jaundice when it is a fimple difeafe; and when complicated with the dropfy, a fcinhcus liver, or odier chronic complaints, it is hardly to be cur- ed by any means. Numberlefs Britifli herbs are extoledfor the cure of this difeafe. The author of die Medicina Brltannica mentions near a hundred, all famous for curing the jaundice. The fad is, the difeafe often goes off of its own accord; in which cafe the laft medicine is always faid to have* performed the cure. I have fometimes however, feen con- fiderable benefit, in a very obftinate jaundice, from a decoction of hempfeed. Four ounces of the feed may be boiled in two Englifh quarts of ale, and fweetened with courfe fugar. The dofe is half an Englifh pint every moining. It may be continued for eight or nine days. I have Iikewife known Harrowgate fulphur water cure a jaundice of very long Handing. It fliould be ufed for fome weeks, and die patient muft both drink and bathe. The foluble tartar is a very proper medicine in the jaundice. A drachm of it may be taken every night and morning in a cup of tea or water-gruel. If it does not open the body, the dofe may be increafed. Perfons fubjed to the jaundice ought to take as much exercife as poflibk, and to avoid all heating and aftringent aliments. CHAP. XXXVII. OF THE DROPSY. Tlir. dropfy is a preternatural fwelling of the whole body, cr fome part of it occafioned by a collection cf watery hun otr. It is _thftinguiflied by different name-;, axcordh - to the part affed- trd, Of thi DROPSY. 235 •4, as tfie anofarcas or colic!!'vn of water under the fkin; the nfciles, or colledion of water in the belly: the hydrops peBeris, or dropfy of the Sreaft; the kyJrocphafos, or dropfy of the brain, &c. CAUSES.----The dropfy is often owing to an hereditary difpo- fition. It may Iikewife proceed from drinking ardent fpiiits, cr other ftrong liquors. It k true almoft to a proverb, that great drinkers die of the dropfy. The want of exercife is alfo a very common caufe of the dropfy. Hence it is juftly reckoned among the difeafes of the fedentary. It often proceeds from exce live evacuations, as frequent and copious bleeding, ftrong purges often repeated, frequent falivations, Sec. The fudden ftoppage of cufto- mary or neceffary evacuations, as the menfes, the hemorrhoids, fluxes of the bel'y, &c. may iikewife caufe a dncyy. I ha-'e known the dropfy occafioned by drinking large quantities of cold, weak, watery liquor, when the body was heated by violent exercife. A low, damp, or marfhy fituation'is Iikewife a frequent caufe of it. Hence it is a common difeafe in moift, flat, fenny countries. It may alfo be brought on by a long ufe of poor watery filet, or of vifcous aliment that is hard of digeftion. It is often the effed of other difeafes, as the jaundice, a fcirrhus of the liver,a \ io- lent ague of long continuance, a diarrhoea, dyfentary, an empyema, or a coftfumption of the lungs. In fhort, wdiatever obftruds the perfpiration, or prevents the blood from being duly prepared, may occafien a dropfy. SYMPTOMS.—The amfa/ca generally begins with a fwelling of the feet and ancles towards night, which for fome time d.fag- pears in the morning. In the evening the parts, if preffed wtih the finger, will pit. The fwelling gradually afeends, and occupies the trunk of the body, the arms, and the head. Afterwards the breathing becomes difficult, the urine is in fmall quantity, and the thirft great; the body is bound, and the perfpiration is greatly obftruded. To rhefe fucceed torpor, heavinefs, a flow wafting fe- ver, and a troublefome cough. This la ft is generally a fatal fymp- tom, as it fhews that the lungs are affected. In an affiles, befides the above fymptoms, there is a fwelling of the belly, and often a find nation, which may be perceived by ftrik- ing the belly on one fide, and laying the palm of the hand on the oppofite. This may be diftinguifhed from a ty.r.pany by the weight of the fwelling, as well as by the fluctuation. When the anafarca and afcites are combined, the cafe is very dangerous. Even a fim- ple afcit-s feldom admits of a radical cure. Almoft all that can be done is, to let off the water by tapping, which feldom affoi J-> more than a temporary relief. When the difeafe comes fuddenly on, and thepatient is young and ftrong, there is reafon, however, to hope for a cure, efpecially if medicine be given early. But if the patient be old, has fed an irregular or a fedentary life, or if there be reafon to fufneft that th: livex-, lungs, or any of the vifcera are unfound, there is great rea- fon to fear that the confequences will prove fatal. REGIMEN.----The patient mud abftain as much as poflibk, from 23^ Or the DROPSY. from all drink, efpecially weak and watery liquors, *nd muft quenefc his thirft widi multard-whey, or acids, as juice of lemmons, oiangcs, forrel, or fuch like. His aliment ought to be dry, of a ftimulating and diuretic quality, as toafted bread, the flefli of birds, or other wild animals toafted: pungent and aromatic vegetables,as garlic, mus- tard, nnfor.s, cieffes, horfe-radifh, rocambole, ftialot, Sec. He may alio eat fea-bifeuit dipt in wine or a litde brandy. This is not only nourififing, but tends to quench diii ft. Some hare been adually cuied of a dropfy by a total abftinence from all liqwids, and living entirely upon fuch things as are mentioned above. If the patient muft have drink, the Spa-water, or Rhenifh wine, with diureftic medicines infufed in it, are the beft. Exercife is of the greateft importance in a dropfy. If the patient be able to walk, dig, or the like, he ought to continue thefe excr- cifes as long as he can. If he is not able to walk or labour, he muft ride on horfeback, or in a carriage, and the more violent the motion fo much the better, provided he can bear it. His bed ought to be hard, and the air of his apartments warm and dry. If he live^ in a damp country, he ought to be removed into a dry one, and, if pos- fible,intoa waimer climate. In aword,ever y method fliould be taken to promote the perfpiration, and to brace the folids. For this pur- pofe it w ill Iikewife be proper to rub the patient's body.two or three times a-day,with a hard cloth, or the flefh-brufh ; and he ought con- ftantly to wear flannel next his fkin. MEDICINE.----If the patient be young, his conftitution good, and the difeafe has come on fodder:ly, it may generally be removed by ftrong vomits, brifk purges, and fuch medicines as promote a difcharge by fweat and urine. Tor nn adult, half a drachm of ipe- cacuanna in powder, and half an ounce of oxymel of fquills, will be a proper vomit. This may be repeated as often as is found ne- effary, three or four days intervening between the dofes. The pa- tient muft not drink much after taking the vomit, otherwife he des- troys its effed. A cup or two of camomile-tea will be fufficient to work it off. Between each vomit, on one of the intermediate days, the patient may take rhe following purge : Jalap in powder half a drachm, cream of tartar two drachms, calomel fix grains. Thefe may be made into a bolus with a little fyrup of pale rofes, and taken early in the morning. The lefs the patient drinks .after it the better. If he be much griped, he may now and then take a cup of chicken broth. The patient may Iikewife take every night at bed-time the fol- lowing bolus: To four or five grains of camphor add one grain of opium, and as much fyrup of orange peel as is fufficient to make them into a bolus. This will generally promote a gentle fweatj which fhould be encouraged by drinking now and then a fmall cup of wine-whey, with a tea-fpoonful of the fpirits of hartfhorn in it. A tea-cupful of the following diuretic infufion may Iikewife be tak- en every four or five hours through the day. Take juniper berries, muftard-feed, and horfe-radifh, of each half an ounce, afhes of broom half a pound; infufe them in a quart d Of the DROPSY. *3T of Rhenifli wine or ftrong ale for a few days, and afterwards ftrain ofi the liquor. Such as cannot take this infufion, may ufe the de- codion of fencka-root. which is both diuretic and fudorific. 1 have known an obftinate cnafarca cured by an infufion of the allies of broom in wine. The above courfe will often cure rm incidental dropfy, if the con- ftitution be good ; but when the diieafe proceeds from a bad hab::, or aii unloiind ftate of the vifcera, ftrong purges and vomits are not to be ventured upon. Ia this cafe, the fafer courfe is to pal- liate the fymptoms by the ufe of fuch medcines as promote the fecretions, and to fupport the patient's ftrength by warm and nour- ifhing cordials. The fecretion of urine may be greatly promoted b;r n'rre. Broo!. cs fays,he knew a young woman who was cured of a koofy by taking a drachm of nitre every morning in a draught o «Je, aker fhe had- been given over as incurable. The powder of fqu.fis is Iikewife a good diuretic. Six or eight grains of it, wi th a fcruple of nftrc, may be given twice a-day in a glafs of ftrong cinnamon-water.
