Ball fays, a large fpoonful of unbruifed muftard-ieed taken every night and morning, and drinking half an Englifh pint of the decodion of tht lops of green broom after it, has performed a cu:c after other powerful medicines had proved ineffectual. I have fomet'mes feen good effeds from cream of tartar hi this difeik. It promotes the difcharges by ftool and urine, and will at lad palliate if it does not perform a cure. The patient may begat by taking an ounce every fecond or third day, and may increafe the quantity to two or even to three ounces, if the ftomach will hear it. This quantity k not however to be taken at once, but divided into three or four dofes. To promote perfpiration,the patient may ufe die decodion offer, c- ka-root, as direded above ; or he may take two tabk-fpoonfuls of Minder erus's fpirit in a cup of wine-whey three or four times a-day. To promote a difcharge of urine, die following infufion of the London hofpitals will Iikewife be beneficial. Take of zedoary-roor two drachms ; dried fquills, rhubarb, and juniper-berries bruifed, of each a drachm ; cinnamon in powder, three drachms ; fait of wormwood, a drachm and a half; infufe ir. an Englifh pint and a half of old hock wine, and when fit for ufe, filter the liquor, A wine-glafs of it may be taken three or four times a-day. In the anafirca it k ufual to fcarify the feet and legs. By this means the water is often difeharged; but the operator muft be cautious not to make the incifions too deep ; they ought barely to pierce through the fkin,andefpecial care m ift be taken.byfpkituouj fomentations and proper digeftives, to prevent a gangrene. In an acites when the difeafe does not evidently and fpeedily give way to purgative and diuretic medicines, the water ought to be let off by tapping. This is a very fimple and fafe operation, and would often fucceed, if it were performed in due time ;but if it be delay- ed till the humours are vitiated, or thebowelsfpoiled, by fogg foak- *5* Ot thi GOUT. ing in water, it can hardly be expeded that any permanent relief will be procured*. r fter the evacuation of the water, the patient is to be put on a courfe of ilrengthening medicines ;as the Peruvian bark ; the elixir of vitriol; warm aromatics, with a due proportion of rhubaib, in- fufed in wine, and fuch like. His diet cught to be dry and nour- ifhing fuch as is recommended in die beginning of the chapter ;and he fhould take as much e> »xcife as he can bear without fatigue. He fnould wear flannel next his fitin, and make daily ufe of the fleftr- brufh. CHAP. XXXVIII. OF THE GOUT. THERE is no difeafe which fhews the imperfedion of medicine, or fets, the advantages of temperance and exercife in a ftrongi er light, than the gout. Excefs and idlenefs are the true fourcei from whence it originally fprung, and all who would avoid it muft be aSive and temperate. Though idlenefs and intemperance are the principal caufes of the gout, yet many oti>er things may contribute to bring on the difor- der in thofe who are not; and to induce aparoxifm in thofe who are fubjed to it ; as intenfe ftudy ; too free anufe of acidulated liquors; night-watching ; grief or uneafinefsof mind ;an obftrudion or de- fed of any of the cuftomary difcharges, as the menfes, fweating of the feet, perfpiration, &c. SYMPTOMS.----A fit of the gout is generally preceded by in- digeftion, drowfmefs, belching of wind, a flight head-ach, fick- nefs and fometimes vomiting. The patient complains of wearinefs and dejedion of fpirits, and has often a pain in the limbs, with a fenfation, as if wind or cold water w^ere pafling down the thigh.— The appetite is often remarkably keen a day or two before tbe fit, and there is a flight pain in pafling urine,and frequently an involun- tary fhedding of tears. Sometimes thefe fymptoms are much more violent, efpecially upon the near approach of the fit ; and it has been obferved, that as is the fever which ufhers in die gout, fo will the fit be ; if the fever be fhort and fharp, the fit will be fo Iikewife; if it be feeble, long, and lingering, the fit will be fuch alfo. But this obfervation can only hold with refped to very regular fits of tbe gout. ' The * The very name of operation is dreadful to moft people, and they wifh to try eyery thing before they have recourfe to it. This is the reafon why tapping fo fel- dom fucceeds to «ur wifh. 1 have had a patient who was regularly tapped once a month for feveral years, and who ufed to eat her ditmer at well after the operation n K nothing happ«m«l, She died at laA Mtkwr-w orn aut by age than by the difeafc. Of the COUT. H9 The^ regular gout generally makes 'its attack in die fpring or beginning or winter, in the following manner: About two or three in the morning, the patient is ieized with a pain in his great toe, fometimes in the heal, and at other times in the ancle or calf of the leg-— This pain is accompanied with a fenfation as if cold water were noai ed upon the part, which is fucceeded by a fliivcring, with fome degree of fever. Afterwards the pain increafes, and fixing among the fmall bones of the foot, the patient feels all the different kinds of torture, as if the part were ftretched, burnt, fqueezed, gnawed, or torn in pieces. The part at length becomes fo exqui- Jitcly fenfible that the patient cannot bear to have it touched, or even fuffer any perfon to walk acrofs the room. The patient is generally in exquifite torture for twenty four hours, from the time of the coming on of ^the fit: he then becomes eafier, the part begim, to fwell, appears red, and is covered with a little moillure. Towards morning he drops afleep, and generally falls into a gende breathing fweat. This terminates the firft paroxyfm, a number of which eonftkutes a fit of the gout; which is longer or fliorter according to the patient's age, ftrength, the feafon of the year, and the difpofition of the body to this difeafe. The patient is always worfe towards night, and eafier in die morning. The paroxyfms however generally grow milder erery day, till at length the difeafe is carried off by perfpiration, urine and the other evacuations. In fome patients this happens in a few days; in others it requires weeks, and in fome months, to finifh the fit. Thofe whom age and frequent fits of the gout have greatly debilita- ted, feldom get free from it before the approach offummer, and fometimes not till it be pretty far advanced. REGIMEN.----As there are no medicines yet known that will care the gout, we fhall confine, our obfervation* chiefly to rcguxiefc, both in and out of the fit. In die fit, if the patient be young and ftrong, his diet ought to be thin and cooling, and his drink of a diluting nature ; but where the t nftitution is weak, and the patient has been accuftomed to live high, this is not a proper time to retrench. In this cafe he muft keep nearly to his ufual diet, and fliould take frequently a cup of ftrong negus, or a glafs of generous wine. Wine-whey is a very prop«r drink in this cafe, as it promotes the perfpiration widiout greatiy heating the patient. It will anfwer this purpofe better if a tea- fpoonfol offal volatile oleofum or fpirits of hartlhorn, be put into a cup of it twice a-day. It will Iikewife be proper to give at bed- time a tea-fpoonful of the volatile tindure of guiaicum in a large drought of warm wine whey. This will greatly promote perfpira- tion through die night. Ab tiie moft fafeand effecacious method of difchargingthe gouty matter is by perfpiration, this ought to be kept up by all means, efpecially in the affected pait. For this purpofe the leg and foot fhould be wrapt in foft flannel, fur, or wool. The lafl is moft rea- dily obtained, and feems to anfwer the purple better than anything elfe. The people of Eaiicaftiire loot upon wool as a kind of Ipe- *fck in the "out. They wrap a great quantity cf it about the leg H® 0» tns GOUT. and foot affected, and cover it with a fkin of foft dreffed leaUicr. This they differ to continue for eight often d:tys,a.id fomcfone; for a fortnight or three weeks, or longer, if the pain docs not ceak. I never knew any external application anfwer fo well in die gain. I have often feen it applied when the fweeling and inflammation were very great, with violent pain, and have found all thefe fymp- toms relieved by it in a few days. The wool which they ufe is generally greafed and carded or combed. They choofe the fofteil which can be had, and feldom or never remove it till die fit be en- tirely gone off. The patient ought Iikewife to be keep quiet an J eafy during the fit. Every thing that affeds the mind difturbs the paroxifm, and tends to throw die gout upon the nobler parts. All external ap- plications that repel the matter are to be avoided as death. They do not cure the difeafe, but remove it fiom a fafer to a more dan- gerous part of the body, where it often proves fatal. A fit of the gout is to be confidered as Nature's method of removing fomething that might prove deftrudive to the body,and all that Ave can do with fafety, is to promote her intentions, and to affift her in expelling the enemy in her own way. Evacuations by bleeding, ftaol, &c. are Iikewife to be ufed with caution, they do not remove the caufe of the difeafe, and fometimes by weakening the patient, prokmg die fit ; but'wdicre the conftitution is able to bear it, it will be of ufe to keep the body gently open by diet, or very mild laxative medicines. Many things Avill indeed fhorten a fit of the gout, and fome will drive it off altogether ; but nothing has yet been found which will do this with fafety to the patient. In pain we eagerly grafp at any thing that promifes immediate eafe, and even hazard life itfelf for a temporary relief. This is the true reafon why fo many infallible remedies have been propofed for the gout, and why fuch numbers have loft their lives by the ufe of them. It would be as prudent to flop the fmall-pox from rifing, and to drive them into the blood, as to attempt to repel the gouty matter after it has been thrown upon the extremities. The latter is as much an effort of Nature to free herfelf from an offending caufe as the former, and ought equally to be promoted. When die pain however is very great, and the patient is reftlefs, thirty or forty drops of laudanum, more or lefs, according to the yiolence of the fymptoms, may be taken at bed-time. This will eafe die pain, procure reft, promote perfpiration, and forward the erifisofthe difeafe. After the fit is over, the patient ought to take a gende dofe or two of the bitter tindure of rhubarb, or fome odier warm ftomach- ic purge. He fliould alfo drink a Aveak infufion of ftomachic bit- ters in fmall Avine or ale, as the Peruvian bark, with cinnamon, Virginian fnake-root.and orange-peel. The diet at this time fhould be light, but nourilhing, and gentk exercife ought to be taken on horfeback, or in a carriage. Out of the fit, it is in the patient's power to do many things to- wards preventing a return of the diforder or rendering the fit, if it .Of tm« GOUT. *4i h fhould return lefs fevere. This however, is not to be attempt ad by medicine. I have frequently known the gout kept off for fe- veral years by the Peruvian bark and other aftringent medicines; but in all the cafes wdiere I had occafion to fee this tried, the per- fons died fuddenly, and, to all appearance, for want of a regular fit of the gout. One would be apt, from hence to conclude, that a fit of the gout, to fome conftitutions, in the decline of life, is ra- dier falutary than hurtful. Though it may be dangerous to ftop a fit of the gout by medicine, yet if the conftitution can be fo changed by diet and exercife, as to teflon or totally to prevent its return,therecertainlycan be no danger in folloAving fuch a courfe. It is well known that the whole habit may be fo altered by a proper Tegimen, as quite to eradicate this difeafe; and thofe only who have fufficient refolution to perfift in fuch a courfe have reafon to exped a cure. r The courfe which we would recommend for preventing the gout, is as follows : In the firft place, univerfal temperance. In the next place fifficient exercife*. By diis we do not mean falmtering about in an indolent manner, but labour, fweat, and toil. Thefe only can render the humours wholefome, and keep them fo. Going early to bed, and rifing betimes, are alfo of great importance. It is like- wife proper to avoid night ftudies, and all intenfe thought. The fupper fhould be light, ar.d taken early. All ftrong liquors, efpe- cially generous wines and four punch, are to be avoided. We would Iikewife recommend fome dofes of' magiefta alba, and rhubarb to be taken every fcring and autumn; and afterwards a courfe of ftomacbic bitters, as tanfy or water tre-foil tea, an infu- fion of gentain and camomile flowers, or a decodion of burdock root, &c. Any of thefe, or an infufion of any wdiolefome bitter that re more agreeable to the patient, may be drank for two or three weeks in March and Odober, twice a-day. An iffue or perpetual blifter has a great tendency to prevent die gout. If thefe were more generally ufed in the decline of life, they Avould not only of- ten prevent the gout, but alfo other chronic maladies. Such as can nffoi d to go to Bath, will find great benefit from bathing and drink- ing the water. It both promotes digeftion, and invigorates the habit. Though there is litde room for medicine during a regular fit of the gout, yet when it leaves the extremities, and falls on fome of the internal parts, proper applications to recall and fix it, become abfolutely neceffary. When the gout affeds the head, the pain of the joints ccafes, and the fwelling difappears, while either fevere head-ach, drowfinefs, trembling, giddinefs, convulfions, or delirium oome on. When it feizes the lungs, great oppreffion, with cough and difficulty of breathing, enfue. If it attacks the ftomach, ex. treme ficknefs, vomiting, anxiety, pain in the epigraftric region* and tofal lofs of ftrength, will fucceed. Whea * Some make a fecret of curing the gout by mo ocular exsrcisb. This fe- cret, however, is as old as Cellus, who ftr»"glv recommends that mode of cure ; and whoever will fubmit to it, in the fulled rxient, may «pj.-& t» rap., falid astir 'permanent advantage^. 2^2 Or thi RHEUMATISM. When the gout attacks the head or lungs, every mtthod mnftbe- taken to fix it in the feet. They muft be frequently bathed in warm water, and acrid cataplafms applied to the foles. Bliftering plaf- tcts ought Iikewife to be applied to the ancle* or calves of the legs. Bleeding in the feet or ancles is alfo neceffary, and warm ftomach-. ic purges. The patient ought to keep in bed for the moft part, if there be any figns of inflammation, and fhould be very careful not to catch cold. If it attacks the ftomach with a fenfe of cold, the moft warm eon- dials are neceffary; as ftrong wine boiled up ivith cinuamon or other fpices; cinnamon-water; peppeTmint-water; and evert brandy or rum*. The patient fliould keep his bed, and indea- vour to promote a fweat by drinking marm liquors; and iff:: fhould be troubled with a naufea, or inclination to vomit, he may drink camomile-tea, or any thing that will make him vomit freely. When the gout attacks the kidneys, and imitates gravel-pains, the patientought to drink freely of a decodion of maifh-mallows, and to have die parts fomented with warm water. An emolient clyfter ought Iikewife to be given, and afterwards an opiate. If the pain be very violent, twenty or thirty drops of laudanum may be taken in a cup of the decodion. Perfons Avho have had the gout fhould be very attentive to any complaints that may happen to them about the time when they have reafon to except a return of the fit. The gout imitates many other diforders, and by being miftaken for them, and treated improperly, is often diverted from its regular courfe, to the great danger of the patient's life. Thofe who never had the gout, but who from their conftitution «r manner of living, have reafon to exped it, ought Iikewife to be very circumfped with regard to its firft approach. If the difeafe, by Avrong conduct or improper medicines, be diverted from its reg- ular courfe, the miferable patient has a chance to be ever after tor- mented with head-aches, coughs, pains of the ftomach and intef- tines ; and to fall at laft a vidim to its attack upon fome of die xicre noble parts. OF THE RHEUMATISM. THIS difeafe has often a refemblance to the gout. It generally attacks the joints widi exquifitc pain, and is fomeumes attended with inflammation and fweelling. It is moft common in the fpring, and toAvards the end of autumn* It is ufually difliuguiflied into acutq rjmd chronic; or the rheumatifm v\ ith or without a fever. CAUSES.----The caufes of a rheumatifm are frequently the fame as thofe of an inflammatory fever, viz. an obftruded perfpira- tion, the immoderate ufe of ftrong liquors, and die like. Sudden •faanges of the weariier, and all quick tranfitions f; .n. heat to cold, are * ji'-iw u faund to \a an ciHcacioUi remedy in this c«l.-. &t the RHEUMATISM. 243 are very apt to.oecafion the rheumatifm. The moft extraordinary cafe of a rheumatifm that I ever faw,where almoft every joint of the boby was diftorted, was a man who ufed to work one part of the day by fire, and the other part of it in water. Very obftinate rheu- matifms have iikewife been brought on by perfons not accuftcmed to it, allowing their feet to continue long wet. The fame effeds are often produced by wet clothes, damp beds, fitting or lying on the damp ground, travelling in the night, &c. The rheumatifm may Iikewife be occafioned by exceffive evacua- tions, or die ftoppage of cuftomary difcharge?. It is often the ef- fed of chronic difeafes, which vitiate the humours ; as the fcurvy, the lues venerea, obftinate autumnal agues,&c The rheumatifm prevails in cold, damp, marfhy countries. It is moft common among the poorer fort of peafants, ▼ho are ill clothed, live In low damp houfes, and eat courfe and unwi.olefome food, which contains but little nourifhment, and is not eafily digefted. SYMPTOMS__The acute rheumatifm commonly begins with Wearinefs, fhivering, a quick pulfe, rettleffnek, thirft, and other fymptoms of fever. Afterwards the patient complains of flying pains, which are increafed by the leaft motion. I'hefe at length fix in the joints, which are often affeded with fwelling and inflam- mation. If blood be let in this difeafe, it has generally the fame .appearance as in the pkurify. In this kind of rhemmatifm the treatment of the patient is nearly the fame as in an acute or inflammatory fever. If he be young and ftrong, bleeding k neceffary, which may. be repeated according to the exigencies of the cafe. The body ought Iikewife to be kept open by emollient clyfters,or cool opening liquors ; as decodions of tamarinds, cream of tartar, whey, fenna tea, and the like. The diet fliould be light, and in fmall quantity, confiding chiefly of" roafted apples, groat-gruel, or weak chicken broth. After the feve- rifh fymptoms have abated, if the pain ftill continues, the patient muft keep his bed, and take fuch things as promote perfpiration, as wine-Whey, with fpiritus Mindereri, Sec. He may Iikewife take, for ,a few nights, at bed-time, in a cup of wine-whey, a drachm of the cream of tartar,and half a drachm of gum of guaiacum in powder, Warm bathing after proper evacuations, has often an exceeding good effed. The patient may either be put into a bath of warm water, or have cloths wrung out of it applied to the parts affeded. Great care muft be taken that he do not catch cold after bathing. The chronic rheumatifm is feldom attended with any confiderable degree of fever, and is genei ally confined to fome particular pait pf the body, asthe fhoulddrs, the back,or theloins. There is feldom any inflammation or fwelling in this cafe. Perfons in die decline of life are moft fubjed to the chronic rheumatifm. In fuch pati- ents it often proves extremely obftinate and fometimes incurable. In this kind of rheumatifm die regimen fhould be nearly the far* a; in the acute. Cool and diluting diet, confiding chiefly of veget- able fubftances/as ftewed prunes, coddled apples, currants or goofe-bcrries boiled in milk, is moft proper. Arbuthnot fins, ''If there be a fpecific in aliment for the rheumatifm, it is certainly wbey;" 344 Or thi RHEUMATISM. whey ;" and adds," mat he knew a perfon fubjcft to this difeafe, who could never be cured by any other method but a diet of whey and bread." He Iikewife fays, " that cream of tartar in water- gruel, taken for feveral days, will eafe lheumatic pains confidera« , bly." This I have often experienced, but found it always more efficacious when joined with gum guaiacum, as already direded.— In this cafe the patient may take the dofe formerly mentioned, twice a-day, and likeAvife a tea-fpoonful of the volatile tindure ofgtnrl guaiacum, at bed-time, in wine-whey. This courfe may be continued for a week, or longer, if the cafe proves obftinate, and the patient's ftrength will permit. It ought then to be omitted for a few days, and repeated again. At the fame time leeches, or a bliftering plafter may be applied to the part af- feded. What I have generally found anfwer better than either of thefe, in obftinate fixed rheumatic paim, is the warm plafter*. I have Iikewife known a plafter of Burgundy pitch worn for fome time on the part affeded, give great relief in rheumatic pains. My ingenious friend Dr. Alexander, of Edingburgh, fays, he has fre- quently cured very obftinate rheumatic pains, by rubbing the part affeded, with tindure of cantharides. When the common tindure did not fucced, he ufed it cf a double or treble ftrength. Cup- ping upon the part affeded, is Iikewife often very beneficial, andfo is the application of leeches. Though this difeafe may not feem to yield to medicines for fome time, yet they ought ftill to be perfifted in. Perfons who are fub- jed to frequent returns of the rheumatifm, will often find their ac count in ufing medicines, whether they be immediately affeded with the difeafe or not. The chronic rheumatifm is fimilar to the gout in this refped,that the moft proper time for ufing medicines to extirpate it, is when the patient is moft free from the diforder. To thofe who can afford the expence, I would recommend the warm baths of Buxton or Matlock in Derbyfhire. Thefe have, ♦ often, to my knowledge, cured very obftinate rheumatifms, and are always fafe either in or out of the St. When the rheumatifm is complicated with fcorbutic complaints, which fe not feldom the cafe, the Hanowgate waters, and thofe of Moffat, are proper. They fhould both be drank and ufed as a warm bath. There are feveral of our own domeftic plants whidh may be ufed ifritk advantage in the rheumatifm. One of the beft is the white raviflard. A table-fpoonful of the feed Of this plant may be taken twice or thrice-a-day, in a glafs of water vr fmall wine. The water- trefoil is Iikewife of great ufe in this complaint. It may be infufed in wine or ale, or drank in form of tea. The groand ivy,camomile, and feveral odier bitters, are alfo beneficial, and may be ufed inthe fame manner. No benefit however is to be expeded from thefe unlefs they may be taken for a confiderable time.Excellent medicines are often defpifed in this difeafe, becaufe they do not perform aa immediate cure ; whereas nothing would be more certain than their •ffed, were they duly perfifUd in. Want of perteverance in the * 3w Appendix, Wintii Plxstex? &t tke SCURVY. 241 •afe of medicines, it one reafon why chronic difeafes are fo feldom eured. Cold bathing, efpecially in fait water, often cures the rheuma- tifm. We would alfo recommend riding on horfeback,and wearing flannel next the fkin. Iffues are Iikewife very proper, efpecially in chronic cafes. If the pain affed the fhoulders, an iffue may bo mad# in the arm ; but if it affed* the loins, it fhould be put into the leg or thigh. Perfons afflided with the fcurvy are very fubjed to rheumatic complaints^ The beft medicines ih this cafe are bitters and mild purgatives. Thefe may either be taken feparately or together1, as the patient inclines. An ounce of Peruvian bark, and half an ounce of rhubarb m powder, may be infufed in a bottle of wine ; and one two or three wine-glaffes of it taken daily, as fhall be found ne- ceffary for keeping the body gendy open. In cafes where the bark itfelf proves fufficiently purgative, the rhubatb may be omitted. Such as are fubjed to frequent attacks of the rheumatifm, ought to make choice of a dry, warm fituation, to avo'd die night-air,wet chothes, and wet feet, as muck as pofiible. Their clothing fliould be warm, and they fhould wear flannel next their fkin, and make frequent ufe of the flefh-brufh. CHAP. XXXIX. OF THE SCURVY. THIS difeafe prevails chiefly in cold northern countries,cfpect> ally in low damp fituations, near large marines, or great quail- tides of ftagnating water. Sedentary people, of a dull melancholy difpofitjfon, are moft fubjed to it. It proves often fatal to failor's on long voyages particularly in fhtps that are not properly venti- lated, have many people on^board, or where cleanlinefs is negled- ed. It is not necefiary to mention the different fpecies into which this difeafe has been divided, as they differ from one another chiefly in degree. What is called the land fcurvy, however,is feldom attend- ed with thofe highly putrid fymptoms which appear in patients who have been long at fea, and which, we ptefume, are rather owing to confined air, want of exercife, and the u lAvholefortie fond eaten by failors on long voyages, than to any fpecifie difference in the dif- eafe. CAUSES_____The fcurvy is occafioned by cold moift air ; bf the long ufe of faked or fmoke-dried provifions, or any kind of food that is hard of digeftion, and affords little nourifhmeof. it may alfo proceed 'from the fuppreffion of cuftomary evacuations ; as the menfes hemorrhoidal flux. Sec. It is fometimes oAving to an hereditary taint, in which cafe a very fmall caufe will excite the lite tit »4« Or tri SCURVY. latent diforder. Grief, fear,and other depreffing paflions, lave a great tendency both to excite and aggravate this difeafe. Thcfame obfervation holds Avith regard to negled of cleanlinefs ; bad cloth- ing ; die Avant of proper exercife ; confined air ; unwholefome food; or any difeafe which gready weakens the body, or vitiates the hu- mours. SYMPTOMS.----This difeafe may be known by unufual Wea- rinefs, heavinefs, and difficulty of breathing, efpecially after mo- tion, rottennefs of the gums, which are apt to bleed on the flighteft touch; a {linking breath; frequent bleeding at the nofe; crackling of the joints ; difficulty ef walking; fometimes a fwelling and fome- times a falling away of the legs, on which there are livid, yelloAv, or violet coloured fpots ; the face is generally of a pale or leaden co, lour. As the difeafe advances, other fymptoms come on; as rotten- nefs of the teeth, haemorrhages or difcharges of blood from different parts of the body, foul obftinate ulcers, pams in various parts, efpe- cially about the breaft, dry fcaly eruptions all over the body, &c. At laft a wafting or hedic fever comes on, and the miferable pa- tient is often carried off by a dyfentary, a diarrhoea, a dropfy, the palfy, fainting fits,or a mortification of fome of the bowels. CURE.----We know no way of curing this difeafe but by purfuing a plan diredly oppofite to that which brings it on. It pro- ceeds from a vitiated ftate of the humours, occafioned by errors in diet, air, or exercife ; and this cannot be removed but by a proper, attention to thefe important articles. If the patient has been obliged to breath a cold, damp or confi. ned air, he fhould be removed, as foon as poflibk to a dry, open, and moderately warm one. If there is reafon to believe that the difeafe proceeds from a fedentary life, or depreffing paflions, as grief, fear, Sec. the patient muft take daily as much exercife in the open air as he can bear, and his mind fhould be diverted by cheer- ful company and other amufements. Nothing has a greater ten- dency either to prevent or remove this difeafe, than conftant cheer- fulnefs and good humour. But this, alas ! is feldom the lot of per- fons afllided with the fcurvy ; they are generallyfuily, peevifh and morofe. When the fcurvy has been brought on by a long ufe of faked provifions, the proper medicine is a diet confifting chiefly of frefh vegetables ; as oranges, apples, lemons, limes, tamarinds, water- creffes, fcurvy-grafs, brook-lime, &c The ufe of thefe, with milk, pot-herbs, new-bread, and frefh bear or cyder, will feldom fail to remove a fcurvy of this kind, if taken before it be too far advanced, but te have this effed they muft be perfifted in a confiderable time. When frefh vegetables cannot be obtained, pickled or preferved ones may be ufed ; and where thefe are wanting, recourfe muft be had to the chymical acids. All the patient's food and drink fhould in this cafe be fharpened with cream of tartar, elixir of vitriol, vi- negar, or the fpirit of fea-falt. Thefe things however will more certainly prevent dian cure th« ifcurvy, for which reafon fea-faring people, efpecially on long voy- age*, ought to lay in plenty of them. Cabbages, onions, goofeber- jies, 0* the SCURVY. *4y ties, and many other vegetables, may be kept a long time by pick- ling, preferv'mg,Sec. and when thefe fail the chymical acids, recom- mended above, which will keep for any length of time, may be ufed. We have reafon to believe, if fhips were well ventilated, had got ftore of fruits, greens, cyder Sec. laid in, and if proper regard were paid to cleanlinefs and warmth, that failors would be die moft healthy people in the world, and would feldom fuffer either from the fcurvy or putrid fevers, which are fo fatal to that ufeful fet of men; but it is too much the temper of fuch people to defpife all precaution; they will not think of any calamity till it overtakes them, when it is too late to ward off the blow. It muft indeed be owned, that many of them have it not in their power to make the provifion we are fpeaking of; but in this cafe it is the duty of their employers to make it for them; and no man ought to engage in a long voyage widiout having thefe articles fe- cured. I have often feen very extraordinary effeds in the land fcurvy from a milk diet. This preparation of Nature is a mixture of ani- mal and vegetable properties, which of all others is the moft fit for relloring a decayed conftitution.and removing that particular acri- mony of the humours, which feems to conftitute the very effence of the fcurvy, and many other difeafes. But people defpife this wholefome and nourifhing food, becaufe it is cheap, and devour with greedinefs, flefli and fermented liquors, while milk is only deemed fit for their hogs. The moft proper drink in the fcurvy, is whey or butter-milk. When thefe cannot be had, found cyder, perry, or fpruce-beer, may be ufed. Wort has Iikewife been found to be a proper drink in the fcurvy, and may be ufed at fea, as malt will keep during the long- eft voyage. A decodion of the tops of the fpruce fir is likeAvife proper. It may be drank in the quantity of an Englifh pint twice a-day. Tar-water may be ufed for the fame purpofe, or decodions of any of the mild mucilagenous vegetables; as farfaparilla, marfh- mallow roots, &c. Infufions of the bitter plants, as ground ivey, the leffer centaury, marfh-trefoil, Sec. are Iikewife beneficial. I have feen peafants in fome parts of Britain exprefs the juice of the laft mentioned plant, and drink it with good effed in thofe foul feor- butic eruptions with which they are often troubled iu the fpring feafon. HarroAvgate Avater is certainly an excellent medicine in the land. fcurvy. I have often feen patients tvho had been reduced to the moft deplorable condition by this difeafe, greatly relieved by drink- ing the fulphur-water, and bathing in it. The chalybeate-water may alfo be ufed with advantage, efpecially with a view to brace the ftomach after drinking the fulphur-water, which though it fhar- pens the appetite, never fails to weaken the powers of digeftion. A flight degree of fcurvy may be carried off by frequently fuc!> mg a little of the juice of a bitter orange lemon. Whe'n the difeafe affeds the gums only, this pradice, if continued for fome time will generally curry it off. We would however recommend tiie hitter orange as n ready preferable to l^mo.n ;k feems to be as good a *4« Oitmi SCROPHULA. a medicine, and is not near fo hurtful to the ftomach. Perhapi our ownforrel maybelitde inferior to either of them. All kinds of fallad are good in the fcurvy, and ought to be eaten Jcry plentifully, as fpinagc, lettuce, parfley, celery, endive, radiih, dandelion, &c. It is amaaing to fee bow foon frefh vegetables in the fpring, cure the brute animals ofanyfcab or foulnefs which is upon their fkins. It is reafonable to fuppofe that dieir effeds Would ba as great upon the human fpecies, Avere they ufed in prop- er quantity for a fufficient length of time. I have feen good effeds in fcorbutic complaints of very long (landing, from the ufe of a decodion of tha root* of water-dock. I* is ufually made by boiling a pound of die frefh root in fix Englifh pints of water, till about one- third of it be confumed. The dofe is from half a pint to a whole pint of the decodion every day. But in all the cafes where I have feen it prove beneficiahU was madt much flronger, and drank in larger quantities. The fafeft way, however, is for the patoent to begin with fmall dofes, and increafe them both in ftrength aad quantity as he finds his ftomach will bear it. It muft be ufed for a confiderable time. I have known fame take k for many months, and have been told of others who had ufed it for feveral years, before they were fenfible of any benefit, but who neverthelefs were cured by it at length- The lepr ofy, which was fo common in the country long ago, feems to have been near a-kin to the fcurvy. Perhaps its appear- ing fo feldom now, may be owing to the inhabitants of Britain eat- ing more vegetable food than formerly, living more upon tea and other diluting diet, ufing lefs faked racat,being more cleanly,bet- ter lodged and clodied,&c.—r-For the cure of this difeafe wo would recommend the fame courfe of diet and medicine as in tha fcuivy. OF THE SCROPHULA, OR KING's EVIL. THIS difeafe chiefly affeds the glands, efpecially thefe of the seek. Children and young perfons of a fedentary life are very fub- jed to it. It is one of thofe difeafes which may be removed by pro- per regimen, but feldom yields to medicine. The inhabitants of cold, damp, marfhy countries, are moft liable to the fcrophula. CAUSES.----This difeafe may proceed from an hereditary taint, from a fcrophulous nurfe, &c. Children who have the misfortune 'to be bom of fick parents, whofe conftitutions have been greatly injured by the pox, or other chronic difeafes, are apt to be affeded with the fcrophula. It may Iikewife proceed from fuch difeafes as weaken the habit or vitiate the humours, as the fmall-pox, meafles, &c. External injuries, as blows, bruifes, and the like, fometimes produce fcrophulous ulcers ; but we have reafon to believe, Avhen this happens, that there has been apredifpofitionin the habit to this difeafe. In fhort whatever tends to vitiate the humours or relax the folids, paves the way to the fcrophula ; as the want of proper / - exercife, too much heat or cold, confined air, unwholefome food, bad water, the long ufe of poor, weak, watery aliments,the negled Oa KING'* EVIL. z49 of cleanlinefs, Sec. Nothing tends more to produce this difeafe in children, than allowing them to continue long Wet*. SYMTOMS-----At firft fmall knots a*<pear under the chin or behind the ears, which gradually increafe in number and fize, till they form one large hard tumour. This often contiaues for a long time without breaking, and when it does break, it only difcharges a thin fanies, or watery humour. Other parts of the body are Iikewife liable to its attack, as the arm-pits, groins, feet, hands, eyes, breafts, Sec. Nor are the internal parts exempt from it. It often affeds the lungs, liver, or fpleen; and I have frequently feen the glands of the myfentery greatly enlarged by it. Thofe obftinate ulcers which break out upon the feet and hands witiifweiling, and little or no rednefs, are of the fcrophulous kind. They feldam difcharge good matter,and are exceedingly difficult to cure. The white fweliings of the joints feem Iikewife to be of this kind. They are with difficulty brought to a fuppuration, and vriieit opened, they only difcharge a thin ichor. There is not a more ge- neral fymptom of the fcropuhi than a fwelling of the upper lip and nofe. REGIMEN.----As this difeafe proceeds, in a great meafore, from relaxation, the diet ought to be generous and nourifliing, but at the fame time light and of eafy digeftion; as well fermented bread, made of feund grain, the flefli and broth of young animals, with now and then a glafs of generous wine, or good ale. The air might to be open, dry, and not too cold, and the patient fhould take as much exercife as he can bear. This is of the utmoft importance. Children who have fufficient exeicife, are feldcm troubled with the fcrophula. MEDICINE.----The vulgar are remarkably credulous with re- gard to the cure of the fcrophula; many of them believing in fhe virtue of the royal touch, that of the feventh fon Sec. The truth is, we know but little either of the nature or cure of this difeafe, and where reafon or medicines fail, fuperftition always comes in their place. Hence it is, that in difeafes which are the mofl difficult to ttnderftand, we generally hear of the greateft number of mira- culous cures being performed. Here, however, the deception is eafily accounted for. The fcrophula, at a certain period of life, often cures of-itfelf; and, if the patient happens to be touched about this time, the cure is imputed to the touch, and not to Nature, who is really the phyfician. In the fame way the infignificant nos- trums of quacks and old women, often gain applaufe when they de- ferve none. There is nothing more pernicious than the cuftom of plying chil- dren in the fcrophula with ftrong purgative medicines. People ima- gine it proceeds from liumours which muft be purged off, without eonfidering that thefe purgatives increafe the debility, and aggravate the difeafe. It has indeed been found, that keeping the body genrly open for fome time, efpecially with fea-water,has a good effed; but The fcrophu'.a. as well as the rickets, is fouid to prevail ia large wtaHafafluiiiig, t»\vns, wheie people livegrofs, b:-c lead ledenury iivw: II h- 35<C Of the SCROPHULA, ,\c. but tins fhould only be given in grofs habits, and in fuch quantity as to procure one, or at moft two ftools every day. Bathing in the fait Avater has Iikewife a very good effect, efpeci- ally in the warm feafon. I have often known a courfe of bathing in falt-water, and drinking it in fuch quantities as to keep the body gently open, cure a fcrophula, after many other medicines had been tried in vain. When falt-water cannot be obtained, the patient may be bathed in frelh water, and his body kept open by fmall quantities of fait and water, or fome odier mild purgative. Next to cold bathing, and drinking the falt-water, we would re- commend the Peruvian bark. The cold bath may be ufed in fum- mcr and the bark in Avinter. To an adult half a drachm of the bark in powder may be given in a glafs of red wine, four or five times a-day. Childien, and fuch as cannot take it in fubftance, may ufe the decodion, made in the following manner: Boil an ounce of Peruvian bark, and a drachm of Winters'sbark, both grofsly powdered, in an Englifh quart of water to a pint: to- wards the end, half an ounce of fliced liquorice-root, and a handful of raifins may be added, which will both reader the decodion lefs difagreeable, and make it take up more of the bark. The liquor mull be ftrained, and two, three, or four table fpoonfuls, according to the age of the patient, given three times a-day. The Moffatt and HarroAVgate waters, efpecially the latter, are Iikewife very proper medicines in the fcrophula. They ought not, howeveT,to be drank in large quantities,but fhould be taken foasto keep the body gently open, and muft be ufed for a confiderable time. The hemlock may fometimes be ufed with advantage in the fcro- phula. Some lay it down as a general rule, that the fea-water is moflproper before there are any fuppuration or fymptoms of tabes: the Peruvian bark, when there are running fores, and a degree of hedic fever; and the hemlock in old inveterate cafes, aproaching to the fcirrhous or cancerous ftate. Either the extract, or the frefh juice of this plant may be ufed. The dofe muft be fmall at firft, and increafed gradually as far as the ftomach is able to bear it. External applications are of little ufe. Before the tumour breaks nothing ought to be applied to it, unlefs a piece of flannel, or fome- thing to keep it warm. After it breaks, the fore may be drefled with fome digeftive ointment. What I have always found to an- fwer beft, was the yellow bafilicon mixed with about a fixth or eighth part of its weight of red precipitate of mercury. The fore may be dreffed with this tAvice a-day ; and if kbe very fungous, and does not digeft well, a larger proportion of the precipitate may be added. * • Medicines which mitigate this difeafe,though they do not cure it, are not to be defpifed. If the patient can be kept alive by any means till he arrives at the age of puberty, he has a great chance to get well; but if he does not recover at this time, in all probabili- ty he never will. There is/no malady which parents are fo apt to communicate to their Or the ITCH. *5* their offspring as the fcrophula, for which reafon people ought to be- ware of marry ing into families affeded with this difeafe. For the means of preventing the fcrophula we muft refer the reader to the obfervations on nurfing at the beginning of the book. OF THE ITCH. THOUGH this difeafe is commonly communicated by infedion, yet it feldom prevails where due regard is paid to cleanlinefs, frefh air and wholefome diet. It generally appears in form -of fmall watery puftules, firft about the wrifts or between the fingers : af- terAvards it affeds the arms, legs, thighs Sec. Thefe puftules are attended with an intolerable itching, eipecially when the patient is warm in bed, or fits by the fire. Sometimes indeed the fkin is co- vered with large blotches or feabs, and at other times Avith a white feurf, or fcaly eruption. This laft is called the dry itch, and is the moft difficult to cure. The itch is feldom a dangerous difeafe, unlefs when it is render- ed fo by negled or improper treatment. If it be fuffered to con- tinue too long, it may vitiate the Avhole mafs cf humours; and if it be fuddenly drove in, without proper evacuations, it may occafion fevers, inflammations of the vifcera, or other internal diforders. The beft medicine yet known for the itch is fulphur,which ought to be ufed both externally and internally. The parts moft affeded may be rubbed Avith an ointment made of the flour of fulphur, two ounces ; crude fal ammoniac finely powdered, two drachms ; hog's lard, or butter, four ounces. If a fcruple or half a drachm of the effence of lemon, be added, it will entirely take away the difagree- able fmell. About the bulk of a nutmeg of this maybe rubbed up- on the extremities at bed-time twice or thrice a-week. It is feldom neeeffary to rub the whole body ; but when it is, it ought not to be done all at once, but by turns,, as it is dangerous to flop too ma- ny pores at the fame time. Before the patient begins to ufe the ointment, he ought,if he be of a full habit, to bleed or take a purge or two. It will Iikewife be proper, during the ufe of it, to take every night and morning as much of the flour of brimftone and cream of tartar, in a litde treacle or new milk, as will keep the body gently open. He fnould beware of catching cold, fliould wear more clothes than ufual, and take every thing warm. The fame clothes, the linen excepted, ought to be Avorn all the time of ufing the ointment ; and fuck clothes as have been worn while the patient was under the difeafe, are not to be ufed again, unlefs they have been fumigated Avith brimftone, and thoroughly cleanfed, other wife they will communi- cate the infedion anew*. I ♦Sir John Prinze obferves, that though this difeafe may frm trifling, the-:- is no •ne in thj army that is more troublefome to cure, as the in!c<dion often lurks in elothes, &c. and breaks out a fecond, or cvsn a third time. The lemc iuconvenieu- cy occurs in private famine.-., uuleis paiticular regard is paid to the changing or cleaning of their cluthc:, which laft is by no means an eafy- operation. H* Of the ITCH. I never knew brimftone, when ufed as direded above,faiI to cure the itch ; and I have reafon to believe, that if duly perfifted in, it never will fail; but if it be only ufed once or tAvice, and cleanlj. nefs negleded, it is no wonder if the diforder returns. The quan- tity of ointment mentioned above will generally be fufficient for the cure of one perfon ; but if any fymptoms i»f the difeafe fliould ap- pear again, the medicine muft be repeated. It is both more fafe and efficacious when perfifted in for a confiderable time than Avhen a large quantity is applied at once. As moft people diflike the fmell of fulphur, diey may ufe in its place the pov/der of white he- lebore-root made up into an ointment, in the fame manner, which will feldom fail to cure the itch. People ought to be extremely cautious left they take other erup. dons for the itch ; as the ftoppage of thefe may be attended with fatal confequences. Many of the eruptive diforders to which chil- dren are liable, have a near refemblance to this difeafe ; and 1 have ofeen known infants killed by. being rubbed with greafy ointments. that make thefe eruptions ftrike fuddenly in, which nature had thrrwn out to preferve the patient's life, or prevent fome other ma- lady. much mifchief is Iikewife don* by the ufe of mercury in this dif- eafe,,, Some perfons are fo fool-hardy as to wafh the parts affeded with a ftrong folution of the corrofive fublimate. Others ufe the mercurial ointment, without taking the leaft care either to avoid cold, keep the body open, or obferve a proper regimen. The con- fequences of fuch condud may be eafily gueffed. I have known even the mercurial girdles produce bad effeds, and would advife every perfon, as he values his health, to beware how he ufes them. Meicury ought never to be ufed as a medicine without the great- eft care. Ignorant people look upon thefe girdles as a kind of charm, without confidering that the mercury enters the body. It is not to be told what mifchief is donebyufmg mercurial oint- ment for curing the itch and killing vermin; yet it is unneceffary for either : the former may be always more certainly cured by ful- phur, and the latter will never be found where due regard is paid to cleanlinefs. Thofe who «ould avoid this deteftable difeafe ought to beware of infeded perfojis, to ufe wholefome food, and to ftudy univer&l cleanlinefs*. CHAP. *The itch is now by cleanlinefs banifhed from every genteel family in Britain. It ftill however prevails among the poorer forts of peafants in Scotland, and among the manufacturers in England 1 hefe are not only fufficient to keep the feeds of the difeafe alive, but to fpread the infeftion among others. It were to be wifhed that fome effeftual method could he deviled for extirpating it altogether. Several coun- try clergymen have told me, that by getting fuch as were infefted cured, and flron- ly rec«m»eading an attention to cleanlinelis, they have baoifhea the itch entirely •ut of their parifhes. Why might not sthcrs do the fagae ? C* yhb ASTHMA. 253 CHAP. XL. OF THE ASTHMA. THE afthma is a difeafe of the lungs, which feldom admits of a cure. Perfons in the decline of life are moft liable to it. It is diftinguifhed into the moift and dry, or humoural and nervous. The former is attended with expectoration or fpitting ; but in the latter the patient feldom fpits, unlefs fometimes a little tough phlegm by the mere force of coughing. CAUSES.—The afthma is fometimes hereditary. It may like- wife proceed from a bad formation of the breaft; the fumes of me- tals or minerals taken into the lungs ; violent exercife, efpecially running ; the obftrudion of cuflomary evacuations, as the menfes, haemorrhoids, &c. the fudden retroceffion of the gout, or ftriking in of eruptions, as the fmall-pox, menfels, flee, violent paflions of the mind, as fudden fear or furprife. In a word, the difeafe may proceed'from any caufe that either impedes the circulation of the blood through the lungs, or prevents their beiag duly expanded by the air. SYMPTOMS------^n afthma is known by a quick laborious breathing which is generally performed with a kind of wheezing noife. Sometimes the difficulty of breathing is fo great, that the patient is obliged to keep in an ered pofture, otherwife he is in dan- ger of being fuffocated. A fit or paroxyfm of the afthma generally happens after a perfon has been expofed to cold eafterly winds, or has been abroad in thick foggy weather, or has got wet, or continu- ed long in a damp place under ground, or has taken forrfe food which the ftamach could not digeft, as pafteries, toafted checfe, or the like. The paroxyfm is commonly ufhered in with liitleffnefs, want of fleep, hoarfenek, a cough, belching of wind, a fenfe of heavinefs about the breaft, and difficulty of breathing. To thefe fucceed heat, fever, pain of the head, ficknefs and naufea, great oppreffion of the breaft, palpitation of the heart, a weak, and fometimes inter- mitting pulfe, an involuntary flow of tears, bilious vomittings, Seo-. All the fymptoms gr©w worfe towards night; the patient is eafier when up than in bed, and is very defirous of cool air. REGIMEN.—The food ought to be light, and of eafy digefti- on. Boiled meats are to be preferred to roafted, and the flefli of young animals to that of old. All windy food, and whatever is apt to fwell in the ftomach, is to be avoided. Light puddings, white broths, and ripe fruits baked, boiled, roafted are proper. Strong liquors of all kinds, efpecially malt-liquor, are hurtful. The pati- ent fhould eat a very light fupper, or rather none at all, and fhould never fuffer himfelf to be long coftive. His clothing fliould be warm, efpecially in the winter feafon. As all diforders of the breaft are much relieved by keeping the feet warm, and promoting the per- • fpiration, a flannel fhirt or waiftcoat,and thick fhoes, will be of An- gular fervice. But 254 Or the ASTHMA. But nothing is of fo great importance in the afthma, as pure and moderately warm air. Afthmatic people can feldom bear either the clofe heavy air of a large toAvn, or the fliarp, keen atmofphere of a bleak hilly country ; a medium thercfore,between thefe is to be chofen. The air near a large town is often better than at a diftance provided the patient be removed fo far as not to be affeded by die fmoke. Some afthmatic patients indeed, breathe eafier in town than in the country ; but this is feldom the cafe, efpecially in towns where much coal is burat. Afthmatic perfons who are obliged to be in town all day, ought at. leaft to fleep out of it. Even this will often prove of great fervice. Thofe who can afford it ought to travel into a warmer climate. Many afthmatic perfons \A'ho can- not live in Britain, enjoy very good health in the fouth of France, Portugal, Spain, or Italy. Exercife is Iikewife of very great importance in the afthma, as it promotes the digeftion, preparation of the blood, &c. The blood of afthmatic perfons is feldom. duly prepared, owing to the proper adion of the lungs being impeded. For this reafon fuch people ought daily to take as much exercife, either on foot, horfeback, or in a carriage, as they can bear. MEDICINE.----Almoft all that can be done by medicine in this difeafe, ii to relieve the patient when feized with a violent fit. This indeed requires the greateft expedition, as the difeafe often proves fuddenly fatal. In the paroxyftn or fit, the body is generally bound; a purging clyftcr,with a folution of afafcetida, ought there- fore to be adminiftered, and if there be occafion, it may be repeat- ed two or three times. The patient's feet and legs ought to be immerfed in warm water, and afterwards rubbed with a warm hand or dry cloth. Bleeding, unlefs extreme weaknefs or old age fliould forbid it, is highly proper. If there be a violent fpafm about the breaft or ftomach,warm fomentations, or bladders filled with Avarm milk and water, may be applied to the part affeded, and warm ca- taplafms to the foles of the feet. The patient muft drink freely of diluting liquors, and may take a tea-fpoonful of the tindure of eaftor and of faffron mixed together, in a cup of valerian tea, twice or thrice a day. Sometimes a vomit has a very good effed, and fnatches the patient, as it \A-ere, from the jaws of death. This however will be more fafe after other evacuations have been pre- mifed. A very ftrong infufion of roafted coffee is faid to give eafe in an afthmatio paroxyfm. In the moift afthma, fuch things as promote expedoration of fpitting, ought to be ufed ; as the fyrup of fquills, gum ammoniac and fuch like. A common fpoonful of the fyrup, or oxymel of fquills, mixed with an equal quantity of cinnamon-water, maybe taken three or four times through the day, and four or five pills made of equal parts of afafoetida and gum-ammoniac,at bed-time*. For *After copious evacuations, large dofes of «ther have been found very effica- cious in removing a fit of the afthma. 1 have Iikewife known the following mixt- ure produce very happ/effecls. To four or five ounces of the folution of gum- ammoniac, add two ounces of fimple cinnamon-water, the fame quantity of bal- famic fyrup, and half an ounce of paregoric elixir. Of this, two table fpoonfuls u:ay be taken every three hours. Of the APOPLEXY. a$c For^the convulfive or nervous afthma, antifpafmodics and bracers are the moft proper medicines. The patient may take a tca-fpoon- ftil of the paregoric elixir twice a day. The Peruvian bark is fome- times found to be of ufe in this cafe. It may be taken in fubftance, or infufed in wine. In fhort, every thing that braces the nerves, or takes off fpafm, may be of ufe in a nervous afthma. It is often relieved by the ufe of affes milk; I have Iikewife known ^ cow^s milk drank warm in the morning, have a very good effed in this cafe. In every fpecies of afthma, fetons and iffues have a good effed; they may either be fet in the back or fide, and fliould never be al- lowed to dry up. We fhall here, once for all, obferve that not only in the afthma, but in moft chronic difeafes, iffues are extreme- ly proper. They are both a fafe and efficacious remedy; and though diey do not always cure^tiie difeafe, yet they will often prolong the patient's life. CHAP. XLI. OF THE APOPLEXY. THE apoplexy is a fudden lofs of fenfe and motion, during which the patient is to all appearance dead; the heart and lungs however ftill continue to move. Though this difeafe'proves often fatal, yet it may be fometimes removed by proper care. It chiefly attacks fedentary perfons of a grofs habit, who ufe a rich and plentiful diet, and indulge in ftrong liquors. People inthe de- cline of life are moft fubjed to the apoplexy. It prevails moft in winter, efpecially in rainy feafons, and very low ftates of the ba- rometer. CAUSES.----The immediate caufe of an apoplexy is a com- preffion of the brain, occafioned by an excefs of blood, or a col- lediou of watery humouis. The former is called a fanguine, and the latter a.fcrous apoplexy. It may be occafioned by any thing that increafes the circulation towards the brain, or prevents the re- turn of the bloodfrom the head ; as intenfe ftudy; violent paflions*, viewing objedsfor a long time obliquely Avearing any thing toa tight about the neck; a rich and luxurious diet; fuppreffion of urine ; foffering the body to cool foddenly after having been great- ly heated : continuing long in a warm of cold bath ; the exceffive ufe * I knew a woman, who in a violent fit of anger was feized with a fanguine apo- plexy. She at firfl complained of extreme pain, --as if da" ;ers had been tluuft tfciough hir head," as fhe expreffed it. Afterwards fhe became coinatow, her pulle fiink very low, and was exec-ding flow. Bybleedine, bliftering, and otft-i evacuations, fhe was kept alive for about a fortnight. When her head was open- td^ a lar^t quantity.of extiavajared hlood was found ia the left ventricle of the brsi*. aj* 0* thb APOPLEXY* "fe of fptceriee, or high feafoned food; excefs of venery;the fudden ftriking in of any eruption; fuffering iffues, fetons, Sec. fuddenly to dry up, or the ftoppage of any cuftomary evacuation; a mercu- rial falivation pufhed too far, or fuddenly checked by cold; wound, or bruifes on the head; long e-xpofure to exceffive cold; poifonous •xhalations, &c. SYMPTOMS, and method of cure.---The ufual forerunners of an apoplexy are giddinefs, pain and fwimming of the head; lofs ©f memory: drowfinefs noife in the ears ; the night-mare ; afpon- taneous flux of tears; and laborious refpiration. When perfons of an apopledic make obferve theft fymptoms, they have reafon to fear the approach of a fit, and fhould indeavour to prevent it by bleeding, a flender diet and opening medicines. In the fanguine apoplexy, if the patient does not die fuddenly, the countenance appears florid, the fac«*ts fAvelled or puffed up, and the blood veffels, efpecially about the neck and temples, are tur- gid ; the pulfe beats ftrong ; the eyes are prominent and fixed, and the breathing k difficult, and performed with a Aborting noife. The excremeats and urine are often voided fpontaneoufly, and the pa- tient is fometimes feized with vomiting. In this fpecies of apoplexy every method muft be taken to lcffen die force of the circulation towards the head. The patient fliould be kept perfedly eafy and cool. His head fhould be raifed pretty high, and his feet fuffered to hang down. His clothes ought to be loofened, efpecially about the neck, and frefh air admitted into his chamber. His garters fhould he tied pretty tight, by which means die motion of the blood from tbe lower extremities will be retarded. As foon as the patient is placed in a proper pofture, he fhould be bled freely in the neck or arm, and, if there be occafion, the opera- linn may be repeated in two or three hours. A laxative clyfter, with plenty of fweet oil, or frefh butter, and a fpoonful or two of common fait in it, may be adminiftered every two hours; and biif- tering-plafters applied between the fhoulders, and to the calves of the legs. As foon as the fymptoms are a little abated, and the patient k able to fwallow, he ought to drink freely of fome diluting opening liquor, as a decodion of tamarinds and liquorice, cream-tartar whey or common whey with cream of tartar diffolved in it. Or he may take any cooling purge, as Glauber's, falts, manna diffolved in an infufion of fenna, or the like. All fpirits and other ftrong liquors are to be avoided. Even volatile falts held to the nofe do mifchief. Vomits, for the fame reafon, ought not to be given, or any tiling that may increafe the motion of the blood towards die head. In the ferous apoplexy,the fymptoms are nearly the fame, only the pulfe is not fo ftrong, the countenance is lefs florid, and the breathing lefs difficult. Bleeding is not lb neceffary here as in the former cafe. It may, however, generally be performed once with fafety and advantage, but fliould not be repeated. The patient fhould be placed in the fame pofture as direded above, and Ihouid have biiftering-plafters applied, and receive opening clyfters in the fame manner. 'Purges are here Iikewife neceffary, and the patient: may Of COSTiVENESS, Sic. HI rmy drink ftrong balm-tea. If he be bc^n^J to f.ve-t, kought to be promoted by drinking fmall wine-whey, or an infufion of carduas beuedidus. A plentiful fweat kept up for a confiderable rime, has often carried off a ferots apoplexy. When apoplecticfy-nptoms proceed from Opium, or other narcotic fubftances taken into the ftomach, vomits are neceffary. The pa- tient is generally relieved as foon as he has difeharged the poifon in this way. Perfons of an apopledic make or thofe who have been attacked by it, ought to ufe a very fpare and flender diet, avoiding ail ftrong liquors, ipiceries, and high-feafonei food. They ought Iikewife to guard againft all violent paflions, and toaA-oi.i die extremes of heat and cold. The head ihouid be fhaved, and daily wafhed with cold water. The feet ought to be kept warm, and never fuffered to con- tinue long wet. The bo#y muft be kept open either by food or me- dicine, and a little blood may be let every fpring and fall. Exercife fliould by no means be negleded ; but it ought to he taken in mode- ration. Nothing has a more happy effed in preventing an apoplexy th m perpetual iilueej or fetons; great care however muft.be taken, not to fuller them to dry up, wkhout open'ng others in their dead. Apopledk perfons ought never td go to reft with a full ftomach, or to lie with their heads low, or io wear any thing too tight, ab.ni; tiiek necks. ^JtTfj$ 5jj^v£^^^~ CHAP. XLII. OF COSTIVENESS. WE do not here mean to treat of thofe additions of the bowgk Avhich are the fymptoms of difeafes, as of the colic, the iliac paflion, Sec. but only to take notice of that infrequeney of flock which fometimes happens, and which in fome particular ceaffou- tims may occafion difeafes. Coltiveaefe miv proceed from drinking.rough red wine-;, or oth- er airingent liquors; too much exercife, efpecially on hon- :iack. It may ir;v; fe\ proceed from a long ufe of cold infipid food, winch d >e; not "udkiently ftimulate the inteftines. Sometimes it k o»vkg to the biie not de 7-ending to the hiteftinci, as in the jaundice; a..d at other times it proceeds from difeafes of die inteftines themfelves, as apalfy, fpafmsi torpor, tumours, a coll dry ftate of die inteftines, O. - Exceffive coftiv^neis is apt to occa feem pains of the head, vomit- ing, colics, and orii.r complaints of the bowels. It is particularly hurtful to hypochondriac and hyfteric perfons, as it generates wind v ^ d other grievous fymptoms. Some people h.-we-er, can bearcof- tivenefs 258 Of COSTIVENESS, &c. tivenefs to a great degree. I know perfons who enjoy pretty good health, yet do not go to ftool above once a-week, and others not above once a-fortnight. Indeed I have heard of fome who do not go above once a-month. Perfons who are generally coftive, fhould live upon a moiftening and laxative diet, as roafted or boiled apples, pears, ftewed prunes, raifins, gruels with currants, butter, honey, fugar and fuch like. Broths with fpinage, leeks, and other foft potherbs, are Iikewife proper. Rye- bread, or that which is made of a mixture of wheat and rye together, ought to be eaten. No perfon troubled with cof- tivenefs,, fhould eat white bread alone, efpecially that which is made of fine flour. The beft bread for keeping the body foluble, is a\ hat in fome parts of England they call meftin. It is made of a mixture of wheat and rye, and is very agreeable t%thofe who are accuftom- ed to it. „ Coftivenefs is increafed by keeping the body too warm, and by every thing that promotes the perfpiration ; as wearing flannel ly- ing too long a-bed, &c. Intenfe thought, and a fedentary life, aTe Iikewife hurtful. All the fecretions and excretions are promoted by moderate exercife without doors, and by a gay, cheerful, fprightly temper of mind. The drink fhould be of an opening quality. All ardent fpirits, auftere and aftringent wines, as port, claret, Sec. ought to be avoid- ed. Malt-liquor that is fine, and of a moderate ftrength is very proper. Butter-milk, whey, and other watery liquors, are iike- wife proper, and may be drank in turns, as the patient's inclination direds. Thofe who are troubled with ooftivenefs, ought if poffible, to remedy It by diet, as the conftant ufe of medicines for that purpofe is attendad with many inconveniencies, and often with bad con- fluences*. I never knew any one get into a habit of taking me- dicine for keeping the body open, who could leave it off. In time the cuftom becomes neceffary, and generally ends in a total relaxa- tion * The learned Dr. Arbuthnot advifes thofe who are troubled with coftivenefs to ufe animal oils, as frefh butter, cream, marrow, fat broths, efpecially thole made of the internal parts of animals, as the liver, heart, midriff. See. He Iikewife recom- mends the cxprtf.Ted oils of mild vegetables, as olives almonds, paffcaches, and the fruits themfelvei ; all oily and mild fruits, as figs; decoftians of mealy vegetables ; thefe lubricate the inteftines; fome laponareous iubfhnces which ftimulate gently, as honey-, hydroinel; or bored honey and water, unrelined fugar, &c. The .Dedlor obierves, that fnch lenitive fubftances arc proper for perfons of dry atrabilarien conllitmiciu, who are fubjeft to affliction of the belly, and tbe pilts, and will operate when Itronger rne.licir.al fiibllances are fomeiimes ineffectual; !>u» that fuch lenitive diet hurts thofe whofe bowels are weak and lax.
