WANT or APPETITE. 659 tion of the bowels, indigeftion, lofs of appetite, wafting of the ftrength, and death. When the body cannot be kept open without medicine, we AvoultJ recommend gentle doles'of rheubarb to be taken twice or thrice a week. This is not near fo injurious to the ftomach as aloes, jalap, or the other draftic purgatives fo much in ufe. Infufions of fenna and manna may Iikewife be taken, or half an ounce of foluble tar- tar diffolved in water-gruel. About the fize of a nutmeg of lenitive eleduary, taken twice or thrice a-day generally anfwers die pur- pofe very well. WANT OF AP PETTI E. TPIIS may proceed from afoul ftomach; indigeftion; the want cf free air and exercife; grief; fear; anxiety; or any of the de- preffing paflions; exceffive heat; the ufe of ftrong broths, fat meats, or any thing that palls the appetite, or is hard of digeftion ; the im. moderate ufe of ftrong liquors, tea, tobacco, opium, &c. The patient ought, if pofiible, to make choice of an open dry air, to take exercife daily on horfeback or in a carriage; to rife betimes; and to avoid all intenfe thought. He fhould ufe a diet of eafy di- geftion; and fliould a-"-oid exceffive heat and great fatigue. If want of appetite proceeds from errors in diet, or any other part of the patient's regimen, it ought to be changed. If naufea and Teachings fhew that the ftomach is loaded with crudities, a vo- mit will be of fervice. After this agentle purge or two of rhubarb, ot any of the bitter purging falts,may be taken. The patient ought next to ufe fome of the ftomachic bitters infufed in wine. Though gentle evacuations be neceffary, yet ftrong purges and vomits are to be avoided, as they weaken the ftomach and hurt digeftion. Elixir of vitriol is an excellent medicine in moft cafes of indi- geftion, weaknefs of the ftomach, or want cf appetite. Fro hi twen- ty to thirty drops of it may be taken'twice or thrke a-day in a glafs of wine or tvater. It may Iikewife be mixed with the tindure of the bark, one drachm of the former to an ounce of the latter, and two tea-fpoonfuls of it taking in wine and water, as above. The chalybeat waters, if drank in moderation, arc generally of confiderable fervice in this cafe. The falt-ivater has Iikewife good effeds; but it muft not be ufed too freely. The water of Harrovr- gate, Scarborough, Moffet, and moft other fpas in Britain, may he ufed with advantage. We would advife all who are affected \: ith indigeftion and want of appetite, to repair to thefe places of public rendezvous. The very change of air, and the cheerful company, will be of fervice, not to mention the exercife, difiipatiou, amuhi. ments, Sec. OF THE HEART-BURN. WHAT is commonly called the hean-burat is n6t a difeafe of that organ, but an uneafy fenfation of h«at or acrimony, about the jiit *6o C? the HEART-TURN. jit of the ftomach, whlth is fometimes attended widi anxictv, nau. fa and vomiting. It may proceed from debility of die ftomach, indigeftion, bile, the abounding of an acid in the ffomach, &c. Perfons Avho ars liable to this complaint, ought to avoid ftale liquors, acids windy or g'-cafy alk"(H,;s, and fhould never ufe violent exercife foon after a hearty meal I know many perfons whoaicver fail to have th- heart-burn, if they ride foon after uinner, provided they have drank ale, vine, or any feimented'liquor ; but are never rroubled Avith it when they have drank i uni or brandy and water, without any fugar cr acid. When the heart-burn proceeds from debility cf the ftomach, or indigeftion, the patient ought to take a dofe Or tA\ro of rhubarb; af- terwards he may ufe infufions of the Peruvian bark : or any other of the flomachic bitters, in wine or brandy. Exercife in the open an will likewiie be of ufe, and every thing that promotes digeftion. When bilious.humours occafion the heart burn, a tea-fpoonful of the fweet fpirit of nitre in a glafs of Avater, or a cup of tea, will generally give eafe. If it proceeds from the ufe ofgreafy aliments, a drpm of brapdy or rum may be taken. If acidity or fournefs of the li ■ v,:ach occafion the heart-burn, ab- forbents are the proper medicines. In this cafe an ounce of pow-_ dered chalk, half an ounce of fine fugar, and a quarter cf an ounce of gum-arabic, may be mixed in an Englifh quart of'water, and a tea cupful of it taken as often as is neceflary. Such as do not chufe chalk may take a tea fpoonful of prepared oyfter-fhells, or of the powder called crabs-eyes, in a glafs of cinnamon or peppermint- water. But die fafeft and beft abforbent is magnefia alba. This not only ads as an abforbent, but Iikewife as a purgative ; whereas chalk and other abforbents of that kind, are apt to lie in the intef- tines, and occafion obflrudions. This powder is not difagreeable, a id may be taken in a cup of tea, or a glafs of mint water. A large tea-fpoonful is the ufual dofe ; but it may be taken in a much greater quantity when there is occafion. Thefe things are now generally made up into lozenges for the conveniency of being car- ried in the pocket, and taken at pleafure. If wind be the caufe of this complaint,the moft proper medicines are thofe called carminatives; as anifeeds, juniper-berries, ginger, canella alba, cardamon feeds, Sec. Thefe may either be chewed, or infufed in wine, brandy or other fpirits. One of the fafeft med- icines of this kind is the tindure made by infufing an ounce of rhubarb, and a quarter of an ounce of the leffer cardamon feedsdn an Englifh pint of brandy. After this has digeftedfor two or three days, it ought to be ftrained, and four ounces of Avhite fugar-candy added to it. It muft ftand to digeft a fecond time till the fugar be diffolved. A table-fpoonful of it may be taken occafionally for a dofe. I have frequently known the heart-burn cured, particularly in pregnant ivomen, by chewing green tea. Two table-fpoonfuls of what is called the milk of gum ammoniac, taken ence or twice a- day, will fometimes cure the heart-burp. CHAP. i Of NERVOUS DISEASES. 26x CHAP. XLIII. OF NERVOUS DISEASES. OF all difeafes incident to mankind, thofe of die nervous kind are the moft complicated and difficult to cure. A volume would not be fuflicient to pointout their various appearances. They imitate almoft every difeafe; and are feldom alike in two different perfons, or even the fame perfon at different times. Proteus-like, they are continually changing fliape ;■ and upon every frefh attack, the patient thinks he feels fymptoms which he never experienced before. Nor do they only affed the body; the mind likewifefuf- fers, and is thereby rendered weak and peevifh. The Ioav fpirits, rimouroufnefs, melancholy and fickknefs of temper, which gener- ally attend nervous, diforders, induce many to believe that they a; e entirely difeafes of the mind; but this change of temper is rather a confequence, than the caufe of nervous difeafes. CAUSES.----Every thing that tends to relax or weaken the body; difpofes it to nerArous difeafes, as indolence, exceffive venery, drinking too much tea, or other weak Avatery liquors warm, fre- quent bleeding, purging, vomiting, &c. Whatever hurts the diges- tion, or prevents the proper wflimilation of the food, has Iikewife this effed; as long falling, excefs in eating or drinking, the ufe of Avindy, crude or unwholefome aliments, an unfavourable pofture of the body, 6Vc. Nervous diforders often proceed from intenfe application te ftudy. Indeed feAv ftudious perfons are entirely free frm them. Nor is this at all to be wondered at; intenfe thinking not only preys upon the fpirits, but prevents the perfon from taking proper exercife, by which means the digeftion is impaired, the nourifhment prevented, the folids relaxed, and the whole mafs of humours vitiated. Grief and difappointment likeivife produce the fame effeds. I have known more nervous patients who dated the commencement of their dis- orders from the lofs of a hufband, a favourite child, or from feme difappointment in life, than from any other caufe. In a word, avhat- ever weakens the body, or deprefl'es the fpirits, may occafion ner- vous diforders, as unwholefome air, want of fleep, great fatigue, difagreeable apprehenfions, anxiety, vexation. Sec. % SYMPTOMS.----We fhall only mention fome of the moft ge- neral fymptoms of? thefe diforders, as it would be both an ufelefs and an endlefs talk to enumerate the whole. They generally begin with Avindy inflations or diflenfions of the flomach and inteftines; the appetite and digeftion are ufually bad ; yet fometimes there is an uncommon craving for food, and a quick digeftion. The food often turns four on the ftomach; and the patient is troubled with vomiting of clear water, tough phlegm, or a blackifh coloured li- quor refembling the grounds of coffee. Excruciating pains are often felt about the navel, attended with a rumbling or mm muring noife in the bcivek. The body is fometimes loofe, but more commonly bound, which occafions a retention of wind and great uneafirefs. i6t Of NERVOUS DISEASES. The urine is fometimes in fmall quantity, at other times very co- pious and quite clear. There is a great ftraitnefs of the breaft, with liirficulty of breathing; violent palpitations of the heart; fud- den flu:hings'of h-at in various parts of the body; at other times a fenfe of et Id, as it water were pouted on them; flying pains in die arms and limbs, pains in the back and belly, refembling thofe oc- cafioned by die gravel; the pulfe very variable, fometimes uncom. monly flow, and at other times very quick; yawning, the h«ccup, frequent fighing,and a fenfe of fuffocation, as if from a ball orJump ef the throat; alternate fits of crying and convulfive laughing; the fleep is unfound, and feldom refrefhing: and the patient is often, troubled with the night-mare. As the difeafe increafes, the patient is molefted with head-achs, cramps, and fixed pains in various parts of the body; the eyes are clouded, and often affeded with pain and drynefs; there is a noife in the ears, and often a dullnefs of hearing; in fhort the whole aai- rr.al fundions are impaired. The mind is difturbed on the moft trii vial occafions, and is hurried into the moft perverfe commotions, inquietudes, terror, fadnefs, anger, diffidence, &c. The patient is apt to entertain wild imaginations, and extravagant fancies; the memory becomes weak and the judgment fails. Nothing is more charaderiftic of this difeafe than a conftant dread of death. This renders thofe unhappy perfons who labour under it peevifh, fickle, impatient, and apt to run from one phyfician to another; which is one reafon Avhy they feldom reap any benefit from medicine, as they have-not fufficient refolution to perfift in any one courfe till it has time to produce its proper effeds. They are Iikewife apt to imagine that they labour under difeafes from \vhi«h they are quite free ; and are very angry if any one attempts to fet them right, or laugh them cut of their ridiculous notions. REGIMEN.----Perfons afflided with nervous difeafes ought never to fall long. Their food fliould he folid and nourifhing,but cf eafy digeftion. Fat meats and heavy fauces are hurtful. All excefs fhould be carefully avoided. They ought never to eat more at a time than they can eafily digeft ; but if they feel themfelves weak and faint betAveen meals, they ought to eat a bit of bread,and drink a glafs of wiue. Heavy fuppers are to be avoided. Tho Wine in excefs enfeebles the body, and impairs the raculties of the mind, yet taken in moderation it ftrengthens the ftomach, and pro- motes digeftion. Wine and water is a very proper drink at meals; but if Avinc four in the ftomach, or if the patient is much troubled with wind, brandy and water will anfwer better. Every thing that is windy or hard of digeftion muft be avoided. All weak and warm liquors are hurtful, as tea, coffee, punch, &c. People may find a temporary relief in the ufe of thefe, but they always increafe die malady as they weaken the ftomach, and hurt digeftion. Above all things, drams are to be avoided. Whatever immediate eafe the patient may feel from the ufe of ardent fpirits, they are fure toag* gravate the malady, and prove certain poifon at laft. Thefe cau- tions are the more neceffary, as moft nerv»us people are peculiarly fond of tea and ardent fpirits j to the ufe of which many of them fall vidims. Exei cif« Or NERVOUS DISEASES. stSj Exercife in nervous diforders is fuperior to all medicines. Rid- ing on horfeback is generally efteemed the beft, as it gives motion to the whole body without fatigueing it. I have known fome pa- tients, however, with whom walking agreed better,and others who were moft benefitted by riding in a carriage. Every one ought to life that which he finds moft beneficial. Long fe£-voyages have an excellent effed ; and to thofe who have fuflicient refolution, we would by all means recommend this courfe. Even change of place, and the fight of new objeds, by diverting the mind, have a great tendency to remove thefe complaints. For this reafon a long journey, or a voyage, is of much more advantage than riding fhort journies near home. A cool and dry air is proper, as it braces and invigorates the whole body. Few things tend more to relax and enervate than hot air, efpecially that which is rendered fo by great fires, or floves in fmall apartments. But Avhen the ftomach or bowels are weak, the body ought to be well guarded againft cold, efpecially in winter, by wearing a thin flannel waiftcoat next the fkin. This wall keep up an equal perfpiration, and defend the alimentary canal from ma- ny impreflions to which it would otherwife be fubjed, upon every fudden change from warm to cold weather. Rubbing the body fre- quently with a flefh-brufh, or a coarfe linen cloth, is Iikewife bene- ficial, as it promotes the circulation, perfpiration, &c. Perfons who have weak nerves ought to rife early, and take exercife before breakfaft, as lying too loag a-bed cannot fail to relax the folids. They ought Iikewife to be diverted,and to be kept as eafy and cheer- ful as pofiible. There is not any thing Avhich hurts the nervous fyf- tem,or weakens the digeftive powers more than fear,grief or anxiety. MEDICINES----Though nervous difeafes are feldom radi- cally cured, yet their fymptoms may fometimes be alleviated, and the patient's life rendered et leaft more comfortable hy proper med- icines. When the patient is coftive, he ought to take a little rhubarb, Or fome other mild purgative, and fliould never foffer his body to he long bound. All ftrong and violent purgatives are however to be avoided, as aloes, jalap, &c. I have generally feen an infofion of fenna and rhubarb in brandy, anfwer very well. This may be made of any ftrength, and taken in fuch quantity as the patient finds neceffary. When digeftion is bad or the ftomach relaxed and Weak, the following infufion of Peruvian bark and other bitters may be ufed with advantage : Take of Paruvian bark an ounce, gentian-root, orange peel, and s coriander feed, of each half an ounce ; let thefe ingredknts be all .bruifed in a mortar, and infufe in a^botde *of brandy or rum, for the fpace of five or fix days. A table-fpoonful of the ftrained li- quor maybe taken in half a glafs of water, an hour before break- fail, dinner and fupper. Few things tend more to ftrengthen the nervous fyftem tiian cold bathing. This p>radice, if duly perfifted in, will produce very ex- traordinar y effeds ; but when the liver or other vifcera are obftrud- ed, or otherwife unfound, the cold bath is improper. It is there- fore to be ufed with very great caution. The moft proper fcafoii* =64 Of MELANCHOLY. for it are fummer and aulumn. It will be fufflcknt, efpecially for perfons of a fpare habit, to go into the cold bath three or fom rimes a-iveek. If the patient be Avcakened by it, or feels chilly for a long time after comming out, it is improper* In patients afflided with wind, I have always obferved the great- eft benefit from the elixir of vitriol. It may be taken in the quan- tity of fifteen, twenty, or thirty drops, tAvice or thrice a-day, in a glafs of water. Tibs both expels wind, ftrengthens the Ifomuch, and promotes digeftion. Opiates are generally extolled in thefe maladies ; but as theyonly palliate the fymptoms and generally afterwardsiincreafe the difeafe, we Avould advife people to be extremely fparing in the ufe of them, left habit render them at laft abfolutely neceffary. It Avould be an eaiy mat.T to enumerate many medicines which have been extolled for relieving nervous diforders ;but whoever wilhes for a thorough cure, inuft exped it from regimen alone ;ive fhall therefore - niit mentioning more medicines, and again recom- mend the ftrideft attention to diet, air, exerciss, and AMtM. MENTS. OF MELANCHOLY. Melancholy is that ftate of alienation or weaknefs of mind whicb renders people incapable of enjoying the pleafores, or performing the duties of life. It is a degree of infanity, and often terminates in abfolute madnefs. CAUSES.----It may proceed from an hereditary difpofition • intenfe thinking, efpecially where the mind is long occupied by one objed ; violent paflions or affedions of the mind, as loi'e, fear, joy, grief, pride, and fuch like. It may alfo be occafioned by exceffive venery ; narcotic or ftupefadive poifons ; a fedentary life ; foli- tude the fuppreffion of cuftomary evacuations ; acute fevers or other difeafes. Violent anger will change melancholy into mad- nefs ; and exceffivecold,efpecially of the loAver extremities,willfcrrc the blood into the brain, and produce all the fymptoms of madnefs. It may Iikewife proceed from the Ufe of aliment that is hard of di- geftion, or which cannot be eafily affimilated ; from a callous ftate of the integuments of the brain, or a drynefs of the brain itfelf. To all which we may add gloomy and miftaken notions of religion. SYMPTOMS.----When perfons begin to be melancholy, they aie timourous ; watchful ; fond of fclitude ; fretful ; fickle ; capti- ous and inquifitive ; felicitous about trifles ; fometimes niggardly, and at other times prodigal. The body is generally bound,the urine thin, and in fmall quantity ; the ftomach and bowels inflated with wind ; the complexion pale ; the pulfe flow and weak. • The func- tions of the mind are alfo greatly perverted, infomuch that the pa- tient often imagines himfelf dead, or changed into fome other ani- mal. Some have imagined their bodies were made of glafs, or o- ther brittle fubftances, and were afraid to move, left they fhould be broken to pieces. The unhappy patient, in this cafe, unlefs care- fully watched, is apt to put an end to his own miferable life. When Oj MELANCHOLY. 265 When die difeafe is cwujg to an obftrudion of cuftomary evacu- ations, or any bodily diforder, it is eafier cured than wdien it pro- ceeds from affedions of the mind, or an herctihaiy taint. A dif- charge of blood from the nofe, loofenefs, foabby eruptions^ the bleeding piles, or the menfes fometimes carry off this difeafe. REGIMEN.----The diet fhould confift chiefly of vegetables of a cooling and opening quality. Animal food, efpecially faked or fmoke-dried fifh or flefh, ought to be avoided. All kinds of fliell- fifh, are bad. Aliments prepaied with onions.garlic, or any thing that generates thick blood, are Iikewife improper. All rinds of fruits that are wholefome may be eaten Avith advantage* Eoerhave gives an inftance of a patient who, by a long ufe of whey, water, and garden fruit, recovered, after having evacuated a great quan- tity of black coloured matter. Strong liquors of every kind ought to be avoided as poifon. The moft proper drink is water, whey, or very fmall beer. Tea and coffee are improper. If honey agrees with the patient, it may be eaten freely, or his drink may be fweetened with it. Infufions of balm-leaves, penny-royal, the roots of wild valerian, or the flowers of the lime-tree, may be drank freely, either by then ifelves,or fweet- ened with honey, as the patient fhall chufe. The patient ought to take as much exercife as he can bear. This helps to diffolve the vicid humours, it removes obftrudions, pro- motes the perfpiration, and all die other fecretions. Every kind of madnefs is attended with a diminifhed perfpiration ; all means ought therefore to be ufed to promote that , necefikry and falutary difcharge. Nothing can have a more dircd tendency to increafe the difeafe than confining the patient to a clofe apartment. Were he forced to ride or walk a certain number of miles every day, it would tend greatly to alleviate his diforder; but it A\roukl have ftill a better effed, if he were obliged to labour a piece of ground. By digging, hoeing, planting, foAving, Sec. both the body and mind would be exercifed. A long journey, cr a vr.yage, efpecially to- wards a warmer climate, with agreeable companions, have often very happy effeds. A plan of this kind, with a ftrid attention to diet, is a much more rational method of cure, than confining the patient Avithin doors and plying him with medicines. MEDICINE.----In the cure cf this difeafe particular attention muft be paid to the mind. When the patient is in a low ftate, his mind ought to be foothed and diverted widi variety ofamufements, as entertaining flories, paftimes, mufk, Cec. This feems to have been the method of curing melancholy among the jrws,as we learn from the flory of king Saul ; and indeed it is a very rational one. Nothing can remove ctifeafes cf the mind fo effeduaily as applica- tions to the mind itfelf, the moft efficacious of which is mufic. The; patient's company ought hkevife to confift of fuch perfons as are agreeable to him. People in this ftate are apt to conceive unac- countable averiioas againft partienkr p.Mrfons ; and .he very fight; of fuch perfons is fufficient to dillrad then /a.a.u., -u.d throw them Into the utmoft perturbation. 1L k Wh« a66 •f *«i PALSY. Wli£n the patient is high, evacuations are neceffary. In this cafe he muft be bled,andhave his body kept openby purging medicines, as marma, rhubarb, cream of tartar, or the foluble tartar. I have feen the laft have very happy effeds. It may be taken in the dofe of half an ounce, diffolved in AA'ater-gruel, every day, for feveral weeks, or even for months, if neceffary. More or lefs may be gi- ven according as it eperares. Vomits have likeAvife a good effed ; but they mull be pretty ftrong, otherwife they will not operate. Whatever increafes the evacuation of urine or promotes perfpi- ration, has a tendency to remoA'e this difeafe. Both thefe fecretions may be promoted by the ufe of nitre and vinegar. Half a drachm of purified nitre may be given three or four times a-day, in any manner that is moft agreeable to the patient; and an ounce and an half of diftilled vinegar may be daily mixed with his drink, Dr. Locker feems to think vinegar the beft medicine that can be given ui this difeafe* Camphire and mufk have Iikewife been ufed in this cafe with advantage. Ten or twelve grains of camphire may be rubbed in a mortar, with half a drachm of nitre, and taken twice a-day, or of- tener if the ftomach will bear it. If it will not fit upon the ftomach in this form, it maybe made into pills with gum afafoetida and Ruf- fian eaftor, and taken in the quantity above direded. If mufk is to be ad mini ftered,a fcruple or twenty-five grains of it may be made into a bolus with a little honey or common fyrup, and taken twice or thrice a-day. We do not mean that all thefe medicines fliould be adminiftered at once ; but whichever of them k given, muft be duly perfifted in, and where one fails another may be tried. As it is very difficult to induce patients in this difeafe to take me- dicines, we fhall mention a few outward applications which fome- times do good ; the principal .of thefe are iffues, fetons, and warm bathing. Iffues may be made in any part of the body, but they generally have the beft effed near the fpine. The difcharge from thefe may be gready promoted by dreffingthem with the mild blif- tering ointment, and keeping what are commonly called the orrice peafe in them. The moil proper place for a feton is betAveen the fhoulder-blades: and it ought to be placed upwards and down- wards, or in the di red ion of the fpine. OF THE PALSY. THE palfy is alofs or diminution of fenfe or motion, or ofbodv in one or more parts cf the body. Of all the affedions called ner- vous, this is the moft fuddenly fatal. It is more or lefs dangerous, •According to the importance of the pare affeded. A palfy of the heart, lungs, or any part neceflary to life, is mortal. When it af- feds the ftomach, the inteftines, or the bladder, it is highly dan- gerous. If the face be affeded, the cafe is bad, as ic fhews that the . difeafe proceeds from the brain. When the part affeded feels cold, is infenfible, or Wades away, or when the j;d-:ueni and memory hcf$\ tu fail, there if fmall hope of a em j. CAUSES. %t the PALSY. *f>7 CAUSES-----The immediate caufe of palfy is any thing that prevents the regular exertion of the nervous power upon any parti- cular mufcle or part of the body. The occafional and predifpofmg caufes are various, as drunkennefs; Avnunds of the brain, or fpinai-. marrow; preffure upon the brain, or net res; very cold or damp air; the fuppreffion of cuftomary evacuations; fudden fear; want of exercife ; or-whatever greatly relaxes the fyftem; as drinking much tea*, or coffee. The palfy may likevrife proceed from wounds of die nerves themfelve*, from the poifonous fumes of metals or mi- nerals, as mercury, lead, arfenic. In young perfons of a full habit, the palfy muft be treated in the fame manner, as the fanguine apoplexy. The patient muft be bled, bliftered, and have his body opened by fharp clyfters, or purgative medicines. But in old age, or where the difeafe proceeds from re- laxation or debility, AAhich is generally the cafe, a quite contrary courfe muft be purfued. The diet muft be warm and invigorating, feafoned with fpicy and aromatic vegetables, as muftard horfe-ra- difh, Sec. The drink may be generous wine, muftard, whey, or brandy and water. Fridion with the flefh brulh or a warm hand, is extremely proper, efpecially on the parts affeded. Biiftering- plafters may Iikewife be applied to the affeded parts with advan- tage. When this cannot be done, they may be rubbed with the vo- latile liniment, or the nerve ointment of the Edinburgh difpenfato- ry. One of die beft external applications is eledricity. The ftiocks, or rather vibrations, fhould be received on the part affeded, and they ought daily to be repeated for feveral Aveeks. Vomits are very beneficial in this kind of palfy, and ought- fre- quendy to be adminiftered. Cephalic fnuff, or any thing that makes die patient fnee?:e, is Iikewife of ufe. Some pretend to have found great benefit from rubbing the parts affeded with nettles; but this does not feem to be any way preferable to bliftering. If the tongue is affeded the patient may gargle his moudi frequently with brandy and muftard; or he may hold a bit of fugar in his mouth wet with the palfy-drops or compound fpirits of lavender. The wild valerian-root is a very proper medicine in this cafe. It may eidier be taken in an infufion wfitiifage-leaves, or half a drachm of it in powder may be given in a glafs of wine three or four times a-day. If the patient cannot ufe the Valerian, he may take of fal volatile oleofum, compound fpirits of lavender, and tindure of caftor each half an ounce; mix thefe together, and take forty or fiiy drops in a glafs of wine, three or four times a-day. A table-fpoon- ful of muftard-feed taken frequently is a very good medicine. The patient ought Iikewife to cheAv cinnamon bark,ginger or odier warm fpicerks. Exercife * Many people imagine that tea h.:» no tendency to hurt the nerve, and that drink- ing the lame quantity of warm -..-.iter would fee equally pernicious. This howc.cr feems to be a miftake Many p>;r!ons diii.k three oi* four cups ot wbiu milkai<d water daily, without feeling any bad confequences ; ytt the lait.e quai ti'y of tea will make their hands fhake l«r twenty-four hours. 1 hat tea ahtCts the ncrvs. ,s Iikewife evident from its preventing fleep, oecafioniog ^id JL....!.., J...meis »i die Sght, ficknels, Sec. 268 Of thb EPILEPSY, Exercife is of the utmoft importance in thepaHy; but the fi- tient mnft beware of coid, damp, and moift air. He ought to v. ear flannel next his fkin; and, if pofiible, fhould remove into a warm er climate. OF THE EPILEPSY, OR FALLING SICKNESS. THE epilepfy is a fodden deprivation of a1*1 the fenfes, whereia the patient falls hidden'y down, and is affeded widi violent con- vulfive motions. Children, efpecially thofe who are delicately brought up, are moft fubjed to it. It more frequently attacks men than Avomen, and is very difficult to cure. When the epilepfy attacks children, there is reafon to hope it may go off about the time of purberty. , When it attacks an; perfon after twenty years cf age, die cure is difficult; but when after forty, a cure is hardly to be expeded, If the fit continues only for a ihort fpace, and returns feldom, there is reafon to hope; but if it continues fong, and returns f:e- quently the profped is bad. It is a very unfavourable fymptom when the patient is feized with the fits in his fleep. CAUSES.----The epilepfy is fometimes hereditary. It may Iikewife proceed from blows, bruifes, or wounds oh the head ; a colledion of water, blood, or ferous humours in the brain; a po. lipus ; tumours or concretions within the fkull; exceffive drink- ing; intenfe ftudy; excefs of veriery; worms; teething; fuppreffion of cuftomary evacuations; too great emptinefs or repletioa; vioknt paflions or affedions of the mind, as fear, joy, Sec. hyfteric affec- tions; contagion received into the body, as die infedion of die fmall-pox, meafels, &c. SYMPTOMS.—An epileptic fit is generally preceded b y un- ufilal wearinefs; pain in the head; dullnefs; giddinefs; noife ia the ears; d4mnefs of fight; palpitation of the heart; difturbed fleep ; difficult breathing; the bowels are inflated with wind; the urine is in great quantity, but thin; the complexion is pale; the extremities are cold ; and the patient often feels, as it were, a ftream of cold air afcending towards his head. In the fit, the patient generally makes an unurual noife; his thumbs are drawn in towards the palms of bis hands; his eyes are diftorted; he ftarts, and foams at the mouth; his extremities are bent or twilled various ways; he often difcharges his feed, urine, and faeces involuntarily; ami is quite deftitute of all fenfe and rea- fon. After the fit is over, his fenfes gradually return, and he com- plains of a kind of ftupor, Aveaiiuefs, and pain of his head; but hat no remembrance of what happened to him during the fit. The fits are fometimes excited by violent affedions of the mind, a debauch of liquor, exceffive heat, cold, or the like. This difeafe, from the difficulty of inveftigating its caufe, and its ftrange fymptoms, was formerly attributed to the wrath of the gods, or the agency of evil fpirits. In modern times it has often, by the vulgar, been imputed to witchcraft or facination. It de- pends ©r FALLING SICKNESS. t6f pend.c, ho\rever,as much upon natural caufes as any other malady; and its cure may often be effeded by per filling in the ufe of proper means. REGIMEN—Epileptic patients ought, if pofiible, to breathe a pure and free air. Their diet fh»uld be light but nourifliing. They ought to drink nothing ftrong, to avoid fwine's flefli, - water-fowl, and Iikewife all windy and oily vegetables, as cabbage, nuts, Sec. They ouphtto keep themfelves cheerful, carefully guarding againft all violent paffiens, as anger, fear, exceffive joy, and the like. Exercife k liktwife of great ufe; but the patient mu't be care- ful to avoid all extremes either of heat or cold, all dangereus fitua- tions, as Handing upon precipices, riding, deep waters, and fuch like. ' MEDICINE.----The intentions of cure muft vary accordirg to the caufe of the difeafe. If the patient be of a fanguine tempera- ment, and there be reafon to fear an obftrudion in the biain, bleeding and other evacuations will be neceffary. When the dif- eafe is occafioned by the ftoppage of cuftomary evacuations, thefe, if poflibk, muft be reftored; if this cannot be done, others may be fubflituted in their place. Iffues or fetons in this cafe have cften a very good effed. When there is reafon to believe that the difeafe proceeds from worms, proper medicines muft ^be ufed to kill, or carry off thefe vermin. When the difeafe proceeds from teething, die body fhould be kept open by emollient clyfters, the feet frequendy bathed in warm water, and if the fits prove ob- ftinate, a bliftering-plafter may be put between the fhoulders, The fame method is to he followed, when epileptic fits precede the eiup- tion of the fmall-pox, or meafles, Sec. When the difeafe is hereditary, or proceeds from a wrerg for- mation of the brain, a cure is not to be expeded. When it is owing to a dcbilby or too great an irritability of the nervous fys- tem, fuch medicines as tend to brace and ftrengthen the nerves may be ufed, as the Peruvian bark, and fleel; or the anti-tpileptis ekduaries, recommended by Fuller and Mead*. The flowers of zine have of late been highly extolled for the cure of the epilepfy. Though this medicine will not be found to anfwer the expedations which have been raifed concerning it, yet in obftinate epileptic cafes it deferves a trial. The dofe is from one to three or four grains, which may be taken either in pills or a bolus as the patient inclines. The beft method is to begin with a Angle grain four or five times a-day, and gradually to increafe the dofe as far as the patient can bear it. I have known this medicine, when duly perfifted in, prove beneficiaL Mufk has fometimes beea found to fucceed in the epilepfy. Tea or twelve grains of it, with die fame quantity of faditious cinna- bar, may be made up into a bulus, and taken every night and morning. Sometimes the epilepfy has been ettrtd by electricity. Convulfiosn-fibt * See Appendix, Electuary for tke i.?iLjFsv. 2?* Of TaF HICCUf. Convulfion-fits proceed from the fame caufes, and muft be trea> ed in the fame manner as the epilepfy- There is one paiticidar fpecies of convulfion-fits which com- monly goes by the name of St. Vitus's dance, wherein the patient is agitated with ftrange motions and gefticulations, which by the common people are generally believed to be the effeds of witch- craft. This difeafe may be cured by repeated bleedings and purg- es ; and afterwards ufing the medicines prefcribed above for the epilepfy, viz. die Peruvian bark and fnake-root, &c. Chalybeate- waters are found to be beneficial in this cafe. The cold bath is Iikewife of fingular fervice, and ought never to be negleded whe« the patient can bear it. OF THE HICCUP. THE hiccup is a fpafmodic or convulfive affedion of die ftomach and mwiriff,arifing from any caufe that irritates their nervous fibres. It may proceed from excefs in eating or drinking; from a hurt of the ftomach; poifons; inflammations or fchirrous tumours of th« ftomach, inteflmes, bladder, midriff, or the reft ofthe vifcera. In gangrenes, acute and malignant fevers, a hiccup is often the fore- runner qf death. When die hiccup proceeds from the ufe of aliment that is flatulent, or hard of digeftion, a draught of generous wine or a dram of any fpiritous liquor, will generally remove it. If poifon be the caufe, plenty of milk and oil muft be drank, as has been formerly recom- mended. When it proceeds from an inflammation of the flomach, Sec. it is very dangerous. In this cafe the cooling regimen ought to be ftrictly obferved. The patient muft be bled, and take fre- quently a few drops of the fweet fpirits of nitre in a cup of ivine. His ftomach fhould Iikewife be fomented Avith cloths dipped in warm Avater, or have bladders filled with warm milk and water ap- plied to it. When the hiccup proceeds from a gangrene or mortification, the Peruvian bark with other antifeptics, are the only medicines which have a chance to fucceed. When it is a primary difeafe, and pro- ceeds from a foul ftomach, loaded either with a pituitous or a bili- ous humour, a gentle vomit and purge, if the patient be able to bear them, will be of fervice. If it-arifes from flatulencies, the carminative medicines direded for the heart-burn muft be ufed. When the hiccup proves very obftinate, rec®urfe muft be had to the moft powerful aromatic andantifpafmodk medicines. The prin- ciple of thefe is mufk ; fifteen or twenty grains of which may be made into a bolus,and repeated occafionally. Opiates are Iikewife of fervice ; but they muft be ufed with caution. A bit of fugar dipped in compound fpirits of lavender, or the volatile aromatie tincture, may be taken frequently. External applications are fome- times alfo beneficial; as the ftomach plafter, or a cataplafm ofthe Venice treacle ofthe Edingburgh or London difpenfatory, applied to the region of tha» ftomach. I lately CRAMP of thi STOMACH,. 271 I lately attended a patient who had almoft a conftant hiccup for above nine weeks. It was frequently ftopped by the ufe of mufk, opium, wine, and other cordial and antifpafmodic medicines, but al- ways returned. Nothing however gave the patient fo much eafe as brifk fmall-beer. By drinking freely of this, the hiccup was often kept off for feveral days, which was more than could be done by the moft powerful medicines. The patient was at length feized with a vomiting of blood, which foon put an end to his life. Up- on opening the body, a large fchirrous tumour was found near the pylorus, or right orifice of the ftomach. The hiccup may be removed by taking vinegar; or by a few Attps ofthe oil of vitriol taken in water. CRAMP OF THE STOMACH. THIS difeafe often feizes people fuddenly, is very dangerous, and requires immediate afliftance. It is moft incident to perfons in the decline of life, efpecially the nervous, gouty, hyfteric, and hypo- chondriac. If the patient has any inclination to vomit, he ought to take fome draughts of warm water, or weak camomile tea, to cleanfe his fto- mach. After this, if he has been coftive, a laxative clyfter may be given. He ought then to take laudanum. The beft way of ad- wiiniftering it is in a clyfter. Sixty or feventy drops of liquid lau- danum may be given in a clyfter of warm water. This is much more certain than laudanum given by the mouth, which is often vomited, and in fome cafes increafe* the pain and fpafms in the ftomach. If the pains and cramps return with great violence, after the ef- feds of the anodyne clyfter are over, another, Avith an equal or •larger quantity of opium, may be given ; and every four or five hours a bolus, with ten or twelve grains of mufk,and half a drachm of the Venice treacle. In the mean time the ftomach oughfc to be fomented with cloths dipped in warm water, or bladders filled with warm milk and Avater fhould be applied to it. I have often feen thefe produce the moft happy effeds. The anodyne balfam may alfo be rubbed on the part affeded ; and an anti-hyfteric plafter worn upon it for fome time after the cramps are removed, to prevent their return. In very violent and lafting pains of the flomach, fome blood •ought to be let, unlefs the weaknefs of the patient forbids it. When the pains or cramps proceed from a foppreflion of the menfsr, bleeding is of ufe. If they be owing to the gout, recourfe muft be had to fpirits, or fome of the warm cordial waters. Bliftering plafters ought Iikewife in this cafe to be applied to the ancles. I have often feen violent cramps and pains of the ftomach removed by covering it with a large plafter of Venice.treacle. «7* Or thi NIGHT-MARE, OFTHE NIGHT-MARE. IN this difeafe die patient, in time of fleep, imagines he feels an Uncommon oppreflion or weight about his breaft or ftomach, which he can by no means fhake off. He groans, and fometimes cries out, though oftener he attempts to fpeak fo vain. Sometimes he in> ngines himfelf engaged with an enemy, and in danger of being kil- led, attempts to run away, but finds he cannot. Sometimes he fan- cies himfelf in a houfe that is on fire, or that he i3 ia danger of be- ing drowned in a river. He often thinks he is falling over a preci- pice, and the dread of being dafhed to pieces fuddenly awakes him. This diforder has been foppofed to proceed from too much blood ; from a ftagjation of blood in the brain, lungs, &c. But it is rather a nervous affedion, and arifes chiefly from indigeftion. Hence we find that perfons of weak nerves, who lead a fedentary life, and live full, are moft commonly afflided with the night mare. Nothing tends more to produce it than heavy fuppers, efpecially when eaten late, or the patient goes to bed foon after. Wind is Iikewife a very frequent caufe of this difeafe ; for which reafon thofe who are afflided with it ought to avoid all flatulent food. Deep thought, anxiety, or any thing that oppreffes the mind, ought alfo to be avoided. A s perfons afflided with the night-mare generally moan, or make fome noife in the fit, they fhould be waked, or fpoken to by fuch as hear them, as the uneaflnefs generally goes off as foon as the patient is awake. Dr. Whytt fays, he generally found a dram of brandy, taken at bed-time, prevent this difeafe. That however is a bad cuftom, and in time lofes its effeds. We would rather have the patient depend upon the ufe of food of eafy digef- tion, cheerfulnefs, exercife through the day, and a light fupper uli- en early, than to accuftom himfelf to drams. A glafs of pepper- mint water will often promote digeftion as much as a glafs of bran- dy, and is much fafer. After a perfon of weak digeftion, however, has eaten flatulent food, a dram may be neceflary. Peifons who are young and full of blood, if trouble! with the night-mare, ought to take a purge frequently, and ufe a fpare diet. OF SWOONINGS. PEOPLE of weak nerves or delicate conftitutions are liable to fwoonings or fainting-fits. Thefe indeed are- feldom dangerous When duly attended to ; but Avhen wholly negleded, or improperly treated, they often prove hurtful, and fometimes fatal. The general caufes of foonings are, fudden tranfition from l cold to heat ; breathing air that is deprived of its proper fpring or •elafticity ; great fatigue ; exceffive weaknefs lofs of blood ; long * falling ; four, gi ief, and other violent paflions or affedions of the mind. It Of SWOONINGS. 273 It is well known, that perfons who have been long expofed to eold, often faint or fall into a fwoon, upon coming into the houfe, efpecially if they drink hot liquor, or fit near a large fire. This might eafily be prevented by people taking care not to go into a warm room immediately after they have been expofed to the cold air, to approach the fire gradually, and not to eat or drink any thing hot, till the bady has been gradually brought into a warm tem- perature. When any one, in confequence of aegleding thefe precautions, faUs into a fwoon, he ought immediately to be removed to a cooler apartment, to have ligatures applied above his knees and elbows, audio have his hands and face fprinkled with vinegar or cold water. He fhould iikewife be made to fmell to vinegar, and fhould have a fpoonful or two of water, if he can fwallow, with about a third part of vinegar mixed Avkh it, poured into his mouth. If thefe ihouid not remove the complaint, it will be neceffary to bleed the patient, and afterwards to give him a clyfter, As air that is breathed frequently loofes its elafticity or fpring, it is no wonder if perfons who refpire in it often fall into a fwoon or fainting-fit. Thy are in this cafe deprived of the very principle of life. Hence it is that fainting-fits are fo frequent in ail crowded a/femblies, efpecially in hot feafons. Such fits, however, muft be confidered as a kind of temporary death ; and to the weak and delicate, they fometimes prove fatal. They ought therefore with the utmoft care to be guarded againft. The method of doing dik is obvious. Let affembly rooms, and all other places of public re- fort, be large and well ventilated; and let the weak and delicate avoid foch places, particularly in warm feafons. A perfon who faints, in foch a fituation, ought immediately to be carried into the open air; his temples Ihouid be rubbed with ftrong vinegar or brandy,and volatile fpirits of falts held to his nofe. Pie fhould be laid upon his back with his head low, and have a lit- tle Avineuor fome other cordial, as foon as he "is.able to fwallow it, poured into his mouth. If the pei ion has been fubjed to hyfteri'c fits,caftor or afafcetida fhould be applied to the nofe, 01 burnt feath- ers, horn, or leather, &c. When fainting-fits proceed from mere weaknefs or exhauftion, which is often the cafe after gie :t fatigue, long fafting, lofs of blood or the like, the patient muft be fupported with gene; oas* cordi- als, as jellies, wines, fpirituous liquors, &c. Thefe however mult be given at firft.in very fmall quantities, and ir.crculed gradually as the patient is able to bear them. PL ought to be a -lov. e J ;o lie quite ftill and eafy upon his back, with lik head low, and fhould have frefh air admitted into his chamber. lik food ih..aid confift of nourifhing broths, fago-grue* with wk.^iew milk,and other things of a light and cordial nature. Thefe things are to be given out of the fit. All that-can be done in the fit is, to let him fmell to a bot- tle of h . igary-Avater, e-.v.t d* hue, or fpirits of nartfhorn, and to rub his temples with warm brandy,or to lay v. compreis dipped in it to the pit of the ftomach. In fainting fits that proceed from fear, grief, or other violent pas- L i .fiu-s 2 74 Or FLATULENCIES, ©a WIND. fions or affedions ofthe m;nd, the patient muft be very cautioufly managed. He fhould be fuffered to remain at reft, and only made to fmell fome vinegar. After he is come ta himfelf he may diink freely of warm lemonade, or balm-tea, with fome orange or lemon- peel in it. It will Iikewife be proper, if the fainting-fits have been long and fevere, to clean the bowels by throwing in an emollient clvber. It is common in fainting fits, from whatever caufe they proceed, to bleed the patient- This pradice may be very proper in ftrong perfens of a full habit; but in thofe who are weak and delicate, or fobjee; to nervous diforders, it is dangerous. The proper method with fuch people is, to expofe them to the free air, and to ufe cordi- al and ftimulating medicines, as volatile falts, Hun^ary-witer, fpir- its of lavender, tindure ot caftor, and the like- OF FLATULENCIES, OR WIND. ALL nervems patients, Avithout exc3ption,are afflided with Avind or flatulencies in the ftomach and bowels, which arife chiefly from the want of tone or vigor in thefe organs. Crude flatulent aliment, as green peas, beans, coleworts, cabbages, and fuch like, may in- creafe the complaint; but ftrong and healthy people are feldom troubled with wind, unlefs they either overload their flomachs, or drink liquors that are in a fermenting ftate, and confequently full of elaftic air. While therefore the matter of flatulence proceeds from our aiimentsjthe caufe which makes air feparate from them in fach quantity as to occafion complaints, is almoft always a fault of the bowels tliemfelves, Avhich are too Aveak either to prevent the pro- dudion of elaftic air, or to expel it after it is produced. To relieve this complaint, fuch medicines ought to be ufed as hive a tendency to expel wind,and by ftrengthening the alimentary canal, to prevent its being produced there*. The lift cf medicines for expelling wind is very numerous; they often however difappoint the expedations of both the phyfician and his patient. The moft celebrated among the clafs of carminatives- are juniper berries; the roots of ginger and tedoary; the feeds of anife, caraway, and coriander; gum afafcetida and opium ; the Avarm waters, tindures, and fpiiits, as the aromatic water, the tindmes of woodfoot, the volatile aromatic fpirit, aether, Sec. , Dr. Whytt fays, he found no medicine more efficacious in ex- pelling wind than cctiier and laudanum. He generally gave the lau- danum in a m'xture with peppermint-water and tindure of caftor, or fweet fpirits of nitre. Sometimes, in place of this, he pave opium in pills with afafcetida. I look npon this ,-■; one of the be'.f carminative ,n -ii- cii'es ; and would rei.ommend it in alt Complii-as J the il'.niich, arilii:.; fr-jra .■iak- ukuee, i:idi_jeiliun, Sec. > Or FLATULENCIES or WIND. '75 in the ftomach or inteftines ; whereas thofe warm midicines com- monly called carminatives,do not often give immediate relief,except wdien the wdnd is in the flomach. With regaid to aether, the Dr. fays, he has often feen very good effects from it in flatulent complaints, where other medicines faued. The dofe is a tea-fpoonful mixed with two table-fpoonfuls of water*. In gouty cafes he obferves, that xther, a glafs of French brandy, or of the aromatic water, or ginger, either taken in fubftance or in- fufed in boiling water, are among the beft medicines for expelling wind. When the cafe of flatulent patients is fuch as makes it improper to give them Avarm medicines inwardly, ths Dodor recommends external application?, which are fometimes of advantage. Equal parts ot the anti-hyfteric and ftomach plafter maybe fpread upon a piece of foft leather, of fuch fize as to cover the greater part of the belly. This fhould be kept on for a confiderable time,providedthe patient be able to bear it; if it fhould give great uneafinefs it may be taken off, and the following liniment ufed in its ftead : Take of Bates's anodyne balfam an ounce ; of the exprefled oil of mace half an ounce ; oil of mint tAA*o drachms. Let thefe ingre- dients be mixed together, and about a table fpoonful Avell rubbed on the parts at bed-time. For ltrengthening the ftomach and bowels, and confequently for "k feuing the production of flatulence, the Dodor recommends the Peruvian bark, bitters, chalybeates,and exercife. In flatulent caf- es, he thinks fome nutmeg or ginger fhould be added to the tine-- ture of the bark and bitters, and that the aromatic powder fhould be joined with the filings of iron. When Avindy complaints are attended with coftivenefs, which ns often the cafe, feAv things will be found to anfwer better than four or five ofthe following pills taken every night at bed-time : Take of afafoetida tAvo drachms ; fuccotrine aloes, fait of iron, and powdered ginger, of each one drachm ; as much ofthe eli-ur proprietstis as will be fufficient to form them into pills. On the other hand, Avhen the body is too open, twelve of fifteen grains of rhubarb, with half a drachm or two fcruples of the Japo- nic confedion, given every other evening, will have very good ef- feds. . In thofe flatulent complaints which come on ae>out the time trie menfes ceafe, repeated fmall bleedings often give more relict than any other remedy. With regard to diet the Doctor obferves, that tea, and likcv/ik all flatulent aliments, are to be avoided ; and that for drink, water with a little brandy or rum, is not only preferable to n.rit liquor, but in moft cafes alfo to Avine. As Dr. Whytt has paid great attention to diis mbjod, and as his fentiments upon it in a great meafure agree with mme, I have ta- ken •Thouch the patient may be<;i» with this quantity, it will be rcceffary to _ir- creafc the dofe gradually as the ftomach can bear it. ifcther u» now L, ;m i.. co.iIkU erably greater dofes than it was in Dr. Wbytt's tims. 1T5 Of LOW SPIRITS. ken the liberty to adopt them ; and fhall only add to his obferva- tions, that exercife is m my opinion fuperior to all medicine, both for pieventing the produdion andlikewife for expelling of flatulen- cies. Thefe effects however are not to be expeded from fauntering about, or lolling in a carriage ; but from labour or fuch adive a- mufement; as g'ive exercife to every peat of the body. OF LOW SPIRITS. ALL Avho have Aveak nerves are fubjed to low fpirits in a great- er or lefs degree. Generous diet, the cold batii, exercife, and amufements are the moft likely means to remove diis complaint. It is greatly increafed by folkude and indulging gloomy ideas, but may often be relieved by cheerful company and fprighdy amufe- ments. When low fpirits are owing to a Aveak relaxed ftate of the fto- mach and bowels, an infufion of the Peruvian bark with cinnamon or nutmeg will be proper. Steel joined widi aromatics may like- wife in diis cafeibe dfed Avith advantage ; but riding and a proper diet are moft to be depended on. When they ari:e fiom foulnefs of the ftomach and inteftines, or obftrudions in the hypocondriac vifcera, aloetie purges will b« proper. 1 have fometimes known the Harrowgate fulphur-water of fervice in this cafe. When Ioav fpirits proceed from a fuppreffion of the manftnial cr ofthe lia:morrhoidal flux, thefe eAracuationsmay either be reftored, or fome other iubftituted in their plaee, as iffues, fetons or the like. Dr. Whytt obferves, that nothing has fuch fudden good effeds in this cafe as bleeding. When low fpirits have been brought on by long continued grief, anxiety, or other diftiefs of mind, agreeable company, variety of amufements, and change of place efpecially travelling into foreign countries, will afford the moft certain relief. Perfons afflided with lew fpirits fhould avoid all kinds of excefs, efpecially of venery and ftrong liquors. The moderate ufe of wane and other ftrong liquors is by no means hurtful ; but when taken to excefs they weaken the ftomach,Vitiate the humours, and deprefs the fpirits. This caution is the more neceffary, as the unfortunate and melancholy often fly to ftrong liquors for relief, by which means they never fail fo precipitate their oAvn deftrudion. OF HYSTERIC AFFECTIONS. THESE Iikewife belong to the numerous tribe of nervous dif- eafes, which may be juftly reckoned the reproach of medicine. Wo- . men of a delicate habit, Avhofe ftomach and inteftines are relaxed, and whofe nervous fyftem is extremely fenfible, are moft fubjed to hyfteric complaints. In fuch perfons an hyfteric fit, as it is called, may be brought on by an irritation of the nerves of the ftomach or inteftines, by wind, acrid humour, or the like. A fudden fuppref- Ot HYSTERIC AFFECTIONS. *77 fion of the menfes often gives rife to hyfteric fits. They may like- wife be excited by violent paflions or affedions of the mind, as fear, grief, anger, or great difappointments. Someumes the hyfteric fit rcfembles a fwoon or fainting fit, dur- ing which the patient lies as in a fleep, only the breathing is fo low as fearce to be perceived. At odier times the patient is a.Ieifed with catchings and ftrong convulfions. The fymptoms which pro- ceed hyfteric fits m*e Iikewife various m different perfons. Some- times the fits come on Avith coldnefs ofthe extremities, yawning and ftretching, lownefs of fpirits, oppreffion and anxiety. At odier times the apprcaih of the fit is foretold by a feeling,, as if there were a ball at the lower part of the belly, which gradually rifes towards the ftomach, wdiere it occafions inflation, ficknefs, and fometimes vomiting ; afterwards it rifes into the gudet, and occa- fions a degree of fuffocation, to which quick breathing, palpitation of the heart, giddinefs of the head, dimnefs of the fight, lofs of hearing, Avidi convulfive motions ofthe extremities and other parts ofthe body, fucceed. The hyfteric paroxyfm is often introduced by an immoderate fit of laughter, aad fometimes it gees off by crying. Indeed there is not much difference between the laughing and crying of an highly hyfteric lady. Our aim is die treatment of this difeafe, muft be to fhorten the fit or paroxyfm when prefent, and to prevent its return. The long- er the fits continue, and the more. frequently they return, the difeafe becomes the more obftinate. Their ftrength is increafed by habit, and they induce fo great a relaxation of the fyftem, that it is with difficulty removed. It is cuftomary during the hyfteric fit or paroxyfm, to beed the patient. In ftrong perfons of a plethoric habit, and where the pulfe is full, this may be proper ; but in weak and delicate conftitu- tion?, or Avkere the difeafe, has been of long Handing, or arifes from inanition, it is not fafe. The beft courfe in fuch difeafes is to roufe the patieut by ftrong fmclls, as burnt feathers, afafcetida, or fpirits of hartihorn, held to the nofe. Hot bricks may abb be ap- plied to the foles of the feet, and the legs, arms and belly may be ftrongly rubbed with a warm cloth. But the beft application is to put the feet and legs into warm water. This is peculiarly proper when the fits precede the flow of the menfes. In cafe of coftivenefs, a laxative clyfter with afafcetida will be proper ; and as foon as the patient can fwallow, two table fpoonfuls of a folution of afafoetida, or of fome cordial julep, may be given*. The radical cure of this diforder will be beft attempted at a time when the patient is moft free from the fits. It will be greatly pro- moted •Whan hyfteric fits are occafioned by fympathy, they may be cured by exciting an oppofite paflion. This is faid to have been the cale of a whole fchool of yo'.mj ladies in Holland, who were all cured by being told, that the firft who was leized fhould be burnt to death But this method of cure, to mv knowledge, will not always fucceed. I would therefore advife, that young ladies wl.o are fubje&to hyfteric fits fhould not be lent to boarding fchools as the difeafe may be caught by imitation. I have known madnefs itfelf brought on by fympathy. 27* Or HYSTERIC AFFECTIONS. moted by a proper attention to diet. A milk and vegetable diet, when duly rcriilled in, will often perform a cure. If hoAvevcr the patient has been accuftomed to a more generous diet, it will not be fafe to leave it off all at once, but by degrees. The moft proper drink ib water with a fmall quantity of fpirits. A cool dry air is the bell. Cold bathing, and every thing that braces the nerves and invigorates the fyftem, is benerkial; but lying too long in bed, or whatever relaxes the body, is hurtful. It is of the greateft impor- tance to have the mind kept conftantly eafy and cheerful, and, if poflibk, to have it always engaged in fome agreeable and intereft- ing purfuit. The proper medicines are thofe which ftrengthea the alimentary canal and the whole nervous fyftem, as the preparations ofiron,the Peruvian bark and other bitters. Twenty drops of the elixir of vitriol, in a cup of the infufion ofthe bark, may be taken twice or thrice a-day. The bark and iron may Iikewife betaken in fubftance, provided the flomach can bear them ; but they are generally given in too imal! doles to have any effed. The chalybeate waters gen- erally prove beneficial in this diforder. If the ftomach is loaded with phlegm, vomits will be of ufe ; but they fliould not be too ftrong, nor frequently repeated, as they tend to relax and weaken the ftomach. If there be a tendency to cof- tivenefs, k muft be removed either by diet, or by taking an opening pill as oken as it fhall be found neceffary. To leffen the irritability of the fyftem, antifpafmodic medicines- will be of ufe. The beft antifpafmedic medicine; are mufk,opium and eafier. When opium difagrees with theftomach,it may either be applied externally, or given in clyfters. It is often fuccefsful in removing thofe periodical head achstowhich hyfteric and hypo- chondriac patients are fubjed. Caftor has in fome cafes been found to procure fleep where opium failed ; for which reafon Dr. Whytt advifes, that they fhould be joined together. He Iikewife recom- mends the airti-hyfteric plafter to be applied to the abdomen*. Hyfteric women are often afflided Avith cramps, in various parts of the body, which are mofl apt to feize them in bed, or when a- fleep. The moft efficacious medicines in this cafe are opium, biif- tering-plafters, and warm bathing or fomentations. When the cramp or fpafm is very violent, opium is the remedy moft to be de- pended on. In milder cafes, immerfing the feet and legs in warm water, or applying a bliftering-plafter to the part affe£led,wiil often be fufficient to remove the complaint. In patients whofe nerves are uncommonly delicate and fenfible, it will be better to omit the bliftering-plafter, and to attempt the cure by opiates, mufk, cam- phire, and the warm bath. Cramps are often prevented or cured by compreflion. Thus cramps in the legs are prevented, and fometimes removed, by tight bandages ; and when convulfions arife from a flatulent diftention of the ♦Though actifpafmodics and anodynes are univcrfally recommended in this dii- eafe, yet all the extraordinary cures that lever knew in hyfteric caie», were per- formed by means of tonic and corroborating median1:*. Or HYPOCHONDRIAC AFFECTIONS. -?7f the inteftines, or from fpafms beginning in them, they maybe often kffened or cured by making a prety ftrong compreflion upon the abdomen by means of a broad belt. A roll of brimftone held in the hand is frequently ufed as a remedy for cramps. Though this feems to owe its effeds chiefly to imagination, yet, as it foaaetimes fuc- ceeds it merits a trial*. When ipafiaas or convulfive motions arife from fharp humours in the flomach or inteftines, no lafting relief can be procured till thefe are either correded or.expelled. The Peruvian bark has fometimes cured periodic convulfions after oth- er medicines had failed. OF HYPOCHONDRIAC AFFECTIONS. THIS difeafe generally attacks the indolent, the luxurious, the Unfortunate, and the ftudious. It becomes daily more common in this country, owing, no doubt, to the increafe of luxury and feden- tary employments. It has fo near a refemblance to the immedi- ately preceding, that many authors confider them as the fame dif- eafe, and treat them accordingly. They require however, a very different regimen ; and the fymptoms of the latter, though lefs vi- olent, are more permanent than thofe of the former. Men of a melancholy temperament, Avhofe minds are capable of great attention, and whofe paflions are not eafily moved, are in the advanced periods of life, moft liable to this difeafe. It is ufually brought on by long and ferious attention to obftrufe fubjeds, grief, the fuppreffion of cuftomary evacuations, excefs of venery, the repulfion of cutaneous eruptions, long-continued evacuations, ob- flrudions in fome of the vifcera, as the liver, fpleen, See. Hypochondriac perfons ought never to faft long, and their food fhould be folid and nourifhing. All acefceat and windy vegetables are to be avoided. Flefh meats agree beft with them, and their drink*, fnould be old claret, or good madeira. Should thefe difa- gree with the flomach, water with a little brandy or rum in it may be drank. Cheerfulnefs and ferenity of mind are by all means to be culti- vated. Exercife of every kind is ufeful. The cold bath is like- wife beneiicial ; and where it does not agree with the patient, fric- tions with the flefh-brufh or a coarfe cloth may be tried. If the pa- tient has it in his power, he ought to travel either by fea cr land. A voyage or a long journey, efpecially towards a warmer climate, will be of more fervice than any medicine. The general intentions of cure in this difeafe, are to ftrengthen the alimentary canal, and to promote the fecretions. Thefe inten- tions will be bell anfwered by the different preparations of iron and the Peruvian bark, Avhich, after proper evacuations, may be taken in the fame manner as direded m the preceding difeafe. Ii *Somr perfons afTicfcd with cramps pretend to reap great bene'ii from fmall btev.c.lo of rolcm.Ty tied ;dl nijjht about iheir feet, ancles, and kni*; »8e Of HYPOCHONDRIAC AFFECTIONS. If the patient be coftive, it will be neceffary to make ufe of fome gentle opening medicine, as pills compofed of equal parts of aloes, rhubarb, and asafcetida, Avith as much of the elixir proprietatis as is neceffary to form the ingredients into pills. Two, three, or four of thefe may be taken as often as it fhall be found needful, to keep the body gently open. Such as cannot beer the afafcetida, may flibftitule Spanifh foap in its place. Though a cheerful glafs may have good effeds in this difeafe, yet all manner of excefs is hurtful. Intenfe ftudy, and every thing that depreffes the fpirits, are Iikewife pernicious. Though the general fymptoms and treatment of nervous difor- ders Avere pointed out in the beginning of this chapter ; yet, for the 'benefit of the unhappy perfons afflided with thofe obftinate and complicated maladies, I have treated feveral of their capital fymp- toms under diftind orfeparate heads. Thefe hoAvcvcr are not to be confidered as different difeafes, but as various modifications of the fame difeafe. They all arife from the fame general caufes, and require nearly the fame method of treatment. There are many other fymptoms that merit particular attention, which the nature of my plan will not permit me to treat of at full length. 1 fhall therefore omit them altogether,and conclude this chapter vrith a few general remarks on the moft obvious means of preventing or avoid- ing nervous diforders. In all perfons afflided with nervous diforders, there k a great de- licacy and fenfibility of the whole nervous fyftem, and an uncom- mon degree of Aveaknefs of the organs of digeftion. Thefe may be either natural or acquired. When OAving to a defed in the conftitu- tion, they are hardly to be removed: but may be mitigated by prop- er care. When induced by difeafes, as long or repeated fevers, pro- fufe haemorrhages, or the like, they prove alfo very obftinate, and • will yield only to a courfe of regimen calculated to reftore and in- vigorate the habit. But nervous affedions arife more frequently from caufes, Avhich it is in a great meafure in our own power to avoid, than from difea- fes, or an original fault in the conftitution, &c. Exceffive grief, intenfe ftudy, improper diet, and negkd of exercife, are the great fources of this, extenfive clafs of difeafes. It has been already obferved, that grief indulged deftroys the appetite and digeftion, depreffes the fpirits, and induces an univer- fal relaxation and debility of the whole fyftem. Inftanccs of this are daily to be feen. The lofs of a near relation, or any other mis- fortune in life, is often fufficient to' occafion the moft complicated feries of nervous fymptom;;. Such misfortunes indeed are not to be avoided, but furely their effeds, by a vigorous and proper exertion of the mind, might be rendered lefs hurtful. For diredions in this matter, we muft refer the reader to the atticle Gmef, in the chap- ter on the paffion s. The effeds of intenfe ftudy are pretty fimilar to thofe occafioned by grief. It preys upon the animal fpirits, and deftroys the appe- tite and digeftion. To prevent thefe effeds ftudious perfons ought according DISORDERS ofthh SENSES. 281 According to the poet, to toy with their boohs*. They fliould never ftudy too long at a time ; nor attend long to one pai ticular fubjed, efpecially if it be of a ferious nature. They ought ltkewiie to be attentive to their pofture, and fliould take care frequently to un- bend their minds by muiic,diverfions, or going into agreeable com- pany. Widi regard to diet, I fhall only obferve, diat nervous difeafes may be induced either by excefs ox inanition. Both of thefe ex* tremes hurt digeftion, and vitiate the humours. When nature ia oppreffed with freih loads of food, before ihe has had time to di- geft and affimilate the former meal, her poAvers are weakened, and the veffels are filled with crude humours. On the other hand, when the food is not fufficiently nourifhing, or is taker* too feldom, the bowels are inflated with Avind, and the humours, for want of regu- lar frelh fupplies of wholefome chyle, are vitiated-. Thefe extremes are therefore with equal care to be avoided. They both tend to in- duce a relaxation, and debility of the nervous fyftem, with all its dreadful train of confequences; But the moft general caufe of nervous diforders,is indolence. The adive and laborious are feldom troubled with them. They are re- ferved for the children of eafe and arfluence,who generally feel their keenell force. All we fhall fay to fuch perfons, is, that the means of prevention and cure are both in their oavu power. If the con- ftitution of human nature be foch, that man muft either labour or fuffer difeafes, furely no individual has any right to exped an ex- emption from the general rule. Thofe however who are willing to take exercife, but whofe occu- pations confine them to the houfe, and perhaps to an unfavourable pofture, really deferve our pity. We have in a former part of the book, endeavoured to lay down rules for their condud ; and fhall only add, that where thefe cannot be complied Wu'tii, their place may, in fome meafure, be lupplied by the ufe of brackg, and ftrengthening medicines, as the Peruvian bark, with other bitters; the preparations of fteel ; the elixir of vitriol, Sec. CHAP. JCLIV. DISORDERS OF THE SENSES. E do not mean to treat of the nature of our ftnfations,er to give a minute defcription r>f the various organs by which they aie performed ; but to point out fome ofthe difeafes to which thefe organs are moil liable, and to fheAV how they may be pre- vented or remedied. OF * Armltrong on fva.th. hi ru w Z02 #fthsEYE\ CF THE EYE. NO organ ofthe body is fubjed to more difeafe* than the eye J nor is there any one of which the difeafes are more difficult to cure. Though more ignorant perfons pretend to cure thefe than any o« ther clafs of difeafes ; yet a very fuperficial acquaintance with the ftrudure of the eye, and the nature of vifion, will be fufficient to convince any one ofthe danger of trufling to them. Thefe difeafes often exceed the {kill of the moft learned phyfician ; hence we may eafily infer the danger of trufling them to ignorant qtfacks, who, without all paradventure, put out more eyes than they cure. But, though the difeafes of the eye can feldom be cared, they might often by due care, be prevented ; and, even Avhere the fight is to- tally loft, many things might be dbne,whi«h are generally negleded to render the unhappy perfon both more ufeful to himfelf and to fociety*. The eyes are hurt by viewing bright or luminous objeds ; keep- ing the head too long in ahanging pofture ; violent head-achs ; ex- ceffive venery ; the long ufe of bitters; the effluvia from acrid or volatile fubftances ; various difeafes ; as the fmall-pox, meaflels, &c. but above all, from night-watching, and candle-light ftudies. Long fafting is Iikewife hurtful to the eyes, and frequent heats and colds are n» lefs pernicious. The eyes are often hurt by the flop- page of cuftomary evacuations ; as mornmg fweats.; fweating of the feet ; the menfes in women ; and the bleeding-piles in men. All kinds of excefs are Iikewife hurtful to the fight, particularly the im- moderate ufe of ardent fpirits, and other ftrong liquors. In all difeafes of the eyes, efpecially thofe attended with inflam- mation, the cool regimen ought to be obferved. The patient muft abftain from all fpiritous liquors. The fmoke of tobacco, fmoky rooms, the vapours of onions and garlic, and all vivid lights and glaring colours, are carefully to be avoided. The drink may be water, whey, or fmall beer; and the aliment muft be light and of eafy digeftion, For preventing diforders of the eyes, iffues and fetons are of prime ufe. Every perfon whofe eyes are tender ought to have one or more of thefe in fome part of the body. It will Iikewife be of ufe to keep the body gently open, and either to bleed or purge every t fpring and fall. All excefs and night fludies are to be avoided. A gutta jtrena or amaurofts is an abolition of the fight without any apparent caufe or fault in the eyes. When it is owing to a decay or waihing of die optic nerve, it does not admit of a cure; but when it proceeds from a compreffion ofthe nerves by redundant hu- mours, thefe may in fome meafure be drained off, and the patient relieved. For this purpofe, the body muft be kept open with the laxative mercurial pills. If the patient be young and of a fanguine habit he may be bled. Cupping, with fcarifications on the back part of the head, will Iikewife be of ufe. A running at the nofe may be promoted by volatile falts, ftimulating powders, &c. But the moft likely means for relieving the patient are iffues or blifters kept open for a long time on the back part of the head, behind the ears, or on the neck. I have known tiiefie reftore fight, even after it had been for a confiderable time loft. Should thefe fail, recourfe muft be had to mercurial falivation; or what will perhaps anfwer the purpofe better, twelve giains of corroiive fublimate of mercury may be diffolved in an Englifh pint and an half of brandy, and a table-fpoonful of it taken twice a-day, drinking half a pint of the decodion of farfaparilla after it. A tataratf is an obftrudion of the oupil, by the intei pofition of fome opaque fubftance which either diminifhes or totally extinguilii- es the fight. It is generally an opacity of chryftaline humour. In a recent or beginning catarad, the fame medicines are to be ufed as in the gutta ferena; and they will fometimes fucceed. But Avhea this does not happen, and the catarad becomes firm, it muft be couched, or rather extraded. I have refolved a recent catarad by giving the patient frequent purges with calomel, keeping a poultice of frefh hemlock conftantly upon the eye, and a perpetual blifter on the neck*. The myopia or fhort-Jightednefs, and the prefbyopia or feeing only e.l too great a diftance, are diforders which depend on the original ftruc- ture or figure of the eye, therefore admit of no cure. The incon- veniencies arifing from them may however be, in fome meafure, re- medied by the help of proper glaffes. The former requires the aid of a concave, and the latter of a convex glafs. Ajlrahifmus, or fquinting, depends upon an irregular eorrtradion of the mufeles of the eye from a fpafm, palfy, epilepfy, or an ill habit. Children often contrad this diforder by having their eyes unequally expofed to the light. They may Iikewife acquire it by imitation from a fquinting nurfe or play-fellow, &c. As this difor-. der can hardly be cured, parents ought to be careful to pre rent it. Almoft the only thing which can be done for it is to contrive a mafkfor the child to wear, Avkich will only permit him to fee ia a ftraight diredion. Spots or J"pecks on the eyes are generally the effed of inflammation, and often appear after the fmall-pox, the meafles, or violent op- thalraias. They are very difficult to cure, and often uecafion total b'indnek. * In bpth thefe cafes ek&ricity merits atrial. :*4 Or THE EAR. bhndnefs. If the fpccks are foft and thin, they may fometimes b» taken off by gentle cauftics and difcuticnts; as\itriol, the" juice of celandine, &c. When thefe do not fucceed, a furgical operation may be tried: the fuccefs of this however is always very doubtful. The blood-fhot eye may be occafioned by a ftroke, a fall, retching, vomiting, violent coughing, &c. I have frequently known it hap. pen to children in the hooping cough. It appears at firft like a bit of fcarlet, and is afterwards of a livid or blackifh colour. This dis- order generally goes off without medicine. Should it prove ob- ftinate, the patient may be bled, and have his eyes fomented with a decodion of comphry roots and elder flowers. A foft poultice may be applied to the eyes ; and the body fhould be kept open by gen- tle purgatives. l he watery or weeping eye is generally occafioned by a relaxation or weaknefs ofthe glandular parts of that organ. Thefe may be braced and ftrengthened by bathing the eye vrith brandy and Ava- ter, Hungary-water, rofe-water, with white vitriol diffolved in it, Sec. Medicines which make a revulfion are Iikewife proper ; as mild purgatives, perpetual blifters on the neck, bathing the feet frequently with hike-warm wateT, &c. When this difeafe proceeds from an obftrudion of the lachrymal dud, or natural paflkge of the tears, it is called afflula lachrymal')^ and can only be cured by a furgical operation*. OF THE EAR. THE fundions ofthe ear may be injured by wounds, ulcers, or any thing that hurts its fabric. The hearing may Iikewife be hurt by exeefllve noife ; violent colds in the head ; fevers ; hard wax, or other fubftances flicking in the cavity of the ear ; too great a degree of moifture or drynefs of the ear. Deafnefs is very often the effed of old age, and is incident to moft people in the declins of life. Sometimes it is owing to an original fault in the ftrudure or formation of the ear itfelf. . When this is the cafe, it admits of no cure ; and the unhappy perfon not only continues deaf, but gener- ally Iikewife dumb, for lifef. When * A weeping or watery eye is often the mark of a fchrophtdous habit. +Though thofe who have the misfortune to be born deaf are gtnerally iuffered to continue dumb, and confequently are in a great meafure loft to fociety, yet nothing is more certain than that fuch perfons may be taught not only to read and ■write, but alto to fpeak and to underftand what others fay to them. Teaching the dumb to fpeak will appear paradoxical to thofe who do not confider that the for- mation of founds is merely mechanical, and may be taught without the afliftance of the ear. This is not only capable of demonftration, but it is actually reduced to practice hy the ingenious Mr. Thomas Braid wood of Edingburgh. This gentle- man has, by the mere force of genius and application, brought the teaching of dumb perfons to fuch a dtgree of perfection, that his fcholars are generally more forward in their education than thole of the fame age who enjoy all their faculties.
