I have often known the inflam- matory fymptoms renewed and heightened, the running increafed, and the cure rendered extremely difficult and tedious, by one fit of exeefllve drinking. When the above treatment has removed the heat of urine, and the forenefs of the gential parts; when the quantity of running is considerably kffened, without any pain or fwelling in the groin or teflicle fupervening; when the patient is free from involuntary erec- tions; and laftely, when the running becomes pale, whitiih, thick, void of ill fmell, and tenaceous or ropy; when all or moft of thefe fymptoms appear, the gononhoea is arrived at its laft ftage, and we may gradually proceed to treat it as a gleet with aftringent and agglutinating medicines. ' OF GLEETS. A Gonorrhoea frequently repeated, or improperly treated, often ends in a gleet, which may either proceed from a relaxation, or from fome remains of the difeafe. It is hoAvever ofthe greateft importance in the cure of the gleet, to know from which of thefe caufes it proceeds. When the difcharge proves very obftinate, and receives little or no check from aftringent remedies, there is ground to fufped that it is owing to the latter; but if the drain is incon- flant, and is chiefly obfervable when the patient is ftimulated by la- fcivious ideas, or upon ftraining to go to ftool, we may reafonably conclude that it is chiefly owing to the former. Inthe cure of a gleet proceeding from relaxation, the principal defign is to brace, and reftore a proper degree of tenfion to the de- bilitated and relaxed veffels. For this purpofe, befides the medi- cines recommended in the gonorrhoea,the patient may have recourfe to ftronger and more poAverful aftringents as the Peruvian bark*, alum, vitriol, galls tor men til, biftort, balduftines, tindure of gum kino, &c. The injedions may be rendered more aftringent by the addition of a few grains of alum, or increafing the quantity of vi- triol as far as the parts are able to bear it. The laft remedy vVhich we fhall mention in this cafe is the cold bath, than which there is not perhaps a more powerful bracer in the wheh compafs of medicine. It ought never to be omitted in this fpecies of gleet, unlefs there be fomething in the conftitution of the patient which renders the ufe of it u if afe. ■ The chief ob- jedions to the ufe of cold bath are a full habit and an unfound ftate of the vifora. The danger from the former may ahvays be kffened * The Peruvian bark may be combined with other aft-ringents, and prepared in the following m inner : Tske of Peruvian bark bruifed fix drachms, of frefh galls bruifed two drachms ; boil them in a pound and an half of water to a pound ; to the ftrained liquor add three ounces ofthe fimple tintture of the bark. A fmall tea-cupful of this m^y be taken three times a-day, adding to each cup fifteen or twenty drops of the acid elix- ir of vitriol. 3©8 Of GLEETS. leffened, if not removed, by purging and bleeding; but the latter is an inform mm table obllick, as the preffure ofthe water, and the fudden contradion of the external veifels, by throwing the blood with too much force upon the internal parts, are apt to occafion rup- tures ofthe veifels, or a flux of humours upon the difeafed organs. But where no objedion of this kind prevails, the patient ought to plunge over head in water every morning fafting, for three or four weeks together. He fhould not however ilay long in the water, and fhould take care to have his fkin dried as foon as he comes out. The regimen proper in this cafe is the fame as was mentioned in the laft ftage'of the gonorrhoea : the diet muft be drying and aftrin- gent, and the drink, Spa, Pyrmont, or Briftol waters, with which a litde claret or red wine may fometimes be mixed. Any perfon may now afford to drink thefe waters, as they can be every where prepared at almoft no expence, by a mixture of common chalk and oil of vitriol. When the gleet does not in the fmalleft degree yield to thefe me- dicines, there is reafon to fufped that it proceeds from ulcers. In this cafe recourfe muft be had to mercury with which the juices may be affeded, as the decodion of China, farfaparilla, faffafras, or the like. Mr. Fordyce fays, hehas feen many obftinate gleets, of two thrct or four years Handing, effcdually cured by a mercurial inundion* when almoft every other medicine has been tried in vain. Dr. Chapman feems to be ofthe fame opinion; but fays, he has always found the mercury fucceed beft in this cafe when joined with tere- binthinate and other agglutinating medicines. For which reafon the Dodor recommends pills made of calomel and Venice terpentine*; and defires that their ufe may be accompanied with a decodion of guaiacum or farfaparilla. The laft kind of remedy which we fhall mention for the cure of ulcers in the urinary gafiage, are the fuppurating candles or bou- gies : as thefe are prepared various ways, and are generally to. be bought ready made, it is needlefs to fpend time in numerating the different engredients of which they are compofed, or teaching the manner of preparing diem. Before bougie be introduced into the urethra, however, it fhould be fmeared all over with fweat oil, to prevent it from ftimulating too fuddenly; it may be fuffered to con- tinue in from one to feven or eight hours, according as the patient can bear it. Obftinate ulcers are not only often healed, but tumours and excrefences in the urinary paffages taken aAvay, and an obftruc- tion of urine removed by means of bougies. Obftinate gleets may be removed by the ufe of of bougies. 1 6 OF • Take Venice turpentine, boiled fo a fufficient degree of hardnefs, half an ounce, Calomel half a drachm. Let tWfe be mixed and formed into fixty pills, of which five or fix may he taken night and momisg. If, durhig the ufe of thefe pill3, the mouth fhould grow fore, or the breath become offenfiv*, they muft be difcontinu- td tmtill thefe fymptoms dilapTrfarg* Of the SWELLED TESTICLES. $09 OF THE SWELLED TESTICLE. THE fwelled tefticle may either proceed from fofodion lately contraded, or from the venereal poifon lui king in the body: the latter indeed is not very common,butthe former frequently happens both in the firft and fecond ftages of a gonorrhoea ; particularly when the running is unfeafonably checked, by ccld, hard drinking, ftrong draftic purges, vioknLexercife, the too early ufe cf aftruv gent medicines, or the like. In die inflammatory ftage, bleedingis neceffary, which muft be repeated according to the urgency of tffeTymptoms*. The food muft be light and the drink diluting. High-feafoned food, flefh, wines, and every thing of a heatingnature,areto beavoided. Fomentations are of fingular fervice. Poultices ef bread and mi).k, fofrened with frefh butter or oil, aie Iikewife veryprcper,and ought conftantly to be applied when the patient is in bed, when he is up the teftkks fliould be kept warm, and fupported by a bag or trufs, which may eafily be contrived in fuch a manner as to prevent the weight ofthe tefticle from haA'ing any effed. If k ihouid be found impi adkable to clear the tefticle by the cooling regimennow pointedou£,andextendedaccording to circum- ftances, it will be neceffary to lead the patient through foch a com- plete antiveneral courfe as lhallenfure him againft any future unea- finefs. For this purpofe, befides rubbing the mercurial ointment on the part; if free from pain, or on the thighs, as direded in the gonorrhoea, the patient muft be confined to bed, if neceffary, for five ox fix weeks, fufpending the tefticle all the while with a bag or trufs, and plying him inwardly Avith ftrong decodions of farfapa- rilla. When thefe means do not fucceed, and there is reafon to fufped a fcrophukis or cancerous habit, either ofwhichmay fupport afoir- ihous induration, after the venereal poifon is correded, the part« fhould be fomented daily with-a decodion of hemlock, the bruifed leaves of which may Iikewife be added to the poultice, and the ex- trad at the fame time taken inwardlyf. This pradice is ilrongly recommended by Dr. Stork in fcirrhous and cancerous cafes : and Mr. Fordyce affures us that by this method he has cured difeafed teftitles of two or three years ftanding, even when ulcerated, and when the fcirrhus had begun to be affeded with pricking and lan- cing pains. OF BUBOES. VENEREAL Buboes are hard tumours feated inthe groins, oc- cafioned by the venereal pcifon lodged in this part. They are of two kinds * I hsve been accuftomed for fome time pail to apply leeches to inflamed tefticlos, which praAice has always bee» followed by the moft happy eficfts. + The extraft of hemlock may be made into puis, znci tak:a in the manner djjfifi- Ud under the article Cancer, 3»© <§f CHANCRES. kinds ; viz. foch as proceedfrom a recent infedion, and fuch as ac- company a confirmed lues. The care of recent buboes, that is, fuch as appear foon after im- pure coition, may be firft attempted by difperfion, and, if that fliould not fucceed, by fuppuration. To promote the difperfion of a bubo, the fame regimen muft be obferved as Avas directed in the firfl ftage ofthe gonorrhoea. The patient muft Iikewife be bled,and take fome cooling purges, as the decodion of tamai inds and fenna, Glauber's falts, and the like. If, by this courfe, the fwelling and other inflammatory fymptoms abate, we may fafely proceed to die ufe of mercury, which muft be continued till the venereal virus is fubdued** But if ihe bubo fhould, from the beginning, be attended with great heat, pain, and pulfation,it will be proper to promote its fup- puration. For this purpofe the patient may be allowed to ufe his ordinary diet, and to take noAv and then a glafs of wine. Emolli- ent cataplafms, confifling of bread and milk foftened with oil or frefh butter, may be applied to the part; and in cold conftitutions, where the tumour advances flowly, white lilly-roots boilded,or flic- ed onions raw, and a fufficient quantity of yellow bafilicon, may be added to the poultice. When the tumour is ripe, which may be known by its conical fig- ure, the foftnefs of die fkin,and a fluduation of matter plainly to be felt under the finger, it may be opened either by a cauftic or alan- cet, and afterwards dreffed with a digeftive ointment. It fometimes however happens, that buboes can neither be dif- perfed nor brought to a fuppuration, but remain hard, indolenttu- mours. In this cafe the indurated glands muft be confumed by cauftic ; if they fliould become fcirrhous, they muft be diffolved by the application of hemlock, both externally and internally, as di- reded in the fcirrhous tefticles. OF CHANCRES. CPIANCRES aie fuperficial, callous, eating ulcers ; which may happen either with or without a gonorrhoea. They are commonly feated about the glands, and make their appearancein the following manner : Firft a little red pimple arifes.which foon becomes point- ed at top, and is filled with a whitifh matter inclining to yellow. This pimple is hot, and itches generally before it breaks : after- wards it degenerates into an obftinate ulcer, the bottom of which, is ufually covered with a vicid mucus, and whofe edges gradually become hard and callous. Sometimes the firft appearar.eerefembles a fimple excoriation of the cuticle ; which, hoAvever, if the caufe be venereal, foon becomes a true chancre. A chancre is fometimes a primary affedion,but it is much oftener fymptomatic,and is the mark of a confirmed lues. Primary chancres difcover * For the difperfion of a bubo, a number of leeches applied to the part affected v. ill be found equally efficacious ts in th*inflamed tefticle. Of CHANCRES. pt difcover themfelves foon after impure coition, and are generally feated in parts covered with a thin cuticle, as the lips, the nipples of women, the glans penis of men, &c*. When a chancre appears foon after impure coition, its treat- ment is nearly fimilar to that of the virulent gonorrhoea. The pa- tient muft obferve the cooling regimen, lofe a little blood and take fome gende dofes of falts and manna. The parts affeded ought frequently to be bathed, or rather foaked,in warm milk and water, and, if the inflammation be great, an emollient poultice or cataplafm may be applied to them. This courfe will, in moft cafes de fuffici- ent to abate the inflammation, and prepare the patieat for the ufe of mercury. Symptomatic chancres are commonly accompanied with ulcers in the diroat, nodurnal pains, fcurvy eruptions about the roots ofthe hair, and other fymptoms of a confirmed lues. Though they may be kated in any of the parts mentioned above, they commonly ap- pear upon the private parts, or the infide of the thigh. They are alfo lefs painful, but frequently much larger and harder than pri- mary chancres. As their cure muft depend upon that of the pox, of which they are only a fymptom, we fhall take no further notice of them, till Ave come to treat of a confirmed luesf. Thus we have related moft of the fymptoms which accompany or fucceed a violent gonorrhoea, and have alfo given a fhort view of their proper treatment; there are, hoAvever, feveral others which fometimes attend this difeafe, as -a. ftrangury, or obftrudion of urine, a phymofis, paraphymofis, Sec. A ftrangury may either be occafioned by a fpafmodic conftric- tion, or an inflammation of the urethra and parts about the neck of the bladder. In the former cafe, the patient begins to void his urine with tolkrable eafe ; but, as foon as it touches the galled or inflamed urethra, a fudden conftridion takes place, and the urine ii voided by fpurts, fometimes by drops only. When the ftrangury is OAving to an inflammation about the neck of the bladder, there is a conftant heat and uneafinefs ofthe part,a perpetual defire to make water, while the patient can only render a few drops, and a trou- blefome tenefnus, or conftant inclination to go to ftool. When die ftrangury is OAving to fpafms, fuch medicines as tend to dilute and blunt the falts of the urine will be proper. For this purpefe, befides the common diluting liquors,fcft and cooling emul- ions, fweetened Avith the fyrup of poppies, may be ufed. Should thefe * When venered ulcers are feated in the lips, the infection may be comunieated by killing. I have feen very obftinate venereal ulcers in the lips, which I have all the reafon in the world to believe were communicated in this manner. Nuries ought to beware of lucklfng infefted children, or having their brer.0* drawn by pcrinrss tainted with the venereal difeafe. This crution is very necriia- ry for nuries who rcfide in the neighbourhood of great towns. + I have found it anf-.\tr extremefy well to fprinkle chancres twice a day with calomel. This will often perform a cure without any other implication whateve*. Jf,the chancres arc upon the clans, they may be wafhed with milk and water, * Mule warm, and afterwards the calomel may be applied ks ?i>ove. 3<* Or CHANCRES. thefe not have the defired effed, bleeding and emollient fomentati- ons, will be neceffary. When the complaint is evidently OAA-ingtoan inflammation about the neck of the bladder, bleeding muft be more liberally performed, and repeated according to the urgency of the fymptoms. After • bleeding, if the ftrangury ftill continues, foft clyfters, with a prop- ter quantiy of laudanum in them, may be adminiftered, and emol- lient fomentations applied to the region of the bladder. At the fiime time, the patient may take every four hours a tea-cupful of barlev Avater, to an Englifh pint of Avhich fix ounces of the fymp of marft.l mallows, four ounces ofthe oil of fweet almonds, and half an ounce of nitre, may be added. If thefe remedies fhould not relieve the complaint, and a total fuppreffion of urine (hould come on, bleed- ing muit be repeated, and the patient fet in a Avarm bath up to the middle. It will be proper in this cafe to dil'condnue the diuretics, and to daw off the water with a catheter ; but as the patient is fel- dom able to bear its being introduced, we would rather recom- mend the ufe of mild bougies. Thefe often lubricate the paffage, and greatly facilitate the difcharge of urine. Whenever they be "in to ftimulate or give any uneafinefi, they may be withdrawn. The phymofis is fuch aconftridion ofthe prepuce over the gian?, as hinders it from being drawn backwards ; the paraphymnfs, on the contrary, is foch a conftridion of the prepuce behind die glans, as hinders it from being brought forward. The treatment of thefe fymptoms is fo nearly the fame Avith that of the virulent gonorrhoea, that we have no occafion to enlarge upon it. In general, bleeding, purging, poultices, and emollient fomentations are fufficient. Should thefe however fail of romoviug the ftridure, and the parts threatened Avith a mortification, twen- ty or thirty grains of ipecacuanha, and one grain of emetic tartar,' may be given for a vomit, and may be worked off with warm wa- ter or thin gruel. It fometimes happens, that, in fpite of all endeavours to the con- trary, the inflammation goes on, and the fomptoms of a beginni'iip* mortification appear. When this is the cafe, the prepuce muft be fcarified Avith a lancet, and, if neceffary, divided, in order to pre- vent a ftrangulation, and fet the imprifoned glans at libc «- y. We fhall not deferibe the manner of performing th?s operation, as it ought always to be done by a furgeon. When a mortification ha* adually taken place, it will be neceffary befidcs performing the a- bove-operations, to foment the parts fiequently with cloths wrung out of a ftrong decodion of camomile flowers and bark, and to ■give the patient a drachm of the bark in powder every tAvoor three hours. With regard to the priapifm, chordee, and other diftortions of thfj penis, their treatment is no way different from that of the gonor- rhoea. When they prove very troublefome,the patient may take a few drops of laudanum at night, efpecially after die operation of a purgative, dirough the day. Of a CONFIRMED LUES. VS OF A CONFIRMED LUES. WE have hitherto treated of thofe affedions in which the vene- real poifon is fuppofed to be confined chiefly to the particular part by which it was received, and fhall next take a view of the lues in its confirmed ftate ; that is, when the poifon is adually received into the blood, and circulating with it through every part of the body, mixes with the feveral fecretions, and renders the whole habit tainted. The fymptoms of a confirmed lues are, buboes in the groin, pain of die head and joints, wdiich are peculiarly troublefome in the night, or when the patient is Avarm in bed ; fcab«- and fcurfs in va- rious parts ofthe body, efpecially on the head, of a yellowifh co- lour, refembling a honey-comb ; corroding ulcers in various parts of the body, which generally begin about the thi oat, from whence they creep gradually, by the palate, towards the cartilage ofthe nofe, which they deftroy ; excrefeences or exoftofes arife in the middle of the bones, and their fpongy ends become brittle and break upon the kaft accident ; at other times they are foft, and bend like Avax : the conglobate glands become hard and callous, and form, in the neck, armpits, groin, and mefentoy, hard move- able tumours, like the king's evil ; tumours of different kinds are iikewife formed in the lymphatic veffels, tendons, ligaments, and nerves, as thegummala, ganglia, nodes, i'pbt, Sec. the eyes are affeded with itching, pain, rednefs, and fometimes with total blindnefs,and the ears with a finging noife, pain, and deafnefs, whilft their in- ternal fubftance is ulcerated and rendered carious ; at length all the animal, vital, and naturalfundions are depraved ; the face become; pale and livid ; the body emaciated and unfit foT motion, and th« miferable patient falls into an atrophy or wafting confumption* Women have fymptoms peculiar to the fex ; as cancers ofthe breaft ; a fuppreffion or overfloAving of the menfes ; the vkites ; hyfteric affections ; an inflammation, abfcefs, fchirrhus, gangrene, cancel, or ulcer of the Avomb ; they are generally either barren of fubjed to abortion ; or, if they brine; children into the world, they have an univerfal eryfipelas, are half rotten, and covered with ul- cers. Such is the catalogue < f fymptoms attendingthisd-c^dful difeafe in its confirmed ftate. Indeed they are feldom ail to be met Avith in the fame perfon, or at the fame time ; fo many of them however, are generally prefent as are fufficient to alarm the patient; and if he has reafon to fufped the infedion is lurking in his body, he ought immediately to fet about the expulfion of it, otherwife the moft tra- gical confequences Avill enfne. The only certain remedy hitherto known in Europe, for the cure of this difeafe, is mercury, which may be ufed in a great variety of forms, with nearly the fame fuccefs*. Sometime ago it was reckon- ed impoffible to cure a confirmed lue> without a falivation. This method * The preparations which I now chitfly, ufe, ia tlw. «*««:'«efl Iwu, ;r- .;«. »«wl Uld calcined mercury. Ji4 Ot a CONFIRMED LUES. method is now however pretty generally laid afide, and mercury fi found to be as efficacious, or rather moie fo, in expelling the vene- real poifon, when adminiftered in fuch a manner as not t* run off by the falivary glands. Though many are of opinion, that the mercurial ointment is as efficacious as any other preparation of that mineral ; yet experience has taught me to think otherwife. I have often feen the moft ob- ftinate venereal cafes, where great quantities of mercurial ointment had been ufed in vain, yield to the fkline preparations of mercury. Nor am I fingular in this opinion. My ingenious friend, Mr. Clare, an eminent furgeon of this city, affures me, that for fome time paft he has employed, in venereal eafes, a faline preparation of mercury with moft happy fuccefs. This preparation rubbed with a fufficient quantity of any mild powder, he applies, in fmall portions to the tongue, where, with a gentle degree of fridion, it is immediately abforbed, and produces its full effed upon the fyftem, without do- ing the leafl injury to the ftomach or bowels ; amaltcr of the great- eft importance in the application of diis moft adive and powerful remedy. y It is impoffible to afcertain cither the exad quantity of medicines that muft be taken, or the time they ought to be continued, in order to perform a cure. Thefe will ever vary according to the conftitution of the patient, the feafon of the year, the degree of infedion, the time it has lodged in the body, Sec. But though it is difficult, as Aftruc obferves, to determine a priori, What quanti- ty of mercury will in the whole be necefiary to cure this diftemper completely, yet it may be judged of a pofjri on,fiom the abatement and ceafing of the fymptoms. The fame author adds, that com- monly not lefs than two ounces of-the ftrong mercurial ointment is fufficient, and .not more than three or four ounces neceflary. The only chemical preparation of mercury which Ave fhall take no- tice of, is the corroffive fublimate. This was fome time ago brought into ufe for the venereal difeafe, in Germany, by the illuftrious Baron Van Swieten ; and was foon after introduced into Britah^by die learned Sir John Pringle, at that time phyfician to the army. The method of giving it is as follWs : One grain of corroffive fub- limate is diffolved in tAvo ounces of French brandy or malt fpirits ; and of this folution, an ordinary table-fpoonful, or die quantity of half an ounce, is to be taken tAviee a-day, and to be continued as long as any fymptoms ofthe diforder remain. To thofe whofe fto- mach cannot'bear the folution, the fublimate may be given inform of pill*. Several roots, woods, and barks, have been recommended for earing the venereal difeafe ; but none of them have been found, up- on * The fublimate may be given in diftilled water, or any other liquid that the patimt choofes. 1 commonly order ten grains to be diffolved in an ounce of the fpirit of wi.ic, for the conveniencv of carriage, andlet the patient take twenty or thirty drops o< it night and morning in half a glafs of l.rrmdv or other Ipiiits. Mr. Debra, an inge kms chymift of this place, informs me, that \v prepare* a lalt of mer- cury mu,;h more mild 7n i gentle in its operauon tlan thr lubliitMte. though .v.ual.y efRcj»ious. Or a CONFIRMED LUES. 3'* ©n experience, to anfwer the high encomiums which had been be- ftowed upon them. Though no one of thefe is to be depended up- on alone, yet, when joined with mercury,* fome of diem have been found to be very beneficial in promoting a cure. One Of the beft we know yet is farfaparilla, which may be prepared and taken ac- cording to the diredions in the Appendix*. * The mezereon-root is Iikewife found to be a powerful affiftant to the fublimate, or any other mercurial. It may cither be ufed along with farfaparilla, as direded in the Appendix, or by itfelf. Thofe who choofe to ufe the mezereon by itfelf, may boil an ounce ,of the frefh bark, taken from the root, in twelve Englifh pints of water to eight adding towards the end an ounce ef liquorice. The dofe of this is the fame as ofthe decoction «f farfaparilla. We have been told that the natives of America cure* the ven- ereal difeafe, in every ftage, by a decodion ofthe root of a plant cal- led Lobelia. It is ufed either frefh or dried ; but we have no cer- tain accounts Avithregardto the proportion. Sometimes they mix oth- er roots with it, as thofe ofthe ranunculus, the ceanothus, &c. but whether thefe are defigned to difguife or affift it, is doubtful. The patient takes a large draught ofthe decodion early in the morning, and continues to ufe it for his ordinary drink through the dayf. Many other roots and woods might be mentioned, which have been extolled for curing the venereal difeafe, as the chinna-root, the roots of foap-wort, burdock, Sec. as alfo the wood guiacumand faffafras; but as none ©f thefe have been found to poifefs virtues fuperior to thofe already mentioned, we fhall for the fake of brevity, pafs thera over, and fhall conclude our obfervations on this difeafe, with a few general remarks concerning the proper management of the patient, and die nature of the infedion. GENERAL OBSERVATIONS. THE condition ofthe patient ought always to be confidered previous to his entering upon a courfe of mercury in any foirn. It would be equally rafh and dangerous to adminifter mercury to a perfon labouring under any violent acute difeafe, as a put rid fever, plcurik 3 * See Appendix. Decoction ofS-.rsapar.illa. + Though we are ftill very much in the dark with regard to the method of curii-r, this diteale among the natives of America, vet it ia generally zffirmtd, that they do curs it wiih Ipeed, iakty and tuccefs, and that without the leaft knowledge of mercury Hence it becomes an objeft of confiderable importance to difcover their method of cu.e. Thismight fureiy.be do»e by making trials of the various plants which are found intho- * parts, and particularly of fuch as the natives are known to nuke uic of. All poopli in a rude ftate take their medicines chiefly from the vegetable kingdom, andaivyfte.i pofTeffed of valuable fecrcts with regard to the virtues of plants, <i>i which more en- lightened natioi,s tie ignorant. Indeed we make no doubt but fome plants of our own growth, were proper pains taken to difcover them, would be found as efl.c;?- cious in curing the vci.creal diieafe as thole of America. It mult however he re- membered, that what will cu:e the venereal diieafe u. or.e country, will not &b.<.iys be found to haveequd fuccefs ia anothej. ji6 Of a CONFIRMED LUES. pleurify, peripneumony, or the like. It would Iikewife be danger- ous in fome chronic cafes ; as the flow hyfteric fever, or laft ftage of a confumption. Sometimes, hoAvever, thefe difeafes proceed from a confirmed lues; in which cafe it will be necelfary to give mercury. In chronic difeafes of a lefs dangerous nature, as the afthma, the gravel, and fuch like, mercury, if neceffary, maybe fafely adminiftered. If the patient's ftrength has been greatly ex- haufted by ficknek, labour, abflinence, or any other caufe, the ufe of mercury muft be portponed, till by time, reft, and a nourifhing diet, it can be fufficiently reilored. Mercury ought rv;t to be adminiftered to women during the men- ftrual flux or when the period is near at hand. Neither ihouid it be given in the laft ftage of pregnancy. If, however, the woman be not near the time of her delivery, and circumftances render it neccif.try, mercury may be given, but in fmaller dofes, and at greater intervals than ufual: with thefe precautions, both the mo- dier and child may be cured at the fame time: if not, the diforde* will at leaft be kepi from growing worfe till the woman be brought to bed, and fufficiently recovered, when a more effedual method may be purfued, which, if fhe fuckles her child, will in all proba- bility be fuflicient for the cure of them both. Mercury ought always to be adminiftered to infants with the greateft caution. Their tender condition unfits them for fupporting a falivation, and makes it neceffary to adminifter even the mildell preparations of mercury to them Avith a fparing hand. A fimilar condud is recommended in the treatment of old perfons, who have die misfortune to labour under a confirmed lues. No doubt the in- nrmaties of age muft render people lefs able to undergo the fatigues of a falivation ; but diis, as was formerly obferved, is never neceffa- ry; befides, we have generally found, that mercury had much lefs effed upon very old perfons, than on diofe who are young* er. Hyfteric and hypochondriac perfons, and fitch as are fubjed to an habitual diarrhoea or dyfentery,or to frequent and violent attacks of the epilepfy, or who are afflided with the fcrophula, or the fcurvy, ought to be cautious in die ufe of mercury. Where any one of thefe diforder s prevails, it ought either, if poffible, to be cured or at leaft palliated, before the patient enters upon a courfe cf mercury. When this cannot be done, the mercury muft be adminiftered in fmaller dofes, and at longer intervals than ufual. The moft proper feafons for entering upon a courfe of mercury, are the fpring and autumn, when the air is of a moderate warmth. If the circumftances of the cafe, however, will not admit of delay, we mnft not defer the cure on account of the feafon, but muft ad- minifter the mercury; taking care at the fame time to keep the pa- tient's chamber warmer or cooler, according as the feafon ofthe year requires. The next thing to be confidered is the preparation neceffary to be obferved before we proceed to adminifter a courfe of mercury. Some lay great ftrefe upon this clrcumftance, obferving, that by previously ©fa CONFIRMED LUES. 3*7 previously relaxing the veffels, and correding any diforder which may happen to prevail in the blood, not only the mercury will be difpofed to ad more kindly, but many other inconvcniencics will be prevented. We have already recommended bleeding and gende purges,pre- vious to the adminiftration of mercury, and, fhall only ncnv add, that thefe are always to be repeated according to the age, ftrength, conftitution, and other circumftances of the patient. Afterwards, if it can be o nveniemly done, the patient ought to batiie once or twice a-day, for a few days, in lukewarm water. His diet in the mean time muft be light, moift, and cooling. Wine, and all heat- ing liquors, alfo violent bodily exercife, and all great exertions of die mind, are carefully to be avoided. A proper regimen is Iikewife to be obferved by fuch as are under a courfe of mercury. Inattention to this not only endangers the patient's life, but often alfo difappoints him of a cure. A much fmaller quantity of mercury will be fufficient for the cure of a per- fon who lives low, keeps warm, and avoids all manner of exeef-, than of one avIio cannot endure to put the fmalleft reftraint upen his appetites; indeed it but rarely happens that fuch are thorough- ly cured. There is hardly any thing of more importance, either for pre- venting or removing venereal infedion, than cleanlinefs. By an early attention to this, the infedion might often be prcA'ented from entering the body ; and where it has already taken place, its ef- feds may be greatly mitigated. The moment any perfon has reafon to fui'ped that he has received the infedion, he ought to wafh the parts with water and fpirits, fweet oil, or milk and water ; a fmall quantity ofthe laft may Iikewife be injeded up the urethra, if it- can be conveniently done. Whether this difeafe at firfl took its rife from dirtinefs, is hard to fay ; but wherever that prevails, the in- fedion is found in its greateft degree of virulence, which gives ground to believe, that a ftrid attention to cleanlinefs, would go far towards extirpating it altogether*. Whea ^* I have not only often feen a recent infeftion carried off in a few days by mcaBS or cleanlinefs, viz. bathing, fomentations, injections, &c. but have Iikewife found it of the greateft advantage in the more advanced ftages of the difeafe. Of this I had lately a very remarkable inflance, in i man wfmfc penis was almoft wholly contain- ed by venereal ulcers; the rhatur had been allowed to continue on the lores, -with- out any Care having been taken to clean them,'till, notwithflanding the ufe of mer- cury and other medicines, it had produced the effects above mentioned. I ordered w'arm milk and water to bp injected three or four times a-day into all the hnoui ulcer., in order t6 wafh' out all the matter; after which they were Ruffed with dry lint to abforb the frefh matter as it was generated. The patient at the fame time toek every day halfa grain of the corrofive lubllmate of meicury. d '.flolved gn an, ounce of brandy, and drank an Englifh quart of the decoftion of farfaparilla By this treatment in about fix weeks, he was perfectly cured ; and, what was very remark- able, a part ofthe penis wa« aftually regenerated. Doctor Gilchrift has given an account of a fpecies of the lues venerea v.! :; h pre- vails in the weft of Scotland, to which the natives give the nsrtie of Siisbins ot SlVVl-s. The Doftor obferves, that thfc fprradingof this dkitafe is chiefly owing & yi» Or a CONFIRMED LUES. When the venereal difeafe has been negleded, or improperly treated, it often becomes a diforder ofthe habit. In this cafe the cure muft'be attempted by reftoratives, as a milk diet, the decoc- tion of farfaparilla, and fuch like, to Avhich mercury may be occa- fionally added. It is a common pradice ia North-Britain to fend fuch patients to drink goat Avhey. This is a very proper plan, pro- vided the infedion has been totally eradicated before hand ; but when that is not the cafe and the patient trufts to the whey for fin- ifhing his cure, he will often be d fappcintei. I have frequently known the difeafe return vtitii all its virulence after a couife of goat whey, even when that courfe had been thought quite fufficient for completing the cure. One ot the moft unfortunate circumftances attending patients in this difeafe, is the neceffity they are often laid under of hurrying the cure. This induces them to take medicine too faft, and to leave it off too foon. A few giains more of medicine, or a few days longer confinement, would often be fufficient to perfed the cure ; whereas, by negled of thefe, a fmall d*gree of virulence is ftill left in the humours, which gradually vitiates, and at length contamin- ates the whole mafs. To avoid this, we vrould advife; that the patient fhould never leave off taking medicine immediately upon the difappearing of the fymptoms, but continue it for fome time after, gradually kffening the quantity,till there is fufficient ground to believe that the difeafe is entirely eradicated. It is not only difficult, but abfolutely impoflible to afcertain the exad degree of virulence that may attend the difeafe; for which reafon it will akvays be a much fafer rule to contintte the ufe of medicine too long, than to leave it off too foon. This feems to be the leading maxim of a modern praditioaer of fome note for the venereal diieafe, who always orders his patient to perform a quar- antine at kaft forty days, during which time he takes forty bottles cf, I fuppofe, a ftrong decodion of farfaparilla, or fome other an- tivenereal fimple. Whoever takes this method, and adds a fuffi- cient quantify of corrofive fublimate, or fome other adive prepar- ation of mercury to the decodion, will feldom fail to cure a con- firmed lues. It is peculiarly unfortunate for>the cure of this difeafe, that not ©ne in ten of thofe who contrad it, are either able or willing to fubmit to a proper plan of regimen. The patient is Avilling to take medicine ; but he muft follow his bufmefs, and, to prevent fufpi- cions, muft eat and drink like the reft of the family. This is the true fource of nine-tenths of all the mifchief arifing from the ven- ereal difeafe. I never knew the cure attended with any great dif- ficulty or danger where the patient fir idly followed the phyfician's advice: but a volume would not be fufficient to point out the dread- ful confequences which proceed from an oppofite condud. Schir- rous. to the nege£f of cleanlinefs and feems to think, that by due attention to that virtue, it might be extirpated. The treatment of this difeafe is fimilar to that of a confiimed .luedur pox. The v^v.s, a difeafe now very common both in America and the Weft-India iflands, may alfo be »ured ia the fame manner. DISEASES of WOMEN*. a* lous tefticles, ulcerous fore throats, madnefs, confumptions, cari- ous bones, and a rotten progeny, are a few of the bleffings derived from this fource. There is a fpecies of falfe reafoning, with regard to this difeafe, which proves fatal to many. A perfon of a found conftitution con- trads a flight degree of the diforder. He gets well without taking any great care, or ufing much medicine, and herce concludes that this will always be the cafe. The next time the difeafe occurs, though ten times more a indent, he purfues the fame courfe, and his conftitution is ruined. Indeed, the different degrees of viru- lence in the fmall-pox are not greater than in this difeafe, though, as the learned Sydenham obferves, in fome cafes the moft fkilful phyficians cannot cure, and in others the moft ignorant eld woman cannot kill die patient in that diforder. Though a good conftitu- tion is alwayfin favour of the patient, yet too great ftrefs may be laid upon it. It does not appear from obfervation, that the moft: robuft conftitution is able to overcome the virulence ofthe venereal contagion, after it has get into the habit. In diis cafe a proper courfe of medicine is ahvays indifpenfably neceffary. ^ Although it is impoffible, on account of the different degrees of virulence, Sec. to lay down fixed and certain rules for die cure of this difeafe, yet the following general plan will always be found fafe, and often fuccefsful, viz. to bleed and adminifter gentle pur- ges with diuretics during the inflammatory ftate, and as foon as die fymptoms of inflammation are abated, to adminifter mercury,in any form that may be moft agreeable to the patient. The fame aiedicine,aflifted by the decodion of farfaparilla, and a proper i eg- imen, will not only fecure the conftitution againft any further prog- refs of a confirmed pox, but will generally perform a complete cure. CHAP. XLVIII. DISEASES OF WOMEN7 WOMEN, in all civilized nations, have the management of domeftic affairs, and it is very proper they fhould, as Na- ture has made them lefs fit for the more adive andlaborious employ- ments. This indulgence, however, is generally carried too far ; and females, inftead of being benefitted by it, arc greatly injured from the ivaiit of exercife and free air. To be fatisfied of this, one need only compare the frefh and ruddy looks of a milk-maid, with the pale complexion of diofe females whofe whole time is fpent Avithin doors. Though nature has made an evident uiitindion between the male and female with regard to bodily ftrength and vigour, yet fhe certainly never meant, either tli.it the cue fliauld be always without, or the other always within doors. Th* I 52» Of the MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. The confinement of females,befides hurting their figure and com- plexion, relaxes their folids, weakens th/ir minds, and diforders all the fundions of the body. Hence proceed obftrudions, indigeft- ion, flatulence, abortions, and the whole train of nervous diforders. Thefe not only unfit Avomen for being mothers and nurfes, but often render them whimfical and ridiculous. A found, mind deptrads fo much upon a healthy body, that where the latter is wanting, the former is rarely to be found. I have always olferved that women who were chiefly employed without doors, in die different branches of hufbandry, gardening and the like, Avere almoft a» hardy as their hufbands, and that their children were Iikewife healthy. But as the bad effeds of confine- men: and inadivjty upon both fexes have been already fhewn, we fhall proceed to point out thofe circumftances in the ftru&ure and defiguof fe-nafei, which fubjed them to peculiar difeafes; the chief of which are their monthly evacuations, pregnancy and shild bearing. Tliefe indeed cannot properly be called difeafes, but, from the de- licacy of the fex, and their being often improperly managed in fuch ■kuaiions, they become the fource of numerous calamities. Or tbe MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. FEMALES generally begin to menftruate-about the age of fif- teen, and leave it off about fifty, which renders thefe two periods the moft critical of their lives. About the firft appearance of this difcharge, the conftitution undergoes a very confiderable change, generally indeed for the better, though fometimes for the worfe. fhe greateft care is now neceffary, as the future health and happi- nefs of the female depend in a great meafure upon her condud a: diis period*. If a girl about this time of life be confined to the houfe, kept conftantly fitting, and neither allowed to romp about, nor employ- ed in any adive bufinefs, which gives exercife to the Avhole body, fhe becomes weak, relaxed, and puny; her blood not being duly prepared, fhe looks pale and wan; her health, fpirit, and vigour decline, and fhe finks into a valetudinarian for life Such is die fate of numbers of thofe unhappy females, who, either from too much indulgence, or their own narroAv circumftances, are, at diis critical period, denied the beaefit of exercife and free air. A lazy indolent difpofition proves Iikewife very hurtful to girls at this period. One feldom meets with complaints from obftrudions among * It is the duty of mothers, and thofe who are intrufted with lh« education of girls, to intruft them early in the conduft and management of thwnfelves at this critical period of their lives. Falle modefty, inattention, and ignorance of what is beneficial or hurtful at thi-, time, ?re the lources of many diteafes and misfortunes in life which a few fenfible leffons from an expeiieuced matron might have prc- ventsd. Nor is care lefs neceffary inthe fubfequent returns of this difcharge. Taking improper food violent affections of the mind, or catching cold at this peri- od, is often fu'.'kientto r-uia the health, or render tfw female eve: after incapable of procreation. Of the MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. 321 among the more adive and induftrious part of the fex ; whereas the indolent and lazy are feldom fteefrom them.Thefe are in a man- ner eaten up by the chlorofis, or green ficknefs, and other difeafes of this nature. We would therefore recommend it to ail who wifh to efcape thefe calamities, to avoid indolence and inadivity, as their greateft enemies,and to be as much abroad in the open air as poffible. ^ Another ihing which proves very hurtful to girls about this pe- riod of life, is unwholefome food. Fond of all manner of trafh, they often indulge* in it, till their wholehumours are quite vitiated. Hence enliie indigeftions, want of appetite and a numerous train cf evils. If ti e fluids be rot duly prepared, it is utterly impoflible that the fecretions ihouid go properly on. Accordingly we find, that fuch girls as lead an indolent life, and eat great quantities of trafh, are not only fubjed to obftrudions of the menfes, but, like- wife to glandular obftrudions ; as the fcrophula, or king's evil, &c. A dull difpofition is alfo very hurtful to gills at this periqd. It is a rare thing to fee a fiprighdy gklwho doesnotenjoygood health while the grave, moving,melancholy creature,prnves the very prey of vapours and hyilerics. Youth, is the feafon for mirth aid cheer- fulnefs. Let it therefore be indulged. It is an abfolute duty. To lay in a ftock of health in time of youth, isas neceffary a piece of prudence, as to me i<e provifion againft the decays of old age,---- While, therefore, wife nature prompts the happy youth to join in iprightly amufements, let not the fevere dietates of hoary ?ge for- bid the ufeful impulfe, nor damp with ferious gloom, the feafon \ deftined to mirth and innocent fcftivity. Another thing very hurtful to females about diis period of life is ftrait clothes. They are fond of a free mape,andfoolifhly imagine that this can be acquired by lacing themfelves tight. Hence, by Squeezing the ftomach and boweis,thty hurt the digeftion, and oc- cafion many incurable maladies. This error is not indeed lb com- mon as it has been ; but, as fafliions change, it may come about again ; Ave therefore think it not improper to mention it. I know many females, who, to this day, feel the direful effeds of that wretched cuftom, which preA'ailed fome years ago, of fg.ee.ting e- very girl into as fmall a fize in the middle as poffible. If urn an in- vention could not poffibj/ have deviled a pradice mere deihiktiv« to health. After a female has arriva^ht that period of life when the menfes ufually h-. gin. to flow, and'they do not' appeai ,but,cn the contrary, her health and fpirits begin to decline, we would advife, inftead of* fhutting the poor girl up in the houfe, and doling her with fteel, afafcetida, and other meufeous drugs, to place her in a fkmu.icn where fhe can enjoy the benefit of frefh air and agreeable company. There let her eat wholefome food, take fufficient exercife, and amufe herfeif in the moft agreeable manner ; and we have little rea- fon to fear, but Nature, thus affiled, will do her proper work.— Indeed (L; feldom fads, unlefs where the fault is on our fide. Thii Rr 322 Of the MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE.: This difcharge in the beginning is feldom fo inftantaneous, as to furprife females unawares. It is generally preceded by fymptoms which foretel its approach ; as a fenfe of heat, weight, and dull pain in the loins ; diftention and hardnefs ofthe breafts ; head ach ; lofs of appetite ; laflkude ; palenefs of the couatenance ; and fometimes a flight degree of fever. When thefe fymptoms appear about the age at which the menflrual flux ufually begins, every thing fhould be carefully avoided which may obftrud that neceffary and falutary evacuation ; and all means ufed to promote it; as fit- ting frequently over the lleams of warm water, drinking warm di- luting liquors, Sec. After the menfes have onae begun ta flow,the greateft care fliould be taken to avoid every thing that may tend to obftrud them. Fe- males ought to be exceeding cautious of Avhat they eat or drink at the time rjiey are out of order. Every thing that is cold, or apt to four on the ftomach, ought to be avoided ; as fruit, butter-milk,aiid foch like- Fiih,aitd all kinds of food that arc hai'd of digeftion are alfo :o be avoided. As it is impoflible to mention every thing that may difagrec with iadividuals at this time, we would recommend it to every female to be very attentive tOAvhat disagrees Avith her- felf,and carefully to avoid it. Cold is extremely hurtful at this particular period. More of the fex date their difeafes from colds, caught while they are out of or- der, than from all other caufes. This ought furely to put them up- on their guard, and to make them very circumfped in their conduct at fuch times. A degree of cold that will not in the leaft hurt them at another time, will at this time be fufficient entirely to ruin their health and conftitution. The greateft attention ought likeAvife to be paid to the mind, which fhould be kept as eafy and cluerful as poffible. Every part ofthe animal economy is influenced by die paflions, but none more fo than this. Anger, fear, grief, and other affedions ofthe mind, of ..en occafion obftrudions of the menflrual flux, which prove abfo- lutely incurable. From whatever caufe this flux is obftruded, except in the ftate of pregnancy,proper means fliould be ufed to reftore it. For this pur- pofe we would recommend fufficient exercife, in a dry, open, and rather cool air ; xvholefome diet, and, if the body be weak and lan- guid, generous liquors ; alfo cheerful company and all manner of amufement*. If thefe fail, recourfe muft be had to medicine. When obftrudions proceed from a weak relaxed ftate of the foh ids, fuch medicines as tend to promote digeftion, to brace the fol- ids and affift the body in pieparing good blood, ought to be ufed. The principal of thefe are iron and the Peruvian bark, with other bitter and aftringent medicines. Filings of iron maybe infufed in wine or ale, two or three ounces to an Englifh quart, and after it has flood for tAvo or three weeks it may be filtered,and about hall * wine glafs of it taken twice a-day : or piepared ftcel may be ta- ken in the dofe of half a drachm, mixed with a little honey or trea- dc,thrcs or four times a-day. The bark and other birters may ei- ther be taken iu fubftance or infufion, as is moft agreeable to the pat.ent. \vuc.i Of the MENSTRUAL DISCHARGE. 3*3 When obftrudions proceed from a vicid ftate ofthe blood; or from women of a grofs or full habit, evacuations, and fuck medi- cines as attenuate the humours, are neceffary. The patient in thi? cafe ought to be bled, to bathe her feet frequently in warm water, to take now and then a cooling purge, and to live upon a fpare thin diet. Her drink Jhould be wfiey, water, or fmall beer, and fhe ought to take fufficient exercife. A tea-fpeonful of the tindure t f black hellebore may alfo be taken twice a-day in a cap of a. arm water. When obftrudions proceed from affedions of the mind, as grief, fear, anger, Ice. every method fhould be taken to ainafe and divert die patient. And that fhe may the more readily forget the caufe of her afflidion, fhe ought, if poffible to be remoA'ed from the place where it happened. A change of place, by presenting the mind with a variety of new objecls, has often a very happy influence in relieving it from the deepeft diftrefs. A foothing, kind, and affa- ble behaviour to females in this fituation, is alfo of the laft impor- tance. An obftrudion of the menfes is often the effed of other maladies. When this is the cafe, inftead of giving medicines to force that difcharge,which might be dangerous, we ought by all means to en- deavour to reftore the patient's health and ftrength. When that is effeded the other will return of courfe. But the menflrual flux may be too great as well as too fmall. When this happens, the patient becomes weak, the colour pale, the appetite and degeftion are bad, and cedematous fwellings of the feet, dropfies and confumptions often enfue. This frequently hap- pens to women about the age of forty-five or fifty, and is very diffi- cult to cure. It may proceed from a fedentary life; a full diet, con- futing chiefly of faked, high-feafoned or acrid food; the ufe of fpiritous liquoTs ; exceffive fatigue; relaxation; a diffolved ftate efthe blood; violent paflions ofthe mind, &c. The treatment of this difeafe muft be varied according to its caufe. When it is occafioned by any error in the patient's regimen, an op- pofite courfe to that which induced the diforder muft be perfoed, and fuch medicines taken as have a tendency to reftrain 'foe flux-, and counterad the morbib affedions ofthe fyftem from whence it proceeds. Tq reftrain the flux, the patient fliould be kept quiet and exfy both in body and mind. If it be very violent, fhe ought to lie in bed, with her head low; to live upen'a cool and flender diet, as veal or chicken broths with bread; and to drink decodions ofneuk- roo-.s, or the greater confrey. If thefe be not fufficient to ftop the flux, ftronger aftringents may be ufed, as Japan earth ahum, elixir of vitriol, the Peruvian bark, &c* The * Two drachms of allum and one of ]..ran earth may be pounded together, and divided into eigh« or nine doses, one of which may be tiken tliree time^a-dee. Perfons whofe flomachs cannot, bear th« allum, may take two tahle-fpoonfu!'; "i the tinfture of rofes three -or four times a-day, to each d^le of which ten d..g.,ot laudanum may be added. 324 Of PREGNANCY. The uterine f.uk may offend in quality as well as in quantity What is ufually called \hej7 ur albus, or whites, is a very common difeafe, and proves extremely hur.fui to delicate women. This difcharge, however, is not akvays white,but fometimes pale, yellow, green or of a blackifh colour; fometimes it is ih.up and corrofive, fometimes foul and foetid, 8tc. It is attended with a pale complex* ion, pain in the back, lofe of apt elite, fwefling of the feet, and Other iigns of debility. It generally proceeds from a relaxed ftate of the body, arifing from indolence, die exceffive ufe ot tea, coffee, or other weak and watery diet. -To remove this difeafe, the patient muft take as much exercife as fhe can bear without fatigue. Her food fhould be folid and nourifh- ing, but of eafy digeftion; and her drink rather generous, as red port or claiet mixed with Pyrmont,Briftol, or line water. Tea and coffee are to be avoideu. I have often known ftrong broths have an exceeding good cried,and fometimes a milk diet alone will perform a cure. The patient ought not to lie too long a bed. When me- dicine is neceffary, we knoAV none preferable to ihe Peruvian bark, which in this cafe ought always to be taken in fubftance. In warm weather the cold bath will be of confiderable fervice. That period of life at which the menfes ceafe to flow, is Iikewife very critical to the fex. The ftoppage of any cuftomary evacuation, however fmall, is fufficient to diforder the whole frame, and often to deftroy life itfelf. Hence it comes to pafs, thatfo many women either fall into chronic diforders, or die about this time. Such of them, however, as furvive it, without contrading any chronic dis- ease, eften become more healthy and hardy than they were before and enjoy ftrength and vigour to a very great age. If the menfes ceafe all of a fudden, in women of a full habit, they ought to abate iomewhat of their ufual quantity of food, efpecially of the more nourifhing kind, as flefh, eggs, Sec. They ought like- wife to take fufficient exercife, and to keep the body open. This may be done by taking, once or twice a- week, a little rhubarb, or an infufion of hiera picra in wine or brandy. It often happens that women of a grofs habit, at this period of life, have ulcerous fores break out about their ancles, or in other parts of their body. Such ulcers ought to be confidered as critical, and fhould either be fuffered to continue open, or have artificial drains fubftituted in their flead. Women who will have fuch fores dried up, are often foon after carried off by acute difeafes, or fall ;nto thofe of a chronic nature. OF PREGNANCY. THOUGH pregnancy is not a difeafe, yet that ftate is often at. tended with a variety of complaints which merit attention, and which If thefe fhould fail, half a drachm of the Peruvian bark, in powder, wiih tea drops of ths elixir of vitriol may be taken, in a glafs of red wine, four time* a-dg'. Of PREGNANCY. 5*5 which fometimes require afliftance of medicine. Some women in- deed are more healthy during their pregnancy than at any ether time; but this is by no means the general cafe: moft of them breed inforrow, and are frequently indifpoied during the whole time of pregnancy. Few fatal difeafes, however, happen during that peri- od ; and hardly any, except abortion, that can be called dangerous. We fhall therefore pay particular attention to it, as it proves gene- rally fatal to the child, and fometimes to the mother. Pregnat women are often afflided witi the heart-burn. The method of treating this complaint has been already pointed out. They ate likewiie, in the more early periods of pregnancy, often harraffed Avith ficknefs and vomiting, efpecially in the morning. The method &f relieving thefe complaints has alfo been fhewn. Both the head-ach and tooth-ach are very troublefome fymptoms of pregnancy. The former may generally be removed by keeping the body gently open,by the ufe of prunes, figs, roafted apples, and: fuch like. When the pain is very violent, bleeding may be neceffary. For the treatment of the latter, we mull refer to that article. Sev- eral other complaints incident to pregnant women might be men- tioned, as a cough and difficulty of bi eathing, fuppreffion and in- conftancy of urine, &)c. ; but as all of thefe have been taken notice of before, it is needlefs to repeat them. Every pregnant Woman is more or lefs in danger of abortion. This fhould be guarded againft with the greateft care, as it not on- ly weakens the conftitution, but renders the woman liable to the fame misfortune afterwards*. Abortion may.happen at any period of pregnancy, but it is moft common in the fecond or third month. Sometimes, however, it happens in the fourth or fifth. If it hap- pens within the firft month it is ufually called a falle conception; if after the feventh month, the child may often be kept alive by proper care. The common caufes of abortion are, the death of the child; weaknefs or relaxation of the mother; great evacuations; violent exercife; raffing great weights; reaching too high; jumping, or ftepping from an eminence ; vomiting ; coughing; convulfion fits; blows on the belly ;falls ; fevers; difagreeable fmells; excefs of blood; indolence; „high living, or the contrary; violent paffion* or affedions of the mind, as fear, grief, &c. The figns of approaching abortion are, pain in the loins, or about the bottom of the belly; a dull heavy pain in the infide of the thighs;a flight degree of coldnefs, or fhivering; ficknefs pal- pitation of the heart; thebreafts become flat and foft; the belly falls; and there is a difcharge of blood or watery humouis from the womb. To * Every mother who procures an abortion does it at the hazard of her life; yet there not a few who run the rifk merely to prevent the trouble of bearing and bring- ing up children. It is fnrely a mofi unnatural crime, and aannot, even in the moft abandoned, be viewed without horror; but in the decent matron, it is ftill more unpardonable.—Thofe wretches -who daily advertife their afliftance to women m this bufinefs, deferve, in my opinion, the maft fevere of all human punjfhmentj. $lf Of CHILD BIRTH. To prevent abortion, we wauld advife women of a weak or re- laxed habit to ufe folid food, avoiding great quantities of tea, and other weak watery liquors; to rife early and go foon to bed ; to fhun damp houfes ; to take frequent exercife in the open air, but to avoid fatigue ; and never to go abroad in damp f/oggy weather, if they can fhun it. Women of a full habit ought to ufe a fpare diet, avoiding ftrong l'-uors, and every thing that may tend to heat the body, or increafe the quantity of Wood. Their diet fhould be of an open- ing nature, coniiiling principally of vegetable fubftances. Every woman w !th child ought to be kept cheerful and eafy in her mind. Her appetites, even though depraved, ought t« be indulged as far as prudence will permit. When any fignsvof abortion appear, the woman ought to be .laid in bed on a matrafs, widi her head low. She fhould be kept quiet, and her mind feothed and comforted. She ought not to be kept too hot, nor to take any thing of a heating nature. Her food fliould confift of broths, rice and milk, jellies, gruels made of oat-meal, and the like, all of Avhich ought to be taken cold. If fhe be able to bear it, fhe fljpuld lofe at leaft half a pound of Wood from the arm. Her drink ought to be barley water fharp- ened with juice of lemon ; or flic may take half a drachm of powdered rrttre, in a cup of water-gruel, every five or fix hours. If the woman be feized with a violent loofenefs, fhe ought to drink the decodion of calcined hartfliorn prepared. If fhe be affeded with vomiting, let her take frequently two table fpoonfuls of the falinc mixture. In general, opiates are of fervice ; but they fliould always be given with caution. Sanguine robuft women, who are liable to mifcarry at a certain time of pregnancy, ought always to be bled a few days before that period arrives. By this means, and obferving the regimen above prefcribed, they might often efcape that misfortune. Though we recommend due care for preventing abortion, we would not be underftood as reftraining pregnant women from their ufual exercifes. This would generally operate a quite contrary way. Want of exercife not only relaxes the body, but induces a plethora, or too great a fulnefs of the veffels, which are two principal caufes of abortion. There are, however, fome women of fo delicate a texture, that it is neceffary for them to avoid almoft every kind of exercife during the whole period of pregnancy. CF CHILD-BIRTH. MANY difeafes proceed from the want of dtte earein child-bed; and the more hardy part of the fex are moft apt to defpife the ne- ceffary precautions in this ftate. This is peculiarly the cafe with young wives. They think, when the labour pains are ended, the danger is over ; but in truth it may only then be faid to be begun. . Nature, if left to herfelf, will feldom fail to expel the fetus ; but proper care and management are certainly neceffary for the recove- ry 0» CHILD-BIRTH. 3*7 ty of the mother. No doubt mifchief may be done by too much as well as by too little care. Hence females,who have the greateft number of attendants in child-bed generally recover worft. But this is not peculiar to the ftate of child-bed. Exceffive cafe always defeats its own intention, and is generally more dangerous tha» none at all*. During adual labour, nothing of a heating nature ought to be given. The woman may now and then take a little panac», and her drink ought to be toafl and water, or thin groat gruel. Spirits, wines, cordial-waters, and other things which are given with a view to ftrengthen the mother, and promote the birth, for the moft part, tend only to increafe the fever, inflame the womb, and retard the labour. Befides,they endanger the woman afterwards, as they often occafion violent and mortal haemorrhages, or difpofe her to eruptive and other fevers. When the labour proves tedious and difficult, to prevent inflam- mations, it will be proper to bleed. An emollient elyller ought like- wife frequently to be adminiftered; and the patient ihouid fit over the fleams of warm water. The paffage ought to be gently rubbed with a little foft pumatum or .frefh butter, and cloths wrung out of warm water applied over the belly. If nature feems to fink, and the woman is greatly exhaufted with fatigue, a draught of generous wine, or fome other cordial, may be given, but net othenvife. Thefe diredions are fufficient in natural labours; and in all preter- natural cafes, a fkilful furgeon, or man-midwife, ought to be called as foon as poffible. After delivery, the woman ought to be kept as quiet and eafy as poffiblef. Her food fhould be light and thin, as'gruel, panado, Sec. and her drink weak and diluting. To this rule, hoAvever, there are many exceptions. I have known feveral women,whofe fpirits could Hot be fupported in child-bed withaut folid food and gene- rous * Though the management of women in child-bed has been praftifed as an em- ployment fince the earlicft accounts of time; yet it ft ftill in moft countries on a ▼ery had footing.Few womea think of following this employment till they are re- du«ed to the neceffity of doing it for bread. Hsnce not one in an hund.ed of them have any education, or proper kuowledgc of their bufinef*. It is true, that Nature, tf left to herfelf, will generally expel the foetus; but it is equally true, that mod women in child bed require to be managed with (kill and attention, and that they are often hurt by the fuperftitious prejudices of ignorant and officious midwives. The milchiet done in this way is much greater than is generally imagined ; moft »t which might be prevented by allowing no women to praftife midwifery but fuch as a-e properly qualified. Were due attention paid to this, it w»uld not only be the niua.j> of laving many lives, but would prevent the neceffity of employing men in this indelicate and difagreeable branch of medicine, \?hich is, on many other ac- counts, more proper for the other fex. + We cannot help takiirg notice of that ridiculous ruflom whiflh ftill prevails iu fome parts of the country.of collecting a number of women together upon fuch occafions. Thefe, inftead of being ufeful, ferve only to crowd the houfe. and oh- ftruft the neccTary attendants. Befides they hurt the patient with their noife : ani often, by their untimely and impertinent advice, do much mifchief, 3** Or CHILD-BIRTH. rous liquors; to fuch, a glafs of wine and a bit of chicken muft be allowed. Sometimes an, exceffive hemorrhage or flooding happens after delivery. In this cak the patient ihouid be laid with her head Ioav, kept cool, and be in all refpeds treated as for an exceffive flux of the menfs. If there be vfokrt pains after delivery, the patient ought to drink plentifully of warm diluting liquors, as grueh or tea Avith a little faf- fron in it; and to take fmall broths, with carraAvay-feeds, or a bit of an orange-peel in them; p\ ounce of the oil of fweet almonds may likeAAdiebe frequently taken in acupof any ofthe above liquors; and if the patient be refllefil, a fpoonful of the fyrup of poppies may now and then be mixed with a cup of her drink. An iaflammation of the worn.b is a dangerous and not unfre- quent difeafe after delivery. It is knoAvn by pains in the loAver part ofthe belly, which are greatly increafed upon touching ; by die tenfion or tightnefs of the parts ; great weaknefs ; change of coun- tenance ; a conftant fever, with a weak and hard pulfe ; a flight delirium or raving ; fometimes inceffant vomiting : a hiccup ..j-a dif- charge of reddifh, flunking, fharp water from the womb ; an incli- nation to go frequently to ftool ; a heat, and fometimes total fup- preffion ot urine. The moft fatal diforder confequent upon delivery is the pr.erp?- ral, or child-bed fever. It generally makes its attack upon the fe- cond or third day after delivery. At this period, if not fooncr, a bilious or putrid loofenefs, of an obftinate and dangerous nature,comes on, and accompanies the dif- eafe through all its future progress. To avoid diis fever, every woman in child-bed ought to be kept perfedly eafy ;her food fhould be light and fimple, and her bed- chamber cool, and properly ventilated. There is not any thing t more hurtful to a woman in diis fituation than being kept too warm. Coftive- nefs is likeAvife to be avoided. This will be beft effeded by the ufe of mild clyfters and a laxative'diet. We fhall conclude our obfervations on child-bod women by re- commending it to diem, above all things, to beware of cold. Poor women, whofe circumftances oblige them to quit their bed too foon, often contrad difeafes from cold, of which they never re- cover. It is a pity the poor are not better taken care of m this fitu- ation. But the better fort of women run the greateft hazard from to< much heat. They are generally kept in a fort of bagnio for the firft eight or ten days, and then dreffed out to fee company. The danger of this condud nmll be obvious to every one.
