All churches are damp, and moft of them cold ; confequent- ly they are the very worll places to which a woman can go to , make her firft vifit after having been confined in a Avarm room for a mouth. OF BARRENNESS. BARRENNESS may be very properly reckoned among the dk- cafes cf females, as few married women who have not children en- joy a good ftate of health. It may proceed from various Gaufe.-., a-; high living, grief, relaxation, Sec. but it is chiefly owing to an ob- ftrudion or irregularity of the menflrual flux. It is very certain that high living vitiates die humours, and pi. • vents fecundity. Wu feldom find a barren woman amcrg the k- bwi„l;»g 332 DISEASE or CHILDREN. bouring poor, while nothing is mare common among the rich and affluent. The inhab:tants of every country are prolific in proportion to their poverty; and i would be an eafy matter to adduce many kftance, of women, who, by being leduced to live entirely upon a n ilk and vegetable diet, have conceived, and brought forth children, though they never had ar.v before. Would the rich ufe the fame fcrt of food and exercife .ts the better fort of peafants, they Avould feldom have caufe to envy their poor vaffals and dependants the bieffing of a numerous and healthy offspring, while they pine in forrow for the want of even a Angle heir to their extenfive do- mains. riflluence begets indolence, which not only vitiates the humours, but induces a general relaxation of the folids; a ftate highly unfa- vourable to procreation. To remove this, we would recommend the following courfe : Firft, fufficient exercife inthe open air; fe- eondlv, a diet confilling chiefly of milk and vegetables*; thirdly, the ufe of aftringent medicines, as fteel, allum, dragon's blood, elixir of vitriol, the Spaw or Tnnbridge Avaters, Peruvian bark, &c. and laftly, above all, the cold bath. Barrennefs is often the confequence of grief, fudden fear, anxie- ty, or any of the paflions which tend to obftrud the menflrual flux. When barrenncfe is fufpeded to proceed from affedions of the mind the perfon ought to be kept as eafy and as cheerful as poffible; all difagieeable objeds are to be avoided, and every method taken to amaie and entertain the fancy. CHAP. XLIX. DISEASES OF CHILDREN. MISERABLE indeed is the lot of man in the ftate of infancy. He comes into the world more helplefs that any other ani- mal, and ftands much longer in need ofthe potedion and care of his parents; but, alas! this care is not always beftowed upon him; and when it is, he often fuffers as much from improper manage- ment as he would have done fr om negled. Hence the officious care of parents, nurfes, and mldwives becomes one ofthe moft fruitful fources of the diforders of infantsf. If * Dr. Cheyne avers, that want of children is oftener the fault of the male than of the female, and ftrongly recommends a milk and vegetable diet to the former as well as the latter; adding that his friend E>. Taylor, whom he calls the Milk-doctor of Croydon, had brought fundry opulent families in his neighbourhood, who had c»»tinued fome years after mania^e without profeny, to have feveral fine children, by keeping both parents, for a confiderable nine, to a milk and vegetable diet. + Of the officious and ill-judged care of midwives, we fhall adduce only one inflance, viz. the common r^uciiee "of torturing infants, by fqueezipg thmr breafts, to draW DISEASES ot CHILEREN. 333 It muft be obvious to every attentive perfon, that the firft dif- eafes of children arife chiefly from their boAvels. Nor is this in the leaft to be wondered at, as they are in a manner poifoned with indigeftible drugs and improper diet as foon as they come in- to the world. Every thing tiiat the toftiach cannot digeft may be. confidered as a poifon ; and unlefs it can be thrown up, or voided by ftool, it muft occafion ficknefs, gripes, fpafmodic affedions of the bowels, or what the good women call inward fits, and at laft convulfions and death. As theie fymptoms evidently arife from fomewhat that irritates the inteftines, doubtlefs the proper method cf cure muft be to expel it as foon as poffible. The moft fafe and effedual method of doing this is by gentle vomits. Five or fix grains of the pow- der of ipecacuanha may be mixed in two table fpoonfuls of water, and fweetened with a little iugar. A tea-fpoor.ful of this may be given to the infant every quarter of an hour till it operates $ or^ what will more certainly anfuer the purpofe, a grain of emetic tartar may be diffolved in three ounces of water, fweetened with a little fyrup, and given as above. Thofe who are willing to ufe the emetic tartar, may give fix or fevon drops of the antimonial wine, in a teafpoonful of water or thin gruel. Small dofes of the ipecacuanha wine will be found more gentle than any of the above arid ought to be preferred. Thefe medicines will not only cleanfe the ftomach, but will gen- erally Iikewife open the body. Should this however not happen, and if die cuild be collivc, fome gentle purge will be neceffary : for this purpofe, fome manna and pulp of caffia may be diilblved in boiling wa.er, a-iidgiven in fmall quantities till it operates ; or, what will anfwer rather better, afc* grains of magnefia alba may be mixed in any kind of food thatk given to the child, and con- tinued till it has die defired effed. If thefe medicines be properly adminiftered, and the child's belly and limbs frequently rubbed Avith a warm hand before the fire, they will leldom fail to relieve thofe affections of the ftomach and bowels fiom which infants fuf- fer fo much. Thefe general diredions include moft of what can be done for relieving the internal diforders of infants. They will Iikewife go a confiderable way in alleviating thok which appear externully,as the rafh,,§-«/?7, or fellon, &c. Theft?", as was formerly obferved, are principally owing to too hot a regimen, and coniequently will be ' moft effedually relieved by gentle evacuations. Indeed, evacua- tions of one kind or other conftitute a principal part ofthe medicine of draw off the milk, as they call it Though a fmall quar-ti'y of moifture is generally found in the breafts of infants, yet, as they are certainly not intended to give fuck, this bu^ht never to be drawn off. I have feen this cruel operation bring on hardnefs, inflammation, and fuppuTation of the breaft; but never knew any ill conleqr-nces from iu> being omitted. When the breafts are hard,.the only application that we would recommend, is a foft poultice, or a little of the diachylon plafter, fprod thin on a bit of toft leather, about the fize of half a crown, and applied over tdch nip- ple. Thefe may be fuffered to continue till the Iwrdnefs dilappcars. ' ."54 Or the MECONIUM. of infants, and will feldom, if adminiftered with prudence, in any of their difeafes, fail to give relief. OFTHE MECONIUM. THE ftomach and bowels of a new born infant are filled Avidi a blackifh coloured matter of the confiftence of fyrup.commonly cal- led the meconium. This is generally paffed foon after the birth, by the mere effort of Nature; in which cafe it is not neceffary to give the infant any kind of medicine. But if it fhould be retained, or not fufficiently carried off, a litde manna or magnefia alba may be given as mentioned above; or,if thefe fhould not be at hand,a com- mon fpoonful of whey, fweetened with alittle honey, or raw fugar, Avill anfwer the purpofe. The moft proper medicine foi expelling the meconium is the mo- ther's milk, which is always at firft of a purgative quality. Were children alloAved to fuck as foon as they fhew an inclination for the breaft, they would feldom have occafion for medicines to dis- charge the meconium ; but even where this is not allowed, they ought never to have daubs of fyrup, oils, and other indigeftibk fluff, crammed down their throats. THE APHTHA? OR THRUSH. THE aphthx are little whitifh ulcers affeding^ the whole infide ofthe mouth, tongue, throat, and ftomach of infants. Sometimes they reach through the Avhole inteftinal canal ;»in which cafe they are very dangerous, and often put an end to the infant's life. If the aphtha? are of a pale colour, pellucid, few in number, foft, fuperficial, and fall eafily off, they are not dangerous , but if opake, yelloAv, brown, black, thick or running together they ought to be dreaded. It is generally thought diat the aphthae owe their origin to acid liumours; Ave have reafon however to believe, that they are more frequently owing to too hot a regimen both ofthe mother and child. It is a rare thing to find a child who is not dofed with Avine, punch, cinnamon waters, or fome other hot and inflaming liquors, almoft as foon as it is born. It is well known that thefe will occafion in- flammatory diforders even in adults; is it any wonder then that they fhould heat and inflame the tender bodies of infants, and fet as it were the Avhole conftitution on a blaze ? The moft proper medicines for the aphthae are vomits, fuch as have been already recommended, and gentle laxatives. Five grains of rhubarb and half a drachm of magnelt* alba may be rubbed toge- ther, and divided into fix dofes, one of which may be given to the infant every four or five hours till they operate. Thefe powders may either be given in the child's food, or a little of the fyrup of pale rofes, and may be repeated as often a? is found neceffary to Of ACIDITIES. 335 keep the body open. It is common in this cafe to adminifter' calo- ' mel ; but as that medicine fometimes occafions gripes, it ought al- ways to be given to infants Avith caution Many tilings have been recommended far gargling the mouth and throat in this difeafe ; but it is not eafy to apply thefe in very young children ; we would therefore recommend it to the nurfe to rub the child's mouth frequeudy wkh a little borax aad honey ; or with the folloAving mixture : Take fine honey an ounce, borax a drachm, burnt allum half a drachm, rofe-water two drachms ; mix them to- gether. Avery proper application in this cafe, is a folution of ten or tAvelve grainsof white vitriol in eight ounces of barley-Avater. Thefe may be applied with the finger, or by means of a bit of foft rag tied to the end of a probe. __i # OF ACIDITIES. THE food of children being for the moft part of an aceflent na- ture, it readily turns four upon the ftomaeh, efpecially if the body be any way difordered. Hence moft difeafes of children are ac- companied widi evident figns of acidity,as green ftools, gripes, &c. Thefe appearances have induced many to believe,that all the difeafes of children Avere owing to an acid abounding in the ftomach and bowels ; but whoever confiders the matter attentively, will find that thefe fymptoms of acidity are oftener the effed than the caufe of their difeafes. Nature evidently intended that the food of children fliould be acefcent ; and unlefs the body be difordered, or the digeftion hurt, from fome other caufe, Ave will venture to fay, that the acefcent quality of their food is feldom injurious to them. Acidity, howe- ver, is often a fymptom of diforders in children, and, as it is fome- times a troublefome one, Ave fhall point out the method of reliev- ing it. When green ftools, gripes, purgings, four fmells, &c. fhew that the bowels abound with an aciddet the child havea little fmall broth Avith light white bread in it; and it fliould have fufficient exercife in order to promote the digeftion. It has been cuftomary in this cafe to give the pearl julep, chalk, crabs eyes, and other teftaceous pow- ders. Thefe, indeed, by their abforbent quality, may corred the acidity ; but they are attended Avith this inconvenience, that they are apt to lodge in the bowels, and occafion collide nefs,whkh may prove very hurtful to the infant. For this reafon they fliould never l»e given unlefs mixed with purgative medicines ; as rhubarb, man- na, and fuch like. The beft medicine which Ave know in all cafes of acidity, is that fineinfipid powder called magnefia alba.It purges,and at the fame time correds the acidity : by which means it not only removes die difeafe, but carries off it's caufe. It may be given in any kind of food, or in a mixture, as recommendedin the Appendix'". Wkei; * Sec Appendix. Laxative .issorbcnt m ixtu^e, 336 GALLING akd EXCORIATION. When the infant is troubled AA-fth gripes, it ought notat firft to be dofed with brandy, fpicerics and other hot things ; but fhould have its body opened with an emollient clyfter, or the medicine mention- ed above ; and at the fame time a little brandy may be rubbed on its belly with a Avarm hand before the fire. I have feldom feen this fail to eafe the gripes of childten. If it fliould happen, however. not to.fucceed, a littU brandy or other fpirits may be mixed with thrice the quantity of warm water, and a tea-fpoonful of it given frequently till the infant be eafier. Sometimes a little peppetmiat water will anfwer this purpofe very well. GALLING AND EXCORIATION. THtioE are very troublefome to children. They happenchiefly about the groin and wrikks of the neck, under the arms, behind -he ears, and in other parts that are moiflened by the fweat or urine. As thefe complaints are, in a great meafure, owing to want of cleanlinefs, the moft effedual means of preventing them, are, to wafh .he parts frequently with cold Avater, to change the linen of- ten, and, in a word, to keep the child in all refpeds thoroughly clean. When diis is not fufficient, the excoriated parts m.-.y be fprinkled with abforbent or drying powders ; as burnt hartihorn, tutty, chalk, crabs claws prepared, and the like. When the parts affected are very fore, and tend to a real ulceration, it will be prop- er to add a little fugar of lead to the powders ; or to anoint the place with the camphorated ointment. If the parts be wafhed widi fpring water, in which a little white vitriol has been diffolved, i" will dry and heal them very poAA'erfully. One of the beft applica- tions for this purpofe, is to diffolve fome fuller's earth in a fufficient quantity of hot water ; and after it has flood till it is cold, to rub itgendy upon the galled parts, once or twice a-day. STOPPAGE OF THE NOSE. THE noflrils of infants are often plugged up with a grofs mucus', which prevents their breathing freely, and likeAvife rendeis it diffi- cult for them to fuck or fwallow. Some in this c ife order,after afokable purge,tAToor three grains of white vitriol diffolved in half an ounce of marjoram-water, and filtered, to be applied now and ihen to the noflrils with alinen rag. Wedelius fays, If two grain? of white vitriol,and the fame quantity of elaterium, bediifolved in half an ounce of marjoram-wa- ter, and applied to the nofe, as above direded, diat it brings away the mucus Avithout fneezing. In obftinate cafes thefe medicines may be tried ; but I have never found any thing neceffary, hefidap rubbing, the nofe at bed-time with Of VOMITING. 337 with a little fweat oil, or frefh butter. This rcfolves the filth, and renders the breathing more free*. OF VOMITING. From the delicate ftate of children, and the great fenfibility of 'their organs, a vomiting or loofenefs may be induced by any tiling that irritates th« nerves ofthe ftomach or inteftines. Hence thefe diforders are much more common in childhood, than in more ad- vanced periods of Ufe. They are feldom however, dangerous, and ought never to be confidered as difeafes, unlefs when they are violent, or continue ib long as to exluuft the ftrength of the pa- tient. Vomiting maybe excited by an over-quantity of food; by food that is of fuch a nature as to irritate the nerves of the ftomach too much ; or by the fenfibility of the nerves being fo much increafed as to render them unable to bear the ftimulus of even the mildeft aliment. When vomiting is occafioned by too much food, it ought to be promoted, as the cure will depend upon cleanfing the ftomach. This may be done either by a few grains of ipecacuanha, or a weak folution of emetic tartar, as mentioned before. When it is owing to food of an acrid or irritating quality, the diet ought to be chang- ed, and aliment of a milder natare fmlibtuted in its Head. When vomiting proceeds from an increafed degree of fenfibility, or too great an irritability of the nerves of the ftomach, fuch. medi- cines as have a tendency to brace and ftr.ing'.hca that organ, and to aba'e its fenfibility, muft be ufed. The firfl of thefe intentions may be anfwered by a flight infufion of the Peruvian bark, Avith the ad- dition of a little rhubarb and orange-peel; and the fecond by the faline drangfos, to which a few drops of liquid laudanum may be occafionally added. Ia obftinate vomitings the operation of internal medicines may be affifted by aromatic fomentations made with wine, applied warm to the pit ofthe ftomach; or the ufe of the ftomach-plafter, with the addition of a little Theriacu. ..' The antimonial wine, which ads both as an emetic and purge, is alfo an excellent medicine in this cafe. If, however, the patient's ftrength will permit, the medicine ought to bo repeated every fix or eight hour:, till the ftools begin to affume a more natu- ral appearance; afterwards a longer fpace may be allowed to inter- vene between the dofes. When it is neceflary to repeat the medi- cine frequently,Jthe dofe ought always to be a little increafed, as it* efficacy is generally diminifhed dy ufe. OF ERUPTIONS. CHILDREN, while on the breaft, are feldom free from erup- tions of on* kind or other. Thtfe, however, are not often danger. ous, and ought never to be dried up but with the greateft caution. The eruptions of children are chiefly owing to improper food and negkd of cleanlinefs.^ If a child be fluffed at all hours with food that its ftomach is not able to digeft, fuch food, not being properly aflirnilated, inftead of aourifhing the body, fills it with grofs hu- mours. Thefe muft either break out in form of eruptions upon the fkin, or remain in the body, and occafion fevers and other imemal diforders. Th at negled of cleanlfocfs is a very general caufe of eruptive Of ERUPTIONS. S3* eruptive diforders, muft be abvious to every olle. The children ofthe poor, and of all who defpife cleanlinefs, are almoft conftantly found to fwarm with vermin, and are generally covered with the fcab, itch, and other eruptions. When eruptions are the effect of improper food, or want of clean- linefs a proper attention to thefe alone will generally be fufficient to remove them. If this fhould not be the cafe, fome drying me- dicines will be neceffary. When they are applied, the body ought at the fame time to be kept open, and cold is carefully to be avoid- ed. We know no medicine that is more, fafe for drying up cutaneous eruptions than fulphur, provided it be prudently ufed.A little of the flour of fulphur may be mixed with frefh butter, oil, or hog's lard, ar.d the parts affeded frequently touched with it. The moft obftinate of all the eruptions incident to children, are the tinea capitis, or fcabbed head, and chilblains. The fcabbed head is often exceeding difficult to cure, and femetimes indeed the cure proves worfe than the difeafe. I have frequently knoAvn children feized with internal diforders, of v.-hkh they died foon after their fcabbed heads had been healed by the application of drying medi- cines". The cure ought always firft to be attempted by keeping the head very clean,cutting off the hair, combing and brufliingaway the fcabs, &c. If this is not fufficient, let the head be fhaved once a-Aveek, wafhed daily with fe ap fuds, and gently anointed with a liniment made of train oil eight ounces, red precipitate, in fine powder, one drachm. And if there be proud flefh, it fhould be touched with a bit of blue vitriol, or fprinkled with a little burnt al- lum. While thefe things are doing,the patient muft be confined to a regular light diet, the body fhould be kept gently open ; and cold, as far as poffible, ought te be avoided. To prevent any bad confequences from flopping this difcharge, it will be proper, efpe- cially in children of a grofs habit, to make an iffue in the neck or arm, which may be kept open till the patient becomes more ftrong and the conftitution be fomewhat mended. Chilblains commonly attack children in cold weather. They are generally occafioned by the feet or hands being kept long wet or cold, and afterwards fuddenly heated. When children are cold, inftead of taking exercife to Avarm themfelves gradually, they run to the fire. OFTEE'lHING. Dr. Arbuthr it obferves, that-above a tenth part of infants die in teething, I y f-vuptoms proceeding from the irritation ofthe tender nervous parts of the jaws, occafioning inflammauons,fevers, convulfions. »-angre.nes, Sec. Thefe iymptoms are in a great meaf- ure owing to the great delicacy and exouifite leniibiliiy of the ner- vous f* fori! at this tk'ie of fife, which k too often increaicu by an effeminate education. Hence it comes to pafs, that children who aie dedcately brought up, alwaysfuffer moft in teetli.ng, ana often fail by convulfive diforders:. /< bout the fixth ot fevrath month the teeth generally begin to make their appearance ; firft, the incifoies, or fore-tech ; next, the canb.i, cr dog-teeth : and, laftly, the molares, or grinders. P bout the feventii year there comes a new fet ; and about the twentieth, the two inner orinders, called denies fapienlia, the teeth ot wifdom. Children aboul the time of cutting their teeth, flaver much, and have generally a loofenefe. Wlun die teething is difficult, efpeci- ally * I was lately favoured with* lttter from Dr. William Turnbull in London a phyfician of great experience, and who, from his former fituation oa rU north-call coait of England, had many opportunities of oblerving the iymptoms and progrefs of this dangerous diieafe. I am lorry the letter came too late to be mferted at length; but as the Doctor'* ieutiinents differ very little from my own, this misfortune is the lefs to be regretted. TheDoftor indeed oblerves, that he never found bliliering of any fervice ; but recommends cataplafms of garlic, tamphor and Vema treacle, to be applied both to the throat and the foles of the feet. lie iinewiie recommends bolufes of eamplior, caftor, valerian root, fait of hartfhoni, and mufk, adapted to the age, ftreugth, &c. of tfie patient ; after which he advifes two fpoonfuls of the following deco&ion : Take of garlic and diftilled vinegar cr.eh an ounce, hyfop- ■water eight ounces ; beat up the ingredients together, gradually i.iixing the water, and adding three ounces of honey. Let the whole "be iii-mcrea over a gentle hre, and afterwards ftrained ior ufe. $4* Of TEETHING. ally when the dog teeth begin to make their way through the gumi, the child has ftartings in his fleep, tumours of the gurus, watchings, gripes, green ftools, the thrufh, fever, difficult breathing, and con- vulfions. Difficult teething requires nearly the fame treatment as an in- flammatory difeafe. If the body be bound, it muft be opened either by emollient clyfters or gentle purgatives ; as manna, magnefia alba, rhubarb, fenna, or the like. The food fliould be light, and in fmall quantity ; the drink plentiful, but weak and diluting, as infufions of balm, or ofthe lime tree flowers ; to which aoout a third or fourth part of milk may be added. If the fever be high, bleeding will be neceffary; but this in very young children ought rfways to be fparingly performed. It is an evacuation which they bear the worft of any. fvrging, vonhting, or fweating, agree much better with them, and are generally more beneficial. Harris, hoAvever, obferves, that, when an inflammation appears, the phyfician will labour in vain, If the cure be not begun with applying a leach under each ear. If the child be feized v.ith convulfion-fits, a bliftering plafter may be applied between the flioulders, or one behind each ear. Sydenham fays, that in fevers occafioned by teething, he never found any remedy fo cfiednal as two, three or fotr drops of fpirits of hartfhorn in a fpoonful of fimple water, or utfier convenient veheicle, given every four hours. The nunker of dofes may be four, five or fix. I have often prefcribed this medicine with fuc- cefs, but always found a larger dofe neceffary. It may be given from five drops to fifteen or twenty, according to the age of the child, andAvhen coftivenefs does not forbid it, three or four drops of laudanum may be added to each dofe. In Scotland, it is very common, when children are cutting their teeth, to put a fmall Burgundy pitch plafter between their flioul- ders. This generally eafes the tickling cough which attends teeth- ing, and is by no means an ufelefs application. When die teeth are cut widi difficulty, it ought to be kept on during the whole time of teething. It may I e enlarged as occafion requires, and ought to be renewed at leaft once a fortnight. Several things have been recommended for rubbing the gums, as oils, mucilages, Sec. but from thefe much is not to be expeded. If any thing of this kind is to be ufed, we would recommend a little fine honey, which may be rubbed on with the finger three or four times a-day. Children are generally at this time difpofed to chew whatever they gel-into their hands. For this reafon diey ought never to be without fomewhat that will yield a little to the preffure of their gums, as a cruft of bread, a wax candle, a bit of liquorice root, or fuch like. With regard to cutting the gums, we have feldom known it of any great benefit. In obftinate cafes, however, it ought to be tried. It may be performed by the finger nail, the edge of a fixpenny piece that is worn thin, or any fharp body which can be with fafe- ty introduced into the mouth ; but a lancet, in a fkillful hand, is certainly the moft proper. In Of tub RICKETS. 343 *In order to render the teething lefs difficult, parents ought to take care that their children's food be light and wholefome, and that their nerves be braced by fufficient exercife without doors, the ufe ofthe cold bath, r<c. Were thefe things duly regarded,they would have a much better effed than teething necklaces, or other nonfen- fical emulets worn for that purpofe. OFTHE RICKETS. THIS difeafe generally attacks children between the age of nine months and two years. It appeared firft in England, about tht time when manufadures began to flourifh, and ftill prevails moftin towns ivhere the inhabitants follow fedentary employments, by which means they negled either to take proper exercife diemfelTes, or to give it to their children. CAUSES.—One caufe ofthe rickets is difeafed parents. Mo- thers of a weak relaxed habit, who negled exercife, and live upon weak watery diet, can neidierbe expeded to bring forth ftrong and healdiy children,or to be able to nurfe them after they are brought forth. Accordingly we find, that the children of fuch women ge- nerally die of the rickets, the fcrophula, confumptions, Or fuch like difffefes. Children begotten by men in the decline of life, who are fubjed to the gout, the gravel, or other chronic difeafes, or who have been often afflided with the venereal difeafe in their youth, are likewifes very liable to the rickets. Any diforder that weakens the conftitution,or relaxes the habit of children, as the fmr.ll pox, meafles, teething, the hooping cough, &c difpofes diem to this difeafe. It may Iikewife be occafioned by improper diet, as food that is either too weak and watery, or fo vifcid that the ftomach oannot digeft it. Bad nurfing is the chief caufe of this difeafe. When the nurf« is either difeafed, or has not enough of milk to nourifh the child, it cannot thrive. But children fiuffer oftener by want of care in nurfes than want of food. Allowing an infant to lie or fit too much, or not keeping it thoroughly clean in its clothes, has the moft per- nicious effeds. The want of free air is Iikewife very hurtful to children in this refped. When a nurfe lives in a clofe fmall houfe, where the air Is damp and confined, and is too indolent to carry her child abroad into the open air,it will hardly efcape this difeafe. A healthy child Ihouid always be in motion, unlefs when afleep ; if it be fuffered to lie or fit, inftead of being toffed and dandled about, it will not thrive. SYMPTOMS.—At the beginning of this difeafe the child's flefli groAvs foft and flabby ; its ftrength is dimioifhed ; it lofes its Wonted cheerfulnefs, looks more grave and compofed than is na- tural for its age, and does not chufe to be moved. The head and belly become too large in proportion to the odier parts ; the face appears full, and the complexion florid. Afterwards the bones begin 344 Or tub RICKETS, begin to be affeded, efpecially in the more foft and fpungy part*. Hence the wrifts and ancles become thicker than ufml; the fpine or back-bone put» on an unnatural fhape; the breaft is Iikewife- often deformed ; and the bones of the arms and legs grow crooked. All thefe fymptoms vary according to the violence of the difeafe. The pulfe is generally quick, but feeble ; the appetite and digeftion for the moft part bad ; the teeth come flowly and wi'Ji difficulty, and they often rot and fall out afterwards. Ricketty children gen- erally have great acutenefs of mind, and an undcrfumding abovr their years. Whither this is owing to their being more in the com- pany of adults than other children, or to the preternatural enlagc- ment of the brain, is not material. RSG1MEN.—As this difeafe is always attended with evident figns of weaknefs and relaxation, our chief aim in the curs muft be to brace and ftrengthen the folids, and to promote digeftion and the due preparation of the fluids. Thefe important ends will be beft anfwered by wholefome nourifliing diet, fuited to the e.^c and ftrength of the patient, open dry air, and fuffieknt ecercife. If the child has a bad nurfe, who either negleds her duty, or doesnot underftand it, fhe fhould be changed. If the feafon be cold, the child ought to be kept warm : and when the weather is hot,it ought to be kept cool ; as fweating is apt to weaken it, and too great a degree of cold has the fame effect. The limb-i fliould be rub- bed frequently widi a warm hand, and the child kept as cheerful as poffible. The diet ought to be dry and nourilhing, as #ood bread, roafted flefh, &c. Bifeuit is generally reckoned the beft bread ; and pige- ons, pullets, veal, rabbits, or mutton roafted or minced, are the moft proper flefh. If the child be too young for flefli meats, he may have rice, millet, or pearl-barley boiled with rafins, to which may be added a little wine and ipice. His drink may be good ciaret mixed with an equal quantity of water. Thofe who cannot afford claret, may give the child noAv and then a Avine-glafs of mild ale,or good porter. MEDICINE.— Medicines are here of little avail. The difeafe may often be cured by the nurfe, but feldom by the phyfician. In children of a grofs habit, gentle vomits and repeated purges of rhu- barb may fometimes be of ufe, but they will feldorn carry off the difeafe ; that muft depend chiefiy upon fuch things as brace and ftrengthen the fyftem : for which purpofe,befides the regimen men- tioned above, we would recommend the cold bath, efpecially in the warm feafon. It muft hoAvever be ufed Avith prudence, as forae ricketty children cannot bear it. The beft timeJ"' r ufk.g thecold bath is in the morning, and the child fliould be well rubbed with a dry cloth immediately after becomes out of it. If the child ihouid be weakened by the cold bath, it muft be dlfeontinued. Sometimes iffues have been found beneficial in this difeafe. They are peculiarly neceffary for children who abound with grofs hu- mours. An infufion of the Peruvian bark in wine or ale would be of fervice, were it poffible to bring them to take it. We might here mention many other medicines which have been recommended for tha Of CONVULSIONS. 34; the rickets; but as there is far more danger in trufling to thefe than in negleding them altogether, we chufe rather to pafs them over, and to recommend a proper regimen as the thing chiefly to be depended on. OF CONVULSIONS. THOUGH more children are faid to die of convulfions than of any other difeafe, yet they are for the moll part only a fymptom of fome other malady. Whatever greatly irritates or ftimulates the nerves, may occafion convulfions. Hence infants whole nerves are eafily affeded, are often thrown into convulfions by any thing thatirritatesthe alimentary canal; Iikewife by teething; ftrait clothes; the approach ofthe fmall pox, meafles, or other eruptive difeafes. When convulfions proceed from an irritation of the ftomach or bowels, whatever clears them of their acrid contents, or renders thefe mild and inoftenfive, will generally perform a cure : where- fore, if the child be coftive, the beft Avay will be to begin with a clyfter and afterwards to give a geatle vomit, which may be re- peated occafionally, and the body in the mean time kept open by gentle doles of magmfia aiba, or fmall quantities of rhubarb mixed with the powder of crabs claws. Convulfions which precede the eruption of the fmall pox or meafles generally go off upon thefe making their appearance. The principal danger in this cafe arifes from the fears and apprehenfions of thofe who have the care of the patient. Convulfions are very alarming, and fiomethkfg muft be done to appeale the affrighted parents, nurfes, &c. Hence the unhappy infant often undergoes bleeding, bliftering, and feveral other operations, to the great dan- ger of its life, when a little time, bathing the feet in Avarm water, and throwing in a mild clyfter, would have fet all to rights. When convulfion fits arife from the cutting of teeth, befides gen- tle evacuations, we would recommend bliftering, and the ufe of antifpafmodic medicines, as the tindure cf foot, afafeetida, or caf- tor. A few drops of any of thefe may be mixed in a cap of white- wine whey, and given occafionally. When convulfions proceed from any external caufe, as the pref- fure occafioned by ftrait clothes or bandages, £ec. thefe ought im- mediately to be removed; though in thiscafe taking away the caufe Avill not always remove the effed, yet it ought rx> be done. It is not likely that the patient will recover, as long as the caufe which firft gave rife to the diforder continues to ad. When a child isfeized with convulfions without having any com- plaint in the bowels, or fymptoms of teething ; or any raih or other difcharge which has been fuddenly dried up; we have reafon to conclude that it is a primary difeafe, and proceeds immediately from the \v aim Cafes of this kind, however, happen but feldom, Avhicb Is vei fortunate, as little can be done to relieve the unhappy patient. V: hen :r d'feafe proceeds from an original fault in the formation or iixaefure ofthe brain itfelf, we cannot exped that k fhould ¥a 346 Or WATER in the HEAD. fhould yield to medicine. But as this is not always the «aufe,evea of convulfions which proseed immediately from the brain, fome attempts fhould be made to remove them. The chief intention to be purfued for this purpofe, is to make fome derivation from the head, by bliftering, purging, and the like. Should thefe fail, iffues or fctons may be put in the neck, or between the ihoulden. OF WATER INTHE HEAD. THOUGH water in the head, or a dropfy of the brain, may affed adults as well as children, yet, as the latter are more pecu- liarly liable to it, we thought that it Avould be moft proper to place it among the difeafes of infants. CAUSES.----A dropfy ofthe brain may proceed from inju- ries done to the brain itfelf by falls, blows, or the like ; it may alfo proceed from an original laxity or weaknefs of the brain ; from fchirrous tumours or excrefcences within the fkull ; a thin watery ftate ofthe blood ; a diminifhed fecretion of uriae; a fudden check Ofthe perfpiration ; and laftly, from tedious and lingering difeafes, which wafle and eonfume the patient. • SYMPTOMS.----This difeafe has at firft the appearance of a flow fever ; the patient complains of a pain in the crown of his head or over his eyes; he fliuns the light; is fick, and fometimes vomits'; his pulfe is irregular and generally low : though he feems heavy and dull, yet he does not fleep : he is fometimes delirious, & frequently fees ogjeds double; towards the end of this commonly fatal difeafe, the pulfe becomes more frequent the pupils are gene- rally dilated, the cheecks flufhed, the patknt becomes comatofe, and convulfions enfue*. MEDICINE.----No medicine has hitherto been found fuffici- ent to carry off a dropfy ofthe brain. It is laudable, however, to make fome attempts, as time or chance may bring many things to light, of which at prefent we have no idea. The medicines gene- rally ufed are, purges of rhubarb or jalap, with calomel or biifter- ing-plafters applied to the neck or back part ofthe head. To which we would beg leave to add diuretics,or medicines which promote the fecretion of urine, fuch as are commended in the common dropfy. A difcharge from the nofe ought Iikewife to be promoted by caufing the patient to fnuft the powder of afaTum, Avhite helle- bore or the like. Some praditioners have of late pretended to cure this difeafe by the ufe of mercury. I have not been fo happy as to fee any inftan- ces * I lately loft a patient in this diCwfe, wh#re a eurioui metaffa'is feened to >.„<* place, The wat-r at firft appeared t» be in the abdomen, afterwards in the breaft, a&d laft *>1 '.11 it suo anted «p t» thtbr«in. wk«r« it f»i*n pr*v«4 bui. Or SURGER*. 347 ces of a cure being pei formed in a confirmed dropfy of the brain; but in fo defperate a malady every thing deferves a trialf. CHAP. L. . OF SURGERY. TO defcribe all the operations of furgery, and to point out tin different difeafes in Avhich thefe operations are neceffary, woald extend this article far beyond the limits allotted to it; we muft therefore confine our obfervations to fuch cafes as moft gene- rally occur, ail in which proper afliftance is either not afked, or not always to be obtained. Though an acquaintance Avith the ftrudure ofthe human body ii indifpenfably neceflary to qualify a man for being an expert forge- on ; yet many things may be done to fave the lives of their fellow- men, in emergencies, by thofe who are no adepts in anatomy. _ It is amazing widi what facility the peafants daily perform operations upon brute animals, which are not of a lefs difficult nature than ma- ny of thofe performed on the human fpecies; yet they feldom fail of fuccefs. Indeed every man is in fome meafure a fergeon whether he wul or not. He feels an inclination to affift his fellow-men in diftrefs, and accidents happen every hour which give occafion to exeicife this feeling. The feelings of the heart, hoAvever when not direded by the judgment, are apt to miflead. Thus one by a rafli attempt to fave hii friend, may fometimes deftroy him; while another for fear of doing amifs, ftands ftill an* fees his bofom friend expire without fomuch as attempting to relieve him, even whenthe means are in his power. As every good man would wifh to flear a courfe different from either of thefe; it av:11 no doubt be agreeable to him to know what ought to be done upon fuch emergencies. OF BLEEDING. NO operation of furgery is fo frequently neceffary as bleeding; it ought therefore to be very generally underftood. But though pradifed by midwives, gardeners, blackfmiths, &c. we have reafon to believe that very few know when it is proper. Even phyficians themfelves t One reafon why this difeafe is feldom or never eured, may be, that it is feldom known till too far advanced to admit pf a remedy. Did parents watch the firft fymptoms, and call a phyfician in due time, I am inclined to thiak that fomething might be done. But thei'e fymptomsare not yet fufttiently known, and are often miftaken even by phyficians themfelves. Of this I lately law a ftriking i..- ftance in a patient attended by an eminent praftitiocer »f :hi*city, *)n> hod -I a- lc-Bg miftaken the difeafe for a twthing. 34* Or BLEEDING. themfelves have been fo much the dupes of theory in this article, as to render it the fubjed of ridicule. It is however an operation cf great importance, and muft,when feafonably and properly perform- ed, be of fingular fervice to thofe in diftrefs. Bleeding is proper at the beginning of all inflammatory fevers,as pleurifies, peripheumonies, Sec. It is likeAvife proper in all topical inflammations, as thofe of the inteftines, womb, bladder, ftomach, kidneys, throat, eyes,&c. as alfo in the afthma, feiatic pains, coughs, head achs, rheumatifms, the apoplexy, epilepfy, and bloody hux. After falls, blows, bruifes, or any violent hurt received either exter- nally or internally, bleeding is neceflary. It is Iikewife neceffary for perfons who have had the misfortune to be ftrangled, drowned, fuffocated with foul air, the fumes of metal or the like. In a woid, whenever the vital motions have been fuddenly ftoptfrom any caufe whatever, except in fwoonings occafioned by mere weaknefs or hyfteric affedions, it is proper to open a vain. But in all diforders proceeding from a relaxation of the folids, and an impoveriflied ftate of the blood, as dropfies, cacochymies, &c. bleeding is im- proper. Bleeding for topical inflammations ought ahvays to be performed as near the part affeded as poffible. When this can be done with a lancet, it is to be preferred to any other method; but where a vein cannot be found, recourfe muft be had to leeches or cupping. The quantity of blood to be let muft always be regulated by the ftrength, age, conftitution, manner of life, and other circumftances relating to the patient. It would be rediculous to fuppofe that a child could bear to lofe as much blood as a grown perfon, or that a delicate lady fhould be bled to the fame extent as a robuft man. From whatever part of the body blood is to be let, a bandage muft be applied between that part and the heart. As it is often ne- ceffary, in order to raife the vein, to make the bandage pretty tight, it will be proper in fuch cafes, as foon as the blood begins to flow, to flacken it a little. The bandage ought to be applied at leaft an inch, or an inch and an half, from the place where the wound is in- tended to be made. Perfons not fkilled in anatomy'ought never to bleed in a vien that lies over an artery or a tendon, if they can avoid it. The former may eafily be known from its puliation or beating, and the latter from its feeling hard or tight like a whip cord under the finger. It was formerly a rule, even among thofe who had the charader of being regular praditioners, to bleed their patients in certain dif- eafes till they fainted. Surely a more ridiculous rule could not be propofed. One perfon will faint at the very fight of a lancet, while another will lofe almoft the whole blood of his body before he faints. Swooning depends more upon the ftate ofthe mind than of the body: befides, it may often be occafioned or prevented by the manner in which the operation is performed. _ Children are generally bled with leeches. This, though fome- times neceffary, is a very troublefome and uncertain pradice. It is impoffible to know what quantity of blood is taken away by leech. es; Of INFLAMMATIONS and ABSCESSES. 3.3 cs ; befides, the bleeding is often very difficult to flop, and die wounds are not eafily healed. Would thofe who pradife bleeding take a little more pains, and accuftom themfelves to bleed children, they Avould not find it fuch a difficult operationas they imagine. Certain hurtful prejudices with regard to bleeding ftill prevail among the country people. They talk, for inftance of head veins, heart-veins, breaft-veins, Sec. and believe that bleeding in thefe will ccitainly cure all difeafes ofthe parts fiom whence they arefoppofed to come, without cenfkkring that all the blood veffels arife from the heart and return to it again ; for which reafon, unlefs in topical inflammations, it fignifks very little from what part of the body the blood is taken. But this, though a foolifh prejudice, is not near fo hurtful as the vuigar notion that the firft bleeding will perform wonders. This belief makes them often pcftpone the operation when neceffary, in order toreferve it for fome more important oc- caiioi*, and, when they think themfelves in extreme danger they fly to it for relief whether it be proper or not. Bleeding at certain fluted periods or feafons have Iikewife bad effeds. It is a common notion that bleeding in the fet c draws the hu- mours downwards, and confequently cures difeaies of the head and other fuperior parts ; but Ave have already obferved that,in all top- ical affedions, the blood ought to be draAvn as near the part as pof- fible. When it is neceffary, however, to bleed iu the foot or hand, as the veins are fmall, and the bleeding is apt to ftop too foon, the part ought to be immerfed in warm water, and kept there till a fuf- ficient quantity of blood be k.t. We fhall not fpend time in deferibing the manner of performing this operation : that will be better learned by example than precept. Twenty pages of defer iption Avould not convey fo juft an idea of the operation as feeing it once performed by an expeit hand. Nei- ther is it neceflary to point out the different parts of the body from whence blood may be taken, as the aim, foot, forehead, temples, neck, Sec. Thefe will readily occur to every intelligent perfon, and the foregoing obfervations will be fufficient for determining which of them is muft proper upon any particular occafion, In all cafes where the intention is merely to leffen the general mafs of blood, the arm is the moft commodious pan ofthe body in which the op- eration can be performed. OF INFLAMMATIONS AND ABSCESSES. FROM whatever caufe an inflammation proceeds, it muft termi- nate either by difperfion, fuppuration, or gangrene. Though it is impoffible to foretel with certainty in which of thele ways any parti- cular inflammation will termmate,yet a probable conjeduremay be formed with regaid to the event, from a knoAvledge cf the pa- tient's age and conftitution. Innammationsbappening in a flight de- free upon colds,and without any previous indifpufiticn,wiilnioftpro- ably b^e difperfed ; thofe which folloAv clofe upon a fever* or hap- pen to perfons of a grofs habit of body, will generally foppurate ; and ?5o Or INFLAMMATIONS a»d ABSCESSES. and thofe Avhich attack very old people, or perfons of a dropfical habft, will have a ftrong tendency to gangrene. If the inflammation be flight, and the conftitution found, the dif- perfion ought always to be attempted. This will be beft promoted by a flender dilating diet, plentiful bkeding, and repeated purges. The part itfelf muft be fomented, and, if the flrin be very tenfe, it may be embrocated with a mixture cf three fourths of fweet oil,and one-fourth of vinegar, and afterwards covered with a piece of Avax- pl after. If, notwithftanding thefe applications, the fymptomatic fever in- creafes, and the tumour becomeslarger,with a i .lent pain and pulfa- tion, it will be proper to promote the fuppuration. The beft appli- cation for this purpofe isa foft poultice which may be renewed twice. a-day. If the fuppuration proceeds but flowly, a raw onion cut fmall or bruifed may be fpread upon the poultice. When the ab- fcefs is ripe or fit for opening, which may eafily be known from the thinnefs ofthe fkin in the moft prominent part o# it, a fluduation of matter which may be felt under the finger, and, generally fpeak- ing, an abatement ofthe pain, it may be opened either with a lan- cet or by means ff cauftic. The laft way in which an inflammation terminates, is in a gan- grene or mortification, die approach of VA'hich may be known by the following fymptoms : the inflammation lofes its rednefs, and be- comes dufkifh or livid ; the tenfion of the fkin goes offand it feels flabby ; little bladders filled with ichor of different colours fpread all over it ; the tumour fubfides, and from a dufkifh complexion be- come black ; a quick low pulfe, with cold clammy fweats, are the immediate forerunners of death. When thefe fymptoms firft appear, the part ought to be dreffed widi London treacle, or a cataplafm made of lixivium and bran. Should the fymptoms become worfe, die part muft be fcarified and afterwards dreffed with bafih'cum foftened with oil of turpen- tine. All the dreffings muft be applied warm. With regard to internal medicines, the patient muft be fupported with generous cordials, and the Peruvian bark exhibited in as large dofes as the ftomach will bear it. If the mortified parts fhould feparate,the wound will become a common ulcer, and muft be treated accord- This article includes the treatment of all thofe difeafes, which, in different parts of the country, go by the names of bites, impoft- humes, whitloes. Sec. They are all abfeeffes in confequence of a previous inflammation, which, if poffible, ought to be difcuffed ; but when this cannot be done, the fuppuration fhould be promoted, and the matter difeharged by an incifion, if neceffary ; afterwards the fore may be dreffed with yellow bafilicum, or fome other dj« geftive ointment. OF Of WOUNDS. 3>» OF WOUNDS. NO part of medicine has been more miftaken than the treat- ment or cure of wounds. Mankind in general believe that certain herbs, ointments, and plafters are poffeffed ofAvonderful healing powers, and imagine that no wound can be cured without the ap- plication of them. It is however a fad, that no external applica- tion whatever contributes towards the cure «f a wound, any other way than by keeping the parts foft, clean, and defending them from the external air, which may be as efedually done by dry lint5as by the moft pompous applications, while it is exempt from many of the bad confequences attending them. The fame obfervation holds with refped to internal applications. Thefe only promote the cure of wounds as far as they tend to pre- vent a fever, or to remove any caufe that might obftrud or impede the operations of nature. It is Nature alone that cures wounds. All that art can do is to remove obftacles,and to put the parts in fuch a condition as is the moft favourable to nature's efferts. With this fimple view Ave fhall confider the treatment of wounds, and endeavour to point out fuch fteps as ought to be taken to facil- itate their cure. The firft thing to be done when a perfon has received a wound, is to examine whether any foreign body be lodged in it, as wood, ftone, iron, lead, glafs, dirt, bits of cloth, or the like. Thefe, if poffible, o^ight to be extraded, and the wound cleaned, before any dreflings be applied. When that cannot be effeded with fafety, on account of the patient's weaknefs, or lofs of blood, they muft be fuffered to remain in the wound, and afterwards extraded when he is more able to bear it. When a wound penetrates into any of the cavities ofthe body, as the breaft, the bowels, tec. or where any confiderable blood-vef- fel is cut, afkilful furgeon ought immediately to be called, other- wife the patient may lofe his life. But fometimes the difchargeof blood is fo great, that if it be not ftopt, the patient may die even before a furgeon, though at no great diftance, can arrive. In this cafe, fomething muft be done by thofe who are prefent. If the wound be in any of the limbs, the bleeding may generally be ftopt by applying a tight ligature or bandage round the member a little above the wound. The beft method of doing this is to put aftrong broad garter round the part, but fo flack as eafily to adm if a fmall piece of flick to be put under it, which muft be twilled, in thefame manner as a countryman does a cart-rope tofecure his loading, till the bleeding flops. Whenever this is the cafe, he muft take care to twifl it no longer, as ftraining it too much might occafion an in- flammation ofthe parts, and endanger a gangrene. In parts where this bandage cannot be applied, various other me- thods may be tried to flop the bleeding, as the application offtyp- ties, aftringents, Sec. Cloths dipped in a folution of blue vitriol in water,, or the flyptic water of the Difpenfatories, may be applied to the $5* Of WOUNDS. the wound. When thefe cannot be obtained, ftrongfpiriis of wine may be ufed. Some recommend the agarh' of the oak as prefer- able to any ofthe otherflyptics ;and indeed it ckferves confiderable encomiums. It is eafily obtained, and ought to be kept in every family, in cafe of accidents. A piece of it muft be laid upon the wound, and covered Avith a good deal of lint, above Avhich a bandage may- be applied fo tight as to keep it firmly on. Though fpirits, tindures, and hot balfams may be ufed, in order to ftop the bleeding when it is exceffive, they are improper at other times. They do not promote but retard the cure, and often change a fimple AA'ound into an ulcer. People imagine, becaufe hot bal- fams congeal die blood, and feem, as it were, ts fodder up the wound, that they therefore heal it; but this is only a deception. They may indeed ftop the flowing blood,by fearing the mouths ofthe veffels ; but, by rendering the parts callous they obftrud the cure. In flight AA-ounds, which do not penetrate much deeper than the fkin, the beft application is a bit ofthe common black flicking plaf" ter. This keeps the fides of the wound together, and prevents the air from hurting it, which is all that is neceffary. When a wound penetrates deep, it is not fafe to keep its lips quite clofe : this keeps in the matter, and is apt to make the wound fefter. In this cafe die beft way is to fill die wound with foft lint, commonly called caddis- It however muft not be fluffed in too hard, otherwife it will do hurt. The lint may be covered with a cloth dipped in oihor fpread with the common wax plafterf ;and the whole muft be kept on by a proper bandage. We fhall not fpend time in defcribing the different bandages that may be proper for wounds in different parts of the body ; com- mon fenfe will generally foggeft the moft commodious method of applying a bandage ; befide, deferiptions of this kind are not eafily underftood or remembered. The firft dreffing ought to continue on for at leaft two days ; af- ter which it may be removed, and frefh lint applied as before. If any part ofthe firft dreffing flicks, fo clofe as not to be removed with * Br.TofTet, in 'tis " Adv it* to tht peeple," gives the following directions for gathering, preparing, and applying the agaric,—■—" Gather in autumn ," lays he, '; while the fine weather lafts, the agaric of the oak, which is a kind of fungus of txcrelcence ilfuing from the woo J of that tree. It cordifts at firft of four parts, which prelent themfelves iuccelfively ; 1. The outward rind or fkin, which mar be thrown away. 2. The part immediately utieUr this rind which is the beft of alb This is to be beat well with a hammer, Lill it becomes foft and v?ry pliable. This is the only preparation it requires, and a flice of it of a proper fize is to be ?pplicd direftly over theburfting open blood veffels. It conftringes. and brings them c]u* together, flops the bleeding, and generally falls off at the end of two days. 3. 1 he third part adhering to the fecond may ferve to ftop the bleeding from the imaller veffels ; and the fourth and laft part may be reduced to powder as conducing > Hie fame purpofe."—Where the agaric cannot he had, fponge may be ufed in its ftead. It muft be applied in the fame manner, and has nearly the fame effects t See Appendix, Wax-Blaster. Of BURinTS. 353 wfth eafe or fafety to the patient, it may lie allowed to continue, and frefh lint dipped in fweet oil laid over it. This will fofeen it, fo as to make it come off eafily at next drcfir.ig. Afterwards the wound may be dreffed tAviee a-day in the fame manner till ft be quite healed. Thofe avIio are fond of falves or ointments, may,af- ter the wound is become very fuperficial, drefs it Avith the yellow bafilicum* ; and if fungus or Avhat is called proud flefh, ihouid rife in the wound, it may be checked, by mixing with the ointment a litde burnt allum or red precipitate of mercury, When a wound is gready inflamed, the moil proper application is a poultice of bread and milk, foftened Avith a little fweet oil or frefh butter. This muft be applied inftead of a plafter, and fhould be changed twice a-day. If the wound be large, and there is reafon to fear an inflamma- tion, the patient fhould be kept on a very low dkt.He muft abftain from flefh ftrong liquors, and every thing that is of a heating na- ture. If hebeof a full habit, and has loft but little blood from the wound, he muft be bled : and, if the fymptoms be urgent, the ope- ration may be repeated. But when the patient has been greatly, AA-eakened by lofs of blood from the Avound,it will be dangerous to bleed himjeven though a fever ihouid enfue. Nature faould nevei be too far exhaufted. It is always more fafe to allow her to ftruggla Avith the difeafe in her own way, than to fink the patient's ftrength by exceffive evacuations. Wounded perfons might to be kept perfectly quiet and eafy. Every thing that ruffles the mind or moves the palfions,aslQve,anger, fear, exceffive joy, Sec. are very hu'tful. They ought above ali things to abftain fiom vencry. The body fhould be kept gently o- pen, either by laxative clyfters, or by a cool vegetable diet, as roafted apples, fteAved prunes, boiled fpinagc, and fuch ike. CF BURNS. IN flight burns Avhkh do not break the fkin, it is cuftomary to hold the part near the fire for a competent time, to rub it v-itii fait or to lay a comprefs upon it dipped in fpirits of wine Or brandy. But AA'hen the burn has penetrated fo deep as to blifter or break the fkin, it muft be dreffed with fome of the liniment for burns menti- oned in the Appendix,or with the emollientandgently dryingoint- ment, commonly called Turner's cerate j- .Tbis may be mixed with an equal quantity of frefh olive oil, and fpread upon a foft rag,and applied to the part affeded. When this ointment cannot be had, an egg may be beat up with about an equal quantity ofthe fweet- eft falad oil. This will ferve very well till a proper ointment can be prepared. When the burning is very deep, nicer the firft tAA'o or three days, it ihouid be dreffed ivith equal parti of yellow bafdi- eum and Turner's cerate mixed together. When * See Appendix, Ysllov." Easi ticv:'. + £« .' ppcrdi". T«.r - : -?.'s Cisjti. W AV 354 Qi BRUISES. WheYi the burn is vioknt or has occafioned a high degree of in- flammation,and there is reafon to fear a gangrene or mertification, the fame means muft be ufed t^ prevent as arc recommended in othej violent inflammations. The patient in this cafe, muft live low, and drink freely of Aveak dilutiug liquor-. He muft Iikewife be bled, and have his body kept open. But if the burnt parts fhould become livid or black,Avith other fymptoms of mortification, it will be neceffary to bathe them frequently Avithwarm camphorated fpir- its of AA-ine tindure of myrrh, or other antifceptics, mixed with a decodion of the bark. In this cafe the bark muft Iikewife be tak- en internally, and the patient's diet muft be more generous, widi wine, &e. As example teaches better than precept, I fhall relate the treat- ment ofthe moft dreadful cafe of this kind that has occured in my pradice A middle aged man of a good conftitution, fell into a large veffel foil of boiling water, and miferably fcaklcd about one half of his body. As his clothes ivere on, the burning in fome parts Avas very deep before they could be got off. For die firft two days the fcalded parts had been frequently anointed with a mixture of lime-Avaterandoil,Avhich is a veryproper application for recent burn- ings. On the third day, when I firft faw him, his fever was high, and his body coftive, for which he vams bled, and had an emollient clyfter adminiftered. Poultices of bread and milk, foftened wilit freih butter, were Iikewife applied to the affeded parts, to abate die heat and inflammation. Hisfever,ftill continuing high,he was bled a fecond time, was kept ftridly on the cooling regimen, took the faline mixture with fmall dofes of nitre, and had an emollient clyfter adminiftered once a day. When the inflammation began to abate, the parts were dreffed Avith a digeftive compofed of broAvn cerate and yelloAv bafilicum. Where any black fpots appeared,they were flightly fcarified, and touched with the tindure of myrrh,and to prevent their fpreading, the Peruvian bark Avas adminiftered. By this rnurfe, the man was fo well in three Aveeks as to be able t# attend his bufinefs. QF BRUISES. BRUISES are generally produdive of Avorfe confequences than wounds. The danger from them does nor appear immedi»tely,by which means it often happens that they are negleded. It is need- kfs to give any definition of a difeafe fo univerfally known ; avj fhall therefore proceed to point out the method of treating it. In flight bruifes it will be fufficient to bathe the part with warm vinegar, to which a little brandy or rum may occafionally be added, and to keep cloths Avet with this mixture conftantly applied to it. This is more proper than rubbing it with brandy, fpirits of wine, or other ardent fp r'ts',which are commonly ufed in fuch cafes. In fome parts of the country thepeafantsapply to a recent bruife acataplafm of fiefh.eew-dang. I have often feen th.k caiaplaim •qvVed Or ULCER§. tS5 applied to violent contusions occafioned by blows, falls,,bruifes,and fuch like, and never knew it fail to have a good effed. When a bruife is very violent, the patient ought immediately to be bled, and put upon a proper regimen. His food fliould be light and cool, and his drink Aveak and of an opening nature; as whey fweetened Avith honey, decodions of tamarinds, barley, tream-tai - tar Avhey, and fuch like. The bruifed part muft be bathed with vi- negar and \A"atcr, as direded above; and a poultice made by boil- ing crumbs of bread, elder-flowers, and camomile flowers,fjn equal quantities of vinegar and water, applied to it. This poultice is pe- culiarly proper when a wound is joined to the bruife. It may be re- newed two or three times a-day. As the ftrudurc of the veffels is totally deftroyed by a violent bruife, there often enfoes a great lofs ot fubftance, which produces an ulcerous fore very difficult to cure. If the bone be affeded, the fore will not heal before an exfoliation takes place; that is, before the difeafed part ofthe bone feparates, and comes out through the wound. This is often a very flow operation, and may even require feveral years to be completed. Hence it happens, diat thefe lores are frequently miftaken for the king's evil, and treated as fuch though in fad they proceed folely from the injury which the folid parts received from die blow. Patients in this fituation are peflcrcd with different advices. Every one who fees them propofes a new remedy, till the fore is much irritated A>.ith various and oppofite applications, that it is of- ten at length rendered abfolutely incurable. The beft method of managing fuch fores is, to take care that the patient's conftitution does not fuffer by confinement or "improper medicine, and to apply nothing to them befides fimple ointment fpread upon foft lint, over which a poultice of bread and milk, with boiled camomile flowers, or the like, may be put to nourifh the part, and keep it foft and warm. Nature, thus affifted, will generally in time opeiate a cure, by throwing off the difeafed parts ofthe bone, after which the fore foon heals. OF ULCERS. ULCERS may be the confequence of wounds, bruifes, or im- pofthumes improperly treated ; they may Iikewife proceed hern an ill ftate of the humours, or what may be called a bad habit of bo- dy. In the latter cafe they ought not to be haftily dried-up, otherwife it may prove fatal to the patient, Ulcers happen moft commonly in the decline of life ; and perfons who negled exercife, and live grofsly, are moft liable to them. They might oft ..a be pi evented by retrenching fome part of the folid food, or by openkig artificial drains, as iffues, fetons or the like. An ulcer may be diftinguifhed from a wound by its difeharging a Am watery humour, which is often fo acrid as to inflame and cor- rode 35* Or ULCERS. rode the fkin; by the .bardnefs and perpendicular fituation of it* fides or edges; by the time of its duration, ike. It requires confiderable fkill to be able to judge whether or not an ulcer ought to be dried up. In general, all ulcers which proceed from a bad habit of die body, fhould be fuffered to continue open, af leaft till the conftitution has been so for changed by proper regimen, or the ufe of medicine, that they feem diipofed to heal of their own accord. Ulcers which are the effed of malignant fevers, or ether acute difeafes, may generally be healed with fafety after die health has beenreilored for fome time. The cure ought not howe- ver to be attempted too foon, or at any time without the ufe of purging medicines and a proper regimen. When wounds or bruifes have, by AA-rong treatment, degenerated into ulcers if the conftitu- tion be good, they may generally be healed Avttii fafety. When ul- cers either accompany chronical difeafes,or come in their ftead,they muft be cautioufly healed. If an ulcer conduces to the patkr.i's, health, from whatever caufe it proceeds, it ought not to be healed ; but if, on the contrary, it waftes the ftrength, and confume's the pa- tient by a flow fever, it fhould be healed as foon as poffible. We would earneflly recommend a ftrid attention to thefe parti- culars to all who have the misfortune to labour under this diforder, particularly perfons in the decline of life; as we have frequently known people throw away their lives by the Avant cf it, while they were extolling and generoufly rewarding thofe whom they ought to have looked upon as their executioners. The moft proper regimen for promoting the cure of ulcers, is to avoid all fpices,faked and high feafoned food, all ftrong liquors,and to leffen the ufual quantity of flefh meat. The body ought to be kept gently open by a diet confiding chiefly of cooling laxative ve- getables, and by drinking butter-milk, whey fweetened with honey, or the like. The patient ought to be kept cheerful, and fhould take as much exercife as he can eafily bear. When the bottom and fides of an ulcer feem hard and callous, they may be fprinkled twice a-day with a little red precipitate of mercury, and afterwards dreffed with the yellow bafdicum ointment. Sometimes it will be neceffary to have the edges of the ulcer fcari- fied with the lancet. Lime-water has frequendy been known to have very happy effeds in the cure of obftinate ulcers. It may'be ufed in the fame manner as dkeded for the ftone and gravel. My late learned and ingenious friend Dr. Whitt ftrongly recom- mends the ufe the folution of corrofive fublimate of mercury in brandy, for the cure of obftinate ill-conditioned ulcers. I have fre- quently found this medicine, when given according to the Dodor's diredions, prove very fuccefsful. The dofe is a table-fpoonful night and morning ; at the fame time wafliing the fore twice or thrice a-day with it. In a letter which I had fiom the Dodor a liu tie before his death, he iufoirr.ed me, « That he obferved wafliing Of DISLOCATIONS. 357 the fore thrice a-day with the folution of a triple ftrength was very beneficial*" A fiftulcus uker can feldom be cured" without an operation It muft either be laid open fo as to have its callous parts deftroyed by fome corrofive application, or they muft be entirely cut aA\ay by the knife ; but as this operation requires the hand of an expert fur- geon, there is no occafion to defcribe it. Ulcers about the anus arc moft apt to become fillulous, and are very difficult to cure. Seme indeed pietend to have found Ward's fiftula paftc very fuccekful in this complaint. It is not a dangerous medicine, and being ei.fi- ly procured, it may deferve atrial ; but as thefe ulcers generally proceed from an ill habit of body, they will feldom yield to any thing except a long courfe of regimen, affifted by medicines which are calculated to corred that particular habit, and to induce an-at- moft total change in the conftitution. CHAP. LL OF DISLOCATIONS. HEN a bone is moved out of its place or articulation, fo as to impede its proper fundions, it is faid to be luxated ox J'flocated. As this often happens to perfons in fituations where no medical afliftance can be obtained, by which means limbs,and even Jives, are frequently loft, we fliall endeavour to point out the meth- od of reducing the moft common luxations, and thofe which re- quire immediate afliftance. Any perfon cf common ienie and ref- olution, who is prefent when a drflocation happens, may often be of .more fervice to the patient, than the moft expert furgeon can after the fwelling and inflammation have come on. When thefe are prefent, itis diflicult to know die ftate of die joint, and danger- « ous to attempt a redudion ; and by waiting till they are gone off^ the mufcles become fo relaxed, and the cavity filled up, that the bone cannever afterAvardsbe retained in its place. A recent diflocation may generally be reduced by extenfioa alone, which muft always be greater or lefs according to the ftrength of the mufcles which move the joint, the age, robuftnefs, «nd:ather circumftances ofthe patient When the bone has been out of its plaee.for a confidarable time, and a fwelling or ii.flam- ■mation has come on, it will be neceflary to bleed the patient, and, afteribmentirrg the part, to apply foft poultices with vinegar to it ■for fome time before the redudion is attempted. AH *Innlce*softhe'lower limbs greatebenefitris often received from tight rollejjj or weariryj j laced flocking, asthis prevents the iiu-'i. of humour* to the lares, and difpofes fhwn. to-hejd, w 35* DISLOCATION o» thi JAW. All that is neceffary after the redudion, is to apply cloths dipt ia yinegar or camphorated fpirits of wine to the part, and to keep it perfedly eafy. Many bad'eonfequences proceed from the negled of this rule. A diflocation feldom happens without the tendons and ligaments ofthe joint being ftretched and fome times torn. When fchefe are kept eafy till they recover dieir ftrengtl^and tone, all goes on very well; but if the injury be increafed by too frequent an ex- ertion of the parts, no wonder if they be found Aveak and difeafed cver after. DISLOCATION OFTHE JAW. THE lower jaw may be luxated by yawning, blows, fall, cheAv- ing hard fubftances, or the like. It is eafily known from the pa- rent's being unable to fhut his mouth, or to eat any thing, as the teeth of the under jaw do not correfpond with thofe ofthe upper; befides, the chin either hangs doAvn, or is thrown towards one fide, and the patient is neither able to fpeak diftindly, nor to fAvalloAV widiout confiderable difficulty. The ufual method of reducing a diflocated jaw is to fet the pa- tient upon a low ftool, fo as an affiftant may hold the head firm by prefling it againft his breaft. The operator is then to thruft hi* two thumbs, being firft ivrapped up with linen cloths that they may not flip, as far back into the patient'smouth as he can, while his fingers are applied to the jaAV externally. . After he has got firm holdof thejaw, he is to prefs it ftrongly downAvards and backwards, by which means the elapfed heads ofthe jaw may be eafily pufhed into their former cavities. The peafants in fome parts of the country have a peculiar way of performing this operation. One of them puts a handkerchief under the patient's chin, then turning his back to that of the patient, pulls him up by die chin fo as to fufpend him from the ground. This method often fucceeds, but we think it a dangerous one, and there- fore recommend the former. DISLOCATION OFTHE NECK. THE neck may be diflocated by falls, violent blows, or the like. In this cafe, if the patient receives no afliftance; he foon dies, which makes people imagine the neck was broken ; it is however, for the moft part only partially diflocated, and may be reduced by almoft any perfon who kas refolutiou enough to attempt it. A complete diflocation of the neck is inftantaneous death. When the neck is diflocated, the patient is immediately deprived of all fenfe and motion ; his neck fwells, his countenance appears bleated; his chin lies upon his breaft, and his face is generally turn- ed towards one fide. To DISLOCATION of the RIBS. 359 To reduce this diflocation, the unhappy perfon fliould immedi- ately be laid upon his back on the ground, and the operator muft place himfelf behimd him fo as to be able to lay hold of his head with both hands, while he makes a refiftance by placing his knees againft the patient's fhoulders. In this pofture he muft pull the head with confiderable force, gently tAvifting it at the fame time, if the face be turned to one fide, till he perceives that the joint is replac- ed, Avhich may be knoAvn from the noife which the bones generally make Avhen going in, the patient's beginning to breathe, and the head continuing in its natural pofture. This is one of thofe operations Avhich it is more eafy to perform than defcribe. I have known inftances of its being happily per- formed even by women,and often by men of no medical education. After the neck is reduced, the patient ought to be bled, and fhould be fuffered to reft for fome days, till the parts recover their proper ■»r.e. --TV?. ."^SSB-^i-- DISLOCATION OFTHE RtBS. As the articulation of the ribs Avith the back bone is very ftrong they are not often diflocated. It does, howrever, fometimes happen, which is a fufficient reafon for our taking notice of it. When a rib is diflocated either upwards or downwards in order to replace it, the patient fhould be laid upon his belly on a table, and the operator muft endeavour to pufh the head of the bone into its proper pdace* Should this method not fucceed, the arm of the difordered tide may be fufpended over a gate or ladder, and, while the ribs are thus ftretched afunder, the heads of fuch as are out of place maybe thruft into their former fituation. Thofe diflocations wherein the heads of the ribs are forced in- wards, are both more dangerous and moft difficult to reduce, as neither the hand noranyinftrumentcan be applied internally to di- rect die luxated heads of the ribs. Almoft the only thing that can be done is, to lay the patient upon his belly over a cafk, or fome gibbous body, and to move the fore part of the rib inward towards the back fometimes fhaking it; by this means the heads of the lux- ated ribs may flip into their former place. DISLOCATION OF THE SHOULDER. THE humerus or upper bone ofthe arm may be diflocated in va- rious dircdions: it happens however moft frequently doAvnAvartk, but very feldom diredly upAvards. From the nature of its articula- tion, as Avell as fiom its expofure to external injuries, this bone is the moft fobjed to diflocation of any iu the body. A diflocatiou ofthe humerus may be knoAvn by a depreflion or cavity on thetop oi the ihoukkr, and an inability to move the arm. When the dii- loc ition is doAvuward or fonvard, the arm is elongated,and a bail or lump *&» DISLOCATION or thk ELBOW. lump i« pcvceivcdunderthe arm-pit: but Arhen it is backward, there appears a rrror.ubera-.ice behind rhe fhoulder, and th. arm is thrown forward towards the breaft. The ufual method of reducing diffokitions ofthe fhoulder is to feat the patient upon a low ftool, and to caufe an aflklant to hold his body fo that it may not give way to the extenfion, while another lays hold of the arm a little above the elbow,and gradually extends it. The operator then puts a napkin under the patient's arm, a kJ caufes it to be tied behind his own neck ; by this, while a fniflcient extenfion is made, he lifts up the head ofthe bone, and with his hands direds it into ks proper place. There are various machine; invented for facilitating this operation, bat the hand of an expert furgeon is always more fafe. In young and delicate patients, 1 hai^e generally found it a very eafy matter to reduce the ihoulder, by ex- tending the arm with one hand, and tilrufting in the head of the boneAvith the other. In making the extention, the arm ought alwa ." bo be a little bent. DISLOCATION OF THE ELBOW, THE bones ofthe fore-arm may be diflocated' in any diredkm. When this is the cafe, a protuberance may be obferved on that fide of the arm toAvards which the bone is pufhed, from which, and the patient's inability to bend his arm, a diflocation of this joint may eafily be known. Two affzftants are generally neceffary for reducing a diflocation of the elbow; one of them muft lay hold ofthe arm above, and the other below the joint, and make a prety ftrong extention, Avhile the operator returns the bones into their proper place. Afterwards die arm muft be bent, and fufpended for fome time with a fling about the neck. Luxations ofthe Avrift and fingers are to be reduced in the fame manner as thofe of the elboAV; viz. by making an extenfion in dif- ferent diredions, and thralling the head ofthe bone into its place. DISLOCATION OFTHE THIGH. WHEN the thigh bone is diflocated forward and downward, th-: Jcnce and foot are turned out and the leg is longer than the other; but when it is drfpiaced backward, it is ufually pufhed upward at the fame time, by which means the limb is fliortened, and the foot is turned inwards. When the thigh banc is difohaced forward and downward, the patient in order to have it reduced, muft be laid upon his back and made fall by bandage:, or held by affiftants, while by others an ex- tenfion is mile by means of flings fixed aboutths bottom of the thigh a httle above the knee. While the extention is made, tiie operat< t muft Of BROKEN BONES, Sec. $fa mufi pu'--» the head ofthe bone outward, till it gets into the fbcket. if the diflocation be outward, the patient muft be laid upon his Face, and, during the extention,the head of the bone mull be pufh- ed inward. Diflocations ofthe knees, ancles, and toes are reduced much in the fame manner as thofe ofthe upper extremities, viz. by making an extenfion iu oppofite diredion-., while the operator replaces the bones. In many cale.i, however, the extenfion alone k fuflicient and the bone wiii ihp into its place merely by puiiing the limb with fufficient force. It is not hereby meant, that iorce alone is iuffle'ent for, the redudion of diflocatiorts. bkill and adtirck wiii often fucceed better than force. 1 have »aiov>n a diflocation of the thigh reduced by one man, after all the force that couia be ufed by fix had proved ineileetu.il. ri-IAP. LI I. OF BROKEN BOxYES.&c. THERE is in moft villages, fome perfon who pretends to the art of reducing fradurcs. Though in general fuch perfons -are very ignoraur, yet fome' of them are very fuccefsfui ; which ev- idently proves, th it a fmail degree of learning, with a fufficient fhare of common fenfe and a mechanical head, will enable a man to be ufeful in his way. We would, however, advife people never to employ fuch operators, Avfien an expert and fkilif il furgeon can be had ; but when that is impracticable, they muft be employed : we fhall therefore recommend the following hints fo their consid- eration : When a large bone is broken, the patient's diet ought m all ref- peds to be the l.itne as in an inikmniatory fevei. He fhould ..kev/ife be kept quiet and cool, and his bodyaipen by emoiiient clyfters ; or, if thefe cannot be convenient1)' adminiftered, by food tiiat is of an opening quality ; as ile reed prunes, apples boiled in milk, boiled fpinage, and the like. It ought IioAAever to be here remarked, tiiat perfons who have been accuftomed to live high, are not all of a fudden to be reduced to a very low diet. This might have fatal ededs.
