There is often a neceffity f >r hid.iking even bad hab t,, in fome meaii-.iv, where she nature of the difeafe m;egit require a different treatment. It wiii generally be neceffary to bleed the patient immediately £fter a fradure, efpeciaily it he be young, of a fo.l habit, or has ac the fame time received any bruife or contufion. This operation ihouid rut only be perfoi ared fooi after the accident .:;g;pens, but if the patient be very feveriih, it may be re^eaeed next day. When feveral ofthe ribs are broken, bieedkg is peculiarly necefiary. If any of the large bones which fupport the body-are broken,the patient mud kee;> Ins bed for fever.ii weeks. It is by no means nec- effary X * Hf32 Of BROKEN BONES, Sec. e.fury, however that he fhould lie all that time, as is cuflomary, upon hk back. This fituation finks the fpirits, galls and frets the patientk firin, and render*, him very uneafy. After the fecond week lie may be gently railed up, and may fit feveral hours, fup- ported by a bed chair, or the like, which will greatly relieve him. Great care, fKrwevcr, muft be taken in raifing him up and laying him down, that he make no exertions himfelf, otherwife the adion of th? mufcles may pull the bone out of its place* It is of great importance to keep the patient dry and clean while in this fituation. By negleding this, he is often fo galled and ex- coriated, that he is forced to keep fhifting places for eafei I have knoAA-n n fradured thigh bene, after it had been kept ftraight for above a fortnight, drfplaced by this means, and continue bent for life, in fpite of all that could be done. It has been cuftomary when a bone was broken, to keep the limb for five or fix weeks continually upon the ftretch. But this is a bad pofture. It is both uneafy to the patient, and unfavourable to the cure. The bed fituation is to keep the limb a little bent. This is the pofture into which every animal puts its limbs when it goes to reft, and in which feweft are upon the ftretch. It is eafily effed- ed, by either laying the patie«t upon his iide, or making the bed fo as to favour this pofition of the limb. Bone-fetters ought carefully to examine ■whether the bofie is not fhattered or broken into feveral pieces. In diis cafe it wiii fome- times be neceffary to have the limb immediately taken off, other- wife a gangrene or mortification may enfue. The horror which attends the very idea of an amputation, often occafions its being delayed in fuch cafes till too late. I have known this principle operate fo ftrongly, that a limb, where the bones were fhattered into more than tAventy pieces, was not amputated before the third day after the accident, when the gangrene had proceeded fo far as to render the operation ufelefs. When a fradure is accompanied with a wound, it muft be dref- fed in all refpeds as a common wound. All that art can do towards the cure of a broken bone, is to lay it perfedly ftraight, and to keep it quite eafy. All tight bandages do hurt. They had much better be wanting altogether. A great many of the bad confequences which fucceed to fradurcd bones are OA''ingto tight bandages. This k one of the ways in which excefs e'"arr, or rather the abi.fe of it, does more mifchiei than w uld be occafioned by the want of it. Some ofthe moft fudden cures of broken bones which were ever known, happened Avherc i;. li.mda- i ■ * * Various pieces of machinery have been contrived for counteracting th-.- ffoceof the mufcles. ind retaining fragments of brek.cn bone? ; but as dilciiptious of thdc without i ravines wouTd be of little ufe, I fhall refei the render to a cluao aii'J . r.fefal performance " on the nature and cure of fractures," la'e'.y publiiu:l 1 y ?n inp-enio'is fvie id Mr. Aitken, furgeon in Edinburgh ; wherein trr1 w-ntlemai l,i: not only p.-ven an account cf the machines recommended i-i fractures byl.MiK-r I'j'^-firs, bis has Iikewife added feveral improvements of hii own, which arc -,-ere,. liarlv ufefri in compound rraf+<"--; and in ca'es where patierts \v;'i 1>* " '- bei.-* ?.-. o'r>.v-d to b« tranfpoiUfu i.um jne place to >n.»tli-i- Ot STRAIN*. 3^3 ges were applied at all. Some method however muft be taken to keep the member Heady; but this may be done many ways without bracing it with a tight bandage. The beft method of retention is by tAA'o or more fplints made of leather or pafteboard. Thefe, if mciftened before they be applied, foon affume the fhape of the included member, and are fuflicient, by the afliftance of a very flight bandage, for all the purpofes of retention. The bandage which wc would recommend, is diat made with twelve or eighteen tails. It is much eafier applied and taken off than rollers, -and anfwers all the purpofes of retention equally well. The fplints fliould always be as long a3 the limb, with holes cut for the ancles when the fradure is in the leg. In fradures of the ribs,Avhere a bandage cannot bo properly ufed, an adhefive plafter may be applied over the p:v t, The patient in this cafe ought to keep himfelf quite eafy, avoidingevery thing that may occafion fneezing, laughing, coughing, or the like. He ought to keep his body in a ftraight pofture, and fliould take care that his ftomach be conftantly diftended, by taking frequently fome light food aad drinking freely of weak watery liquors. The moft proper external application for a fradure is cxycrate or a mixture of vinegar and water. The bandages fhould be Avet with this at every dreffing. OF STRAINS. STRAINS are often attended with Avorfe confequences than bro- ken bones. The reafon is obvious; they are generally negled,d. When a bone is broken, the patient is obliged to keep the member eafy, becaufe he cannot make ufe of it; but when a joint is only ftrained, die pei fon finds he can ftill make a drift to move it, is forry to lofe his time for fo trifling an ailment. In this way he' de- ceives himfelf, and coin'erts into an incurable malady Avhat might have been remolded by only keeping the part eafy for a icav days. ' Country people generally immerfe a ftrained limb in cold Avater. This is very proper provided it be done immediately, andnot kept in too long. But the cuftom of keeping the part rmmerfed in cold water for a long time is certainly dangerous. It relaxes inftead of bracing the part, and is'rnorc likely to produce a difeafe than re- move one. Wrapping a garter, or fome other bandage, pretty tight about the ftrained part, is Iikewife of ufe. It helps to reftore the proper tone ofthe veffels, and prevents the adion of the parts from increas- ing the difeafe. It fhould not however be applied too tight. I have frequently known bleeding near the affeded part have a very good effed; hut Avhat we would recommend above all is eafe. It is moi : to 364 Of RUPTURES. to be depended on than any medicine, and feldom fails to remove the complaint*. OF RUPTURES.. CHILDREN and old people are moft liable to this difeafe. U the former it is generally occafioned by exceffive crying, cough- ing, vomiting o>- the like. In the latter it is commonly the effect, of blows or violent exertions of the ftrength, as leeping, carrying great weights, &c. In both a related habit, indolence and an oily or very moift diet, difpofes the body to this difeafe. A rupter fometimes proves fatal before it is difcovered. When- ever fickne'srvomiting, and obftinate coftivenefs gives reafon to fuf. ped an obftrudion of the boAA-ek, ail thofe place? where ruptuies ufually happen, ou'fit carefully to be examined. The pro'.ulion cf a very fmall part of die gut will occafion all thefe fymptoms; and, it not returned in due time, will prove fatal. On the firft appearance of a rupture in an infant, it ought to be laid upon its back, with its head very low. While in this pofture, if the gut docs not return of kielf, it may eafily be put up by gen- tle preffure. After it is returned a piece of tticking-plaftu may be applied over the part, and a proper trufs or bandage mull be con- ftantly worn for a confiderable time. The method of making and applying thefe rupture bandages for children is pretty well known. The child muft, as far as poflibk, be kept from cr; ing and tiosa ail violent exertions, till the rupture is quite healed. In adults, when the gut has been forced down with great violence, or happens from any caufe to be inflamed, there is often great diffi- culty in returning it, and fometimes the thing is quite impradicable without an operation ; a defcription of Avhich is foreign to our pur- pofe. As I have been fortunate enough, however, ahvays to fuc- ceed in my attempts to return the gut, without having recourfe to any other means than what are in the power of every man, I lhall briefly mention the method which I generally purfue. After the patient has been bled, he muft be laid upon his back, with his head very low, and his breech railed high with pillows. In this fituation flannel-ehoths wrung out of a decodion of mallows and camomile- flowers, or, if thefe are not at hand, of Avarm water, muft be applied for a coniideiable time. A clyfter made of this decodion, with a large fpooniul of butter and an ounce or two of fak, may be afterwards thrown up. If thefe ihouid not prove fuc- cefsful, recourfe muft be had to preffure. If the tumour be very hard, confiderable force will be neceflary; but it is not force alone which *A great many external applications are recommended for fbains. fome of ■which do good and others hurt. The following are fuch as may be ufed with the greateft lafty.viz. poultices made of ftale beer or \inegar and oat-mtal, camphorat- ed fpiiits of wine, Mindererus' fpirits, volatile liniment, volatile aromatic tpiri: diluted with a double quantity of water, and the common ioiuc:it_tior., •■•/iih <r.i sedition of brandy or fpirit of wine. Qv CASUALTIES. 3% which fucceeds here. The operator at the fame time that he makes a preffure with the palms of his hands, muft with his fingers artful- ly condud the gut in by the fame aperture through which it came out. The manner cf doing this can be much eafier conceived than deferibed. Should thefe endeavours prove inefledual, clyfters of the fmoke of tobacco may be tried. Thefe have been cfun known to fueceed where every other method failed. There is reafon to believe that, by perfiftkg in the ufe of thefe, and iuch other means as the circumftances of the cafe may fuggeft, moft hernias nftght be reduced without an operation. Cutting for the hernia is a nice and difficult matter. I would therefore advife furgeons to try every method of returning the gut before they have recourfe to the knife. I have once and again fucceeded by perfe- vering in my endeavours, after eminent furgeons have declared the redudion of the gut impradicable Avitfiout an operation*. An adult, after the gut has been returned muft wear a fteel bandage. It is needkfs to deferibe this, as it may always be had ready-made from the artiils. Such bandages are generally uneafy to the wearer for fometinie, but by cuftom they become quite eafy. No perfon who has had a rupture after he arrived at man's eftate ihouid ever be without one of thofe bandages. Perfons who have a rupture ought carefully to avoid all violent exercife, carrying great Aveights, leaping, running and the like. They ihouid likeAvife avoid Avindy aliment and ftrong liquors j and Ihouid carefully guard againlt catching cold. . CHAP. LIU. OF CASUALTIES. JT is certain that life, when to all appearance loft, may often, by due care, be reilored. Accidents frequently prove fatahmere- becaufe proper means are not ufed to counterad their effeds. No perfon ought to be looked upon as killed by any accidentun- lefs Avhere the ilructure of the heart, brain, or fome organ neceffary to life, is evidently deftroyed. The adkn of thefe organs may be fo far impaired as even to be for iome time imperceptible, when life is by no means gone. In this cafe, hoAvever, if the fluids be fuffered to groAv cold, it will be impoffible to put them again in mo- tion, even though the folids fhould recover their power of ading. Thus, Avhen the motion of the lungshasbeen flopt by unwholefome vapour * I would here beg leave to recommend it to every practitioner, when his patient complains of pain iu the belly with obflii,ate coftivenet., to examine the groins and f,<iy place where a rupture may happen, in order that it may be r imcdiately re- duced. J3v i.e-Je&ing this, many pciifh who were not lulpecied to have had rup- luivs till after they were dead. I have iuiowti this happen where half a do^en of die {acuity were i.: attendance. 366 SUBSTANCES stopt BETWEEN vapour, die adion ofthe heart by a ftroke on thebreaft,or the func- tions ot the brain by a blow on the head, if the perfon be fuffered to grow cold, he will in all probability continue fo; but, if the body be kept warm, as loon as the injured part ha» recovered its power of ading, the flu;ds will again begin to move, and all the vital func- tions will be reftoied. It is a horrid cuftom immediately to confign over to death every perfon who has the misfortune, by a fall, a blow, or the like, to be deprived ofthe appearance of life. The unhappy perfon inftead of being carried into a warm houfe, and hud by the fire, or put to a warm bed, is generally hurried away to a church, or a barn, or fome other cold damp houfe, where, after afruidefs.attem.pt lias been made to bleed him, perhaps by one who knew nothing of the matter,!1' is given over for dead, and no further notice taken of him. This condud feems to be the refult of ignorance, fupported by an ancient fuperftitious notion, which forbids the body of any perfoti killed by accident to be laid in an houfe that is inhabited. What the ground of this fuperftition may be, we fhall not pretend to inquire; but furely the condud founded upon it isconttary to all the principles of reafon, humanity, and common fenfe. When a perfon feems to be fuddenly deprived of life, our firft bufinefs is to inquire into the caufe. We ought carefully to obferve whether any fubftance be lodged in the windpipe or gullet; and if that is the cafe, attempts muft be made to remove it. When un- wholefome air is the caufe, the patient ought immediately ;o be re- moved out of it. If die circulation be fuddenly ftopped, from any caufe whatever, except mere weaknefs, the patient fhould be bled. If the blood does not flow, he may be immerfed in warm water, or rubbed with warm cloths, &c. to promote the circulation. When the caufe cannot be fuddenly removed, our great aim muft be to keep up the vital Avarmth, by rubbing the patient with hot cloths, or fait, and covering his body Avith Avarm fand, afhes or the like. I ihouid now proceed to treat more fully of thofe accidents, which without immediate afliftance, would often prove fatal, and to point out the moft likely means for relieving the unhappy fufferers; but as I have been happily anticipated in this part of my fubjed by the learned and humane Dr. Tiffot, I fhall content myfelf with feled- jng fuch of his obfervations as feem to be the moft important, and adding fuch of my own as have occurred in the courfe of prac- tice. OF SUBSTANCES STOPT BETWEEN THE MOUTH AND STOMACH. THOUGH accidents of this kind are very common, and ex- tremely dangerous, yet they are generally the effed of careleffnefs. Children fhould be taught to chew their food well and to put no- thing into their mouths which it would be dangerous for them to fwal- low. But children are not the only perfoiis guilty of this piece of imprudence the MOUTH and STOMACH. 3*7 •mprudence. I know many adults who put pins, nails, and other fharp pointed fubftances in their mouths upon every occafion, and fome who ever: fieep with the former there all night. This condud is exceedingly injudicious, as a fit of coughing, or twenty other ac- cidents, may force over the fubftance before the perfon is aware'*. When any fubftance is detained in the gullet,thereare tAvo ways of removng it, viz. either by extrading it or pufhirg it down. The fafeft and moft certain way is to extrad it; but this is not al- ways the eafieft ; it may therefore be more eligible fometimes to diruft it down, efpecially when the obftruding body is of iuch ana-» ture, that there is no danger from its reception into the lkr?.vh. The fubftances which may be pufhed down without danger are,ail common nourifliing ones, as bread, flefh, fruits, and the like. All indigeftible bodies, as cork, wood, bones, pieces of metal, and fuch like, ought if pofiible to be extraded, efpecially if tbefe bodies be! lharp pointed, as pins, needles fifh-bones, bits of glafs, &c. When fuch fubftances have not paffed in too deep, we fhould endeavour to extrad them with our fingers, which method often fucceeds. When they are loAver we muft make ufe of nippers, or a fmall pair of forceps, fuch as furgeons ufe. But this attempt to extradrarely fucceeds,if the fubflance be of a flexible nature, and has defcended far into the gullet. If the fingers and nippers fail, or cannot be duly applicd,crotch- cts a kind of hooks, muft be employed. Thefe may be made at once, by bending a piece of pretty ftrong iron wire at one end. It muft be introduced in the flat way ; and for the better conduding it, there fliould Iikewife be a curve or bending at the end it is held by, to ferve as a kind of handle to it; which has this further ufe,thaf it may be fecured by a firing tied to it, a circumftance not to be omitted in any inllrument employed on fuch occafions,to aAroidfuclt 111 accidents as have fometimes enfued from thefe inftruments flip- ping out of the operater's hand. After the crotchet has paffed be- low the fubftance that obftruds the paffage, it is drawn up again., and hooks up the body along Avith it. The crotchet is alfo very conArenient, Avhen a fubflance fomewhat flexible,asa pin orfifh-bone flicks acrofs the gullet, the hook, in fuch cafes, feizing them about their middle part crooks and thus difengages them ; or if they arc very brittle fubftances, fetA'es to break them When die obftruding bodies are fmall, and only flop up a parr ofthe paffage, and Avhich may either eafily elude the hook,or ftrait- ten it by their refifl.tnce, a kind of rings, made either of wire, wool, or filk, may be ufed. A piece of fine wire of a proper length mav be bent into a circle, bout the middle, of about an inch diameter, and the long unbent fides brought parallel, and near each other ; thefe are to be held .n the hand, and the circular part or ring intro- duced into the gullet, in order to be conduded about the obftrud- ing body, and !o to extrad it. More flexible ring.-, may be made * A woman i.i o,..- of ibe hafpi'.-ls of tins city lately difeharged a great nuin'x ; •>»' ph.s, wh .m ihe h.i,i fwallowd ■« the courfe of her bu-Snefs, through v.t i.1.-.eri«- her fid*. $61 SUBSTANCES stopt BETWEEN of wool, thread, filk, or fmall pick-tiue.id, which mag bo waxei for their greater ftrength and confole.ici*. One of thefe is to be tied foft to a handle ofipm Avirc, %vhak-bone, or any kind <»f flexi- ble Avood, and by this means introduced, in order to furround the obtruding fubilartce, and to draAV it out. Several of thefe rings paffed through one another may be ufed, the more certainly to lay hold of the obftruding body, which may be involve! by one, if another fhould mils it. Thefe rings have one advantage, which is, that Avhen the fubftance to be extraded is «nce laid hold of, it m.i\ then by turning the handle, be retained fo ftrongly in the rng thus twifted, as to be moved every way, wnicli muft in many cafes be a confiderable advantage. Another material employed on thefe unhappy occafions, is the fponge. Its property of fwelling confiderably on being wet is the principal foundation of its ufefulnefs here. If any fubftance is ftopt in the gullet, but AArithout filling up the whole paffage, a bit of fponge may be introduced into that part which is unflopt, and beyond the fubftance. The fponge foon dilates, and grows larger in this moift fituation, and indeed the enlargement of it may be forwarded by making the patient fwallow a few drops of water. Afterwards it is to be drawn back by the handle to which it is fas- tened ; and as it is nOAV too large to return through the fmall cavi- ty by which itwas conveyed in, it draws out the obftruding body a- lone; with it. The compreflibility of fponge is another foundation of its ufeful- nefs in fuch cafes. A pretty large piece of fponge may be com- preffed or fqueezed into a fundi fize, by winding a firing of tape rlofely about it, Avhich maybe eafily unwound,and Avithdrawn,after the fponge has been introduced. A bit of fponge may Iikewife be compreffed by a piece of whale-bone fplit at one end ; but this can hardly be introduced in fuch a manner as not to hurt the patient. I have often known pins and other fharp bodies, which had ft tick *n the throat, brought up by caufing the perfon to fwallow a bit of tough meat tied to a thread, and drawing it quickly uplngain. This is infer than fwallowing iponge, and will often anfwer the purpofe equally well. When all thefe methods prove unfucccfsfol there rem:7ns one more, Avhich is, to make the patient vomit : but this can ic.ocek be any fervice, unlefs when fuch obftruding bodies are limply en- ;<; tge.I in, and not ho.ikel or iluck into the fides ofthe gulkt,as in this cafe vomiting might fometimes occafion further mifchief. If the patient can fwallow, vomiting m.iv be excited by taking Ivilf a drachm or two fontples of ipecacuanha in powder nude inio a draught. If he <s not able to fwallow, an attempt may be made to excite vomiting, by tickling his thi -y.it with a feather ; and if that fliould not fucceed, a clyfter of tob.-.cco may be adminillered. It is made by boiling a:i ounce of tobacco in a fufficient quantity of- water ; thk ha* of ecu. been found to fucceed, when other attempts to excice vomiting had failed. Whea the obftruding body is of fuch a nature that it may with i aleiy be pufhed downwards, this may be attempted by nie.ms of a wax- the MOUTH aud STOMACH. ^j wai-candle oiled, and a little heated, fo as to make it flexible; or a piece of whale-bone, wire, or flexible wood, with a fponge fatt- ened to one end. # , m m Should it be impoflible to extrad even thofe bodies which it is dangerous to admit into the ftomach* we muft then prefer the leaft of two evils, and rather run the hazard of pufhing them down than fuffer the patient to perifh in a few minutes; and we ought to fcru- ple this refolution the lefs,as a great many inftances have happened, Avhere the fwalioAving of fuch hurtfal and mdigeftible fubftances has been followed by no diforder. Whenever it is manifeft that all endeavours, either to extrad or pu!h down the fubftance, mull prove ineffodual, they fliould be dis- continued; becaufe the inflammation occafioned by perfifting in them might be as dangerous as the obftrudion itfelf. Some have died in confequences of the inflammation, even after the body which caufed the obftrudion had been entirely removed. While the means recommended above are making ufe of, the pa- tient fhould often fwallow, or, if he cannot, he fhould frequendy receive by injedion through a crooked tube or pipe that may reach down to the gullet, fome emollient liquor, as warmjmilk and^vater, barley-water, or a decodion of mallows. Injedions of this kind not only foften and footh the irritated parts, but, when thrown. m with force, arc often more fuccefsful in loofening the obftrudion dun all attempts Avith inftruments. When, after all our endeavours we are obliged to leave the ob- ftruding body in the part, the patient muft be treated as if he had an inflammatory difeafe. He fliould be bled, kept upon a low diet, and have his whole neck furrounded with emollient poultices. The like treatment muft alfo be ufed, if there be any reafon to fufped an inflammation ofthe paffage, though the obftruding body be re- moved. . , . , . A proper degree of agitation has fometimes loofened the inhering body more effedually than inftruments. Thus a blow on the back has often forced up a fubftance which ftuck in the gullet; but this is ftill more proper and efficacious when rhe fubftance gets into the wind-pipe. In this cafe vomiting and fneezing are Iikewife to be excited. Pins which ftuck in the gullet have been frequently dis- charged by riding on horfe-back, or in a carriage. # When any indigeftible fubftance- has been forced down into the ftomach, the patient fhould ufe a very mild and fmoth diet, con- fining; chiefly of fruits and farinaceous fubftances, as puddings, pot- taee, aud foups. He ihouid avoid all heating and irritating things, l*gwine pundi, pepper, and fuch like; and his drink fhould be milk and Avater. barley-water, or wdiey. When foe gullet is fo ftrongly and fully clofed, that the patient can receive no food by the mouth he muft be nounfhed by clyfters of foup, jelly, and the like. # , When the patient is in danger of being immediately fuffoca.ed, and all hope of freeing the paffage is vanifhed, fo that death feems at hand, if refpiration be not reilored ; the operation of bronchoto- %, or opening of die wind-PiPe, muft be diredly performed. As 373 Of DROWNED FERSONS. this operation is neither difficult to an expert furgeon, nor very painful to the patient, and is often die only method which can be taken to preferve life in thefe emergencies, Ave thought* proper to mention it, though it fhould only be attempted by perfons fltilled in furgery. # OF DROWNED PERSONS. WHEN a perfon has remained above a quarter of an hour un- der Avater there can be no confiderable hopes of his recovery. But as feA-eral circumftances may happen to have continued life, in fuch an unfortunate fituation, beyond the ordinary term, we would never too foon refign the unhappy objed to his fate,but try every mnhod for his relief, as there are many well attefted proofs «f the recovery of perfons to life and health av'io had been taken out of the water apparently dead, and who remained a confiderable time without exhibiting any figns of life. The firft thing to be done, after the body is taken out ofthe Ava- ter, is to convey it as foon as poffible to fome convenient place where the neceffary operations for his recovery may be performed. In doing this, care muft be taken not to bruife or injure the body by carrying it in any unnatural pofture with the head doAvnwards,or the like. If an adult body, it ought to be laid on a bed,or on ftraw with the head a little raifed.and carried on a cart,or on men's fhoul- ders, and kept in as natural and eafy a pofture as poflibk. A fmall body may be carried in the arms. In attempting to recover perfons apparently drowned, the prin- cipal intention to be purfued is, to reftore the natural warmth, upon ivhicli all the vital fundions depend; and to excite thefe fundions "by the application of flimulants, not only to the fkin, but Iikewife to the lungs inteftines, &c, Though cold was by no means the caufe ofthe perfon'9 death, yet it Avill prove an effedual obftacle to his recovery. For this reafon, ftripping him of his w^et clothes, his body muft be ftrongly rubbed for a confiderable time with coarfe linen cloths, as warm as they can be made; and, as foon as a well-heated bed can be got ready, he may be lain in it, and the rubbing fhould be continued. Warm cloths ought likeAvife to be frequently applied to the flomach and boAvek, and hot bricks, or bottles of Avarm water, to the folesof his feet, and to tli? palms of his hands. Strong volatile fpirits fliould be frequently applied to the nofe; .v.\d the fpine ofthe back and pit of the ftomach may be rubbed w:;h Avarm brandy or fpirit of Avine. The temples ought alfo to be chat,-! wi-h volatile fpirits; and ftimulating powders, as that of to- bacco or maTkrum,m:'.y be bloAvn up the noflrils. To venew die breathing a ftrong perfon may blow his own breath into the patient's mouth with all the force he can holding his nos- tril at the fame tiiFe- When it can be perceived by the rifing ofthe Biieftor belly that the lungs are filled with air, the perfon ought to "daiift Of DROWNED PERSONS. 371 defift from blowing, and fhould prefs the breaft and belly fo as to expel the air again ; and this operation may be repeated for fome time, alternately foliating and depreffing the lungs fo as to imitate natural refpiration. If the lunga canr.ot he inflated in this manner, it may be attempt- ed by blowing through one ofthe noitrils; and at the fame time keeping the other clofe. Dr. Monro for this purpofe recommends a Avooden pipe fitted at one cud for filling the noftril, and at the other for being blown into by a perfon's mouth,or for receiving the pipe of a pair of bellows, to be employed for the fame purpofe, if neceffary. When rdr crnr.ot ae forced into the cheft by the mouth or nofe, it may be neceffary to make an opening into the wind-pipe for this purpofe. It is needkfs, hoAvever, to fpend time in deferibing this operation, as it fliould not be attempted unlefs by perfons fkilled in furgery. To Ytirmriate the inteftines,the fume of tobacco maybe thrown up in form of clyfter. There are .various pieces of apparatus contriv- ed for this ruTpofe whichmay be ufed when at hand; but where thefe cannot be obtained, the bufinefs may be done by a common tobacco pipe. The bowl ofthe pipe muft be filled widi tobacco avcII kindkd, and, after the fmall lube has been introduced into the fun- dament, die fmoak may be forced up by blowing through a piece of paper full of holes wrapped round the mouth of the pipe, or by bloAving through an empty pipe, the month of Avhich is applied clofe to that of the other. This may alfo be done in the following man- ner : A common clyfter pipe with a bag mounted upon it may be introduced into the fundament, and the rnouth ofthe bag maybe applied round the fmall end of a tobacco pipe, in the bowl of which tobacco is to be kindled, and the fmoke blown up as direded above. Should it be found impracticable to throw up the fmoke of tobacco, clyfters of warm water, with the addition of a little fait and fome wine or fpirits, may be frequendy adminiftered. This may be done by a common clyfter-bag and pipe ; but, aa it ought to be thrown well up, a pretty large fyringe will anfwer the purpofe better. While thefe things are doing, fome of the attendants ought to be preparing a warm bath, into which the perfon fliould be put, if the above endeavours prove incffedual. Where there are no con- veniencies for ufing the warm bath, the body may be covered with warm fait, fand,afhes, grains, or fuch like. Ttffot mentions an in- ftauce of a girl who was reftored to life, after fhe had been taken out ofthe water, fwelled, bloated, and to all appearance dead, by laying her naked body upon hot afhes, covering her Avkh others equally hot, putting a bonnet round her head, and a flocking round her neck, fluffed with the fame, and heaving coverings overall. After fhe had remained half an hour in this lit nation, her pulfe re- turned, fhe recovered fpeech, and cried out, I freeze, I freeze ; a lit- tle cherry-brandy was given her, and fhe remained hurried as it were under the afhes for eight hours ; after which fhe avus taken out, without any other complaint except that o'laflitude or weari- nefs', which went off in a few days. The Dodor mentions Iikewife 37* Or DROWNED PERSONS. an inftance of a man who was rcftored to life, after he had remain- ed fix hours under water, by the heat of a dunghill. Till die patient fhews fome figns of life, and is able to fwallow, it would be ufelefs and even dangerous to pour liquors into his mouth. His lips however, and tongue, may be frequently wet with a feather dipt in warm brandy or odier ftrong fpirits; and, as foon as he bas recovered the poAver of fwallowing, a little warm wine, or fome other cordial, ought every now and then to be adminiller- ed. Some recommend a vomit after the patient is a little reanimated; but if he can be made to puke without the fickening draught,it will be more fafe : this may generally be done by tickling the throat and fauces with an oiled feather, or iome other foft fubitance,which will not injure the parts. Tiffot in this cafe recommends the oxymel of fquills, a table fpoonful of which, diluted with water, may be given every quarter of an hour, till the patient has taken five or fix dofes. Where that medicine is not at hand, a ftrong infufion of fage, camomile-flowers, or carduus bentdiSus, fweetened widi hon- ey, or fome warm water, with the addition of a little fait, may, he fays, fupply its place. The dodor does not intend that any of thefe things fhould be given in fuch quantity as to occafion vomit- ing. He thinks emetics in this fituation are not expedient. We are by no means to difeontinue our afliftance as foon as the patients difcover fome tokens of life, fince they fometimes expire after thefe firfl appearances of recovering. The warm and ftimu- lating applications are ftill to be continued, and fmall quantities of fome cordial liquor cught frequently to be adminiftered. Laftly, though the perfon fhould be manifeflly re-animated, there fome- times remains an oppreffion, a cough, and feverifhnefs, which effec- tually conftitute a difeafe. In this cafe it will be neceffary to bleed the patient in the arm and caufe him to drink plentifully of barley- water, elder-flower-tea, or any other foft pedoral infufions. Such perfons as have the misfortune to be deprived of the ap- pearances of life, by a fall, a blow, fuftocation, or the like, muft be treated nearly in the fame manner as thofe who have been for fome time under Avater. I once attended a patient who was fo fluned by a fall from a horfe, that for above fix hours he fcarcely exhibited any figns of life ; yet this man by being bled, and proper methods taken to keep up the vital warmth, recovered, and in a few days was perfedly well. Dr. Alexander gives an inftance to the fame purpofe in die Edinburg Phyfical and Litterary Effays, of a man who was to all appearance killed by a blow on the breaft, but re- covered upon being immerfed for fometime in warm water. Thefe and other inftances of a fimilar nature, which might be adduced, amount to a full proof of this fad, that many of thofe unhappy perfons who lofe their lives by falls, blows, and other accidents, Wight be faved by the ufe of proper meant duly perfifted ia. Or NOXIOU5 VAPOURS. m NOXIOUS VAPOURS. AIR may be many ways rendered noxious, or even deftrudiveto animals. This may either happen from its vivifying principle being deftroyed, or from fubtle exhalations with which it is impregnated. Thus ar that has paffed through burning fuel is neither capable of fupporting fire nor the life of animals. Hence the danger of ikep- ing in clofe chambers with coal fires. Some indeed fuppofe the danger here proceeds from the fulphurcous oil contained in the coal, which is fet at liberty and diffufed all OAj#the chamber ; while others imagine it is owing to the air ofthe r<Wm being charged with phlogifton. Be this as k may, it is a filtration carefully to be avoid- ed. Indeed it is dangerous to fleep in a fmall apartment with afire of any kind. 1 lately faw four perfons who had been fuffocatedby flecpinc in an miartmcnt Avhere a fmall fire of coal had been left bmvang. The vapour which exhales from Avine,cyder, beer,or other liquors in the ftate of fermentation, contains fomething poifonous, Avhich kills in the fame manner as the vapour of coal. Hence there is al- ways danger ingoing into cellars Avhere a large quantity of thofe li- quors, is in a ftate of fomentation, efpecially if they have been clofe fluit up for fome time. Thue have been many inftances of perfons flrucl: dead on entering fuch places, and of others who haArc with difficulty efeaped. When fubterraneous caves, that have been very long fliut, are opened, or when deep wells are cleaned, which have not been emptied for feveral years, the vapours arifing from them produce the fame effeds as thofe mentioned above. For this reafon no perfon ought to venture into a well, pit, cellar, or any place that is damp and has been long fhutup,till the air has been fufficiently purified.by burning gunpowder in it. It is eafy to know, as has been obferved, in a former part of this work, when the air of fuch places it unwhole- fome, by letting down a lighted candle, throwing in burning fuel, or the like. If thefe continue to burn, people may fafely venture in ; but where they are fuddenly extinguifhed,no one ought to en- ter till the air has been firft purified by fire. The offenfive fmell of lamps and of candles, efpecially when their flames are extinguilhed, operate like other vapours, though with lefs violence, and lefs fuddenly. There have however been inftances of people killed by the fumes of lamps which had been extinguifhed in a clofe chamber,and perfons of weak delicate breafts generally find themfelves quickly oppreffed in apaitments illumin- ated with many candles. Such as are fenfible of their danger in thefe fituations, and re- treat feafonably from it, are generally relieved as foon as they get into the open air, or, if they have any remaining uneafinefls,a little water and vinegar,or lemonadedrank hot; affords them relief. But when they are fo far poifoned as to have loft their feeling and underftanding, the following means muft be ufed for their reco- very. Th? 3*4 EFFECTS of EXTREME COLD. The pafier.t fhould be expofed to a very pure, frefh.and open air, and volatile falts, or odier ftimulating fubftancer., held to his nofe. He fhould next be bled in the arm,or if that doesnot fucceed,in the neck. His legs ought to be put into Av.trm AA^ater and Avell rubbed. As foon as he can fwallow, fomc-lemonade,or water and vinegar, widi the addition of a little nitre, may be gk'en him. Nor are fharp clyfters by any means to be negleded ; thefe may be made, by adding to the common clgfter3 fyrup of buckthornand tindure of fenna, of each two ounces; or, in their flead, half an •unce of Venice turpentine diffolved in the yolk of an egg. Should thefe things not be at hand, two or three large fpoonfuls of com- mon fait may be put into the clyfter. The fame means, if neceffa- ry, which were recommended intiieformerpartof this chapter,may be ufed to reftore the circulation, warmth, Sec. Mr. Toffach, furgeon at Alloa, relates die cafe of a man fuffo- •ated by the fleam of burning coal,whom lie recovered by blowing his breath into the patient's mouth, bleeding him in the arm and eaufing him to be well rubbed and toffed about. And Dr. Frewen, ef Suffer, mentions the cafe of a young man who was ftupified by the fnioak of fea-coal, but Avas recovered by being plunged into cold water, and afterwards laid hi a warm bed. The prr.dice of plunging perfons fuftbeated by noxious vapours in cold Avater, woukl feen io be fopported by the common experi- ment of fuffocating dogs in fhe grotto del cant, and afterwards reco- vering them, by throwing them into the neighbouring lake. ---*•%,.«*'-'•* •** EFFECTS OF EXTREME COLD. WHEN cold is extremely fevere, and a perfon is expofed to it for a long time, it proves mortal, in confequence of its flopping the circulation in the extremities, and forcing too great a proportion of blood towards the brain ;fo that the patient dies of a kind of apoplexy, preceeded by great fleepinefs. The traveller, in this fit- ' Uation, who finds himfelf begin to grow drowfy, fhould redouble his efforts to extricate himfelf from the eminent danger he is ex- pofed to. This fleep, which he might confider as fome alleviation •f his fufferings, would, if indulged prove his laft. Such violent effeds of cold are happily not very common in this •ountry ; it frequently happens, howeATer, that the hands or feet of travellers are fo benumbed or frozen,as to be in danger of a morti- fication, if proper means are not ufed to prevent it. The chief danger in this fituation arifes from the fudden application of heat. It is very common when the hands or feet are pinched with cold,to hold them to the fiie ; yet reafon and obfervation fhew that this is a mo ft dangerous and imprudent pradice. Every peafant knows, if frozen meat, fruits, or roots of any kind be brought near the fire, or put into warm water, they will be def- troyed, by rottennefs or a kind ot mortification ; and that the only way to recover them, is to immerfe them for fome time in very cold water EFFECTS of EXTREME HEAT. 375 water. The fame obfervation holds with regard to animals in this .. condition. When the hands or feet are greatly benumbed with cold, they ought eidier to be iznmerfed in cold water, or, rubbed withfnow, till they recover their natural warmth and fenfibility; after which the perfon may be removed into an apartment a little warmer, and may drink fome cups of tea, or an infufion of elder flowers fweetened with honey. Every perion muft have obferved, whsn hishands AA-ero even but flightly affeded with cold, that the beft way to warm them was by wafliing them in cold water, and continuing to rub thera well for fome time. When a perfon has been fo long expofed to the cold, that all ap- pearances of life are gone, it will be neceffary to rub him all over with fnow or cold water; or, what will anfwer better, if it can bs obtained, to immerfe him in a bath of the very coldeft water. There is the greateft encouragement to perfift in the ufe of thefe means, as Ave are affuredthat perfons who had remained in the fnow, or had been expofed to the freezing air during five or fix fucceflive days, and who had difcovered no marks of life for feveral hours, have neverthelefsbeen revived. I have always thought that the whitloes, kibes, chilbrains, and other inflammations'of the extremities, which are fo common among the peafants in the cold feafons, were chiefly occafioned by their fud- den tranfitions from cold to heat. After they have been expofed to an extreme degree of cold, they immediately apply their hands and feet to the fire, or, if they have occafion, plunge them into warm water, by which means, if a mortification does not happen, an in- flammation feldom fails to enfue. Moft ofthe ill confequences from this quarter might be eafily avoided, by only obferving theprecau- tions mentioned above. EFFECTS OF EXTREME HEAT. THE effeds of extreme heat, though not fo common in this coun- try, are no lefs fatal, and much more fudden than thofe of cold. In hot countries people frequently drop down dead in the flreets, ex- haufted with heat and fatigue, lathis cafe, if any warm cordial can be poured into the mouth, it ought to be done. If this cannot be effeded, they may be thrown up in form of a clyfter. Volatile fpirits and other things of a ftimulating nature, may be applied to the fkin, which ihouid be well rubbed widi coarfe cloths,Avhipped with nettles, or other ftimulating things. Some of the ancient phy- ficians are faid to have reftored to life perfons apparently dead, by beating them with rods* CHAP. 17« FAINTING FITS. CHAP. LIV. OF FAINTING FITS, AND OTHER CASES WHICH REQUIRE IMMEDIATE ASSISTANCE. STRONG and healthy perfons, who abound with blood, are often feized with fudden fainting fits, after violent exercife, drinking freely of warm or ftrong liquors, expofure to great heat, intenfe application to ftudy, or the like. In fuch cafes the patient fhould be made to fmell to fome vinegar. His temples,forehead,and wrifts,ought at thefame time to be bath- ed with vinegar mixed with an equal quantity of Avarm water ; and two or three fpoonfuls of vinegar, with four or five times as much water, may, if he can fwallow be poured into his mouth. If the fainting proves obftinate, or degenerates into a fyncopt, that is, an abolition of feeling and underftanding, the patient rnuft be bled. After the bleeding, a clyfter will be proper, and then he fhould be kept eafy and quiet, only giving him every half hour a cup or two of an infufion of any mild vegetable, with the addition of a little fugar and vinegar. When fAvoonings, which arife from diis caufe, occur frequently in the fame perfon, he fhould, in order to efcape them, confine him* felf to a light diet, confifling chiefly of bread, fruits, and other ve- getables. His drink ought to be A/ater or fmall beer, and hefhoulj lleep but moderately and take much exercife. But fainting fits proceed much oftener from a defied than an ex- cefs of blood. Hence they are very ready to happen after great evacuations of any kind, obftinate watching, want of appetite, or fuch like. In diefe, an almoft dired oppofite courfe to that men- tioned above muft be purfued. The patient fhould be laid in bed, with his head low and being covered, fhould have his legs, thighs, arms, and his whole body rubbed ftrongly with hot flannels. Hungary-water, volatile falts', or ftrong fwelling herbs, as rue,mint, or rofemary, may be held to his'nofe. His mouth may be Avet with a little rum or brandy; and if he can fwallow, fome hot wine, mixed AvitJi fugar and cinnamon, which is an excellent cordial, may be poured into his mouth. A comprefs of flannel dipt in hot wine or brandy muft be applied to the pit of his fiom ach, and warm bricks,or bottles filled with hot water laid to his feet. As foon as the patient is recovered a little, he fhould take fome ftrong foup or broth, or a litde bread or bifcuitibaked in hot fpiced wine. To prevent the return ofthe fits,he ought totake often, but in fmall quantites, fome light yet firengthening nouriftiment,sspan- ado, made Avith foup inftead of Avater, neAv laid eggs lightlv poached, chocolate, light roail meats, jellies, and fuch like. Thofe Of FAINTRING FITS: 37? Tlr.fi: fainting-fits, which are the effed of bleeding, or ofthe ■riolent operation of pur/es, belong to this clafs. Such as happen after artificial bleeding are feldom dangerous, generally terminating ajs foon as the patient is laid upon the bed; indeed perfons fubjedt ro this kind fhould afo ays be bled lying, in order to prevent it. Should the fainting hoAvever continue longer than ufual, volatile fpirits mty be held to the nofe, and rubbed on the temples, &c. When fainting is the effed of too ftrong or acrid purges or vomits, the patient mult be treated in all refpeds as if he had taken poifon. He fhould be made to drink plentifully of milk, warm water, and oil, barley Avater or foch like, emollient clyfters will Iikewife be proper, and the patient's ftrength fhould afterwards be recruited, by giving him generous cordials, and anodyne medicines. Faintings are often occafioned by indigeftion. This may either proceed from the quantity or quality of the food. When the for- mer of thefe is the caufe,' the cure will be beft performed by vo- miting, Avhich may be promoted by caufing the patient to drink a weak infufion of cumomile-floAvers, cara'uus beneiitlus, or the like. When thediibrder proceeds from the nature of the food, the patient as in the cafe of weaknefs, mull be revived by ftrong fmells, &c. afoer which he ihonld be made to fwallow a large quantity of light warm fluid, which may fierce to drown, as k were, the offending matter, to foften its acrimony, and either to effed a difcharge of it by vomiting, or force it doAvn into the inteftines. Even difagreeablefmells Will fometimes occafi-in fwoonings, efpe- cially in people of weak rterves. When this happens, the patient fhould be carried into the open air, have ftimulating things held to his nofe, and thofe fubftances which are difagreeable to him ought immediately to be removed. But we have already taken notice of fwoonings Avhich ^rife from nervous diforders, and foall therefore fay no more upon that head; Fainting-fits often happen in the progrefs of difeafes. In the be- ginning of putrid difeafes they generally denote an oppreflion at the ftomach, or a mafs of corrupted liumours, and they ceafe after evac- uations either by vomit or ftool. When they occur at the beginning of malignant fevers, they indicate great danger. In each of thefe cafes, vinegar ufed both externally and internally is the beft reme- dy during the paroxyfm, and plenty of lemon-juice and watev after It. SwoYmings whibli happen in difeafes accompanied with great evacuations, mull be treated like thofe which are OAving to weaknefs -jr.d the evacuations ought to be Yefirabi-fo, When they happen to- n wards the end of a violent fit of an intermitting fever, or at that of 'each exacerbation of a continual fever, the patle ,:t muft bi fifppan- ed by fmall draughts of wine and water. Delicate and hyfteric Avomen are very liable to fvooning or fain*- kng-!ks after delivery. Thefe might be often prevented by generous cordials, and the admiffron of frefii air. When they are occaiioned by exceffive flooding, it ought by all means to be retrained. They are generally the effed of mere Aveaknefs or exhauftion. Dr. En- gleman, relates the cafe of a avoman " in child bed, avIio, after "having been happily delivered, fudd-rdy foiled and lay upw^'ds i7* Of INTOXICATION. " of a quarter of an hour apparently dead. A phyficianVu': fent •* for; her oivn maid, in the mean Avhile, being out of patience at " his delay, attempted to affift her herferf, and extending herfelf up. " en her miftrefe, applied her moutii to herk, blew in as much " breath as fhe poflibly could, and in a very fhort time theexhaus- " ted woman awaked as out of a profound fleep; when proper things " being giving her, fhe foon recovered. " The maid being afked hoAv fhe came to think of this expedient •* faid fhe had feen it pradifed at Altenburgh, by the midAvives, up- "on children, with the happieft effed." We mention this chiefly that other midAvives may be induced to follow fo laudable an example. Many children are born Avlthout any figns of life, and others expire foon after the birth, who might, without all dotibt, by proper care, be reftored to life. From whatever caufe fainting-fits proceed, frefii :tir is akvays of the greateft importance to the patient. By not attending to thk cir- cumftance, people often kill their friends while they are endeavour- ing to fave thenr. Alarmed at the patients fituation, they call in .». crowd of people to his afliftance, or perhaps to wknefs his exit, whofe breathing exhaufts the air, and increafes the danger. There is not the leaft doubt but this pradice which is very common among the lower fort of people,often proves fatal, efpecially to the deli- cate, and fuch perfons as fall into fainting-fits from mere exhauftion or the violence of fome difeafe. No more perfons ought ever to be admitted into die room Avhere a patient lies in a fwoon than are abfolutely neceffary for his afliftance, and the Avindows ofthe apart- ment fhould always be opened, at lead as fa? as to admit a ftream' of frefh air. Perfons fubjed to frequent fwooriings,or fainting-fits, fhould neg- led no means to remove the caufe of them, as their confequences n^e always injurious to the conftitution. Every fainting-fit leaves the perfon in dejedion and weaknefs; the fecretions are thereby fufpended, the humoars difpofed to ftagnation, coagulations and obftruftions are formed, and, if die motion of the blood be to- tally intercepted, or very confiderably checked, pnlypups are fome- times formed in the heart or larger veffels. The only kind of fwoorsjngs not to be dreaded are thofe Avhich fome time mark the erifts in fevers; yet even thefe ought, as foon as poffible, to be re- moved. OF INTOXICATION THE effeds of intoxication are often fatal. No hindof pnilhri kills more certainly than an over-dofe of ardent fpirits Sumeti nes, by d-eilroying the nervous energy, they put an end to life at once; •bat in general their effed~s are more flow,and in many refpeds fim- ilar to riiofi-' of opium. Other kinds of intoxicating liqu ts may prove fatal Avhen r;7:cn to excefs, as Avell as ardent fpirit ; but they rtvivgenera'lv b: difeharged by vomiting, which ought always hi' eve e::c:ted when die Horn. rh is ovc* char'". 1 with hgu. t. < Of INTOXICATION. 379 More of thofe unhappy perfons, who die intoxicated, lofe their lives from inability to condud themfelves,than from th* deitrudive quality of the liquor. Unable to walk, they tumble down, and lie in fome awkward pofture, which obftruds the circulation or breath- ing, and often continue in this fituation till they die. No drunken perfon fhould be left by himfelf, till his clothes have been loofened, and his body laid in fuch a pofture as is moil favourable for con- tinuing the vital motions, difcharging the contents of the ftomach, Sec. The beft pofture for difcharging the contents of the flomach is to lay the perfon upon his belly ; when afleep he may be laid on his fide, with his head a little raifed, and particular care muft be taken that his neck be no way bent, twilled, or have any thing too tight about it. The exceffive degree of thirft occafioned by drinking ftrong li- quors, often induces people to quench it by taking what'is hurtful. I have known fatal confequenscs eA'ea from drinking freely of milk after a debauch of wine or four punch ; thefe acid liquors, together with the heat of the ftomach, having coagulated the milk in fuch a manner that it could never be digefted. The fafeft drink after a debauch is water with a toaft, tea, infufions of balm, fage, barley- tvater and fuch like. If the perfon wants to vomit, he may drink a Aveak infufion of camomile flowers, or lukewarm water and oil ; but in this condition vomiting may generally be excited by ordy tickling the throat with die finger or a feather. Inftead of giving a detail of all the different fymptoms of intoxi- cation Avhich indicate danger, and pro poling a general plan of treatment for perfons in this fituation, I fhall briefly relate the hif- tory of a cafe which lately fell under my own obfervaticn,Avherein • moil of thofe fymptoms ufually reckoned dangerous concuircd,a:4 where the treatment was fuccefsful. A young man about fifteen years of age, had, for a hire, drank ten glaffes of ftrong brandy. He foon after fell fail afleep, and con- tinued lo that fituation for feveral hours, till at length his uneafy manner of breathing, the coldnefs of the extremities, and otjier threatening fymptoms, alarmed hk friends, and made them fend forme. I found him ftiii fleeping, his countenance ghaftly, and his fkin covered with a cold clammy fweat. Almoft die only figns of life remaining were, a deep laborious breathing, and a cqnyul- liv.e motion or agitation of his bowels. I tried to roufe him, but in vain, by pinching, fliaking, applying volatile fpirits, and other ftimulating things to his nofe, Sec. A few ounces of blood Avere Iikewife taken from his arm, and a mixture cf vinegar and water was poured into his mouth; but, as he could not fwallow, very little of diis got into the ftomach. None cf thefe things having the leaft effed, and the danger feeming to increafe, I ordered his legs to be put into warm water, and a fharp clyfter to be immediately adminiftered. This gave him a ftool, and was the firft thing that relieved him. It was afterwards repeated with the fame happy effed, and feemed to be %he chief caufe of his recovery. He then began to fhew fome figns of life, took drink vvhaa k a\ aj offered hijp| and came gradually to his kr.fe.. He continued, however, 3«» • SUFFOCATION and STRANGLING. however, for feveral days Aveak and feverifh, and complained much, of a forenefs in his bowebj, Avhich gradually went off, by means ol a flender diet, and cool mucilaginous liquors. OF SUFFOCATION AND STRANGLING. THESE may fometimes proceed from an infardion ofthe lungs, produced by vicid clammy humours, or fpafmodic affedion ofthe nerves of that organ. Perfons who feed grofsly, and abound in rich blood, are very liable to niffocating fits from the former of thefe caufes.' Such ought as foon as they are attacked, to be bled, to receive an emollient clyfter, and to take frequently a cup of di- luting liquor Avith a little nitre in it. They fhould Iikewife receive the fteams of hot vinegar into their lungs by breathing. Nervous and afthmatic perfons are moil fubjed to fpafmodic af. fedions of the lungs. In this cafe the patient's legs mould be im. merfed in warm water, and the fleams of vinegar applied as above. Warm diluting liquors ihouid Iikewife be drank ; to a cup of which a tea-fpoonful of the parygoric elixir may occafionally be added. Burnt paper,feathers, or leather, may be held to the pa- tient's nofe, and fiefh air fliould be freely admitted to him. Infants are often fuffocated by the careleffnefs or inattention of their nurfes*. An infant when in bed fhould always be laid fo,that it cannot tumble down with its head under the bed clothes ; and when in a cradle, its face ought never to be covered. A fmall de- gree of attention to thefe two fimple rules Avould fave the lives of many infants, and prevent others from being rendered weak and fickly all their days by the injuries done to their lungs. Inflead of laying down a plan for the recovery of infants who are fuffocated, or over-laid, as it is termed by their nurfes, I fhall give the hiffory of a cafe^related by Monfieuf Janin, of the Royal College of Surgery at Paris, as it Avas attended with fu#cefs, and contains almoft every thing that can be done on fuch occafions. A nurfe having had the misfortune to over-lay a child, he was called in, and found the infant Avithout any figns of life ; nflpulfa- tion in the arteries, no refpiration, fne face livid, the eyes open, dull, and tarnifhed, the nofe full of fnivel, the mouth gaping, in fhort, it was almoft cold. Whilft fome linen clothes and a parcel of afhes were warming, he had the boy unfwathed. and laid him in a warm bed, and on the right fide. He then was rubbed all over with * Thefe accidents are not always the efft&s of carelcrthcfs. I have known an in- fant overlaid by its mother being feized in the night with an hyfleric-fit. This aught to ferve as a caution againft employing hyfteric women as nurfes, and (hould Iikewife teach fuch women never to lay an iafand in the fame bed with themfelves, b'it \n a fmall tWjawnt one . SUFFOCATION and STRANGLING- 381 $wth fine linen, for fear of fretting his tender and^ delicate fkin. As foon as the aflieshadreceived then duedegree of heat,Mr.Janin buried him in them, exceptthe face,placed him on the fkleoppcfite to that on which he had been at firft laid, and covered him with a blanket. He had a bottle of c-aude luce in his po.cket,which he pre- fented to his nofe from time to time; and between Avhiles fome puffs of tobacco were blown up his noflrils: to v thefe fucceeded the blowing into his mouth, and fqueezing tight his nofe. Animal heat began thus to be excited gradually ; the pulfations of the temporal artery Ave re foon felt,the bieathing became more frequent and free, and the eyes clofed and opened alternately. At length the child fetched fome cries expreffiveof his -rant ofthe breaft, which be'ing^ applied to his mouth he catched at it with avidity,and fucked as if nothing had happened to him. Though the pulfations of the arte- ries were by this time very well re-ellabhlhed,and it was hot weath- er yet Mr.Janin thought it advifeabk to lcavehis little patient three quarters of an hour longer under the allies. He was afterwards ta- ken out, cleaned and dreffed as ufual ; to Avhich a gentle fleep fuc- ceeded, and he continued pevfedly well. Mr. Janin mentions Iikewife an example of a young man who had hanged himfelf through defpair,to Avhom he adminiftered help as effcdually as in the preceding cafe. Mr. Glover, furgeon in Dodors Commons, London, relates the cafe of a perfon who was rcftored to life after twenty-nine minutes hanging, aud continued in good health for many years after. The principal means ufed to reftore thismaii to life were,opening the temporal artery and tiia external jugular ; rubbing the back, mouth and neck, with a quantity of volatile fpirits and oil ; ad- miniftering the tobacco clyfter bygmeansof lighted pipes, and ftrong fridions cf the legs and arms. This courfe had been continued for about four hours, when an incifion was made into the windpipe,and air blown ftrongly through a canula into the lungs. About tAventy minutes after tin's, the blood at the artery began to run doAvn the face, and a flow pulfe Avas juft perceptible at the wrift. The frictions were continued for fome time longer; his pulfe became more fre- quent, and his mouth and nofe being irritated with fpirit offal am- moniac, he opened his eyes. Warm coidials were then adminiller- ed to him, and in two days he was fo well as to be able to walk eight miles. Thefe cafes are fufficient to fheAv what may be done for the reco- very of thofe unhappy papfons v. ho ftrangjfe themfelves. ia a fit of defpair. OF PERSONS WHO EXPIRE IN CONVULSION FITS. CONVULSION fits often conftjtutc the laft fcene of acute or chronic diforders. When this is the cafe there can remain but fmall hojjes of die patients recovery after expiring in a fit. Bur. Aiken a perfon 6$z PERSONS WHO EXPIRE a perfon who appears to be in perfed health, is fuddenly fefeed with a convlfion fit, and feems to expire, fome attempts ought al ways to be made to reftore him to life. Infants are moft liable m ronvulfions, and are often carried off very fuddenly by one or more fits about the time of teething. There are many well authenticated accounts of infants having been reftored to life, after they had to all appearance expired in convulfions : but we fhall only relate th« following inftance, mentioned by Dr. Johnfon in his pamphlet on 'the practicability of recovering pcfons vifibly dead. In the parifh of St. Clemens in Colchefter, a child of fix months old, lying upon its mother's lap, having had the breaft, was feized widi a ftrong convulfion fit, which lafted fo long, and ended with fo total a privation of motion in the body, lungs, and pulfe, that it was deemed abfolutely dead. It was accordingly ftripped, laid out? the pafling bull ordered to be tolled, and a coffin to be made; but a neighbouring gendeAvoman who ufed to admire the child, hearing of its fudden death, haftened to the houfe, and upon examining the child, found it not cold, its joints limber, and fancied that a glafs fhe held to its mouth and nofe was a little damped with the breath ; upon which fhe took the child in her lap, fat down before the fire, rubbed it, and kept it in gende agitation. In a quarter of an hour fhe felt the heart begin to beat faintly ; fhe then put a lit- tle of the mother's milk into its mouth, continued to rub its p^lms and fole-, found the child begin to move,and the milk was fwal- loAved ; and in another quarter of an hour fhe had the fatisfadion of reftoring to its ufoonfolate mother the babe quite recovered, eager to lay hold of the breaft, and able to fuck again. The child throvei had no more fits, is grownup, and at prefent alive. Thefe means, which are certainly in the power of every perfon, were forficient to reftore to life an infant to all appearance dead, and aaIio in all probability, but for the ufe of thefe fimple endeav- ours, Avould have remained fo. There are however many other things which might be done in cafe the above ihouid notfucced ; as rub- bing the body with ftrong fpirits, covering it with warm afhes or fait, blowing air into the lungs, throwing up warm ftimulating clyfters or the fmoke of tobacco into the inteftines, and fuch like. When children are dead born, or expire foon after the birth, the feme means ought to be ufed. for their recovery, as if they had ex- pired in circumfiances fimilar to thofe mentioned above. Thefe diredions may Iikewife be extended to adults, attention being always paid toth$ age and other circumftances of the patient. The foregoing cafes and obfervations afford fufficient proof of the fuccefs Avhich may attend the endeavours of perfons totally ig- norant of medicine, in affifting thofe who are fuddenly deprived of life by amy accident or difeafe. Manyfads of a fimilar nature might be adduced, were it neceffary; but tiiefe, it is hoped, will be fuffici- ent to call up the attention of the public, and to excite the humane and benevolent to exert their utmoft endeavours for the prefervation of their fellow-men. The fociety for the recovery of drowned perfns, inftituted at Am< fterdam in the year 1767, had the fetisfadion to find diat no fewer thta *N CONVULSION FITS. 38J than 150 perfons, in thefpace of four years, had been faved by the means pointed out by diem, many of whom oAved their prefervation to peafants and people of no medical knowledge. But the means Ufed With,fo much efficacy iu recovering drowned per:ons are, Avith • equal fuccefs, applicable to a number of cafes Avhere the poAvers of life feem in reality to be only fufpended, and to remain capable of renewing all their fundions, on being put into motion again. It is fhocking to refled, that for Avant of this confideration many perfons have been committed to the grave in whom the principles of life might have been revived. The cafes wherein foch endeavours are moft likely to be attended with fuccefs, are all thofe called fudden deaths from an inevitable caufe, as apoplexies, hyfter ics, faintings, and many other diforders wherein perfons in a moment fink down and expire. The various cafualties in which they may be tried are, fuffocations from the folphurecus damps of mines, coalpits, &c. the unwholefome air of long unopened wells or caverns ; the noxious vapours arifing from fermenting liquors; the fleams of burning charcoal; folphure- cus mineral acids; arfenical effluvia, &c- . The various accidents of drowning, ftrangling, arm apparent deaths, by blows, falls, hunger, -told, Sec. Iikewife furnifh oppor- tunities of trying fuch endeavours. Thofe perhaps who to appear- ance are killed by lightning, or by any violent agitation of the pas- fions, as fear, joy,furprife, and fuch like, might alfo be frequently recovered by the ufe of proper means, as blowing ftrongly into the lungs, &c. The means to be ufed for the recovery of perfons fuddenly depriv- ed of life, are nearly the fame in all cafes; they are pradicable by every one who happens to be prefent at the accident, and require no great expence, and lefs flrill. The great aim is toreftore the-Avarmtb and vital motions. This may in general be attempted by means of heat, fridions, bleeding, blowing air into the lungs, adminiftering clyfters and generous cordials. Thefe muft be varied according to circumftances. Common fenfe and the fituation of the patient, will fuggeft the proper manner of conduding them. Above all we would recommend perfevcrance. People ought never to defpair on account of difeouraging circumftances, or to leave off their endea- vours as long as their is the leaft hope of fuccefs. Where much good and no hurt can be done, no one ought to grudge his labour. It Avere greatly to be wifhed, that an inftitution fimilar to that of Amfterdam, Avas eftablifhed, upon a more extenfive plan, in Great* Britain; and that a reA*&rd was allowed to every one Avho fliould be ir.ftrumental in reftoring to life a perfon feemingly dead*.
