CHAP. LV. (CAUTIONS CONCERNING COLD BATHING, AND DRINKING THE MINERAL WATERS. AS it is noAV fafhionable for perfons of all ranks to plunge into the fea, and drink the mineral waters, I was delirousof render- ing this Avork ftill more extenfively ufeful, by the addition of fome pradical remarks on thefe adive and ufeful medicines Finding it impoffible to bring thefe obfervations within fo narrow a com- pafs as not to fwell the book, already too large, into an enormous fize, I refblved to confine myfelf to a few hints or cautions ; which may be of fervice to perfons Avho bathe, or drink the mineral wa- ters, Avithout being able to put themfelves under the care of a phy- fician. No part of the pradice of medicine is of greater importance, or merits more the attention of the phyfician, as many lives are loft, and numbers ruin their health, by cold bathing, and an imprudent ufe of the mineral Avaters. On fome future occafion 1 may proba- bly refome diis fubjed, as I know not any work that contains a fuf- ficient number of pradical obfervations to regulate the patient's con- Hud in the ufe of thefe adive and important medicicines.' We have indeed many books on the mineral Avaters, and fome of them are written with much ingenuity; but they aTe chiefly employ- ed in afcertaining the contents ofthe Avaters by chymical analyiis. This no doubt has' its ufe, but it is by no means of foch importance as fome may imagine. A man may know the chymical analvfis of all the articles in the materia medica, Avithoht being able properly to apply any one of them in the cure of difeafes. One p?.rre 0i pradical obfervations is Avorth a whole volume of chymical analy- tic But Avhere are foch obfervations to be met Avith ? Few pl.v- ficians are in a fituation to make them, ancMewer ftill are qualified for fuch a tafk. It can only be acc^rnplifhed by praditioners who refide at the fountains, and who pof elfing minds fuperior to local prejudices are capable of diftinguilhigg difeafes with accuracy, and of forming a found judgment refpeding the genuine effeds of me-1 dicines. Without DRINKING MINERAL WATERS. 385 Without a proper difcrimination with regard to the difeafe and the conftitution of the patient, the moft poAveiful medicine is more likely to do harm than gocd. Every one knows that the fame phy- fician who,by cold bathing,curedAuguftus,by an imprudent ule of the fame medicine, killed his heir. This induced the Roman fenate to make laws for regulating the baths, and preventing the numerous evils whkh arofe from an imprudent and promifcuous ufe of thofe elegant and fafliioaaable pieces of luxury. But as no fuch laws exifl in this country, every one does that which h right in his own eyes, and of courfe many muft do wrong. People are apt to imagine that the fimple element of Avater can do no hurt, and that they may plunge into it at any time with impu- nity. In this, however, they are much miftaken. I have known apoplexies, occafioned by going into the cold bath,fevers excited by flaying too long in it, and other maladies fo much aggravated by its continued ufe, that they could never be wholly eradicated. Nor are examples wanting either in ancient or modern times, ofthe baneful confequences which have arifen alfo from an injudicious ap- plication ofthe warm bath; but as Avarm baths are not fo common in this country, and are feldom ufed but under the diredion of a phyfician, I fhall not enlarge en that part of the fubjed. Immerfion in cold water is a cuftom Avhich lays claim to the moft remote antiquity : indeed it mull have been coeval with man him- felf. The neceffity of water for the purpofe of cleanlinefs, and the pleafure arifing from its application to the body in hot countries, muft very early have recommended it to the human fpecies. Even the example of other animals Avas fufficient to give the hint to man. By inftindmany of them are led to apply cold water in this manner j and fome, when deprived cf its ufe, have been known to languiih, and even to did But whedier the pradice of cold bathing arofe from neceffity, reafoning, 01 imitation, is an inquiry of no impor- tance ; our bufinefs is to point out the advantages which may be derived from it, and to guard people againft an improper ufe of it. The cold bath recommends itfelf in a variety cf cafes, and is pe- culiarly beneficial to the inhabitants of populous cities; who in- dulge in idlenefs, and lead fedentary lives. In perfons of this de- feription the adion of the folids is always too weak, which induces a languid circulation, a crude indigefted mafs of humours, and ob- ftrudtions in the capillary veffels and glandular fyftem. Cold Avater, from its gravity as Avell as its tonic power, is well calculated either to obviate or remove thefe fymptoms. It accelerates the motion of the blood, promotes the different fecretions, and gives permanent vigour to the folids. But all thefe important purpofes A-vill be more effentially anfwered by the application of fait water. This ought not only to be prefeied on account of its fuperior gravity, but likevvife for its greater peAver of ftimulating the fkin, Avhich promotes the perfpiration, and prevents the patient from catching cold. It is neceffary, however, to obferve, that cold bathing is more likely to prevent, than to remove obftrudions ef the glandular or lymphatic A a a 386 Of COLD BATHING, an» lymphatic fyftem. Indeed, when thefe have arrived at a certain pitch, they are not to be removed by any mean', In this caijs the cild bath, will only aggravate the fyn.pt■ >ms, and hvai y the unhap- py patient into an untimely grave. It is therefore ofthe utmoft .m- portance, previous to the patient's entering upon the ufe of the cold bath, to determine whether or not he labours under any obligate obftrudions of the lungs or other vifcera; and where this is the caie cold bathing ought ftridly to be prohibited*. In what Is called a plethoric ftate, or too great a ftill nefs ofthe body, it is likcAvife dangerous to ufe the cold bath, widiout due preparation. In thii «afe there is great danger of barfting a blood veffel, or oecafioning an inflammation of th: brain or fome of the vifcera. ■ This precaution is the more necedkry to citizens, as moft of them live full, and are of a grofs habit. 'Yet, what is very re- markable, thefe people refort in crowds every fetifon to the fea-fide, andplung in the water without the leaft confideration. No doubt they often efcape with impunity; but does Sthis give a fandion to the pradice ? Perfons of this defcription ought by no means to bathe, unlefs the body has been previoufly prepared by fuitable e- viieuations. Another clafs of patients, who ftand peculiarly in need of the bracing qualities of cold water, is the nervous. This Includes a great number of the male, and almoft all the female inhabitants of great cities. Yet even thofe perfons ought to be cautious in ufing die cold bath. Nervous peeple have often weak bowels, and may, as well as others, be fubjed to congeftions and obftrudions ofthe vifcera; and in this cafe they will not be able to bear the effeds of cold water. For them, and indeed for all delicate people, the beft plan would be to accuftom themfelves to it by the moftplcafing and gentle degrees. They ought to begin with the temperate bath, and gradually ufe it cooler, till at length the cold proves quite agreea- ble. Nature revolts againft all greattranfitions: and thofe who do violeo^e to her didates,have often caufe to repent of their tern-iky. Whtrever cold bathing is pradifed, there ought Iikewife to be tepid baths for the purpofe mentioned above. Indeed it k the prac- tice of iome countries to throAV cold water over the patient as foon as he comes out of the warm bath ; but though this may not injure a Rutlian peafant,we daienot recommend it to the inhabitants of this country. The ancient Greeks and Romans, we are told, Avhen covered with fweat and duft, tiled to plunge into rivers, Aviriu uz receiving the fmalkfl injury. Though they might often efcape dan- gerf.oro this imprudent condud, yet it was certainly contraiv to found reafon. I have known many robuft men throw ;vway the'r lives * fl-e 'a:e celebrated Dr. Smollet has indeed f«iid, that if he woe perfuaded h« f^da/i deer ir, t'v. lu.-ig*. he would jump into theculd luih ; but litre the l/odor ev J. n-|y fSevvs in->.«: courage than difc/etion ; and that he was more a man.of wit i'nu a phy'i.ci. -. very oac w!i! allow. A nervoes afthma, 01 an ati'jphy. h;j\ be i.i.ii.i-.en for a p ilrnmory c-jr.lumption; yet, in the two former, the. cold bntii proves >: <n : et.r'-' .el. Iboii^h I r.ever knew it fo in thel.iiii"-. lutVd all r1 e phtln wcii r .'.....is I ever :«..-, a *e lie... u.<:d the cold L;- b, «>..ie tvickir.ly Li-.-rt b> a.. DRINKING MINERAL WATERS. $%t lives by fuch an attempt. We would *ot however advife patient* to go into the cold water when the body « chilly; as much exercife at leaft, ought to be taken, as may excite a gentle glow all over the body, but by no means fo as to overheat iu To young people, and particularly to children,cold bathing is of the laft importance. The lax fibres render its tonic -powers pecu- liarly proper. It promotes their growth, incre-.tfes their ftrength*, and prevents a variety of difeafes incident to childhood. Were in- fants early accuftomed to the cold bath, it Avould feldom difagree with them ; and we fhould fee fewer inftances of the fcrophula, rickets, and other difeafes which prove fatal to many, and make others miferable for life. Sometimes indeed, thefe diforders ren- der infants incapable of bearing the fhock of cold water; but this is owing to their not having been early and regularly accuftomed to it. It is however neceffary here to cautiotiyoung men againft too frequent bathing; as I have known many fatal confequences re- fult from the daily pradice of plunging into rivers, and continuing there too long. The moft proper time of the day for ufing the cold bath is na doubt die morning, or at leaft before dinner ; and the beft mode, that of quick immerfion. As cold bathing has a conftant tenden- cy to propel the blood and other hnmours towards the head, it our'ht to be a rule always to wet that part as foon as poffible. By due attention to this circumftance, there is reafon to believe,thatvi- elent head-achs, and other complaints, which frequently proceed from cold bathing, might be ctten prevented. The cold bath, when too long continued in, not only occafioni^ an eicelfive flus of humours towards th* head, but chills the blood, cramps the mufcles, relaxes the nerves and wholly defeats the in- tention of bathing. Hence, by not adverting to this circumftance, expert fwimmcrs are often injured, and fometimes even loofe their lives. All the beneficial pUrpofes of cold bathing are anfwered by one immerfion at a time ; and the patient ought to be rubbed dry the moment he comes out of the water, and fhould continue to take exeflcik for fome time after. When cold bathing occafions chilnefs, lofs of appetite, liftlefs- nefs, pain of the bread or bowels, a proftration of ftrength, or vio- lent head-achs, it ought to be difcontinued. TliOugh thefe hints are by no means intended to point out all the cafes Avhere cold bathing may be hurtful, nor toilluftrate its exten- five utility as a medicine ; yet it is hoped they may ferve to guard people againft fomeof thofe errors into which,from mere inattention they are apt to fall, and thereby not only endanger their own lives, but bring an excellent medicine into difrepate. . OF • The celebrated Gslen fay«, that immsrfion in cold wxtf-r is fit only i'jr d-,e youag »f lions and bears ; and recommends warm bathing, as conducive to ne. growth and ftrength of infants. How egregionfly do the gvcr.teft men err. wherever they loic fight of'fafts, and fnbfhtutc reafoning in phyfie in pkgi o. •bferva'tlon and wp*ne*<t! 33S ©n COLD BATHING, an» OF DRINKING THE MINERAL WATERS. The internal ufe of water, as a medicine, is no lefs an objed «* the phyfician's attention than the external. Pure elementary Avatei is indeed the moft inoffenfive of all liquors, and conftitutes a prin- cipal part of the food of every animal. But this element is often impregnated with fubftances of a very adive and penetrating nature; and of fuch an infidions quality, that, while they promote certain fecretions, and even alleviate fome difagreeable fymptoms, they weaken the poAvers of life, undermine the conftitution, and lay the foundation of worfe difeafes than thofe which they were employed to remove. Of this every praditioner muft have feen inftances ; and phyficians of eminence have more than once declared that they have known more difeafes occafioned than removed by the u.e of mineral waters. This doubtlefs has proceeded from the abufe of thefe powerful medicines, which evinces the neccflkg cf ufing them with caution. By examining the contents ofthe mineral waters Avhich are moft ufed in this country, we fhall be enabled to form an idea of the dan- ger which may arife from an improper application of them either externally or internally, though it is to the latter of thefe that the prefent obfervations are chiefly confined. The waters moft in ufe for medical purpofes in Britain,are thofe impregnated with falts, fulphur, iron, and mephitic air, cither fepai rately, or varioufly combined. Of thefe the moft poweiful is the faline fulphureous waterof Harrowgate, of which I have had more occafion to obferve the pernicious confequence-;, when improperly ufed, than of any other. To this, therefore, the folloiving remarks will more immediately relate,though they will be found applicable to all the purging waters in the kingdom which are ftrong enough to rnerit attention*: The errors which fo often defeat the intention of drinking the purgative mineral waters, and which fo frequently prove injurious to the patient, proceed from the manner of ufing .them, the quan- tity taken, the regimen purfued^or ufing them in cafes where they are not proper. A very hurtful prejudice ftill prevails in this country, that all difeafes muft be cured by medicines taken into the flomach, and that the more violent thefe medicines operate, they are the mora likely to have the defired effed.This opinion has proved fatal to thou- fands,and will,in all probability, deftroy many more before it can be wholly * The greateft clafs of mineral waters in this country is the chalybeate. In many partsof Britain thefe ar« to be found in almoft eveiy field ; but thofe chiefly in ule for medical purpofes, are the purging chalybeat.es, the waters of Scarborough. Chtl terham, Thorp Arch, Nevil Holt, &c. Ot thole which do not purge, the waters of Tunbridge ftand in the higheft repute. The faline purging waters, as tho!e of Ac- ton, Epfom, Kilburn, &c. are alfo in very general efteem ; but the fountains moft frequented by the fick in this country.are thofe to which the minerals i.rg'arta cer- Jajri degree of heat, as Bath, Briftol, Baxton, &c. DRINKING MINERAL WATERS. 389 wholly eradicated. Purging is often ufeful in acute difeafes,and in chronical cafes may pave the Avay for the operation of other medi- cines ; but it will feldom perform a cure ; and by exhaufting the ftrength ofthe patient, will often leave him in a worfe condition than it found him. That this is frequently the cafe Avith regard to the more adive mineral waters, eyery perfon converidnt in thefe matters will readily allow. Strong ftimulants applied to the ftomach and bowels for a length ot time, muft tend to weaken and deftroy their energy ; and Avhat ftimulants are more adive than fait and fulphar, efpecially when thefe fubftances are intimately combined, and carried through the fyftem by the penetrating medium of water ? Thofe boAvels muft be ftrong indeed, Avhich can withiland the daily operation of fuch adive principles for months together,and not be injured. This how- ever is the plan purfued by moll of thofe who drink the purging mineral waters, and whofe circumftances will permit them to con- tinue long enough at thofe fofkonable places of refoit. Many people imagine that eAery thing depends on the quantity of watcr'taken, and that the more they drink they will the luoner get avcIL This is an egregious error ; for while the unhappy pa- tient diirdcs he is by this means eradicating his diforder he is often in fad undermining the powers of life, and ruining his conftitution. Indeed nothing can do this io eifedually as Aveakening the powers of digeftion by the improper application of ftrong ftimulants. The very effence of health depends on the digeftive organs performing their due fundions, and the moft tedious maladies are all conneded with indigeftion. _ Drinking the Avater in too great quantity, not only injures the boAA'els and occafions indigeftion, but generally defeats the inten- tion for Arhich it is taken. The difeafes for the cure of which min- eral waters are chiefly celebrated, are moilly of the chronic kind ; and it is well known that focji difeafes can only be cured by the IIoav operation of alteratives, or fuch medicines as ad by in- ducing a gradual change in the habit. This requires length of time, and never can be affeded by medicines which run off by ftool, and operate chiefly on the firil paffages. Thofe who Aviih for the cure of any obftinate malady from the mineral waters, ought to take them in fuch a manner as hardly to produce any effed whatever on the bowels. With this view a half pint glafs may be drank at bed time*, and the fame quantity an hour before breakfaft, dinner, and fupper. The dofe, however, muft vary according to circumftances. Even the quantity men- tioned above will purge fome perfons, while others will drink twice as much Avithout being in the leaft moved by it. Its operation on the bowels is the only llandard for ufing die ivater as an altera- tive * When I fppeV of drinking a glaiV <*>f the water over night, I muft beg leave to caution 'thofe who follow -jflis plan againft eating heavy (uppers. The late Dr. Daultry of York, who was the firft thr.t brought th« Harrogdtc wrtrn. into n-pute, ufed to advife his patients to dr.nk a glafs before they went tr> bed ; the confe- quence of which was, that having eat a flefh fupper, and the water, operating in the ni( nt, they were often tormented with gripes and obliged to call for medical affiil- anee. £5® On COLD BATHING, aa* tire. No more ought to b* taken than barely to move the body ; nor is it always neceffary to carry it tiik length, provided the wa- ter gees off by the other emundorics, and doe* not occafion a chilnefs, or flatulency in the ftomach or bowels. When the water is intended to purge, the quantity mentioned above may be all ta- ken before breakfaft, I tvould not only caution patients who drink the purging miner- al waters over night to avoid heavy fugptrs, but alio from eating heavy meak at any time. The ftimulus of water, impregnated with falts, feems to create a foife appetite. I have feen a delicate per- fon, after drinking the Harrowgate waters of a morning, eat a fo<..akfaft fufficient to haveferved tAvo ploughmen, devour a plenti- ful dinner of fkfh and fifh, and, to crown all, eat fuch a fupper as might have fatisfied a hangry porter. All this, indeed, the ftomach feemed to crave ; but this craving had better remain not quite fatisfied, than that the ftomach fhould be loaded with what exceeds its power. To flarve patients was never my plan : but I am clearly of opinion, that, in the ufe of all the purging mineral waters, a hght and rather diluting diet is the moft proper ; and that no perfon, during fuch a courfe, ought to cat to the full extent to what his appstite craves. To promote the operation of mineral water, and to carry them through the fyftem, exercife is indifpenfably neceffary. This may be taken in any manner that is moft agreeable to the patient ; but he oupht never to carry it to eicefs. The beft kinds of exercife are thofe conneded with amufement. Every thing that tends to ex- hilirate fpirits, not only promotes the operation of the waters, but ads as a medicine. All who refor* to the mineral waters ought therefore to leave every care behind, to mix with the company,and to make themfelves as cheerful and happy as poffible. From this condud, affifted by the free and ivholeibme air of thofe fafhionable places of refort, and alfo the regular and early hours which are ufually kept, the patient often receives more benefit than from ufing the Avaters. But the greateft errors in drinking the purging mineral waters arife from their being ufed in cafes where they are abfolutely im- proper, and adverfe to the nature ofthe difeafe. When people hear of a wonderful cure having been performed by fome mineral wa- ter, they immediately conclude that it will cure every thing, and accordingly fwallow it down, when they might as well take poifon. <> Patients ought to be well informed, before they begin to drink the more adive kinds of mineral waters, ofthe propriety ofthe courfe, and fliould never perfift in ufing them when they are found to ag- gravate the diforder. In all cafes where purging is indicated, the faline mineral waters will be found to fulfil this intention better than any other medi- cine. This operation, if taken in proper quantity, is generally mild ; and they are neither found to irritate the nerves, nor debili- tate the patient fo much as the other purgatives. Asa purgative, thefe waters are chiefly recommended in difeafes •f the firft paffages, accompanied with, or proceeding hem, inac* UtitT DRINKING MINERAL WATERS. tivityof the ftomach and bowels, acidity, indigeftion, vitiated bile, worms, putrid fordes, the piles, and jaundice. In moft cafes of thfc kind they are the beft medicines that can be adminiftered. Butwhen ufed with this view, it is fufficient to take them tAviee, or at moft three times a-week, fo as to move the body three or four times ; and it will be proper to continue tin* courfe for feme weeks. But the operation of the moie adive mineral waters knot confin- ed to the bowels. They often promote die difcharge ef urine, and not unfrequendy increafe the perfpiration. This flews that they are capable of penetrating into every part of the body, and of ftim- ulating the whole fyftem. Hence arifes their efficacy in remov- ing the moft obftinate of all diforders, obftruaions of the glandular and lymphatic fyflems. Under tills clafs is comprehended die fcro- phula or King's evil, indolent tumours, obftrudions of the liver, fpleen, kidneys, and mcfenteric gland*. When thefe great purpof- es are to be effected, the waters muft be ufed in the gradual man- ner mentioned above, and perfifted in for a ler.gth of time. It will be proper however ncAv and then to difcontinuc their ufe for a few days. The next great clafs of difeafes where mineral waters are found to be beneficial, are dicfe of die fkin, as the itch, fcab, tetters, ringworms, fcaly eruptions, leprofies, blotches, foul ulcers, &c. Though thefe may feem fuperficial,yettheyare often the moft obfti- nate which the phyfician has to encounter, and not unfrequendy fet his fkill at defiaace : but they will fometimes yield-to the applica- tion of mineral waters for a fufficient length of time, and in moft cafes of diis kind thefe waters deferve a trial. The faline fulphure- ous waters, fuch as thofe of Moffat in Scotland, and Harrowgate in England, are the moll likely to fucceed in difeafes ofthe fkin ; but for this purpofe it will be neceffary notoaly to drink the waters,but Iikewife to ufe them externally. To enumerate more particularly the qualities of the different mi- neral waters, to fpecify thofe difeafes in which they are refpedively indicated, and to point out their proper modes of application,would be an ufeful, and by no means a difagreeable employment ; but as the limits prefcribed to thefe remarks will not allow me to treat the fubjed at more length, I fhall conclude by obferving, that whene- ver the mineral waters are found to exhauft the ftrength, deprefs the fpirits, take away the appetite, excite fevers, diftend die boAV- els, cr occafion a cough, they ought to badifcontinned. CHAP. ,*„ Tlisfe Cawtions having been printed and fold feparately for th« acrommo- daiion qf thofe who had purchafed tMe former editions of this book, has induced fo..:e perfons to confider them zs a treatife on fea-bathing and drinking the mineral Witer., ; whereas the author's fole inteniion was to furnifh a few general hints to per- iods who frequ-e:u thole fafhionable places of retort, witho.it puttir- theni'dve) «.(iir the care of a phyfician. As he loaks upon this fubjeft however to be ofi'.r gr,-aicft importance to the fi*k, he pledges himfelf to treat it at more length on a L>u Mire oceaiion. $9* OBSERVATIONS CONCERNING DIET, CHAP. LVI. OBSERVATIONS CONCERNING THE DUCT OF* THE COMMON PEOPLE, RECOMMENDING A METHOD OF LIVINO LESS EXPENSIVE, AND MORE CONDUS1YE TO HEALTH, THAN THE PRESENT. EXPERIENCE proves that not a feAV of the difeafes incident to the inhabitants of this country, are owing to their mode of living. The vegetable produdions they confume, fall confiderably fhort ofthe proportion which they ought to bear to the animal part of their food. The conftant ufe of bread, and animal fubftances, excites an unnatural thirft, and leads to theimmoderate ufe of beer and other ftimulating liquors, which generate difeafe and reduce the lower orders of people to a ftate of indigence. To teach the poor man hoAv to live cheaper and better, is the defign of the fol- lowing pages. Though the common people of this country live at a greater ex- pence than any where elfe, it does not follow that they live better. They are ftrong indeed, but by no means healthy ; and it is found that, from an attachment to a particular mode of living, they are more liable to difeafe and death in foreign climes, than the inhabi- tants of any other country. It is certainly proper that the pcoT man fhould be inftruded in every thing that can make his little earnings go as far as poffible, or which can add to the comfort of himfelf and family. Nor can ceconomy in living be deemed trivial, in a country where the riches depend on the cbeapnefs of labour. It is allcdged that the Englifh" are fo much attached to their own modes of living, that no argument w ill induce them to make the fimalleft change. Habits are indeed obftinate things, efpecially thofe Avhich relate to diet ; but there are proofs that the Englilh are not inflexible even in this matter. The mode of living among the lower orders has been gready changed in my time, and I am forry to fay, not for the better. The people of England have too much good fenfe not to liften to reafon provided due care were taken to inftrud them. But here the people may be truly faid "to perifh for want of knowledge." No means have been ufed to give them proper inftrudion. Hurtful cuftoms have been fuffered to prevail, till they have ftruck fuch deep root that it awII not be an eafy matter to eradicate them. The difficulty, however, is not unfurmountable. A few experi- ments of reform would have the effed to render it as agreeable as it is falutary. Adults have many old prejudices to overcome,but the cafe is dif- ferent in regard to children. They may be taught to ufe any kind »f food, and what they ufe when young they will love when old. If I caa OBSERVATIONS on ALIMENT. t can introduce a different method of feeding children, my purpofe will be anfAvered. This alone will, in time, effed a total change in the genetal mode of living. The late diftrefs of the poor has called forth many publications intended for their relief. Moft of them, however, were adapted only for the particular occafion, and not cajJ^Rited to prevent the return of like evils. The following obfervations. it is hoped, will have a more permanent effed. They are intended to recommend a plan of living, wiricliAviil render the people lefs dependent on bread and animal food, for* their fubfiftance, & confequently notfo liable to fuffer from a fc a re kg or dearth of either of thefe articles in future. Particular attenrion has been paid to the fubftkutes for bread, as the fcaroty of thi, article proves peculiarly dillreifing t« the poor. It will appear from :.u following pages, that bread is by no means fo much a neceffary of life as is generally imagined, and that its place may, in many mdances, be fopplkd by a variety of other fa- rinaceous fubftances. GENERAL OBSERVATIONS ON ALIMENT. NO creature eats fuch a variety of food as man. Intended for an inhabitant of every climate, he devours :he produdions of them all; and if they do not l'uit his pala; e, y agree with his ftomach, he calls ia the aid of cook., -y, an avt peculiar to himfelf; by Avhich many things that, in a crude fta*e, .-.ould pro\e hurtful, or even poifonous a^e rendered wholefome and falutary. The obvious divifion of food is into animal and vegetable. To fay that man was intended by nature for ufing either the one or the other alone, w mid be abfurd. His ftrudure and appetite prove that he was formed for both. Judgment, hoAvever, is requifite in adjufting the due proportions of each, fo as to avoid the inconveni- ences, arifing from an extreme on either hand. Though animal food is more nourifhing than vegetable, it is not fufj to live on that alone. Experience has fhcAVii that a diet con- iifting folely on animal food, excites thirft and naufea, occafions putrefcence in the ftomach and boAvek, and finally brings on violent griping pains with cholera and dyfentery. Animal food is lefs adapted to the fedentary than the laborious, and kaft of all to the ftudious, whofe diet ought to confift chiefly of vegetables. Indulging in animal food renders men dull, and unfit fo' the purfuits of fcience, efpecially when it is accompanied with the free ufe of ftrong liquors. The phetiv.ric or perfons of a full habit, fliould eat fparingly cf animal food. It yields far more blood than vegetables tukgn in the fame quantity, and of courfe may induce inflammatory diforders. It ads as a ftimulus to the Avhole fy-fem, by which means the circu- lation ofthe bfood is greatly accelerated. I air. inclined ro think, that confumptions, fo common in En?-* land. ...c inparto>ving to th; great ufe of animal food. Though B b b the 394 Of BREAD. the Ptithlfu Pulmonale is not, properly fpeaking, an ink.-.mmufory difeafe,yet it generally begins with fymptoms of inflammation, an* is often accompanied with them through its whole progrefs. But the difeafe moft common to this country is the fcurvy. One finds a dafh of it in almoft every family, and in fome the taint is very deep. A difeafe fo general muft have a general caufe, and there is none fo obvious as the great quantity of animal food de- voured by the natives. As a proof that fcurvy arifes from this caufe, Ave are in poffeffion of no remedy for that difeafe equal to the free ufe of frefh vegetables. By the uninterrupted Ufe of animal food a putrid diathefis is in- duced in the fyftem, which predifpofes to a variety of diforders. I am fully convinced that many of thofe obftinate complaints for which we are at a lofs to account, and find it ftill more difficult to Cure, are the effeds of a feorbutic taint lurking in the habit. Improper diet affeds the mind as well as the body. The chole- ric difpofition ofthe Englifh is almoft proverbial. Were I to ai- fign a caufe, it would be, their living fo much on animal food. There is no doubt but this induces aferocity of temper unknown to men whofe food is chiefly taken from the vegetable kingdom. Though thefe and fimilar confequences may arife from the excefs of animal diet, we are far from difcouraging its ufe in moderation. In ail cold countries it is certainly neceffary; but the major part of the aliment ought neverthelefs to confift of vegetable fubftances. There is a continual tendency in animal food, as Avell as in the hu- man body itfelf, to putrefadion, which can only be counteraded by the free ufe of vegetables. With regard to the proportion of vegetable food to that of animal, .great nicety is by no means required.lt muft vary according to the circumftanres, as the heat of the weather, the warmth of the cli- mate, and the like. The vegetable part, however, where nothing forbids, ought certainly to preponderate, and I think in fhe pro- portion of at leaft two to one. The exceffive confumption of animal food is one great caufe of the fearcity of grain. The food that a bullock affords bears but a fmall proportion to the quantity of vegetable matter he confumes. I am no enemy to good fiuft, as an article of diet; but the greater part of Avhat is ufed in this country, by the lower orders of the people, is mere trafh. Fruit ihouid be eaten in the early part of the day, AAlien the ftomach is not loaded Avith food, and it never ought to be eaten raw till it be thoroughly ripe. of bread: BRE \T>, or fomething refembling it makes a part of the diet of all nations. Hence it is emphatically- denominated theflvj'«f life. It may however be ufed too freely. The late Dr. kotheigilJ avus of ©pinion, and-1 perfoftiy agree with him, that moll people- :-.at more bread than Avas coiu'icfoe to health. I do not mtan to inllnuatc Chat bread is uiiuhokfome, but that the bell thin;, rr. g. \n -\ rinirt- Or BREAD. 395 ful when taken to excefs. A furfeit of bread is more dangerous than ot any other food. Omnis replctio mala, replctio pants pffima, The French confume vaft quantities of bread ;but its bad effeds are prevented by their copious ufe of foups and fruits, which have lit- tle or no fhare in the diet of the common people of England. One important ufe of bread is to form amafsfit for filling upthe allimentavy canal,and carrying the nutritious juices along that paf- fage in fuch a flats, as to render them fit to be aded upon by the ladeal obforbents, which take up the nourifhment and convey it to the blood. In this light bread may be confidered as a foil from whence the nourifhment js drawn. I do not fay that bread contains no nourifhment, but that its ufe, as an article of diet, does notfole- ly depend on the quantity of nutriment it contains,but in fome mea- fure on its fitnefs as a vehicle for conveying the nutritious particles through the intcftinal tubes. Hence it follow s, that the fineft bread is not always the beft adapted for anfwering the purpofes of nntri- tion. The richeft food will not nourifh an animal, unlefs the alimentary canal is fufficiently diftended. A dog has been fed on the richeft broth, yet could not be kept alive ; while another, which had only the meat boiled to chip and water, throve very well. This fhews the folly of attempting to nourifh men on alimentary poAvders and other concentrated food. The great art therefore of preparing food, is to blend the nutri- tive part of the aliment with a Sufficient quantity of fome light fari- naceous fubftance, in order to fill up the canal, without overcharg- ing it with more nutritious particles than arc neceffary for the fup- port of die animal. This may be done either by bread,or other fa- rinaceous fubftances, of which there is a great variety, as will ap- pear from the fequil. Bread is one ofthe moft cxpenfive modes of ufing grain, and not adapted to the narrow circumftances of the lower orders ofthepeo- ple, as it is burthenfome with too heavy additional charges, in paf- ing through the hands of both the miller and the baker. Befides, the former often grinds down extraneous matter with the wheat, and the latter a» frequently bakes it up with the addition of lime, chalk, album, and other pernicious fubftances. Since the articles of diet have become branches of manufadure, the public neidier know what they eat, nor what they drink. People imagine, as the fineft flour contains the greateft quantity of nourifhment, that it muft therefore be die moft proper for mak- ing into bread ; but this by no means follows. The fineft flour comes the neareft toftarch,which,though it may occafionally prove a good medicine,makes bad bread. Houfeholdbread,Avhich is made, by grinding doAvn the whole grain, and only feparating the coarfer bran, is without doubt the moft wholefome. The beft houfehold bread I ever remember to have ate, was in the county of York. It was what they call mefiin bread, and con- fided of wheat and rye ground together. I am not quire certain a/. to the proportron, but I think there might be tAvo parts ofthe for- mer to one ofthe latter. This bread when well fermented, eats 7ght v 39« Of BREAD. light, is of a pleafant tafte, and fiduble to the bowels. After ufing it for fome years, I found that bread made entirely of flour wa? neither fo agreeable to the palate, nor fo conducive to health. Bread is often fpoilcd to pleafe the eye. The artificially whiten- ed, drying, fluffing bread, though made of the heart of the wheat, is in reality the worft of any , yet «ftds is foe bread which moft peo- prefcr and the poorer lort will c it no other. All the different kinds of grain are occafionally made intobrcad fome giving preference to one and feme to anutherraccording toear- iy cuftom and prejudice. The people of South Britain generally prefer bread made ofthe fineft wheat flour, while thuie of the nor- thern counties eat a mixture of flour and oat meal, or ryemeal,and many give the preference to bread made of oatmeal alone. The common people of Scotland alfo eat a mixed bread, but more fre- quently bread of oatmeal only. In Germany the common bread is made of rye, and the American Labourer thinks nobradfo flrengthening as that which is made of Indian com; nor do imuch doubt but the Laplander thinks his bread made ofthe bones of fifties is the beft of any Bread made of different kinds of grain is more wholefome than v.hat is made of one only, as their qualities ferve to coned one a- nother. For example, wheat flour, efpecially the fineft kind, being of a flarchy nature, is apt to occafion conftipatir n. Bread made cf ryemeal, on the other hand, proves often tr.o foppery for the bow- els. A due proportion of thefe makes the beft bread. For the more adive and laborious I would recommend a mixture of rye with the ftronger grains, as peas, beans, barley, oats, Indian corn, and die like. Thefe maybe blended in many different ways: they make a hearty bread for*a labouring man, and to ufe his own language, they lie longer ©n his flomach than bread made of wheat flour only. Barley bread paffes too quickly through the aliment- ary canal to afford time for conveying the proper nourifhment; but bread made of barley mixed with pcafe is very nourifhing. When potatoes,or boiled grain, are ufed, bread ceafes to be a neceffary article of diet. During the late fcarcky of bread, I made it a rule not to cat above one half the quantity I ufed to do, and I found no inconve.niency whatever from the change. Nay, fome told me, that for a confiderable time they had left off the ufe of bread altogether, without experiencing any change in the ftate of their health. A great part ofthe breadconfumed in thiscountry is by children. It is always ready, and'when the child calls for food, a piece of bread is put into its hand, to fave the trouble of dreffing any other kind of viduals. Of many children this is the principal food, but it is far from being the moft proper. Children arc often troubled with acidities ofthe flomach and bovrels ; and it is well known that bread mixed with water, and kept in a degree of heat equal to that ofthe human ftomach foon turns four. During the late fcarcity, many of the labouring men, and even artificers, could not earn as much money as was fufficient to keep their families in the article of bread only. It is certain, hoAvever, that Cf BREAD. 39f that on a different plan, fuch families might have lived very com- fortably. Many of the articles of diet are cheaper than bread, and equally Avhokfomc. Above one half of the expence of living might be faved by a, due fokdion oftheartiek'- of diet. The Englifh labourer lives chiefly on bread, which being ac- companied with other dry, and often fait food, fires his blood, a:.d excites an unquenchable thirft, fo that, his perpetual cry is for drink. But the greateft confumption of bread is occoffered by tea. It is f.iid that the fuhjeds of Great Britain confume a greater quantity cf that herb, than the whole inhabitants of all the other nations of this quarter of the globe. The loweft woman in England muft have her tea, and the chlMren generally fhaie it with her. As tea contains no nourifhment, either for young or old, there muft of courfe be bread and butter to eat along Avith it. The quartern loaf will not go far among a family of hungry children, and if we add the cod of tea, fugar, butter, and milk, the expence of one meal will be more than Avill be fuflicient to fill their beliies Avith wholefome food three times a day. There is reafon to believe that one half the bread confumed in England is ufed to tea, Avithout an hearty meal over being made of it. Th? higher ranks ufe tea as a luxury, while the lower orders make a diet of it. I had lately occafion to fee a ft. iking inftance of diis in a family that was rcprefe ited to me as in diftrefs for want of bread. I fent them a little money, and was informed that they ran widi it diredly to the tea-ihop. A To a heavy, fluggifh, phlegmatic man, a moderate ufe of tea may not prove pernicious ; but where there is a debilitated ftomach and an irritability of fibre, it never fails to do much hurt. Widi many it has the effed to prevent fleep. Tea will induce a total change of conftitution in the people of this country. Indeed it has gone a great way towards effeding that evil already. A debility, and confequent irritability of fibre, are become fo common, that not only women, but even men, are affeded Avith them. That clafs of difeafes which for want of a better name, we call nervous, has made almoft a complete conqueft of the one'fex, and is making hafty flrides towards vanquifhing the other. Did Avomen know the train of difeafes induced by debility, and hoAv difagreeable thefe difeafes render them to the other fex, they would fhun tea as the niofl deadly poifon. No man can love a womaiie--it.cn up a-.fob, vapours, or Wiifhed down with difeafes arifing from relaxation. It is not tea taken as a beA-erage after a full rrtal or in a crowded affembly,that I fo much condemn, though I think fomething as el- egant and lefs pernicious might be fubftituted in its place. The mifchief occafioned by tea arifes chiefly from its being fubftituted for folid food. This'is fo much the cafe at pie *< id, thatdiadl time to fpare, f think it could not be better employed than writing a- gainft this deftrudivc drug. 3*8 9f BOILED GRAIN. Op BOILED GRAIN. THOUGH farinaceous, fubftances,of one kind or'another,make a neceffary part ofthe food of man, yet there can be no reafon why fuch fubftances fliould always affume the name and form ot bread. Many of diem are more wholefome, and not lefs agreeable in other forms. Bread is often ufed merely to fave the trouble of eookery ; and being portable, is the moft convenient article of diet for carrying abroad. It does not, however, admit cf a doubt,that mere grain is eaten boiled, though not in this country,than is made into bread ; and that this mode of cookery is the moft wholefome. Simple boiling pre - eludes all adulteration, and is an operation much lefs laborious and artificial than baking-. The moft general article of diet among mankind, is rice. This maybe made into a variety of difhes ; but fimple boiling is all that is required, to render it a proper fubflitute for bread. It may either be eaten alone or with milk. In the eaft, it is ufed with meat, in the fame manner as we do bread. The people of this country be- lieve that rice proves injurious to the eyes, but this feems to be without foundatioa, as it has »o fuch effed on thofe who make it the principal part of their food. Many other kinds of grain, will, when boiled, make good fubfti- tutes for bread. Even thofe which make a harfh and unpleafantfort of bread, are often rendered very palatable by boiling. This is the cafe with all the leguminous clafs of plants, as peafe, beans, &c. Even oats and barley are more agreeable, as well as more whole-* fome, when boiled, than made into bread. All alloAv that peafe and beans boild, when young, are a great luxury. But Avhen old, they are equally wholefome, and, Avhen properly cooked, by no means unpleafant. There are few who do not relifh peafe pudding, and even prefer it to bread. Beans are not fo fit for this purpofe ; but they make an excellent ingredient inthe poor man's broth, and whoever eats this broth will find little occa- fion for bread. Peafe and beans contain an equal quantity of fugar with wheat, Oats, or barley,and at the fame time agreater proportion of oil,con- fequently are more nourifhing. This fad is confirmed by daily ex- perience. On thofe farms where peafe and beans are raifed in great abun- dance, the labourers are much fed on that fort of grain ; but when removed to farms where they are fed with other kinds of grain.they foon complain of a diminution of ftrength, and requeft a fupply of peafe meal as formerly. Nature feems to have pointed out the propriety of the extenfive ufe of peafe and beans ; it being a fad, that when crops of that kind are duly alternated with crops of wheat, barley, or oats, the fertility of the foil may be maintained, without reft or manure, for many years together ; where as, if the latter be raifed, on the fame Of BOILED GRAIN. 391 foil for feveral years fucceflively, they render it barren, fo that, ivithout reft or manure, iu fertility cannot be preferved. The people in England are but little accuftomed to the ufe cf boiled grain, though in many countries it is eaten as a luxury. Boil- ad bailey is a great favourite with the Dutch, and is eaten with milk, butter, or mokiffes. It is the principal food of Dutch fai- lors, who, in general, are both healthy and robuft. Barley is one ofthe beft ingredients in foup. Count Rumford fays, it poffeffes the quality of lithing or thickening foups, in a fuperior degree to any other grain. We have reafon, hoAvever, to believe, that grits, or coarfe oatmeal, will anfwer that purpofe ftill better. ^Oatmeal is frequendy made into bread; but is much more wholefome, as well as agreeable food, when made into hafty pud- ding, and eaten with milk. The peafants in many parts of Britain make two meals a day of it, while their children almaft wholly fub- fift on it; and it is well known that both old and young who are thus fed, are healdiy and robuft. The opinion of oatmeal being heating, asd occafioning fkin dif- eafes, is wholly without foundation. Bread made of oatmeal, when not leavened, will fometimes occafion the heart-bum; but this is no proof of its heating quality. • Unleavened bread, of wheat or any other grain, produces the fame effed on a debilitated ftomach. Oatmeal thoroughly boiled feldom gives the heart-burn. Perfons who are fed on oatmeal bread, or hafty puddings, are not more fubjed to difeafes of the fkin, than thofe who live on wheat meal. Cutaneous diforders proceed more from the want of clean- linefs, dian from any particular aliment. The French, to far from dunking diat oatmeal is heating, fpeak of it as poffeffed of a cool- ing quality ; and even the Englifh give oatmeal, or grit gruel, to lying-in Women, and fick people of every defcription, Avhich fhews that they are*inconfiftent Avith themfelves, in alleging that the blood is fired by the ufe of oatmeal. A lieutenant ofthe army, reading at a country village within a few miles of Edingburgh, with a wife and ten children, having no other income than his half pay,fed the whole of his children with hafty pudding and butter-milk only, from a conviction that it W2S the moft Avholefome and full diet, that fell within the reach of his narrow circumftances. They greAr apace, and it Avas the univerfal remark ofthe neighbourhood, that they Avere asfprightly, healthy, and rob lift, as other children, and at the fame time perfedly free from all fkin difeafes. Children are feldom Well, unlefs their bodies are kept gently open. But this is more likely to be the cafe when fed on oatmeal and milk, than when their bellies are crammed with trafhy fub- ftance made ofthe fineft flour ; yet this in England is the common food of children. I have feen an infant fluffed four or five times a-day with this kind of food. Their needs no conjurer to tell the confequence. A late Author, a man of learning but the dupe of prejudice, has, by a ridkalous definition, endeavoured to reprefent oats as proper foorf 4-o« Or BUTTER. food for horfes only. I Avifh the horfesin England devoured a final- ler quantity of tiiat giain, and the people mure. Few thing-; Avould have a greater tendency to le.Ten the expence of living. The oats in North Britain are of a fupcrior quality, and I hope the people will long have the fenfe to ufe them as an article of diet. Indian corn ib Iikewife faid to make the bell food when boiled. Count Rumford obferves, that of all things it makes the bell pud- ding, and that he has made a hearty meal of it, fauce included, for five farthings. What makes good paddings will make good dump- iiugs, and thefe will, a: any time, fopply the place of bread. The Count alfo remarks, that the negroes inAmerka prefer Indian corn t> rice ; and that the Bavarian peafants prefer it to wheat; that it might be imported from North x\merica at about four or five fhil- lings p.r bulhel; that, when made into flour, it Avould coll only one pe.my farthing per pound; and that it is highly nutritious, and the cheapeft food known. During the late fcarcity a large quantity of this grain was imported; but fuch is the averfion of the common people of this country to every fort of food to which they are not accuftomed, drat they refufed to purchafe it, and the merchants A^ere very great }oltri, by the importation. On the fame principle the German, till Avithin thefe few years, could not be induced to cat potatoes, though now they are become extremely fond of them. The American, the Italian, and the German, all cook Indian corn, in the fame way as the North Briton does his oatmeal, by making it into haily pudding. It maybe eaten in a variety of ways. Some eat it with fauce compofed of butter and brown fugar, orbut* ter and molaifes. Others eat it with milk only. In either it makes a good cheap vmd wholefome diet, by no means difagreeable to thofe who are accuftomed to it. The only other grain we fhall mention as beft when boiled, is buck Avheat: It is of a very mucilaginious nature, and of courfe highly nutritious. In feveral paits of Europe, it conftitutes a princi- pal part of the food of the lower people. In former times it was eaten in Ruflia, not by the lower claifes only, even the nobility made ufe of it. Boiled, and then buttered, it was fo great a fa- vourite of the great Czar Peter, that he is faid to have feldom fupped on any thing elfe. OF BUTTER. IT has been faid that the Englifh have a tiioufand religions and but one fauce. It muft be allowed that they ufe butter with almoft every kind of food. Butter, though a good article of diet, may be ufed too freely,and in this country, I am convinced, that is the cafe. To Aveak flomachs it is hurtful, even in fmall quantities, and when ufed freely, it proves prejudicial to the ftrongefl. Butter, like other things of an oily nature, has a conftant tendency to turn rancid This procefs, by the heat ofthe ftomach, is greatly accelerated, infomuch that many pecgk, foon after eating butter, complain Or BUTTER. Q91 cgmplain of its rifing in their ftoir.achs, in a ftate highly difagree- able. Oils of every kind are with difficulty mixed with watery flu- ids. This is the reafon why butter floats on the flomach, and rifes in fuch an unpleafant manner. Perfons afflided with bile fliould ufe butter very fparingly. Some feeptical authors doubt Atrhether or not; aliment of any kind has an effed on the bile. One tiling, however is certain, that many pa- tients, afflided with complaints which Avere fuppofed to be occa- fioned by bile, have been completely cured by a total abftinence from butter. The moft violent bilious complaints that I ever met with, aacvc evidently occafionedby food that became rane'd on the flomacfoas the cholera morbus and the like. Nor can fuch complaints be cur- t*l, till the rancid matter is totally evacuated by vomiting andpurg- ing. Butfuppofing butter did not poflefs the quality of becoming ran- cidon the ftomach, it may neverthelefs, prove hurtful to digeftion. Oils of all kinds are of a relaxing quality, and tend to impede the adionof digeftion. Hence the cuftom of giving rich broths and fat meats to perfons Avho have a voracious appetite. The free ufe of butter, and other oily fubftances, not only tend; to relax the ftomach, and-impede its action, but to induce a debility of the folids, which paves the way to many maladies. In a coun- try where two thirds ofthe inhabitants lead fedentary lives, a debi- lity of fibre muft predominate. Whatever inereafes that debility ought to be avoided. Children, without exception, are difpofed to difeafes arifing from relaxation. Butter of courfe, ought to be given to them with a fparing hand. But is this the cafe ? By no means. Bread and but- ter conftitutes a great part of the food of children, and I am con- vinced that the grofs humours Avith which they are frequently troubled, are partly oav ing to this food. As children abound with moifture, bread alone is, generally fpeaking,better for them than bread and butter. I liave been aftonifhed to fee the quantities of butter eaten by grofs Avomen Avho lead fedentary live;. Their tea bread is general- ly contrived fo as to fuck up butter like a fpunge. What quanti- ties of crumpets and muffins they will devour in a morning;, foaked with this oil; and afterwards complain of indigeftion, when they have eaten what ivould overload the ftomach of a ploughman. Dr. Fothergiil is of opinion, that butter produces the nervous or fick headach.fo common among the women of this country. As a proof of this, it is often cured by an emetic. Oils, in certain quantities, excite naufea, and even vomiting. They muft of courfe prove unfriendly to digeftion. A Dutch fail- or, Ave are told, can digeft train oil. So may an Englifh failor j but it would be very improper food for a London lady. To fome ofthe leaner farinaceous fubftances, as die potatoe and the like,buttcr makes a very proper addition; but eating it to flefh and fifh of almoft every defcripticn, is certainly wrong. The fleih eaten in this country is generally fat enough widiout the addition, Ccc ef 4«2 Of FRUITS akb ROOTS. of butter, and the more oily kinds of fifli, as falmon or herrings, are lighter on the ftomach, and eafier digcfted when eaten without it. Butter israther a grofs food, and fitted for the athletic and labo- rious, than the fedentary and delicate. It is lefs hurtful when ea- ten frefh than faked. Salt butter certainly tends to induce fkin difeafes, and I am inclined to think, the free ufe of it at fea may have fome fhare in bringing on that dreadful malady, fo deftrudive to our brave failors, thefeafcurvy. There is a method of rendering fait butter lefs hurtful,but it feems »ot to be known in England. What I mean is to mix it with an equal quantity of honey, and keep it for ufe. In diis w-.y it may be given to children with greater freedom. In Nordi Britain this method of mixing butter with honey is well known, and from a common proverb, I take the cuftom to be very ancient. Butter, in itfelf, is not near fo hurtful, as when combined with certain other things. For example : bread made with butter is al- moft indigeftible, and paftries of every kind are little better; yet many people almoft live upon paftry, and it is univerfally given to children. It is little better, however than poifon, and never fails to diforder their flomachs. The fond mother cannot pafs a paftry fhop without treating her darling boy with fome of the dainties, and then wonders how he got the cough, or cholic. I have known a man feemingly in perfed health, who by eating a penny-Avorth of paftry, as he paffed along the ftreet, was feized with foch an afthmatic fit, that he was obliged to be carried home, and had neaily loft his life. This occured^ whenever he inadvertent- ly ate any thing baked with butter. Every thing diat proves very injurious to health ought, as far as poffible, to be prohibited, by laying a high duty upon it. A duty on paftry would be ferving the publick in more refpeds than one. It would fave many lives; and leifen fome tax on neceffaries Cheefe, as a diet, is Iikewife injurious to health. It fliould ne- ver be eaten but as a defert. It occafions conftipation, fires the blood, and excites a conftant craving for drink. It is very impro- per for fedentary, and hardly to be digefted even by the athletic. If men will live on dry bread, poor cheefe, fait butter, broiled bacon, and fuch like parching food, they wiii find their way to the ale-houfe, the bane of the lower orders, and the fource cf half the beggary hi the nations. OF FRUITS AND ROOTS. Fruits and roots form a large clafs of fuhftitutc; for bread. The latter, being produced under ground, arc lefs liable to fuffo- from the inclemency of the feafons than grain. Men who v.iih to inflame the minds ofthe multitude may inveigh againft the fubftitutes for bread; but reafon and found fenfe fay, the more fobftkutes for bread the better. When one fails, lecouife can be had to another. Ia Of FRUITS and ROOTS. AM In warm climates the inhabitants have many fubftitutes for bread, and as their feafons are more uniform than ours, they can gener- ally depend on the plant, or whatever it is, proving produdive. The plantain tree, commonly called the indian fig, which has from time immemorial been cultivated in South America, bears fruit of a fweetifh tafte, which will diffolve in the mouth without chewing. It is eaten either raw, frkd or roafted. When intended to fupply the place of bread, it is gathered before it is ripe, and eaten either boiled or roalled. The banna is nearly ofthe fame nature, but its fruit is greatly fupcrior both in tafte and flavour. The inhabitants of the South Sea, or Ladrone iflands, are fupplied with bread from a tree, which has been lately imported into our Weft India Iflands, and will it is Loped, be found to anfwer the fame purpofe there. It has a flight degree of fweetnefs, but not much flavour. It refembles new bread, and requires to be roafted before it is eaten. Thofe who have tailed it fay, that it is in no refped fupcrior to the pot.ttoe. In fome of the Weft India iflands the inhabitants fupply the place of grain by making bread from the root of a fhrub called the caffada, or caffava. Though, to my tafte, diis bread is very iniip- id, yet the natives are fond of it to fuch a degree that I have known fome of them eat it, during their refidence in England in prefer- ence to the fineft London bread. But the moft general fubftitutes for bread in the Weft Indies are the yams. There are three different fpecies of this plant, the roots of which are promifcuoufly ufed for bread. They are laid to be very nutritious, of eafy digeftion, and A\-hen properly dreffed, are by fome preferred to the beft wheaten bread. The tafte is fome- what like the potatoe, but more lufcious. The negroes generally eat them boiled, and beaten into a mafh. The white people have them ground into flour, and make bread and puddings of them. They can be preferved for fevetal feafons, without lofing any cf their primitive goodnefs. Of all the fubftitutes for bread in Europe, the potatce k the moft extenfively ufeful. This plant is a native of Peru, and has been in Europe about two hundred years. Like moft other important dif- eoveries, it made but a flow progrefs, and is ftill tar from being fo generally cultivated as it deferves to be. It is indeed known in moft parts of Europe, but its culture is beft underftood in Ireland and the northern parts of England. At Harwich, however, the preference is given to the Dutch potatoes,brought over by the pack- ets between diat place and Helvoet Sluys. There is a light fandy foil in Holland very favourable to the culture of that iiicftimable root. As this plant thrives in eve. v ti71,and feidomfuffers from the in- clemency of the feafons, v. c rs&Cc blame ourfelves if we fuffer a fam- iae to cxift. Indeed ue fuch thing ever can be, where due attention is paid to the culture of __ .tatoes. A far greater quantity of fari- naceous food can be railed on an acre of ground planted Avith po- tatoes, than fown with any kind of grain. It is not uncommon to ha,ve areturn of forty for one.-.They are not fo hearty a food as corn, but no mail will ever perifh for hunger who can have potatoes. Potatoe 4»4 Of FRUITS and ROOTS. Potatoes abound with an infipid juice, which induces fome fo think that they are not very nutritious. Fads, however, are againk this opinion. Some ofthe rtouteft men aat: know, arc brought up on milk and potatoes. Dr. Pearfon, who has beftoAved fome pains in analizing this root, fays, that potatoes and water alone, Avith com- mon fait, can iiourifh men completely. They differ in colour and confiftence, but not materially with regard tf> their nutritive quali- ties. Some think the firm kind are the moft nutritious ; but the Irifh, who muft be good judges, give the preference to the mealy. Tht difference, however, depends much on the mode of cooking them. More than half the fubftance of potatoes confifts of water, and experience fhews, that that mode of cooking, which moft diminifhes their mcftlure, is to be preferred. In London they arc drenched in water and wafhed before they are brought to market, which ac- counts, in a great meafure, for the bad quality of the London po- tatoes. They are dreffed in a variety of ways, but fimple boiling or roafting feems to be all the cooking they require, to render them a proper fubftitute for bread. Sorrie are fond of making bread of them. This, in my opinion, is marring both. Why manufadure any thing into bread, which requires only the aid of fire to make it fuch ? Nobody thinks of making dough of the bread fruit ;but the potatoe might with as great propriety be called the bread root, as' it is made into bread by the fame procefs. Stewed mutton and- potatoes make not only a nourifhing but a vfery palatable difh. The excefs ofthe fat of the mutton,which when ethenvife cooked fuftains great lofs, is thus preferved, by being ab- forbed by the potatoes. It is, hoAvever, to be obferved, that when potatoes are ufed in broth or flews, they ought previoufly to be boiled, and the Avater thrown away, as it contains fomething delete- reous. Simple boiling or roafting is fufficient to prepare potatoes to fupply the place of bread, but when they are intended to ferve as a meal, they require fomething of a foftening nature, as milk, butter, or both. What a treafure is a milch cow and a potatoe gar- den, to a poor man with a large famfly, who lives in the country { Yet, with a little attention from landlords and farmers, almoft eve- ry man might be fo accommodated. What a fource of real wealth and population ! Men would multiply, and poverty, unlefs among the profligate, be unknown. Horfes are fometimes fed with pota- toes, and become very fond of them. With the addition ot a fmall quantity of hay, they are found to be fufficiently nourifliing. I would beg leave to recommend, both to landlords and farmers, a careful perofal of Earl WincheVfea's excellent letter to Sir John Sinclair, on the advantages of cottagers renting lands. This hu- mane Nobleman takes up the matter in a truly patriotic light, and fliews that farmers, inftead of leffening the number of poor, do ev- ery thing they can to multiply them ; and I am forry to fay that, fo far as my obfervation goes, it agrees entirely with his lordfhip's, Some think that the potatoe,unlefs it is made into bread,will not feeep. An accident taught mc the contrary; Many yaars ago a friend Of FRUITS an© ROOTS. 405 triend ef fiiTne fent me a potatoe, after it had been roafted in an oven, on account of its fingular figure. I laid it on a fhelf among fome other things of the like kind, and was furprifed on removing them many years after,to find the potatoe quite freih though as dry as a bone. On grating it down it was perfectly fweet; and as fit for making foup as the day it was roafted. I apprehend that no- thing made into bread would have kept fo long. Pofterity will hardly believe that a fearcity cf bread could befek in Britain, at a time when it was knoAvnthat a fufficient quantity cf farinaceous food could be raifed in one county for the inhabitants oi the whole Ifland. Let proper encouragement be given to the cul- ture of potatoes, and fet famine at defiance. Many other domeftic roots, fprouts,&c,'are very wholefome, and may occafionally iupply the place of bread. Of thefe Mr. Bryant of Norwich reckons above forty ; but we fhall only take notice, by way of fpecimen, of the moft ufeful and produdive. It is worthy of remark, that no nation can be very populous, which does not draw a great part of its food from under ground. The Jerufalem artichoke is a native of Brazil, but having been long cultivated in this country, it is too well known to need any defcription. From its tafte,which is like that of artichoke bottom ., it Avould feem to be nutritious, and as far from being unpleafant to die pallate. Some reckon it windy, but this may be correded in die cooking, by warmfpices, and as the plant is very produdive,we would recommend it to be ufed in the faffie manner as potatoes and the other farinaceous roots. Ofthe efeulent roots in this country, the parfnip is reckoned the moft nourifhing. It is Iikewife of eafy digeftion, and is agreeable to moft palates. Some indeed diflike it on account of its fweetneis ; but that is a proof of its nutritive quality ,fugar being the moft nou- rifhing thing in nature. We are told that, in the north of Ireland, the poor people make beer from this root. There is not any plant that affords a more ftriking proof of the benefits of culture than the turnip. In its Avild ftate it is good for litde or nothing ; but, when properly cultivateddt not only affords wholefome nouriflimeut for man, but furnifhes the principal winter foodfor cattle. There is a fpecies of this plant which grows ia North britain, called the yellow turnip, which is fweet, and cf a foperior quality to thofe produced in the fouth, particularly about London, which are bitter and ftringy. The yellcxrv turnip is the moft nourifhing, and alfo the moft hardy in fuftaining the winter. It is eaten with milk to cure the confumption and fcurvy.Margraaf fays, he could exttad no fugar from the turnip, which affords ground to conclude, that it is net fo nutritive as certain other roots. Not only the root of the turnip, but the tops, when young, make very pleafant greens. The fpiouts, if gathered when very tender, make an excellent fallad. The carrot, like the turnip, is good for little in its natural ftate, being fmall, tough and ftringy. Manured it grows large, fuccukiit and of a pleafant flavour. It ought, however, to be eaten young, Qtherwife it lies on the ftomach, and is hard of di&eilkii.
