Salfafy, ikirrets, and the feveral kinds of beets, are all pleafant and nourifliing. They are Iikewife of eafy digeftion, and may be <ireffed in a variety of ways. Margraaf has by experiment difcov- ered, thai both ikirrets and beets contain a confiderable quantity of fugar. Though the extrading a faccharine fait from thefe plants may be no objed while we poffefs the Weft-India Iflands, yet it ferves to fhew that they poffefs a quantity of nutritious matter, fuf- ficient to give them a rank among the articles calculated to fupply the place of bread. The onion, we are told, was a great favourite in Egypt four thoufand years ago, and Dr. Haffelquefl fays, it is not to be won- dered at, for whoever has tailed the onions of Egypt muft allow, that none can be better in any part of the globe. There, he fays, they are fweet, though in many countries they are ftrong andnau- feous. There they are foft,whereas in northern countries they are hard ; and their coats fo compad, that they are difficult to digeft. This very quality may however recommend themin countries where food is fcarce. The Dodcr obferves, that the Turks eat them roaft- ed with their meat as we do bread^and are fo fond of them that they wifh to be indulged with this difh in paradife. From the Dodcr's account one would be induced to believe,that the onion ufed in Egypt was of a different fpecies from ours ; but I am rather inclined to think it may depend on the mode of culture,as well as on the warmth of c'timate and the difference of foil, as we find in the fouthern parts of Europe they are milder than in the more northernly. In Spain they are very mild, and a root weigh- ing tAvo pounds will grow from a fingle feed. Onions are dreffed in a variety of ways, but, in regard to whole- fomenefs, there is no method better than fimple boiling. By this method of cooking, they are rendered mild, of eafy digeftion, and gc off without leaving any difagreeable heat in the ftomach or bow- els. Many fhun them on account ofthe ftrong difagreeable fmell they communicate to the breath. Mr. Bryant fays,this may be re- medied, by eating a few raw parfley leaves immediately after,which will effcdually overcome the feent of the onions,and Iikewife caufe them to fit more eafy on the ftomach. The leek is genet ally reckoned among pot-herbs ; but as the root is the part chiefly ufed, the confideration of it comes under the pre- fent head of difcuffion. Indeed,it is as properly a root as the onion, which grows chiefly above ground. The leek, as well as die onion, is faid to be a conftant difh at thetables of the Egyptians,who chop them fmall, and eat them with their meat. The leek is ufed as a pot-herb in moft parts of Britain, efpecially m Wales, where the natives are faid to be fond of it. In Scotland a full grown fowl and a fmall piece of fait beef, fleAved with a large quantity of leeks, is a very favorite difh. In my opinion the leek is not fo generally ufed any wliere as it deferves tc be. There is no ingredient goes into foup that is more wholefome, or that gives Et.-a, better flavour,than leeks. They are in many refpeds medicinal, and Of BROTHS akb SOUPS. 4«j and to my tafte, as an ingredient in foups, they are greatly fuperior to the onion, or any other pot herb whatever. It is a fad worthy of obfervation, that the boiling of vegetable fubftances thoroughly, extricates a confiderable quantity of air uid makes them lefs liable to produce flatulency. I could mention a great many more efculent plants which mig.it occafionally fupply the place of bread, but the above fpecimenu fuflicient to fheAvhow liberal nature is in fupplying man with food„ provided he will take the trouble of cultivating and cooking it. Mr, Bryant, in his hiftory of efculent plants, enumerates above -four hundred and fifty, each of which affords a wholefome nourifhment, and may occafionally be ufed in place/of bread. OF BROTHS AND SOUPS. THESE may Iikewife be confidered as fubftitutes for bread. If properly made they will ferve both tor bread and drink. Though broth is a difh of the greateft antiquity, and may be confidered as extremely delicious, yet it is not a favourite in this country. Here the people are fond of what they call folids ; yet thofe very folids they make into broth, by fwallowing as much drink after them as they can get. The only difference is, the foreigner makes his broth in a pot, and the Englifhman makes his in the flomach. A very fenfible anonymous writer obferves, that in England a pound of meat makes fimply a pound of food; Whereas in any other country in Europe, that quantity of animal food,Avhcn fteAved down with vegetables and Scotch barley, will produce an ample meal for half a dozen people. Hence he juftly infers that among the variety of fchemes which may have been devifed by the humane for relieA> ing the diftrefses of the poor, a better and more enter.five charity cannot be devifed than that of iniliuding them in a new mode of cookery. The fame author adds, that the refult of his experiments on this fubjed had exceeded his moil fanguine expedations, and that each day gave him frefh proofs ofthe excellency of his plan for teaching the poor and needy to find themfelves in a wholefome and palata- ble diet, at the cheapeftlrate, in which little or no bread was requir- ed. He concludes by afferting that there is fcarce a place in diis kingdom where twenty perfons may not have a wholefome, hearty, and palatable meal for three fhillings. This anonymous letter is followed by one from Colonel Poynter, two from Dr. Johnfton, ofthe royal hofpital at Haflar, addrelfed to Admiral Waldegrave, and one from the Admiral himfelf, writ- ten for infertion in a public paper. They contain a A'ariety of re- ceipts for making cheap, wholefome, and nourifhing difhes for the poor. Tliefe difhes confift chiefly of broths, foups and flews, or what the'y call pottage, and are calculated to make a hearty and plentiful meal without bread or drink. 1 am inclined to pay the more attention to thefe letters, as they feem all to have been written by gentlemen of obfervation. The pamphlet is fold byLongm.m andDebret, for a charitable price, at, the fmall priee of three pence. The r 4*3 Or BROTHS anb SOUPS. , The writer who has paid moft attention to the improvement of cookery for the benefit of the poor, k Count Rumford. In his eco- nomical and philofophical effays, he has given fuch a variety of forms for making Avholefome, cheap and nourifhing fourj, flews, and other difhes for common ufe, that little more feems neceffary to be faid on the fubjed. I lhall only obferve that the mode of living on broths, foups, hafty-pudding, aad fuch like, fo warmly and juft- ly recommend by the Count, has been pradifed in the northern parts of this kingdom from time immemorial. There the food of the common people is hafty-pudding Avith milk for breakfaft and fupper, and broth,Avith vegetables and meat, for dinner. The poor- fort often make broth without meat ; but they all ufe vegetables in great abundance, and fometimes they fupply the place of meat with butter. As the hafty pudding and milk make a compleat meal, no bread is neceffary cither at fupper or breakfaft ; nor is much requir- ed at dinner, as the broth is made thick with bailey, cabbage, and a variety of other vegetables or pot-herbs. Cabbage is a favourite ingredient in the Scotchman's brotfo It is feldom madeAtithout this article, which is not eaten fo early as in England. It is there fuffered to groiv to maturity, and when that is the cafe there is no plant more produdive. This the Germans know well, and make it into four crout, one of the beft antidotes againft the fcurvy Avith which avc are acquainted. This kind of diet not only faves bread but drink. The labourer who lives on hafty-pudding and foups, feldom has occafion for drink ; while he who is burnt up with dry bread and cheefe, or fait meat broiled, has a continual thirft, and fpenels the greater part of his earnings in liquor. This, by ading as a powerful ftimulus, may make him do more work for fornetime, but it generally cuts him off in the middle of his days. The Englifh labourer, who works hard and drinks hard, feldom lives long, and is an old man when he fhould be in his prime. The roafting of meat is a wafteful mode of cookery, which ought to be avoided by the poorer fort of people, as much of the fubftance, and the moft nutritive parts, are loft by fcorching, and fly off by evaporation* 1 knoiv it avIII be faid, that I recommend flops in place of folid food. They are fuch flops, however, as the greateft heroes of an- tiquity lived upon; and tho' 1 havevifited moft parts ofthe ifland, I know of no b:t( ter men than thofe who live in the manner defcrib- cd above, nor aie the people any where more healthy,or longer lived. Broth is not only a diih of great antiquity, but one'that can be made in a great variety of ways. It receives into its compofition animal and vegetable fubftances of every kind that are ufed in diet, and it may be feafoned fo as to fuit cA-ery palate. Indeed, people early accuftomed to eat broths properly made, aie generally fond of them for their Avhole lives. It Avould be difficult to affign a reafon why the inhabitants of South Britain fhould diflike a difh fo much, reiiihed by other na- tions. Cuftom, no doubt:, fettles all thefe thkgs; but Iioav culloms arife is not fo clear a matter, If an alteration in diet is to be intro- duced 0» BROTHS ahd SOUPS. 4«* cWed with effed, it muft begin with children. Whatever men are accuftomed to eat when young, they generally prefer for the reft of their lives. Were the children in South Britain taught to eat hafty- pudding, with milk, for breakfaft and fupper; and broth with ve- getables and meat boiled in it, for dinner, they would relifh thefe difhes as long as they lived, would find little occafion for bread,and ftill lefs for drink ; and would thrive better than on their prefent food. What parents love themfelves, they generally give to their chil- dren, without any regard to its being proper for them or not. I have feen a father who was fond of ftrong beer, make his fon, an infant,guzzle it at every meal ; and the mother who delights in tea, does not fail to give it to her daughter whenever fhe eakcs it to her- felf. By this condud, the fon becomes a tippler, and daughter ftps tea in place of folid food, until fhe is eaten up with vapours and o* ther nervous diforders. Count Rumford fays, brown foup is the common breakfaft ofthe Bavarian peafants, to which they occafionally add bread. This he avers is infinitely preferable in all refpeds to that pernicious wafh, tea, with which the lower claffes of the inhabitants of this Ifland drench their ftomachs and ruin their conftitutions. He adds, that a fimple infufion of this drug, drank boiling hot, as the poor gener- ally drink it is certainly poifon, which, though it be fometimes flow in its operatioa.never fails toprodueefatal effeds,even in the ftrong - cft conftitution, where a free ufe of it is continued for a confiderable lengdi of time. The German on his polenta, the American on his mufh, and the North Britain on his hafty-pudding, can make a hearty breakfaft for a tenth part of what a tea-breakfaft would coft, while it is infinitely more wholefome. It has Iikewife the advantage that no bread i§ neceffary. I have been often told, when recommending foups to the poor, that they had not time to make them,and that they could not afford fuel on account of its price, as it is dear in great towns. They can however, find fuel twice a-day to boil a tea-kettle, and time to make the tea, which is a more tedious operation, by far, than making a* Utiefs of hafty-pudding. For a great part of the yeareven the poor* eft perfon muil have a little fire ; and it would require no more to make a comfortable mefs of foup, which is always beft when made with a flow fire. The mode of living that I would recommend to the lower orders of the pcople,with a view to fave expence and improve their health, is to fubftitute occafionally other farinaceous fubftances in the place of bread, as potatoe, Sec. to give up in a great meafure the ufe of roaft- ed, baked,and broiled meats, and to fupply their place with broths, foups, flews, and foch like, made with a little meat and plen- ty of vegetables ; to give to children, and to grown people who will eat it, for breakfaft, milk-porridge,or hafty-pudding with milk, fmall beer,or molaffes. This will be found a more wholefemebreak- full than tea, Avhile it is much cheaper and requires no bread*. CON. * The celebrated Dr. Huftlantl, in his art of prolonging life, fayi, the moderate ■ fe ot foup. is certainly *ot hurtful ; and it is (jugular that people fc«uld ima- D^a fine 4*» CONCLUSION. CONCLUSION. Although the place of bread may be occafionally fupplkd by farinaceous roots and other vegetables, yet we would by no means wifh todifcourage the culture of grain. The culture of grant is the culture of men. While the hufbandman is ram ng food for his fel- loAv-creatures, he is laying the foundation of health and longevity to himfelf and his offspring. Innumerable benefits are conneded with the culture of grain. While the artificer is fitting in fome a Awkward pofture, breathing confined and perhaps contaminated air, the cultivator ofthe foil rifes with the iun,eats his wholefome meal of milk and farinaceous food, hies him to the field, where he fpends the day in.ufeful labour, inhales the frefh breezes, and at eve re- turns home wkh a keen appetite, to enjoy his fimple repaft and found repofe. It has been faid,as artificers Can earn more money than thofe who cultivate the ground, that arts ought to be encouraged, and grain, if neceffary, imported. No manufodure is equal to the manufac- ture of grain. It fupplies food for man and beaft, while the furplus, by being exported, enriches the nation. Nor is it fubjed to the un- certainty of other manufadures. They often depend on fafliion and caprice, but the neceffaries of life will ahvays find their value fome- where. Tho' I am convinced that fome regulations are wanting for the encouragement of agriculture, I do not confider it as my pro- vince to didate the wifdom of th*legislature^ They know their duty and I have reafon to believe that they are inclined to pay it all due attention! I will venture hoAvever to aftert, that if proper encouragement were given to agriculture,Britain would at all times not only have a fofficiency of grain for her own confumption, but a furplus for exportation. This would contribute more to her real wealth, the happinefs of her people, and the liability of her government than either the increafe of her trade, the flourifhing of her manufadures, ©r the e::;eauon of her territory. It gine it tends too much to relax the ftomicb. Doe* not all oftr Jrink, even though cold, become- in a few mint tes a kind of warm foup in the flomach ; and does not the ftomach retain the fame temp«ij:uie during the whole day ? ]ic carchil only net to ule it hot, in toe great quantity at one time, or too watery. It is attended eve* Avi:!i great advantage.?. It fupplies the place of drink,particularly to men of lcttcis, women, and all thofe who do n.a diin>,or dri ik very little except ot table,and who when they give over foup, receive into their blood to > little moillv.re. And it is here to Uc remarked, that fluids ufed in the form oi fojps unite much better and iooncr with our juices than when chunk cold and ia a'. On this recount foup is a great preventive cf dryneis and rigidity in tin; body, and therefore, the 1 e,l i,ourifhrr.-nt iu. old people, andthofewho pro of an arid temperament. It even fupplies the j/.cc of medicine. After catching cold,in nervous head-achs,cholics, and c liferent kinds cf cramp in the flomach, warm foup is of excellent fervice. It in, y ferve as a proof ofthe utility, or at leall harmlelfncfs qf lou>v-. hen I remark that run forefathers,whi* certainly u„u more ftrength than we. CONCLTJTZC& #ii It is a matter of real regret and wonder that Britain, at a time when agriculture is cultivated as a fcience, fhould not be able to raife grain for the fupply of her own inhabitants, but become every year more dependent on foreign ftates for even the r.eceffaries of life. Until an adequate remedy can be found for this growing evil, the free ufe of the various fubftitutes for bread cannot fail to alleviate the calamities of the poor, and to reduce doe price of labour. The great confumption of animal food,and the immenfe number. of horfes kept in this country, are to be reckoned among the caufes ofthe fcarcity of grain. Mr. Mackie computes the number of horfes in this country to be about two millions, and that every horfe, oil an average, confumes the produce of three fertile acres : confe- quently the produce of fix millions.of fertile acre3 is annually con- fumed by horfes. Thefe Avould produce a quantity of grain more than fufficient to maintainhalf the inhabitants of Great-Britain. Two hundred and fixty thoufand cf thefe animal* are kept for pleafure. I fhall be told that they contribute to health. That I deny. Did our ladies of fafnion and fine gentlemen make ufe of their limbs, inftead of being dragged about in carriages, they would both bene- fit tiiemfelvea, and the public. I fhall conclude the remarks with the advife of die humane and benevolent Thomfem : " Yeg«n'rous Eritcr.s 1 venerate the plough, ''; And o'er your hills End long withdrawing vales " Let autumn fpread hertreafurcs to the fen " Luxuriant and unbounded. As the fea "Jar through his azure turbulent domain " Yeur empire owns, and frena a thoufand flioros " Wafts all the pomp of life into yctir ports ; " So with fupcrior boon m*y your rich foil " Exub'rant Nature's better bleffings pour " O'er ev'ry land, the naked nations clothe, " And be thecxhate'Jeflsgran'ry of a world. '' APPENDIX. Containing a list of*iM?Lzsand of fuch MEDICINAL » PREPARATIONS as ought to be kept in rcauinefs f for private practice : The method of preparing and compounding foch MED- ICINES as are recommended in the former part ofthe book, xviihthe addition of feveral others of a fimilar na- ure. Remarks on the DOSES, USES, and manner of apply- ing the different preparations. Medietmcntorum varietas ignorentiafliu eft.—Bacoh. INTRODUCTION.' IGNORANCE and fuperftition have attributed extraordinary medical virtues to almoft eArery produdion of nature. That fuch virtues were often imaginary, time and experience have fufficiently fliewn. Phyficians, however, from a veneration for antiquity, ftill retain in their lifts of medicim: many things which owe their repu- tation entirely to the fuperftition and credulity of our anceftors. The inftruments of medicine will always be multiplied, in pro- portion to men's ignorance of the nature and caufe of difeafes; when, thefe are fufficiently underftood, the method cf cure wiii be fimple and obvious. Ignorance of the real nature and permanent properties of thofe fubftances employed in the cure of difeafes, is another reafon vriiy they have been fo greatly multiplied. Phyficians thought they could effed by a number of ingredients, what could not be done by any one of them. Hence arofe thofe amazing farragoes which have fo long difgraced the medical art, and which were efleemed power- ful in proportion to the number of fimples that entered their com- pofition. The great variety of forms into which almoft every article of medicine has been manufadured, affords another proof of the im- perfedion ofthe medical art. A drug which is perhaps moft effi- cacious in the fimplcftform in which it can be adminiftered,hasbeen neverthelefs ferved up in fo many different fhapes, that one would be induced to think the whole art of phyfic lay in exhibiting medi- cine under as many different modes as poflibk. Different forms of medicine, no doubt, have their ufe; but they ought never to be wantonly increafed. They are by no means fo neceflary as is generally imagined. A few grains ofpoAvdered rhubarb, jalap,or ipecacuanha, will adually perform all that can be done by the different preparations of thefe roots, and may alfo be exhibited in as fafc and agreeable a manner. The fame obfervation hold! INTRODUCTION.' At a holds widi regard to the Peruvian bark, and msny other fimples, of which the preparations are>very numerous. Multiplying the ingredients of a medicine, not only renders it more expenfive, but alfo lefs certain, loth in its dofe and operation. N ir is this all. The compound, when kept, is apt to fpoil, or ac* quire qualities of a different nature. When a medicine is rendered more fafe, efficacious, or agreeable, by the addition of another, they ought, no doubt, to be joined; in all other cafes, they arc bet- ter kept afunder. The combination of medicines embarrafles the phyfician,.and retards the progrefs of medical knowledge. It is impt .Tilde to afcertain the precife effed of any one medicine, as long as it is combined with others, either of a iimilar or diflimilatf nature. In the exhibition of medicfn:, regard fhould not only be had to fimplicity, but Iikewife to elegance. Patients feldom reap much benefit from tilings that are highly difagreeable to their fenfes. To tafte or fmell like a drug, is become a proverb; and to fay truth, there is too much ground for it. Indeed no ait can take away the difagreeable take and flavour of fome drugs, without entirely des- troying their efficacy, it is poffible, however, to render many medi- cines lefs difguftful, and others even agreeable; an objed highly deferving the attention of all who adminifter medicine. The delign of the following pages is,to exhibit fuch a lift of drugs and medicines as may be neceffary for private pradice. They are confiderably more numerous indeed than thofe recommended in the former part of the Book, out are ftill greatly within the number contained in the niofl reformed difpenfatories. The fame medicine is feldom exhibited under different forms; and Avhere different me- dicines anfwer neatly the fame intention, there is commonly no more than one of them retained. Multiplying forms ef medicine for the fame intention tsnds rather to bewilder than affift the young praditioner, and the experienced phyfician can never be at a lofs to vary his prefcriptions as occafion requires. The chemical and other difficult preparations are for the moft part emitted. All of them that are ufed by any private praditioner aie not worth preparing. He will buy them much cheaper than he can make them. Great care hoAvever is neceflary to obtain them genu- ine. They are often adulterated, and ought never to be pur chafed uniefs from perfons of known veiacky. Such of them as are in common ufe, are incerted in the lift of drugs and medicines. Their proper dofes, and manner of application, are mentioned in the prac- ticed part ofthe Book, wherever they are prefcribed. Such articles of medicine as are to be found in die houfe or gar- den of almoft every peafant, as barley, eggs, onions, &c. are like- wife for the moft part, omitted. It is needlefs to fwell a lift of me- dicines with fuch things as can be obtained whenever they are want- ed, and which fpcil by being kept. The preparation-- made and fold by diftillers and confedioners are alfo generally left out. Thefe people, by operating upon a larg- er plan generally make things better, while it is in their power to afford them m«ch cheaper, than they can be prepared bv anv pri- va: ■: hand. - ' The **5 INTRODUCTION. The quantity ordered of every medicine is as fmaU as well couli be prepared, both to prevent unneceffary expence, and that the medicine might not fpoil by keeping., Almoft every medicine fuf* fers by being kept, and fliould be ufed as foon after it has been pre- pared as poffible. Even fimple drugs are apt to fpoil, and fhould therefore be laid in fmall quantities; they either rot, are confumed by infeel ■-, or evaporate fo as to lofe their peculiar take or flavour, and often become quite infignificant. In the preparations of medicines,I have generally followed the moil approved difpenfatories; but have taken die liberty to differ from them Avhenever my own obfervations, or thofe of other prac- deal AA-riters, on whofe judgment I could depend, fuggefted a* Improvement. In feveral compofitions, the ingredient on wh'ch the efficacy of the medicine chiefly depends is increafed, while the auxiliaries, which are generally ordered in fuch trifling quantities as to be of no importance, are left out, or only fuch of diem retained as are neceffary to give the medicine a proper confiftence, or the like. The colouring ingredients are Iikewife for the moft part omitted. They increafe the price and bulk ofthe medicine, without adding any thing to its value. It would be well if they were never ufed at all. Medicines are often adulterated for the fake of a colour. Acrid and even poifonous fobflances are for this purpofe, fome- times introduced into thofe medicines which ought to be moil bland and emollient. Ointment of elder, for example, is often mi*- ed with verdegrife to give it a fine green colour, which entirely fruftrates the intention of that mild ointment. Thofe Avho wifh to obtain genuine medicines fhculd pay no regard to their colour. Some regard is HkeAvife paid to expence. Such ingredients as greatly increafe the price of any compofiticn, without adding con- fiderably to its virtue, are generally either omitted, or fomewhat lefs expenfive fubftituted in their place. Medicines are by no means powerful in proportion to their price. The cheapeft are often the beft ; befides, they are the leaft apt to be adulterated, and are al- ways moft ready to be obtained. With regard to the method of compounding medicines, 1 have ger.cnfop followed that which feemed to be the moft fimple. and natural, mentioning the different fteps of the procefs in the fame order in which they ought to be taken, Avithout paying an impli- cit regard to the method of other difpenfatories. For many ofthe remarks concerning the preparation, &c. of medicine1;, I have been obliged to the author ofthe New Difpenfa- »jry. The other obfervations are either fuch as have occurred t« myklf in pradice, or have been fuggefted in the courfe of reading, by authors whofe names I am not able diflindly to recoiled. I have followed the alphabetical order, both with regard to the fimples and preparations. A more feientific method Avould have been agreeable to fome perfons, but lefs ufeful to the generality of readers. The different claffes of medicine have no great pepend- ence upon one another, and, Avhere they have, it is hard to fay Via ji fliould ftand foil or Ixfc ; no doubt the fimple preparations ought INTRODUCTION. 4iS ought to precede the more compound. But all the advantages arif- ing from this method of arrangement, do not appear to be equal to that fingk one, of being able, on the fir ft opening of the book, to find out any article, which, by the alphabetical order, is rendered quite eafy. The dofe of every medicine is mentioned Avhenever it appeared neceflary. When this is omitted it is to be underftood diat the me- dicine may be ufed at difcretion. The dole mentioned is always for an adult, unlefs when the contrary is exprefled. It is not an eafy matter to proportion the dofes of medicine exadly to the different agesjconftitution, Sec. of patients; but, happily for mankind, math- ematical exadnefs is by no means neceflary. Several attempts have been made to afcertain the proportional dofes for the different ages and conftitutions of patients ; but after aH that can be faid on this fubjed, a great deal muft be left to the judgment and fkill ofthe perfon who adminiflers the medicine. The following general proportion may be obferved ; but they are by no means intended for exad rules. A patient between twenty and fourteen may take two thirds of the dofe ordered for an adult j from fourteen to nine, one half; from nine to fix, one third ; from fix to foor, one fourth ; from four to two, one fixth ; from two to one a tenth ; and below one, a twelfth. Difpenfatories are ufually written in the Latin language. Evert. authors who write in Englifh, generally give their prefcriptinns in Latin ; and fome of them fliewfo great an attachment to the lan- guage, as firft to write their recipes in it and afteiwards tranilate them ; while others, to compromife the matter, write the one half in Latin and the other in Englilh. What peculiar charm a medi- cal prefcription, Y.hen written in Latin, may have, I fhall not pre- tend to fay ; but have ventured to make ufe of the plaineft Englifh X could, and hope my prefcriptions will fucceed no Avorfefor it. The Apothecary's weights, and the Eaglifh wine meaf- yres, are ufed throughout the whole book, the different denomina- tions of which will appear from the following Table ; A Pound contains tivelve ounces. An Ounce - eight drachms. A Drachm - three fcruples. A. Scruple - twenty grains. A Gallom contains eight pints. A Pint - fixteen cunces. An Ounce « eight drachms. A Spoonful is the meafure of half an ounce. MEDICINAL PREPARATIONS. BALSAMS. THE fubje& of this fe&ion is not the natural balfams, but cer- tain compositions, which, from their being fuppofed to pof- fefs balfamic qualities, generally go by that name. This clafs of medicines was formerly very numerous, and held in great efteem ? modern practice, however, has juftly reduced it to a very narrow coir.pafs. Anodyne balfam. Take of white fpanifh foap, one ounce; opium unprepared, two drachms -r rectified fpirit of Avine, nine ounces. Digeft tbem to- gether in a gentle heat three days: then ftrain off the liquor, and add to it three drachms of camphor. This balfam, >'as its title expreffes, is intended to eafe pain. It is of fervice iu violent flrains and rheumatic complaints, wheanot attended with inflammation. It muft be rubbed with a warm hand on the part affecled} or a linen rag moiftened with it may be appli- ed to the part, and renewed every third or fourth hour, till the pain abates. If the opium i» left out, this will be the Saponacious Balfam. Locatelk's Balfam. Take of olive oil one pint; Strafburg turpentine and yellow wax, of each half a pound rred faunders, fix drachms. Melt the wax with fome part of the dil over a gentl* fire; then adding the remaining part ofthe oil and the turpentine; afterwards mix in the faunders previoufly reduced to a powder, and keep them ftirring together till the balfam is cold. This balfam is recommended in erofions of the inteftines, the dy- fentery, hemorrhages, internal bruifes, and in fome complaints of; the breaft. Outwardly it is ufed for healing and cleanfing wounds and ulcers. The dofe, when taken internally, is from two fcruples to two drachms. The vulnerary Balfam. Take of b.en«oin> powdered, three ounces ; balfam of Peru, two- ounces ; hepatic aloes, in powder, half an ounce ; rectified fpirk o£ wine, two pints. Digeft them in a gentle heat for three days, and then ftrain the Balfam. This balfam, or rather tinfture, is applied externally to heal re- cent wounds and brtaifes. It is Iikewife employed internally to re- move coughs, afthmas, and other complaints cf the breaft. It is faid to eafe the cholic,cleanfe the kidnies,and to heal internal ulceis,S:c. The dofe is from twenty to fixty drops. This, though a medicine of fome value, does not deferve the ex- travagant encomiums Avhich have been beftoA\eJ on it. It ha* been celebrated under the different names of The Cot::i.;ander*s Bal- fam. Percian Balfam, Balfam cf Berne, IVads'$ Balfam, Friar's BA-- fk't*, Jefuis's Drops, Turlington's Drop;f Sec. BOLUSES. APPENDIX, **§ BOLUSES. AS bolufes are intended for immediate ufe, volatile falts and o- ther ingredients improper for being kept, are admitted into their compofition. They are generally compofed of powders, with a proper quantity of fyrup, conferve,or mucilage. The lighter pow- ders are commonly made up with fyrup,and the more ponderous,as mercury &c. with conferve ; but thofe of the Kghter tend would be more conveniently made up with mucilage, as it intreafes then. bulk lefs than the other addition*, and Iikewife occafions the medi- cine to pafo down more eafily. Aftringent Bolus. Take of alum, in powder fifteen grains ; gum kino, five grains j fyrup a fufficient quantity to make a bolus. ' In an exceftive How cf the menfes, and other violent difcharges of blood, proceeding from relaxation,this bolus may begu'en ever? four or five hours, till the difcharge abates. Diaphoretic Bolus. Take of gum guaiacum,in powder,ten grains; flowers of fulphur and cream of tartar, of each one fcruple i fimple fyrup, a fufficient quantity. In rheumatic complaints,and diforders of the fkin, this bolus may be taken twice a day. It will alfo be of fervice in the htflaramator/ quinfey. Mercurial Bolus. Take of calomel, fix grains; conferve of rofes, half a drachm. Make a bolus. Where; mercury is necefiary, this bolus may be taken twfee Of thrice a-AA-eek. It may be taken ever night •, and if it does not opc» rate, a feiv grains of jalap will be proper next day to carry it off. Bolus $f Rhubarb and Mercury. Take of the beft rhubarb, in powder, from a fcruple to half * drachm ; of calomel, from four to fix grams ; fimple fyrup^a fufh- cient quantity to make a bolus. This is a proper purge in hypochondriac eonflittttioris : but ifsf principal intention is to expel worms. Where a fttoriger purge is neceffary,jalap maybe ufed hiftead of*he rhubarb. Pecloral Bolus. Take of fperma-ceti, a fcruple ; gum ammoniac, ten grams ; fafe of hartfhom, fix grains ; fimple fyrup, as much as will make them into a bolus. This bolus is given in colds and coughs of lorg ftanding, afthmas, and beginning confumptions of the lungs. It is generally proper to bleed the patient before he begins to ufe it, Purging Bolus. Take of jalap, in powder, a fexuplfc; cream or taitar.tAvo fcru- ples. Let diem be rubbed together, and formed into a bolus, with fimple fyrup. Where 3.20 APPENDIX. Where a mild purge is wanted, this will anfwer the purpofe very well. If a ftronger dofe is. neceffary, the jalap may be increafed to half a, drachm or upwards* CATAPLASMS AND SINAPISMS. CATAPLASMS poffes few or no virtues fuperior to a poultice, which may be fo made, as, in moft cafes, to fupply their place,. They are chiefly intended either to acl as difcutients, or to piomote fuppuration ; and as they may be of fervice in fome cafes, we fhall give a fpecimen of each kind. Difcutient Cataplafm. Take of barley-meal, fix ounces ; trefh hemlock leaves, bruifed, two ounces ; vinegar a fufficient quantity. Boil the meal and hem- lock in the vinegar for a little, and then add two drachms of the fu- gar of lead. Ripening Cataplafm. Take of white lilly root, four ounces ; fat figs and raw onions, bruifed, of each one ounce ; yellow baflilicum ointment, two oun- ces, gum galbanum, half an ounce ; linfeed meal, as much as ne- ceflary. Boil the roots along with the figs in a fufficient quantity of, water ; then bruife and add to them the other ingredients, fo as to form the whole into a foft cataplafm. The galbanum muft be pre- vioufly diffolved with the yolk of an egg. Where it is neceffary to promote fuppuration,this cataplafm may be ufed by thofe who chufe to be at the trouble and expence of mak- ing it. For my part, I have never found any application more pro- per for this purpofe than a poultice of bread and milk, with a fuffi- cient quantity of either boiled or raw onion in it, and foftened with oil or frefh butter. Sinapifms. Sinapifms are employed to recal the blood and fpirits to a weak part, as in the palfy and atrophy. They are alfo of fervice in deep feated pains, as the fciatica, &c. When the gout feizes the head or the ftomach, they are applied to the feet to bring the diforder to thefe parts. They are Iikewife applied to the patient's foles in the low ftate of fevers. They fhould not be fuffered to lie on however, till they have raifed blifters, but till the parts become red, and will con- tinue fo when preffed with the finger. The finapifm is only a poultice made with vinegar inftead of milk, and rendered warm and ftimulating by the addition of muftard, horfe-radifh, or garlic. The common finapifm is made by taking crumbs of bread and muftard-feed in powder, of each equal quantities ; ftrong vinegar, as much as is fufficient, and mixing them fo as to make a poultice. When finapifms of a more ftimulating nature are wanted, a little bruifed garlic maybe added to the above. CLYSTER'S- CLYSTFR$. 42J CLYSTERS. THIS clafs of medicines is of more importance than is general- ly imagined. Clyfters ferve not only to evacuate the contents of the belly, but alfo to convey very active medicines into the fyf- tem. Opium for example, may be adminiftered in this way Avhen it will not fit upon the flomach, and alfo in larger dofes than at any time it can be taken by the mouth. The Peruvian bark may Iike- wife be, Avith good effect, adminifteiedjin form of clyfter to perfons who cannot take it by the mouth. A fimple clyfter can feldom do hurt, and there are many cafes where it may do much good. A clyfter even of warm water, by ferving as a fomentation to the parts, may be of confiderable fervice in inflammations of the bladder, and the lower inteftines, Sec. Some fubftances, as the fmoke of tobacco, maybe thrown into the bowels in this way, which cannot by any other means whatever. This may eafily be effecled by means of a pair of hand-bellows, with an apparatus fitted to them for that purpofe. Nor is the ufe of clyfters confined fo medicines. Aliment may alfo be conveyed in this way. Perfons unable to fwalloAv, have been, for a confiderable time, fupported by clyfters. Etmllient Clyfter. Take of linfeed tea, and new milk, each fix ounces. Mix them. If fifty or fixty drops ef laudanum be added to this, it will fup- ply the place of the Anodyne Clyfter. Laxative Clyfter. Take of milk and water each fix ounces; fweet oil or frefh but- ter, and brown fngar, of each two ounces. Mix them. If an ounce of Glauber's fait or two table fpoonfuls of common fait, be added to this, it will be the Purging Clyfter. Carminative Clyfter. Take of camomile floivers, an ounce; anife-feed, half an ounce, Boil in a pint and a half of Avater to one pint. In hyfteric and hypochondriac complaints this may be adminift- ered inftead of the Fcetid Clyftcr, the fmell of which is fo difagreea- ble to moft patients. Oily Clyfter. To four ounces ofthe infufion of camomile flowers, add an eoual quantity of Florence oil. e This clyfter is beneficial in bringing off the fmall worm; lodged in the lower parts of the alimentary canal. When given fo chil- dren the quantity muft be proportionably kffened. Starch Clyfter. Take jelly of flarch, four ounces; linfeed oil, half an ounce. Liquify the jelly over a gentle fire, and then mix in the oil. In the dyfentery or bloody flux, this clyflermay be adminiftered after 42 a APPENDIX. after every loose ftool, toheal the ulcerated inteftines and blunt the fharpnefs of corroding humours. Fmty or fifty drops of laudanum mag he added; in which cafe, it Will generally fupply the place of the aftringent clyfter. Turpentine clyfter. Take of common decoction, jten ounces; Ventre terpentine, dif- folved with the yolk of an egg, half on ounce ; florence oil, one ounce. Mix them. The diuretic clyfter is proper irt obftriitfions of the urinary paf- fages, and in coliky complaints proceeding.from gfaveh Vinegar clyfter. This clyfter is made by mixing three ounces oftfhiegar wifh five of Avater gruel. It anfwers all the purpofes of a common clyfter , with the pecu- liar advantage of being proper either in inflammatory or putrid dif- orders, efpecially in the latter. c3* We think it unneceirary to give more examples of this clafs of medicines, as ingredients adapted to any particular intention may be occafionally added to one or other of the above forms. COLLYRIA, OR EYE-WATERS. EYE-WATERS have been multiplied without number, almoft every perfon pretending to be pOlTefled of fome fecret prepa- ration for the cure of foteeyed; I have examined many of them, and find that they are pretty much alike, the bafis of moft of them being either alum, vitriol or lead. Their cffefts evidently are, to brace and reftore the tone ofthe parts : hence they aie principally of fervice in flight inflammations; and in that relaxed flat*of the parts which is inducedU>y obftinate ones. Camphor is commonly added to thefe compoffitions; btit as it fel- dom incorporates properly with the water, it oaft be of little ufe. B< it,.; and other earthy fnbftances, as they do not diffolve in water, are likeAvife unfit for this purpofe,. Collyrium of Alum. Take of alum, half a drachm; agitate it well together with-the svhite of one egg. This is the collyrium of Riverius. It is ufed In ififlammatJon of the eyes, to allay heat, and reftrain the flux of humours. It muft be fpread upon linen, and applied to the eyes j but fliould not be kept on above three or four hours at a time. Vitriolic Collyrium. Take of white vitriol, half a drachm; tofe Avater, fix ounces'. Diffolve the vitriol in the water- and filter the liquor. This, though fimple, is perhaps equal in virtue to moft of the celebreted collyria. It is an ufeful application in weak, watery, and Inflamed eyes. Though the flighter inflammations will generally yield to it, yet in thofe of a more obftinate nature the afliftance of bkedmg aad Wiftciing will often be neceflary. When CONFECTIONS. 4*S When a ftrong aftringent is judged proper, a double or triple quantity of the vitriol may be ufed. I have feen a folution of four times the ftrength of the above ufed with manifeft advantage. Collyrium of Lead. Take fugar of lead, and crude fal amoniaOr, of each four grains. Diffolve them in eight ounces of common water. Forty or fifty drops of laudanum may be occauonaliy added to this collyrium. Thofe who chufe may fubftitute inftead of this the collyrium of lead recommended by Qoulard ;-wbich is made by putting twenty- five drops of his Extrad of Lead to eight ounces of watcr,aad add- ing a tea-fpoonful of brandy. Indeed, common water and brandy wrfhout any other addition, will in many cafes anfwer very well as a collyrium. An ounce or the latter may be added to five or fix ounces of the former: and the! -eyes, if weak, bathed with it night and monaing. CONFECTIONS. CONFECTIONS containing above fixty ingredients are fltH to be found in fome ofthe moft reformed difpenfatories. A*. moft ot their intentions, however, may be more certainly, and aa effectually anfwered by a few glades of wine or grains of opium, we fhall pafc over this clafs of medicine very flightly. Japonic Confection. Take Japan earth, three ounces ; tormentil root, nutmeg, oliba* n»m, of each two ounces ; opium diffolved iri a fufficient quantity of Liibon Avine, a drachm and a half ; fimple fyrup and conferve of? rofes, of each fourteen ounces. Mix and make them into an elec^ iuai y. This fupplies the place ofthe Diafcordium. The dofe pf this eleduary is from a fcruple to a drachm. CONSERVES AN Ft PRESERVES. EVERY Apothecary's ffcop was formerly fo full of thefe prepa*. ratjon?, that "it might have paffed for a confectioner'sware-- houfe. They poffefs very few medicinal properties, and may rather be elafjed among fweetmeate than medicines. They are fometimes, hoAvever, of ufe, for reducing into bolufes or pills fome of the more ponderous powders, as the preparations of iron, mercury and tin. Conferves are conipofitions of frefh vegetables and fugar, beater* together into an uniform mafs, In making thefe preparations, the leaves of vegetables muft be freed from their ftoclcs, the flowers from their cups, and the yellow of or-aage-peel taken off with a rafp. They are then to be pounded in a marble mortar, with a wooden peftle, into afrnoothmafs ; after which thrice their Aveighi. a -MISur 'S ponwpnly addedVy degrees.and the beating conti- nued till they are uniformly mixed ; but the conferve will be better itomy twice its weight of fugar be -Added. Thoif 424 APPENDIX. Thofe who prepare large quantities of conferve generally reduce the vegetables to a pulp by the means of a millgand afterwards beat them up with the fugar. Conferve of red Rofes. Take a pound of redrofe buds, cleared of their heels; beat them Avell in a mortar, and, adding by degrees tAA-o pounds of double-re- fined fugar, in powder, make a conferve. After the fame manner are prepared the conferves of orange-peel, lofemary floAvers,fea-worm-wood,-»of the leaves of wood-forrel, &c. The conferve of rofes is one ofthe moft agreeable and ufeful pre- parations belonging to this elafs. A drachm or two of it, diffolv- ed in warm milk, is ordered to be given as a gentle reftringent in weaknefs of the ftomach, and Iikewife in phthifical coughs, and fpitting of blood. To have any confiderable effecls, hoAvever, it muft be taken in large quantities. Conferve of Sloes. This may be made by boiling the floes gently in water,being care- ful to take them out before they burft ; afterwards exprefling the juice, and beating it up with three times its weight of fine fugar. In relaxation ofthe uvula and glands of the throat, this makesaft excellent gargle, and may be ufed at difcrction. Preferves are made by fleeping or boiling frefii vegetables,firft in Water, and afterwards in fyrup, or a folution of fugar. The fubjetf is either preferved moift in the fyrup, or taken out and dried, that the fugar may candy upon it. The laft is the moft ufeful method. Candied Orange-peel. Soak Seville prange-peel in feveral waters, till it loofes its bitter- nefs ; then boil it in a folution or double-refined fugar in water, till it becomes tender and tranfparent. Candied lemon-peel is prepared in the fame manner. It is needlefs to add more of thefe preparations, as they belong rather to the art of the confectioner than to that of the apothecary* DECOCTIONS. ATER readily extracts the gummy and faline parts ofvegi etables ; and though its action is chiefly confined to thefe,yet the refinous and oily being intimately' blended Avith a gummy and faline, are in great part taken up along with them. Hence watery decoftions and infufions of vegetables, conftitute a large, and not unufeful, clafs of medicines. Although moft vegetables yield their virtues to water, as well by infufion as decodtion, yet the latter is often neceffary, as it faves time, and does in a few minutes what tiie other would require hours, and fometimes days, to effeft. The medicines of this clafs are all intended for immediate ufe. Decoction of Ahhoca. Take ofthe roots of maifhmallows, moderately dried, tlire* ounces ; raifins of the fun, one ounce ; water three pints. Bell w DECOCTIONS. 4*5 Boil the ingredients |n the water till one third of it is confirmed ; afterwards ftrain the decodion and let it ftand for fome time tofefr- de. If the roots be thoroughly dried, they muft be boiled till one half the water be confumed. . In coughs, and fharp defludions upon the lung*, this decoction may be ufed for ordinary drink. The common De*ottion. Take of camomile flowers, one ounce ; elder flowers, and fweet fennel feeds, of each half an ounce ; water, tAvo quarts. Boil them for a little, and then ftrain the decodion. Medicine equally good may be prepared by infufing the ingre- dients for fome hours in boiling water. This decodion is chiefly intended as the bafis of clyfters, to which other ingredients may be occafionally added. It will like- wife ferve as a common fomentation, fpirit of wine or other things being added in fuch quantity as the cafe may require. Decoction of Logwood. Boil three ounces of the fhavings, or chips, of logwood, in four pints of water, till one half the liquor is wafted. Two or three ounces of fimple cinnamon water may be added to this decodion. In fluxes of the belly, where the ftronger aftringents are improp- er, a tea-cupful of this decodion may be taken with advantage three or four times a-day. Decoction of the bark. Boil an ounce ofthe Peruvian bark, grofsly powdered, in a pint and a half of water to one pint; then ftrain the decodion. If a tea fpoonful of the weak fpirit of vitriol be added to this medicine, it renders both more agreeable and efficacious. Compound decoction of the bark. Take of Peruvian bark and Virginian fnake-root, grofsly pow- dered, each three drachms. Boil them in a pint of water to one half. To the ftrained liquor add an ounce and a half of aromatic water. Sir John Pringle recommends this as a proper medicine towards the decline of malignant fevers, when the pulfe is low, the voice weak, and the head affeded with a ftupor but with little delirium* The dofe is four fpoonfuls every fourth or fixth hour. Decoction cf Sarfiparilla. Take of frefh farfaparilla root, fliced and bruifed, two ounces ; fliavings of guaiacum ivood, one ounce. Boil over a flow fire, in three quarts of water, to one ; adding towards the end, half an ounce of faiTafras woodj three drachms of liquorice. Stiain the decodion. This may either be employed as an affiftant to a courfe of mer- curial alteratives, or taken after the mereury has been ufed for fome time. It ftrengthens the ftomach, and reftores flefh and vigour to habits emaciated by the venereal difeafe. It. may alfo be taken in the rliumatifm, and cutaneous diforders proceeding from foulnefs of t'ff 42-5 APPENDIX. ofthe blood and juices. For all thefe intentions it Is greatly prc£ erable to the Decodion of Woods. This decodion may be taken, from a pint and a half to tvr« quarts in the day. The folloAving decodion is faid to be fimilar to that ufed by Kennedy, in the cure of the venereal diieafe, and may fupply the place of Liibon diet drink : Take farTa-pai ilia, three ounces ; liquorice and mev.erion root, of each half an ounce ; ihavings of guaiacum and falfifras wood^ of each one ounce ; and crude antimony, poAvdered an ounce and a half. Infufe thefe ingredients in eight pints of boiling water for twenty four hours, then boil them till one half of the water is con" fumed ; afterwards ilraui the dec idion. This decodion may be ufed in die fame manner as the preced- ing- Decoction, of Stneka. Take of Seneka rattle-fnake root, one ounce ; water, a part and a half. Boil to one pint, and drain. This decodion is recommended in the pleurify, dropfy, rheuma«< tifm, and fome obftinate diforders of the fkin. The dofe U two ounces, three or four times a-day, or oftener, if the ftomacn wiii bear it. White Decoction. Take of the pureft chalk, in powder, two ounces ; grim atabic, half an ounce ; water, three pints. Boil to one quart, and ftrain the decodion. This is a proper drink in acute difeafes, attended with, or inclin- ing to, a loofneefs, and where acidities abound in the ftomach or bowels. It is peculiarly proper for children when ailiided with fournefs of the ftomach, and for perfons who are fubjed to th* heartburn. It may be fweetened with fugar, as it is nfed, and tAvo or three ounces of fimple cinnamon water added to it. An ounce of powdered chalk, mixed with two pints of water, will occafionally fupply the place of this decodion, and alfo of the chalk julep. DRAUGHTS. THIS is a proper form for exhibiting fuch medicines as are in-1 tended to operate immediately, and which do nocn^cd to hi frequently repeated, as purgej, vomfo, and a feAv others, AA'hich are to be taken at one dole. Where a medicine requires to be ufed for any length of time, it is better to mike up a larger quantity of it at once, which faves both trouble and e-cpence. Anodyne D>-a it Jit. Take of liquid laudanum, twenty five drops ; fimple cinnamon- water, an ounce ; common fyrup, tAvo drachms. Mix them. In exeefllve pain, where bleeding is not neceifary, and In great reftlefsnefs, thi- composing draught m»y be-tak-.-i. a:vi repeated oc- cafionally. dr.: relic ELECTUARIES. 4** Diuretic Draught. Take of the diuretic fait, two fcruples; fyrup of poppies, two •brachms: fimple cinnamon-water, and common water, of each an •unce. This draught is of fervice in an obftrudion or deficiency of urine-. Purging Draughts. Take of manna, an ounce; fbluble tartar, or Rochel fait, from three to four drachms. Diffolve, in three ounces of boiling water; to which add Jamaica pepper~u?ater, half an ounce. As manna fometime* will not fit upon the ftomach, an ounce or ten drachms ofthe bitter purging falts, diffolved in four ounces of water, may be taken Inftead ofthe above. ' Tho.e Avho cannot take falts, may ufe the folloAving draught: Take of ja'ia < in powder, a fcruple ; common water, an ounce ;• aromatic tindure, fix drachms. Rub the jalap with twice its weight •f fugar, and add to ir, the other ingredients. Sweating draught. Take fpirit of Mindcrerus, tAvo ounces; fait of hartfhom, five grains; fimple cinnamon-water, and fyrup of poppies, cf each half an ounce. Make them into a draught. In recent colds and rheumatic complaints, this draught is of fer- vice. To promote its effeds, hoAvever, the patient ought to drink freely of warm water gruel, or of fome other weak diluting liquor. Vomiting Draughts. Take of ipecacuanha in poAvder a fcruple; water, an ounce ; fimple fyrup a drachm. Mix them.—Perfons who require a ftronger vomit.may add to the above half a grain, or agrain,ofemetic tartar. Thofe Avho do not chufe the poAvder, may take ten drachms of the ipecacuanha Avine; or half an ounce of the wine, and an equal quantity of the fyrup of fquills. ELECTUARIES. ELECTUARIES are generally compofed ofthe lighter powders, mixed with fyrup, honey, conferve, or mucilage, into fuch a eonfiftence, that the powders may neither fepaiate by keeping, nor the mafs prove tooftiff for lwallowing. They receive chiefly the mild- er alterative medicines,'and fuch as are not ungieatful to the palate, Aftringent ekduaries, and fuch as have pulps of fruit in them, fhould be prepared only in fmall quantities; as aftringent medicines lofe their virtues by being kept in this form, and the pulps of fruit are apt to ferment. For the extradion of pulps it will be'neceflary to boil unripe. fruits, and ripe ones if they are dried, in a fmall quantity of water till they become foft. The pulp is then to be preffed out through a ftrong hair ficve, or thin cloth,and afterwards boiled to a due con- fluence, in an earthern veffel, over a gentle file, taking care to pre- vent the matter from burning by continually ftirring it. The pulps of fi uit that arc both ripe and frefh may be preffed out without any previous boiling. Lenitive *at APPENDIX. Lenitive Electuary. Take ef fenna, in fine powder, eight ounces; coriander fted, al- fo in powder, four ounces; pulp of tamarinds and of French prunes, each a pound. Mix the pulps and powders together, and with a fufficient quantity of fimple lyrup, reduce the whole into an electu- ary. A tea-fpoonful of this eleduary, taken two or three times a-day, generally proves an agreeable laxative. It Iikewife ferves as a con* venient vehicle for exhibiting more adive medicines, as jalap*, fcammony, and fuch like. This may fupply the place ofthe eleduary of Caffia. Electuary for the dyfentery. Take of the Japonic confedion, two ounces; Locatelli's balfam, one ounce; rhubarb in powder half an ounce; fyrup of marfh- jnallows, enough to make an eleduary. It is often dangerous in dyfenteries to give opiates and aftringents, vrithout interpofing purgatives. The purgative is here joined with thefe ingredients, which renders this a very fafe and ufeful medi- cine for the purpofes exprefled in the title. About the bulk of a nutmeg fhould be taken twice or thrice a- day, as the fymptoms and conftitution may require. Electuary for the Epilepfy. Take of Peruvian bark in powder, an ounce; of powdered thi, and wild valerian root, each half an ounce j fimple fyrup, enough to make an eleduary. Dr. Mead direds a drachm of an eleduary fimilar to this to be taken even and morning, in the epilepfy, for the fpace of three months. It will be proper, hoAvever, to difcontinue the ufe of it for a few days every now and then. I have added the powdered tin, becaufe the epilepfy often proceeds from worm?. Electuary for the Gonorrhara. Take of lenitive eleduary, three ounces; jalap and rhubarb, i» powder, of each two drachms; nitre, half an ounce ; fimple fyrup, enough to make an eleduary. During the inflammation and tenfion of the urinary paffage* which accompany a virulent gonorrhaea, this cooling laxative may bo ufed with advantage. The dofe is a drachm, or about the bulk of a nutmeg, two or three times a-day; more or lefs, as may be neceffary to keep the body gently open. An eleduary made ofthe cream of tartar and fimple fyrup will betafionally fupply the place of this. After the inflammation is gone off, the following eleduary may be ufed: Take of lenitive eleduary, two ounces; balfam ofcapivi, one Ounce; gum guaiacum and rhubarb,in poAvder, of each two drachms j ■fimple fyrup, enough to make an eleduary. The dofe is the fame as of the preceding. EleSuatp EMULSIONS. 429 Electuary ofthe Bark. Take of Peruvian bark, m powder, three ounces; cafcarilla, half an ounce; fyrup of ginger, enough to make an eleduary. Ia the cure of obftinate intermitting fevers, the bark is aififled by the cafcarilla. In bedic habits, however, it will be better to leave out the cafcarilla, and put three drachms cf crude fal am- moniac in its Head. Electuary for the Piles Take flowers of fulphur, one ounce; cream of tartar, half ao ounce; treacle a fufficient quantity to form an eleduary. A tea-fpoonful of this may be taken three or four tia.es a-day. Electuary for the Palfy. Take of powdered muftard-feed, and conferve of rofes, each an •unce ; fyrup of ginger, enough to make an eleduary. A tea-fjoenful of this may be taken three or four times a-day. EleUuary for the Rheumatifm. Take of conferve of rofes, two ounces; cinnabar of antimony, levigated, an ounce and a half; gum guaiacum,in poA\der,an ounce; fymp of ginger., a fuflicient quantity to make an eleduary. In obftinate rheumatifms, which are not accompanied with a fe- ver a tea-fpoonful of this eleduary may be taken twice a-day with eonfiderable advantage. EMULSIONS. EMULSIONS, befides their ufe as medicines, are alfo prope: vehicles for certain fubftances, which could not otheiwife be conveniently taken in a liquid form. Thus camphor, triturated with almonds,readily unites with water into an emulfion. Pure oils, bal- fams, refins, and other iimilar fubftances, are Iikewife rendered mifcible with water by the intervention of mucilages. Comynon Emulfion. Take of fweet almonds an ounce; bitter almonds, a drachm; wa- ter, two pints. Let the almonds "be blanched, and beat up in a marble mortar adding the water by a little and little, fo as to make an emulfion; afterwards let it be ftrained. Arabic Emulfion. This is made in the fame manner as the above, adding to the al- monds, while beating two ounces and an half ot the mucilage of gum arabic. Where foft cooling liquors are neceflary, thefe emulfions may be ufed as ordinary drink. Camphorated Emulfion. Take of camphor, half a drachm ; fweet almonds, half a dozen; wfcite fugar, half an ounce j mint water, eight ounces. Grind the camphor 43« APPENDIX. camphor and almonds well together in a ftone mortar, and add by degrees the mint water; then ftrain the liquors, and diffolve in it the fugar. In fevers, and other diforders which require the ufe of camphor, a table fpoonful of this emulfion may be taken every two or three hours. Emulfinn of gum Ammoniac. ICfoe of gum ammoniac, two drachms; water, eight ounces. Grind the ^utn with the water poured upon it by little and little, till it is diffo.ved Thiseoiuliion is ufed for attenuating tough, vifcid phlegm, and promoting expedoration. In obftinate coughs, two ounces of the iyrup of poppies may be added to it. The dofe is two table fpoon- fuls thres or four times a day. Oily Emulfion. Take of foft water, fix ounces; volatile aromatic fpirit, two drachms ; Florence oil, an ounce; fluke them well together, and add, of fymple fyrup, haifan ounce. In recent colds and coughs, this emulfion is generally of fervice; but if the cough proves obftinate, it will fucceed better, when made wkh the paregoric elixir of the Edingburgh Difpenfatory, inftead- of the volatile aromatic fpirit. A table-fpoonful of it may be ta- ken every two or three hours. EXTRACTS. EXTRACTS are prepared by boiling the fubjed in water, and evaporating the ftrained decodion to a due confiftence. By this procefs fome of the more adive parts of plants are freed from the ufelefs, indiffoluble earthy matter, Avhich makes the larger fhare of their bulk. Water, however, is not the only menftruum ufed in the preparation of extrads; fometimes it is joined Avith fpirits, and at other times redified fpirit alone is employed for that purpofe. Extrads are prepared from a variety of different drugs, as the bark, gentian, jalap, &.»..; but, as they require a troublefome and te- dious operation, it will be more convenient for a private pradition- er to purchafe what he needs of them from a profeffed druggiil, than to prepare them himfelf. Such of them as are generally ufed are. Inferted in our lift of fuch drugs and medicines asare to be kept for private pradice. FOMENTATIONS. FOMENTATIONS are generally intended either to eafe pain, by taking offtention and fpafm; or to brace and reftore the tone snd vigour of thofe parts to Avhich they are applied. The full of thelc intentions may generally be anfwered by Avarm Avater, and rhe fecond by cold. Certain fubftances, however, are ufually added to water with a view to highten its effects, as anodyne, aromatics, *..7ringents, Sec. We Hia.ll there fubjoin a few of the moil ufeful medicated GARGLE& 43 * medicated fomentations, that people may have it in their power to make ufe of them if they chufe. Anodyne Fomentation. Take of white poppy-heads, two ounces ; elder flowers, half aa ounce ; Avater, three pints. Boil till one pint is evaporated, and (Irani out the liquor. This fomentation, as its title exprefFes, is ufed for relieving acute pain. Aromatic Fomentation. Take of Jamaica peppc, half an ounce ; red wme, a pint. Boil them for a little, and then ftrain the liquor. This is intended; not only as a topical application for external complaints, but alio for relieving the internal parts. Pains ofthe boAvels, which accompany dyfenteries and diarrhasas, flatulent cholics, uneafiners ofthe ftomach, and retchings to vonvt, are fre- quently abated by fomenting the abdomen and region of the ftom- ach with the Avarm liquor. Common Fomentation. Take tops of ivormwood and camomile floAvers, dried, of each tAvo ounces ; watcrtwo quarts. After a flight boiling, pour off the liquor. Brandy or fpirit of wine may be added to this fomentation, in fuch quantity as the particular circumftances of the caie fhall re- quire ; but thefe are not ahvays neceffary. Emollient Fom-nalion. This is the fame as the common decodion. Strengthening lometdation. Take of oak bark, one ounce ; granate peel, half an ounce; alum, tAvo drachms ; fmith's forge water, three pints. Boil the Avater with the bark and peel to the confumption of one third; then ftrain the remaining decodion and diffolve alum in it. This aftringent liquor is employed as an external fomentatio* io weak parts ; it may alfo be ufed internally. —■»«saEli-'*-»— GARGLES. HOWEVER trifling this clafs of medicines appear, they are by no means without their ufe. They feldom indeed cure difeafes, but they often alleviate very difagreeable fymptrms ; as parchednefs of the mouth, foulnefs of the tongue and fauces, Sec. they are peculiarly ufeful in fevers and fore throat--. In the latter, a gargle will fometimes remove the diforder, and in the former fc\~ things are more refrefliing or agreeable to the patient, than to have bis mouth frequently wafhed Avith fome foft de-ergent gargle. One advantage of thefe medicines is, that they are eafily pre- pared. A little barley Avater and honey may be had nny where ; and if to thefe be added as much vinegar as will give them an agreeable fharpnefs, they will make a very ufeful gargle for foft- Iftiing and cleanfing the mcuth. Gargle*. 4-3* APPENDIX. Gargles have the beft effed when injeded with a fyringe. Attenuating Gargle. Take of water, fix ounces; honey, one ounce ; nitre, a drachm and a half. Mix them. This cooling gargle may be ufed either in the inflammatory quin- fey, or in fevers, for cleaning the tongue and fauces. Common gargle. Take of rofe-water, fix ounces; fyrup of clove July-flowers, half an ounce; fpirit of vitriol, a fufficient quantity to give it an agreea- ble fhaipnefs. Mix them. This gargle, befides cleanfing the tongue and fauces, ads as a gentle repellent, and will fometimes remove a flight quinfey. Detergent Gargle. Take ofthe emollient gargle, a pint; tindure of myrrh, an ounce; honey two ounces. Mix them. When exulcerations require tobecleanfed,or the excretion of tough vicid faliva promoted, this gargle will be of fervice. Emoliient Gargle. Take an ounce of marfhmallow roots,and two or three figs; boil them in a quart of water till near one half of it be confirmed; then ftrain out the liquor. If an ounce of honey, and half an ounce of fpirit of fal-ammon- iac, be added to the above, it will then be an exceeding good atten- vating gargle. This gargle is beneficial in fevers, where the tongue and fauces are rough and parched, to (often tliefe parts, «tid promote the dif- charge of faliva. The learned and accurate Sir John Pringle obferves, that in the inflammatory quinfey, or ftrangulation of the fauces, little benefit arifes from the common gargles; that fuch as are of an acid nature do more harm than good, by contrading the emundoricsof the fal- iva and mucus, and thickening thofe humours; that a decodion of figs in milk and water has a contrary effed, efpecially if fome faj- ammoniac be added : by which the faliva is made thinner, and the glands brought to fecrete more freely; a circumftance always con- ducive to the cure. INFUSIONS. VEGETABLES yield nearly the fame properties to water by infufions as by decodion; and though they may require along- er time to give out their virtues in this Avay, yet it has feveral ad- vantages OArer the other; fince boiling is found to diffipate the finer parts of many bitter and aromatic fubftances, without more fully extrading their medical principles. The author of the New Difpenfatory obferves, that even from thofe vegetibles which are weak in virtue, rich infufions may be ob- tained, by returning to liquor upon frelh quantities of the fubjed, the INFUSIONS-. 433 1 he water loading itfelf more and more with the adive parts ; and that thefe loaded infufions are applicable to valuable purpofes in medicine, as they contain in a fmall compafs the finer, more fubtle, and adive principles of vegetables, in a form readily mifcible with the fluids of the human body. Bitter Infufion. Take tops ofthe leffer centaury and cammomile flowers, of eaeh half an ounce ;yellow rind of lemon and orange peel,carefully freed from the inner white part, of each tAvo drachms. Cut them in fmall pieces, and infufe them in a quart of boiling water. For indigeftion, weaknefs of the ftomach, or want of appetite,. a tea-cupful of this infufion may be taken twice or thrice a-day. Infufion of the Bark. To an ounce of the bark, in powder, add four or five table-fpoon- fuls of brandy, and a pint of boiling water. Let them infufe for two or three days. • -...,••■ This is one ofthe beft preparations of the bark for weak fto- machs. In diforders where the corroborating virtues of that medi- cine are required, a tea-cupful of it may be taken two or three times a-day. Infufion of Carduus-. Infufe an ounce of the dried leaves ofthe carduus benedidus, or bleffed thiftle, in a pint of common water, for fix hours, without Ueat ; then filter the liquor through paper. This light infufion may be given with great benefit, in weaknefs of the ftomach, where the common bitters do not agree. It may be Savoured at pleafure with cinnamon,or other aromatic materials. Infufion of Linfeed. Take of linfeed two fpoonfuls ; liquorice root, fliced, half an iounce ; boiling water three pints. Let them ftand to infufe by the fire for fome hours, and then ftrain off the liquor. If an ounce of the leaves of colt's foot be added to thefe ingre- dients, it will then be the Pedoral Infufion. Both thefe are emolli- ent mucilaginous liquors, and may be taken with advantage as or- dinary drink in difficulty of making AA'ater ;and in coughs and oth- er complaints of the bieaft. Infufon cf Rofes. Take of red rofes, dried, half an ounce ; boiling water, a quart; vitriolic acid, commonly called oil cf vitriol, half a drachm ; loaf fugar an ounce. Infufe the rofes in the water for four hours,in an unghized earth- *n veffel ; afterwards oour in the acid, and having ftrained the li- quor add to it the fugar. In an exceffive flow of the menfes, vomiting of food, and other haemorrhages, a tea-cupful of this gentle a ngen > fo'fon may ' « taken every three or fou. hours, "t iikewue makes... receding good garble. Asthe quantity of rofes ufed here cs.r have folic or no eflt^ an eg1 ail; f-; S l 4S* APPENDIX. equally valuable medicine may be prepared by mixing the acid an« water without infufion. Infufion of Tamarinds and Senna. Take of tamarinds, one ounce ; fenna, and chryftals of tartar, each two drachms. Let thefe ingredients be infufed four or five hours in a pint of boiling water ; afterwards let the liquor be ftrain- ed, and an ounce or two of the arotaatic tindure added to it. Per- fons who are eafily purged may leave out either the tamarinds or lthe cryftals of tartar. This is an agreeable cooling purge. A tea-cupful may be giveii every half hour till it operates. This fupplies the place ofthe DecoSion ofTamarindt and Senna. Spanifh Infufion. Take of Spanifh juice, cut into fmall pieces, an ounce ; fait of tartar three drachms. Infufe in a quart of boiling water for anight.- To the ftraitftd liquor add an ounce and an half of the fyrup of poppies. In refcent colds, coughs, and obftrudions of the breaft, a tea- cupful of this infufion may be taken three or four times a-day. Infufion for the Palfey. Take of horfe-radifh root fhaved, muftard-feed bruifed, each four" ounces j outer rind of orange-peel, one ounce. Infufe them in two quarts of boiling water, in a clofe veffel for twenty-four hours. In paralytic complaints, a tea-cupful of this warm ftimulating medicine may be taken three or four times a-day. It excites the ac- tion of the folids, proves diurettc,and if the patient be kept warm promotes perfpiration. If two or three ounces of the dried leaves of marfh-trefoil be ufed Inftead of the muftard, it will make the Antifcerlmt'tc Infufion. JULEPS. THE bafis of juleps is generally common water, Orfome fimple diftilledwater, Avith one third er one fourth its quantity of diftilled fpiritous water, and as much fugar or fyrup as is fufficient to render the mixture agreeable. This is fharpened with vegetable or mineral acid?, or impregnated with other medicines fuitable te the intention. Camphorated Julep. Take of camphor,one drachm ; redified fpirit ofwine,ten drops} double refined fugar, half an ounce: boiling diftilled water one pint. Rub the camphor firft with the fpirit of wine, then Avith the1 fugar ; laftly, add the Avater by degrees, and ftrain the liquor. In hyfterical and other complaints where camphor i9 proper, this julep may be taken in the dofe of a fpoonful or two as often as the flomach will bear it. Cordial Julep. Take of fimple cinnamon water, four ouriees ; Jamaica pepper* water, two ounces ; volatile aromatic fpirit, and compound fpirit of lavender* MIXTURES. 4S5 lavender, of each two drachms; fyrup of orange-peel, an ounce* Mix them. This is given in the dofe of two fpoonfuls three or four times a day, in diforders accompanied with great weaknefs and depreffion •f fpirits. Expectorating Julep. Take ofthe emulfion of gum ammoniac, fix ouncei; fyrup of fquills, two ounces. Mix them. In coughs, afthmas, and obftrudions of the breaft, two table- fpoonfuls of this julep may be taken every three or four hours. Mufk Julep. Rub half a drachm of mufk well together with half an ounce of fogar, and add to it gradually, of fimple cinnamon and peppermint water, each two ounces;of the volatile aromatic fpirit, two drachms. In the low ftate of nervous fevers, hiccupping, convulfions, and other fpafmodic affedions, two table fpoonfuls of this julep may be taken every two or three hours. Saline Julep. Diffolve tAvo drachms of fait of tartar in three ounces of frefh lemon juice, ftrained; when the effervefcence is over, add, of mint water, and common water, each two ounces ; of fimple fyrup, one ounce. This removes ficknefs at the ftomach, relieves vomiting, promotes perfpiration, and may be of fome fervice in fevers,efpecially of the inflammatory kind. Vomiting julep. Diffolve four grains of the emetic tartar in eight ounces of water, and add ro it half an ounce ofthe fyrup of clove July-flowers. In the beginning of fevers, where there is no topical inflammation, this julep may be given in the dofe of one table-fpoonful every quar- ter of an hour till it operate.. Antimonial vomits ferve not only to evacuate the contents of the ftomach, but Iikewife to promote the different excretions. Hence they are found in fevers to have nearly the fame effed as Dr. James Powder. MIXTURES. A MIXTURE differs from a julep in this refped, that it re- ceives into its compotition not only falts, cxtrads and other fubftances diffoluble in water, but alfo earths, powders, and fuch fubftances as cannot be diffolved. A mixture is feldom either an elegant or agreeable medicine. It is neverthelefs neceffary. Many ■ perfons can take a mixture, who are not able to fwallow a bolus or an eleduary; befides, there are medicines which ad better in this than in any other form. Aftringent mixture. Take fimple cinnamon Avater and common water, of each three •unces; fpirkous cinnamon water, an ounce and a half; Japonic eonfedion, half an ounce. Mix them, 1r 435 APPENDIX. In dyfenteries which are not of long ftanding, after the neceflary evacuations, a fpoonful or two of this mixture may be taken every four hours, interpofingevery fecond or third day a dofe of rhubarb. Diuretic Mixture. Take of mint water, five ounces; vinegar of fquills, fix drachms; fweet fpirit of nitre, half an ounce; fyrup of ginger, an ounce and an half. Mix them. In obftrudions of the urinary paffages, two fpoonfuls of this mixture may be taken twice or thrice a day. Laxative abforbent Mixture, Rub one drachm of magnefia alba in a mortar with ten or twelve giains ofthe beft Turkey rhubarb, and add to them three ounces of common water;, fimple cinnamon water, and fyrup of fugar, of each one ounce. As moft difeafes in infants are accompanied with accidents, this mitxure may either be given with a view to corred thefe, or to open the body. A table-fpoonful may be taken for a dofe, and repeated three times a day. To a very young child half a fpoonful will be fuflicient. When the mixture is intended to purge, the dofe may either be increafed, or the quantity of rhubarb doubled. This is one of the moft generally ufeful medicines for children with which I am acquainted. Saline Mixture. Diffolve a drachm ofthe fait of tartar in four ounces of boiling water; and Avhen cold, drop, into it fpirit of vitriol till the effervef- cence ceafes; * then add of peppermint-water, two ounces, fimple fyrup, one ouace. Wliere frefh lemons cannot be had, this mixture may occafional- ly fupply the place ofthe faline julep. Squill Mixture. Take of fimple cinnaman-water, five ounces; vinegar of fquills, one ounce; fyrup of marflimallows, an ounce and a half. Mix them. This mixture, by promoting expedoration, and the fecretion of urine, proves ferviceable in afthmatic and dropfical habits. A table fpoonful of it may be taken frequently. OINTMENTS, LINIMENTS AND CERATES. NOTWITHSTADINQ the extravagant encomiums which have been beftowed on different preparations of this kind, with regard to their efficacy in the cure of wounds, fores, &c. it is beyond a doubt, that the moft proper application to a green wound is dry lint. But though ointments do not heal wounds and fores, yet they ferve to defend them from the external air, and to retain fuch fubftances as may be neceffary for drying, deteiging, de- stroying proud flefh, and fuch like. For thefe purpofes, however. OINTMENTS, &c. 437 it will be fufficient to infert only a few of the moft fimple forms, as ingredients of a more adive nature can occafionally be added to them. Yelloru Bajilicum Ointment. Take of yallow wax, white refin, and frankincence, each a quar- ter of a pound; melt them together over a gentle fire ; then add of hogs' lard prepared, one pound. Strain the ointment while warm. This ointment is employed for cleanfing and healing wounds and ulcers. Ointment of Calamine. Take of olive oil, a pint and a half; white wax, and calamine ftone levigated, of each half a pound. Let the calamine ftone, re- duced into a fine powder, be rubbed with fome part ofthe oil, and afterwards added to the reft of the oil and wax previoufly melted together, continually ftirring them till quite cold. This ointment, which is commonly iknown by the name of Tur- ner's Cerate, is an exceeding good application in burns and excoria- tions from whatever caufe. Emollient Ointment. Take of palm oil, two pounds; olive oil, a pint and a half; yel- Ioav wax, half a pound; Venice turpentine, a quarter of a pound. Melt the wax in the oils over a gentle fire; then mix in the turpen- tine, and ftrain the ointment. This fupplies the place of Alth* Ointment. It may be ufed for an- notating inflamed parts, See Eye Ointment. Take of hogs' lard prepared, four ounces ; white wax, two drachms; tutty prepared, one ounce; melt the wax with the lard over a gentle fire, and then fprinkle in the tutty, continually ftirr- ing them till the ointment is cold. This ointment wilfbe more efficacious, and of a better confiftence, if two or three drachms of camphor be rubbed up with a little oil, and intimately mixed Avith it. * Another. Take of camphor, and calamine ftone levigated, each fix drachms; verdegrife well prepared, two drachms: hogs' lard,and mutton fuet, prefteired, of each two ounces. Rub the camphor well with the powder; afterwards mix in the lard and fuet, continuing the triture till they he perfedly united. This ointment has been long in efteem for difeafes ofthe eyes. It ought, however, to be ufed with caution, when the eyes are much inflamed or very tender. Iffue Ointment. Mix half an ounce of Spanifh flies, finely powdered, in fix ounces of yellow bafilicum ointment. ± This ointment is chiefly intended for dreffing blifters, in order to keep them open during pleafure. Ointment 43* APPENDIX. Ointment of Lead. Take of olive oil half a pint; white wax, two ounees; fugar o£ lead, three drachms. Let the fugar.of lead, reduced into a fine powder, be rubbed up with fome part of the oil, and afterwards added to the other ingredients, previouflv melted together, conti- nually ftirring them till quite cold. This cooling and gentle aftringent ointment may be ufed in all cafes where the intention is to dry and fkin over the part, as in fclad- ing, &c, Mercurial Ointment. Take of quickfilver, two ounces; hogs' lard, three ounces; mut- ton fuet, one ounce. Rub the quickfilver with an ounce of the hogs' lard in a warm mortar, till the gloubles be perfedly extin- guifhed; then rub it up with the reft of the lard and fuet previouf- ly melted together. The principal intentron of this ointment is to convey mercury in- to the body by being rubbed upon the fkin. Ointment of Sulphur.
